\newcommand{\AlertTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{1.00,0.00,0.00}{\textbf{#1}}}
\newcommand{\AnnotationTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.38,0.63,0.69}{\textbf{\textit{#1}}}}
\newcommand{\AttributeTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.49,0.56,0.16}{#1}}
\newcommand{\BaseNTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.25,0.63,0.44}{#1}}
\newcommand{\BuiltInTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.00,0.50,0.00}{#1}}
\newcommand{\CharTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.25,0.44,0.63}{#1}}
\newcommand{\CommentTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.38,0.63,0.69}{\textit{#1}}}
\newcommand{\CommentVarTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.38,0.63,0.69}{\textbf{\textit{#1}}}}
\newcommand{\ConstantTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.53,0.00,0.00}{#1}}
\newcommand{\ControlFlowTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.00,0.44,0.13}{\textbf{#1}}}
\newcommand{\DataTypeTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.56,0.13,0.00}{#1}}
\newcommand{\DecValTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.25,0.63,0.44}{#1}}
\newcommand{\DocumentationTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.73,0.13,0.13}{\textit{#1}}}
\newcommand{\ErrorTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{1.00,0.00,0.00}{\textbf{#1}}}
\newcommand{\ExtensionTok}[1]{#1}
\newcommand{\FloatTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.25,0.63,0.44}{#1}}
\newcommand{\FunctionTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.02,0.16,0.49}{#1}}
\newcommand{\ImportTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.00,0.50,0.00}{\textbf{#1}}}
\newcommand{\InformationTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.38,0.63,0.69}{\textbf{\textit{#1}}}}
\newcommand{\KeywordTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.00,0.44,0.13}{\textbf{#1}}}
\newcommand{\NormalTok}[1]{#1}
\newcommand{\OperatorTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.40,0.40,0.40}{#1}}
\newcommand{\OtherTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.00,0.44,0.13}{#1}}
\newcommand{\PreprocessorTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.74,0.48,0.00}{#1}}
\newcommand{\RegionMarkerTok}[1]{#1}
\newcommand{\SpecialCharTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.25,0.44,0.63}{#1}}
\newcommand{\SpecialStringTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.73,0.40,0.53}{#1}}
\newcommand{\StringTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.25,0.44,0.63}{#1}}
\newcommand{\VariableTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.10,0.09,0.49}{#1}}
\newcommand{\VerbatimStringTok}[1]{\textcolor[rgb]{0.25,0.44,0.63}{#1}}

% Headings: Orbitron, neon. Pandoc emits report-class \chapter / \section /
% \subsection. titlesec*-form is "easy" and supports section and below; for
% \chapter use the full \titleformat with empty label/spacing slots since
% secnumdepth is forced negative (no numbering).
\titleformat{\chapter}[hang]{\sffamily\bfseries\Huge\color{neonCyan}}{}{0pt}{}
\titlespacing*{\chapter}{0pt}{20pt}{20pt}
\titleformat*{\section}{\sffamily\bfseries\LARGE\color{neonMagenta}}
\titleformat*{\subsection}{\sffamily\bfseries\Large\color{accentPink}}
\titleformat*{\subsubsection}{\sffamily\bfseries\large\color{neonCyan}}

% Hyperref already configured by pandoc; override link colors to neon.
% \AtBeginDocument because pandoc loads hyperref AFTER our header-include,
% so \hypersetup is undefined when this file runs. linktoc=all ensures the
% TOC entries (text + page number) inherit linkcolor; without it, dotted
% leaders and page numbers stay near-invisible NavyBlue on the dark bg.
\AtBeginDocument{%
    \hypersetup{%
        linkcolor=neonCyan,%
        urlcolor=neonMagenta,%
        citecolor=neonGreen,%
        filecolor=neonCyan,%
        linktoc=all,%
    }%
}

% Pandoc syntax-highlight blocks live inside a \Shaded environment defined
% in pandoc's template. Override its background to bgCard with a thin border.
% \AtBeginDocument because pandoc defines \Shaded after our header-include.
\AtBeginDocument{%
    \renewenvironment{Shaded}{%
        \begin{tcolorbox}[%
            colback=bgCard,colframe=borderDim,boxrule=0.4pt,arc=1pt,%
            left=4pt,right=4pt,top=2pt,bottom=2pt,%
            before skip=4pt,after skip=4pt%
        ]\color{neonGreen}\ttfamily\small%
    }{\end{tcolorbox}}%
}

% Cyberpunk cover page — replaces pandoc's default \maketitle. Big stryke
% wordmark in Orbitron, neon cyan/magenta accents, version + date in dim
% terminal-style mono. \@title / \@author / \@date are populated by pandoc
% from --metadata.
\makeatletter
\renewcommand{\maketitle}{%
    \begin{titlepage}%
    \begin{center}%
    \vspace*{2cm}
    {\sffamily\bfseries\fontsize{12pt}{14pt}\selectfont\color{neonCyan}%
        \rule[0.4ex]{1.5cm}{0.4pt}\hspace{0.5em}// STRYKE //\hspace{0.5em}\rule[0.4ex]{1.5cm}{0.4pt}}%
    \par\vspace{2.5cm}%
    {\sffamily\bfseries\fontsize{72pt}{80pt}\selectfont\color{textPrim}\@title\par}%
    \vspace{1cm}%
    {\color{neonMagenta}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{1pt}\par}%
    \vspace{0.8cm}%
    {\sffamily\bfseries\fontsize{20pt}{24pt}\selectfont\color{neonMagenta}%
        LANGUAGE REFERENCE\par}%
    \vspace{0.4cm}%
    {\sffamily\fontsize{10pt}{12pt}\selectfont\color{textDim}%
        \itshape every builtin · keyword · alias · extension\par}%
    \vfill
    {\ttfamily\color{neonGreen}\Large \@date\par}%
    \vspace{0.5cm}%
    {\ttfamily\color{textDim}\normalsize \@author\par}%
    \vspace{1.5cm}%
    {\sffamily\fontsize{8pt}{10pt}\selectfont\color{borderDim}%
        \rule[0.4ex]{1cm}{0.3pt}\hspace{0.4em}MENKETECHNOLOGIES\hspace{0.4em}\rule[0.4ex]{1cm}{0.3pt}\par}%
    \end{center}%
    \end{titlepage}%
}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-parallel-primitives}
\chapter{Parallel Primitives}\label{parallel-primitives}

18 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pmap}}{pmap}}\label{pmap}

Parallel \texttt{map} powered by rayon\textquotesingle s work-stealing
thread pool. Every element of the input list is processed concurrently
across all available CPU cores, and the output order is guaranteed to
match the input order. This is the primary workhorse for CPU-bound
transforms in stryke --- use it whenever you have a pure function and a
large list. Pass \texttt{progress\ =\textgreater{}\ 1} to get a live
progress bar on STDERR for long-running jobs.

Two equivalent surface syntaxes: • Block form ---
\texttt{pmap\ BLOCK\ LIST} --- element bound to \texttt{\_} • Bare-fn
form --- \texttt{pmap\ FUNC,\ LIST} --- single-arg function name as
first argument

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# Block form}
\NormalTok{my @out = pmap \{ \_ * 2 \} 1:1\_000\_000}
\NormalTok{my @hashes = pmap sha256 @blobs, progress =\textgreater{} 1}
\NormalTok{1:100 |\textgreater{} pmap \{ fetch("https://api.example.com/item/$\_") \} |\textgreater{} e p}

\NormalTok{\# Bare{-}fn form (works for builtins and user{-}defined subs)}
\NormalTok{my @hashes = pmap sha256, @blobs, progress =\textgreater{} 1}
\NormalTok{fn dbl = \_ * 2}
\NormalTok{my @r = pmap dbl, (1:1\_000\_000)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pmaps}}{pmaps}}\label{pmaps}

Streaming parallel \texttt{map} --- returns a lazy iterator that
processes items across all CPU cores using a persistent worker-thread
pool. Unlike \texttt{pmap} which eagerly collects all results into an
array, \texttt{pmaps} yields results as they complete through a bounded
channel, so downstream consumers see output immediately. Output order is
non-deterministic (completion order). Each worker thread reuses a single
interpreter instance, making it faster than \texttt{pmap} for large
inputs.

Best for: • Pipelines with \texttt{take}/\texttt{head} --- avoids
processing the full list • Very large inputs where holding all results
in memory is impractical • Streaming pipelines where you want
progressive output

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{range(0, 1e9) |\textgreater{} pmaps \{ \_ * 2 \} |\textgreater{} take 10 |\textgreater{} ep}
\NormalTok{t 1:1e6 pmaps \{ expensive(\_) \} ep}
\NormalTok{range(0, 1e6) |\textgreater{} pmaps \{ fetch("https://api/item/$\_") \} |\textgreater{} pgreps \{ \_{-}\textgreater{}\{ok\} \} |\textgreater{} ep}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pmap\_chunked}}{pmap\_chunked}}\label{pmap_chunked}

Parallel map that groups input into contiguous batches of N elements
before distributing to threads. This reduces per-item scheduling
overhead when the per-element work is very cheap (e.g. a few arithmetic
ops). Each thread receives a slice of N consecutive items, processes
them sequentially within the batch, then returns the batch result. Use
this instead of \texttt{pmap} when profiling shows rayon overhead
dominates the actual computation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @out = pmap\_chunked 100, \{ \_ ** 2 \} 1:1\_000\_000}
\NormalTok{my @parsed = pmap\_chunked 50, \{ json\_decode \} @json\_strings}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pgrep}}{pgrep}}\label{pgrep}

Parallel \texttt{grep} that evaluates the filter predicate concurrently
across all CPU cores using rayon. The result preserves the original
input order, so it is a drop-in replacement for \texttt{grep} on large
lists. Best suited for predicates that do meaningful work per element
--- if the predicate is trivial (e.g. a single regex on short strings),
sequential \texttt{grep} may be faster due to lower scheduling overhead.

Two equivalent surface syntaxes: \texttt{pgrep\ \{\ BLOCK\ \}\ LIST} or
\texttt{pgrep\ FUNC,\ LIST}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# Block form}
\NormalTok{my @matches = pgrep \{ /complex\_pattern/ \} @big\_list}
\NormalTok{my @primes = pgrep \{ is\_prime \} 2:1\_000\_000}
\NormalTok{@files |\textgreater{} pgrep \{ {-}s \_ \textgreater{} 1024 \} |\textgreater{} e p}

\NormalTok{\# Bare{-}fn form}
\NormalTok{fn Demo::even = \_ \% 2 == 0}
\NormalTok{my @e = pgrep Demo::even, 1:10  \# (2,4,6,8,10)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pgreps}}{pgreps}}\label{pgreps}

Streaming parallel \texttt{grep} --- returns a lazy iterator that
filters items across all CPU cores. Unlike \texttt{pgrep} which eagerly
collects all matching items, \texttt{pgreps} yields matches as they are
found through a bounded channel. Output order is non-deterministic
(completion order). Each worker thread reuses a single interpreter
instance.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{range(0, 1e6) |\textgreater{} pgreps \{ is\_prime(\_) \} |\textgreater{} take 100 |\textgreater{} ep}
\NormalTok{t 1:1e9 pgreps \{ \_ \% 7 == 0 \} take 10 ep}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pfor}}{pfor}}\label{pfor}

Parallel \texttt{foreach} that executes a side-effecting block across
all CPU cores with no return value. Use this when you need to perform
work for each element (writing files, sending requests, updating shared
state) but don\textquotesingle t need to collect results. The block
receives each element as \texttt{\_}. Iteration order is
non-deterministic, so the block must be safe to run concurrently.

Two equivalent surface syntaxes: \texttt{pfor\ \{\ BLOCK\ \}\ LIST} or
\texttt{pfor\ FUNC,\ LIST}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# Block form}
\NormalTok{pfor \{ write\_report \} @records}
\NormalTok{pfor \{ compress\_file \} glob("*.log")}
\NormalTok{@urls |\textgreater{} pfor \{ fetch}
\NormalTok{    p "done: $\_" \}}

\NormalTok{\# Bare{-}fn form}
\NormalTok{fn task = ep "did $\_"}
\NormalTok{pfor task, (1, 2, 3)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{psort}}{psort}}\label{psort}

Parallel sort that uses rayon\textquotesingle s parallel merge-sort
algorithm. Accepts an optional comparator block; the two sort candidates
are \texttt{\_} and \texttt{\_1}. For large lists (10k+ elements), this
significantly outperforms the sequential \texttt{sort} by splitting the
array, sorting partitions in parallel, and merging. The sort is stable
--- equal elements retain their relative order.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @sorted = psort \{ \_ \textless{}=\textgreater{} \_1 \} @big\_list}
\NormalTok{my @by\_name = psort \{ (\_){-}\textgreater{}\{name\} cmp (\_1){-}\textgreater{}\{name\} \} @records}
\NormalTok{@nums |\textgreater{} psort \{ \_ \textless{}=\textgreater{} \_1 \} |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pcache}}{pcache}}\label{pcache}

Parallel memoized map --- each element is processed concurrently, but
results are cached by the stringified value of \texttt{\_} so duplicate
inputs are computed only once. This is ideal when your input list
contains many repeated values and the computation is expensive. The
cache is a concurrent hash map shared across all threads, so there is no
lock contention on reads after the first computation.

Two equivalent surface syntaxes: \texttt{pcache\ \{\ BLOCK\ \}\ LIST} or
\texttt{pcache\ FUNC,\ LIST}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# Block form}
\NormalTok{my @out = pcache \{ expensive\_lookup \} @list\_with\_dupes}
\NormalTok{my @resolved = pcache \{ dns\_resolve \} @hostnames}

\NormalTok{\# Bare{-}fn form}
\NormalTok{my @resolved = pcache dns\_resolve, @hostnames}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{preduce}}{preduce}}\label{preduce}

Parallel tree-fold using rayon\textquotesingle s \texttt{reduce} ---
splits the list into chunks, reduces each chunk independently, then
merges partial results. The combining operation \textbf{must be
associative} (e.g. \texttt{+}, \texttt{*}, \texttt{max});
non-associative ops will produce incorrect results. Much faster than
sequential \texttt{reduce} on large numeric lists because the tree
structure allows O(log n) merge depth across cores.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $total = preduce \{ \_ + \_1 \} @nums}
\NormalTok{my $biggest = preduce \{ \_ \textgreater{} \_1 ? \_ : \_1 \} @vals}
\NormalTok{my $product = preduce \{ \_ * \_1 \} 1:100}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{preduce\_init}}{preduce\_init}}\label{preduce_init}

Parallel tree-fold with an explicit identity element. Same engine as
\texttt{preduce} (rayon\textquotesingle s \texttt{reduce\_with}), but
\texttt{INIT} seeds every chunk so empty / single-element inputs return
a sensible value instead of \texttt{undef}. The combining operation
\textbf{must be associative AND} \texttt{INIT} must be its identity
(\texttt{INIT\ ⊕\ x\ ==\ x}); otherwise different chunk boundaries
produce different sums.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $total   = preduce\_init 0, \{ \_ + \_1 \} @nums      \# sum, identity 0}
\NormalTok{my $product = preduce\_init 1, \{ \_ * \_1 \} @nums      \# product, identity 1}
\NormalTok{my $biggest = preduce\_init {-}inf, \{ \_ \textgreater{} \_1 ? \_ : \_1 \} @vals}

\NormalTok{\# Histogram merge — identity is the empty hash.}
\NormalTok{my \%h = \%\{ preduce\_init +\{\}, \{ merge\_hash(\_, \_1) \} @per\_chunk\_hashes \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Without an identity, prefer \texttt{preduce} (no init) or
\texttt{pmap\_reduce} (fused mapper + reducer).

See also: \texttt{preduce}, \texttt{pmap\_reduce}, \texttt{reduce}
(sequential), \texttt{fold} (sequential with init).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pmap\_reduce}}{pmap\_reduce}}\label{pmap_reduce}

Fused parallel map + tree reduce. Each input element is mapped in
parallel via rayon; results are combined pairwise by the reducer block.
The reducer must be associative (\texttt{(a⊕b)⊕c\ ==\ a⊕(b⊕c)}) ---
order of pairwise combines is not fixed.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# Two{-}block form: pmap\_reduce \{ MAPPER \} \{ REDUCER \} LIST}
\NormalTok{my $sum = pmap\_reduce \{ \_ * 2 \} \{ \_ + \_1 \} @list}

\NormalTok{\# Chunked aggregation — mapper returns a per{-}chunk arrayref, reducer merges.}
\NormalTok{my $grads = @batches |\textgreater{} pmap\_reduce \{}
\NormalTok{    my $idxs = \_}
\NormalTok{    [ batch\_grad($w, $b, $XS, $YS, $idxs), len(@$idxs) ]}
\NormalTok{\} \{}
\NormalTok{    [ \_{-}\textgreater{}[0] + \_1{-}\textgreater{}[0], \_{-}\textgreater{}[1] + \_1{-}\textgreater{}[1] ]}
\NormalTok{\};   \# ← trailing \textasciigrave{};\textasciigrave{} required (see CAVEAT below)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Caveats:}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Trailing \texttt{;} required} when followed by another
  statement. The parser keeps looking for an optional third block after
  the reducer, and at file scope it silently absorbs the next statement
  (e.g. a \texttt{p\ "…"} or \texttt{my\ \$x\ =\ …}). Always terminate
  with \texttt{;}. Tracked in \texttt{docs/BUGS.md} as BUG-301.
\item
  \textbf{Closure capture of large arrays is unreliable.} Pre-capture an
  arrayref outside the call (\texttt{my\ \$XS\ =\ \textbackslash{}@xs})
  and dereference inside the worker rather than relying on direct
  \texttt{@xs} lexical capture.
\item
  \textbf{\texttt{rand()} in the mapper is parallel-correlated.}
  Per-task \texttt{rand()} calls across workers produce statistically
  correlated values; prefer chunked Monte-Carlo where each task runs its
  own inner loop.
\end{itemize}

See also: \texttt{pmap} (eager parallel map), \texttt{pmaps}
(streaming), \texttt{par\_reduce} (verbose builder).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pany}}{pany}}\label{pany}

\texttt{pany\ \{\ COND\ \}\ @list} --- parallel short-circuit
\texttt{any}. Splits the list across cores via rayon, evaluates
\texttt{COND} on each element, returns truthy as soon as \textbf{any}
chunk reports a hit and cancels the rest. Returns the matching element
(truthy) or \texttt{0} (no match).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# Found in O(N/cores), often much faster — early{-}stops once any worker hits.}
\NormalTok{if (pany \{ \_ \textgreater{} 1\_000\_000 \} @samples) \{ p "found a large sample" \}}
\NormalTok{pany \{ is\_prime(\_) \} 10**9 : 10**9 + 50    \# "is there a prime in this range?"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{pall} (parallel \texttt{all}), \texttt{pfirst} (return
the matching value), \texttt{any} (sequential), \texttt{pgrep} (full
filter).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pfirst}}{pfirst}}\label{pfirst}

\texttt{pfirst\ \{\ COND\ \}\ @list} --- parallel \texttt{first}. Like
\texttt{pany} but returns the \textbf{matching element} rather than just
a truthy flag. Workers run concurrently across chunks; the first hit (by
*completion order*, not input order) wins and the remaining workers are
cancelled. Returns \texttt{undef} if no element matches.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $hit = pfirst \{ \_{-}\textgreater{}\{status\} eq "FAILED" \} @records}
\NormalTok{my $prime = pfirst \{ is\_prime(\_) \} 10**12 : 10**12 + 1000}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Note:} output is non-deterministic when multiple elements match
--- workers race. For first-by-input-order use sequential \texttt{first}
or \texttt{pfirst\_in\_order} (where supported).

See also: \texttt{pany}, \texttt{first}, \texttt{pgrep}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{puniq}}{puniq}}\label{puniq}

\texttt{puniq\ @list} --- parallel deduplication. Returns the unique
elements of \texttt{@list}, with the original input order preserved
(first occurrence wins). Elements are keyed by stringification
(\texttt{"\$\_"}), so \texttt{1} and \texttt{"1"} deduplicate together.
Backed by a concurrent hash set.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @uniq    = puniq @log\_lines}
\NormalTok{my @hosts   = puniq map \{ \_{-}\textgreater{}\{host\} \} @records}
\NormalTok{my @hashes  = puniq pmap sha256, @files     \# parallel hash + dedup}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

For exact-key (no stringification) dedup, build the set manually with
\texttt{mysync\ \%seen} + \texttt{pmaps}. For *count* of unique items
use \texttt{hll\_count} (HyperLogLog) which is O(1) memory.

See also: \texttt{uniq} (sequential), \texttt{dedup},
\texttt{hll\_count}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pflat\_map}}{pflat\_map}}\label{pflat_map}

Parallel flat-map: map + flatten results. Each element produces zero or
more output values via the block/function, and the outputs are
concatenated in input order.

Two equivalent surface syntaxes: \texttt{pflat\_map\ BLOCK\ LIST} or
\texttt{pflat\_map\ FUNC,\ LIST}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# Block form}
\NormalTok{my @out = pflat\_map expand @list}

\NormalTok{\# Bare{-}fn form}
\NormalTok{fn expand = (\_, \_ * 10)}
\NormalTok{my @r = pflat\_map expand, (1, 2, 3)   \# (1, 10, 2, 20, 3, 30)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pflat\_maps}}{pflat\_maps}}\label{pflat_maps}

Streaming parallel flat-map. Like \texttt{pmaps} but each block
iteration may emit zero or more values, all of which are spliced into
the downstream iterator (no intermediate vec). Runs on the persistent
worker-thread pool, output order is non-deterministic, and downstream
\texttt{take}/\texttt{head} cancels remaining work.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# One{-}to{-}many expansion in parallel}
\NormalTok{range(0, 1e6) |\textgreater{} pflat\_maps \{ [\_, \_ * 10] \} |\textgreater{} ep}

\NormalTok{\# Drop and explode: yield only odd{-}derived pairs}
\NormalTok{1:1\_000\_000 |\textgreater{} pflat\_maps \{ \_ \% 2 ? (\_, \_ * \_) : () \} |\textgreater{} take 20 |\textgreater{} ep}

\NormalTok{\# Tokenize a big corpus in parallel}
\NormalTok{glob("*.txt") |\textgreater{} pflat\_maps \{ slurp(\_) |\textgreater{} split /\textbackslash{}s+/ \} |\textgreater{} pgreps \{ len \_ \} |\textgreater{} ep}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

For eager flat-map use \texttt{pflat\_map} (returns a vec). For
sequential lazy flat-map use \texttt{flat\_maps}.

See also: \texttt{pmaps}, \texttt{pflat\_map}, \texttt{flat\_maps},
\texttt{flat\_map}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{fan}}{fan}}\label{fan}

Execute BLOCK or FUNC N times in parallel (\texttt{\_} = index 0..N-1).
With no count, defaults to the rayon pool size (\texttt{stryke\ -j}).

Two equivalent surface syntaxes: • Block form ---
\texttt{fan\ N\ \{\ BLOCK\ \}} or \texttt{fan\ \{\ BLOCK\ \}} • Bare-fn
form --- \texttt{fan\ N,\ FUNC} or \texttt{fan\ FUNC}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# Block form}
\NormalTok{fan 8 \{ work(\_) \}}
\NormalTok{fan \{ work(\_) \} progress =\textgreater{} 1}

\NormalTok{\# Bare{-}fn form}
\NormalTok{fn tick = ep "tick $\_"}
\NormalTok{fan 8, tick}
\NormalTok{fan tick, progress =\textgreater{} 1   \# uses pool size}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{fan\_cap}}{fan\_cap}}\label{fan_cap}

Like \texttt{fan} but captures return values in index order. Two surface
syntaxes: \texttt{fan\_cap\ N\ \{\ BLOCK\ \}} or
\texttt{fan\_cap\ N,\ FUNC}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# Block form}
\NormalTok{my @results = fan\_cap 8 \{ compute(\_) \}}

\NormalTok{\# Bare{-}fn form}
\NormalTok{fn compute = \_ * \_}
\NormalTok{my @squares = fan\_cap 8, compute}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-shared-state-concurrency}
\chapter{Shared State \& Concurrency}\label{shared-state-concurrency}

11 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mysync}}{mysync}}\label{mysync}

Declare shared variables for parallel blocks
(\texttt{Arc\textless{}Mutex\textgreater{}}).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{mysync $counter = 0}
\NormalTok{fan 10000 \{ $counter++ \}   \# always exactly 10000}
\NormalTok{mysync @results}
\NormalTok{mysync \%histogram$1}

\NormalTok{Compound ops (\textasciigrave{}++\textasciigrave{}, \textasciigrave{}+=\textasciigrave{}, \textasciigrave{}.=\textasciigrave{}, \textasciigrave{}|=\textasciigrave{}, \textasciigrave{}\&=\textasciigrave{}) are fully atomic.}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{async}}{async}}\label{async}

\texttt{async\ \{\ BLOCK\ \}} --- schedule a block for execution on a
background worker thread and return a task handle immediately.

The block begins executing as soon as a thread is available in
stryke\textquotesingle s global rayon thread pool, while the calling
code continues without blocking. To retrieve the result, pass the task
handle to \texttt{await}, which blocks until the task completes and
returns its value. If the block panics, the panic is captured and
re-raised at the \texttt{await} call site. Use \texttt{async} for
fire-and-forget background work, overlapping I/O with computation, or
launching multiple independent tasks that you later join. For structured
fan-out with index-based parallelism, prefer \texttt{fan} or
\texttt{fan\_cap} instead.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $task = async \{ long\_compute() \}}
\NormalTok{do\_other\_work()}
\NormalTok{my $val = await $task}

\NormalTok{\# Overlapping multiple fetches:}
\NormalTok{my @tasks = map \{ async \{ fetch("https://api.example.com/$\_") \} \} 1:10;}
\NormalTok{my @results = map \{ await \_ \} @tasks}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{spawn}}{spawn}}\label{spawn}

\texttt{spawn\ \{\ BLOCK\ \}} --- Rust-style alias for \texttt{async};
schedules a block on a background thread and returns a joinable task
handle.

This is identical to \texttt{async} in every respect --- same thread
pool, same semantics, same \texttt{await} for joining. The name exists
for developers coming from Rust\textquotesingle s \texttt{tokio::spawn}
or \texttt{std::thread::spawn} who find \texttt{spawn} more natural. Use
whichever reads better in your code; mixing \texttt{async} and
\texttt{spawn} in the same program is perfectly fine since they share
the same underlying pool.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $task = spawn \{ expensive\_io() \}}
\NormalTok{my $val = await $task}

\NormalTok{my @handles = map \{ spawn \{ process \} \} @items;}
\NormalTok{my @out = map \{ await \_ \} @handles}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{await}}{await}}\label{await}

\texttt{await\ \$task} --- block the current thread until an async/spawn
task completes and return its result value.

If the background task has already finished by the time \texttt{await}
is called, the result is returned immediately with no scheduling
overhead. If the task panicked, \texttt{await} re-raises the panic as a
die in the calling thread, preserving the original error message and
backtrace. You can \texttt{await} a task exactly once; calling
\texttt{await} on an already-joined handle is a fatal error. For waiting
on multiple tasks, simply map over the handles --- stryke does not yet
provide a \texttt{join\_all} primitive, but \texttt{map\ await\ @tasks}
achieves the same effect.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $task = async \{ 42 \}}
\NormalTok{my $result = await $task              \# 42}

\NormalTok{\# Await with error handling:}
\NormalTok{my $t = spawn \{ die "oops" \}}
\NormalTok{eval \{ await $t \}                     \# $@ eq "oops"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pchannel}}{pchannel}}\label{pchannel}

\texttt{pchannel(N)} --- create a bounded multi-producer multi-consumer
(MPMC) channel with capacity N, returning a \texttt{(\$tx,\ \$rx)} pair.

The transmitter \texttt{\$tx} supports
\texttt{-\textgreater{}send(\$val)} which blocks if the channel is full
(backpressure). The receiver \texttt{\$rx} supports
\texttt{-\textgreater{}recv} which blocks until a value is available,
and \texttt{-\textgreater{}try\_recv} which returns \texttt{undef}
immediately if the channel is empty. Both ends can be cloned and shared
across threads --- clone \texttt{\$tx} with
\texttt{\$tx-\textgreater{}clone} to create additional producers, or
clone \texttt{\$rx} for additional consumers. When all transmitters are
dropped, \texttt{-\textgreater{}recv} on the receiver returns
\texttt{undef} to signal completion. Use \texttt{pchannel} to build
producer-consumer pipelines, rate-limited work queues, or to communicate
between \texttt{async}/\texttt{spawn} tasks.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($tx, $rx) = pchannel(100)}
\NormalTok{async \{ $tx{-}\textgreater{}send(\_) for 1:1000}
\NormalTok{    undef $tx \}}
\NormalTok{while (defined(my $val = $rx{-}\textgreater{}recv)) \{}
\NormalTok{    p $val}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{\# Multiple producers:}
\NormalTok{my ($tx, $rx) = pchannel(50)}
\NormalTok{for my $i (1:4) \{}
\NormalTok{    my $t = $tx{-}\textgreater{}clone}
\NormalTok{    spawn \{ $t{-}\textgreater{}send("from worker $i: \_") for 1:100 \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{undef $tx  \# drop original so channel closes when workers finish}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pselect}}{pselect}}\label{pselect}

\texttt{pselect(@receivers)} /
\texttt{pselect(timeout\_ms\ =\textgreater{}\ N,\ @receivers)} --- wait
on multiple \texttt{pchannel} receivers; returns
\texttt{(\$index,\ \$value)} for the first receiver to produce a value.
Equivalent to Go\textquotesingle s
\texttt{select\ \{\ case\ v\ :=\ \textless{}-ch1:\ …\ case\ v\ :=\ \textless{}-ch2:\ …\ \}}.

The optional \texttt{timeout\_ms} clause returns \texttt{(-1,\ undef)}
if no receiver delivers within the deadline. Closed channels (all
senders dropped) return \texttt{(i,\ undef)} so the caller can detect
end-of-stream per receiver.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($tx\_a, $rx\_a) = pchannel(8)}
\NormalTok{my ($tx\_b, $rx\_b) = pchannel(8)}
\NormalTok{spawn \{ $tx\_a{-}\textgreater{}send($\_) for 1:100 \}}
\NormalTok{spawn \{ $tx\_b{-}\textgreater{}send($\_ * 10) for 1:100 \}}
\NormalTok{while (1) \{}
\NormalTok{    my ($idx, $val) = pselect $rx\_a, $rx\_b}
\NormalTok{    last unless defined $val}
\NormalTok{    p "from \#$idx: $val"}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{\# With deadline:}
\NormalTok{my ($idx, $val) = pselect timeout\_ms =\textgreater{} 500, $rx\_a, $rx\_b}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{pchannel}, \texttt{spawn}, \texttt{async},
\texttt{barrier}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{barrier}}{barrier}}\label{barrier}

\texttt{barrier(N)} --- create a synchronization barrier that blocks
until exactly N threads have arrived at the wait point.

Each thread calls \texttt{\$bar-\textgreater{}wait} and is suspended
until all N participants have reached the barrier, at which point all
are released simultaneously. This is useful for coordinating phased
parallel algorithms where all workers must complete step K before any
worker begins step K+1. The barrier is reusable --- after all threads
are released, it resets and can be waited on again. Internally backed by
a Rust \texttt{std::sync::Barrier} for zero-overhead synchronization.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $bar = barrier(4)}
\NormalTok{for my $i (0:3) \{}
\NormalTok{    spawn \{}
\NormalTok{        setup\_phase($i)}
\NormalTok{        $bar{-}\textgreater{}wait                      \# all 4 threads sync here}
\NormalTok{        compute\_phase($i)}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ppool}}{ppool}}\label{ppool}

\texttt{ppool(N,\ fn\ \{\ ...\ \})} --- create a persistent thread pool
of N worker threads, each running the provided subroutine in a loop.

The pool is typically paired with a \texttt{pchannel}: workers pull
items from the channel\textquotesingle s receiver, process them, and
optionally send results to an output channel. Unlike \texttt{pmap} which
is a one-shot parallel transform, \texttt{ppool} keeps threads alive for
the lifetime of the pool, making it ideal for long-running server-style
workloads, background drain loops, or scenarios where thread startup
cost would dominate short-lived \texttt{pmap} calls. Workers exit when
their input channel is closed (all transmitters dropped). The pool
object supports \texttt{-\textgreater{}join} to block until all workers
have finished.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($tx, $rx) = pchannel(100)}
\NormalTok{my $pool = ppool 4, fn \{}
\NormalTok{    while (defined(my $job = $rx{-}\textgreater{}recv)) \{}
\NormalTok{        process($job)}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{$tx{-}\textgreater{}send(\_) for @work\_items}
\NormalTok{undef $tx                             \# signal completion}
\NormalTok{$pool{-}\textgreater{}join                           \# wait for drain}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{deque}}{deque}}\label{deque}

\texttt{deque\ LIST} --- create a double-ended queue initialized with
the given elements.

A deque supports efficient O(1) insertion and removal at both ends via
\texttt{-\textgreater{}push\_front(\$val)},
\texttt{-\textgreater{}push\_back(\$val)},
\texttt{-\textgreater{}pop\_front}, and
\texttt{-\textgreater{}pop\_back}. It also supports
\texttt{-\textgreater{}len} and iteration. Internally backed by a Rust
\texttt{VecDeque}, it is ideal for sliding window algorithms, BFS
traversals, or any scenario where you need fast access to both ends of a
sequence.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $dq = deque(1, 2, 3)}
\NormalTok{$dq{-}\textgreater{}push\_front(0)}
\NormalTok{$dq{-}\textgreater{}push\_back(4)}
\NormalTok{p $dq{-}\textgreater{}pop\_front                      \# 0}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{heap}}{heap}}\label{heap}

\texttt{heap\ LIST} --- create a min-heap (priority queue) from the
given elements.

Elements are heapified on construction so that
\texttt{-\textgreater{}pop} always returns the smallest element in O(log
n) time. \texttt{-\textgreater{}push(\$val)} inserts a new element, also
in O(log n). The heap supports \texttt{-\textgreater{}peek} to inspect
the minimum without removing it, and \texttt{-\textgreater{}len} for the
current size. Internally backed by a Rust \texttt{BinaryHeap} (inverted
for min-heap semantics), it is the go-to structure for top-K queries,
Dijkstra, and merge-K-sorted-lists problems.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $h = heap(5, 3, 8, 1)}
\NormalTok{p $h{-}\textgreater{}pop                             \# 1 (smallest first)}
\NormalTok{p $h{-}\textgreater{}peek                            \# 3 (next smallest)}
\NormalTok{$h{-}\textgreater{}push(0)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{set}}{set}}\label{set}

\texttt{set\ LIST} --- create a set (unique unordered collection) from
the given elements.

Duplicate values are collapsed on construction so the set contains each
value exactly once. The set object provides
\texttt{-\textgreater{}contains(\$val)} for O(1) membership testing,
plus \texttt{-\textgreater{}union(\$s)},
\texttt{-\textgreater{}intersection(\$s)},
\texttt{-\textgreater{}difference(\$s)}, and \texttt{-\textgreater{}len}
methods. Internally backed by a Rust \texttt{HashSet} for performance.
Use \texttt{to\_set} to convert an existing iterator into a set.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $s = set(1, 2, 3, 2, 1)}
\NormalTok{p $s{-}\textgreater{}contains(2)                     \# 1}
\NormalTok{p $s{-}\textgreater{}len                             \# 3}
\NormalTok{my $both = $s{-}\textgreater{}union(set(3, 4, 5))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-pipeline-pipe-forward}
\chapter{Pipeline \& Pipe-Forward}\label{pipeline-pipe-forward}

13 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}}}{\textbar\textgreater{}}}\label{section}

Pipe-forward operator --- threads LHS as first argument of RHS call.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{"hello" |\textgreater{} uc |\textgreater{} rev |\textgreater{} p              \# OLLEH}
\NormalTok{1:10 |\textgreater{} grep \_ \textgreater{} 5 |\textgreater{} map \_ * 2 |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{$url |\textgreater{} fetch\_json |\textgreater{} json\_jq \textquotesingle{}.name\textquotesingle{} |\textgreater{} p}
\NormalTok{"hello world" |\textgreater{} s/world/perl/ |\textgreater{} p     \# hello perl}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Zero runtime cost (parse-time desugaring). Binds looser than
\texttt{\textbar{}\textbar{}}, tighter than \texttt{?:}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{thread}}{thread}}\label{thread}

Thread-first macro --- chain stages where the threaded value is injected
as the \textbf{first} argument (like Clojure\textquotesingle s
\texttt{-\textgreater{}}).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{} @data grep \{ \_ \textgreater{} 5 \} map \{ \_ * 2 \} sort \{ \_ \textless{}=\textgreater{} \_1 \} |\textgreater{} join "," |\textgreater{} p}
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{} " hello " tm uc rv lc ufc sc cc kc tj p  \# short aliases}
\NormalTok{fn div = \_0 / \_1}
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{} 10 div(2) p                               \# div(10, 2) = 5}
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{} 10 div(\_, 2) p                           \# explicit \_ position: div(10, 2) = 5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Spellings: \texttt{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{}}, \texttt{thread},
\texttt{t}. Stages: bare function (\texttt{uc}, \texttt{tm}, \ldots),
function with block (\texttt{map\ \{\ …\ \}}, \texttt{grep\ \{\ …\ \}}),
\texttt{name(args)} call where \texttt{\_} is the threaded-value
placeholder, or \texttt{\textgreater{}\{\}} anonymous block.
\texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} terminates the macro.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}}}{\textasciitilde\textgreater\textgreater{}}}\label{section-1}

Thread-last macro --- chain stages where the threaded value is injected
as the \textbf{last} argument (like Clojure\textquotesingle s
\texttt{-\textgreater{}\textgreater{}}).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fn div = \_0 / \_1}
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{} 10 div(2) p                              \# div(2, 10) = 0.2}
\NormalTok{{-}\textgreater{}\textgreater{} 10 div(\_, 2) p                          \# explicit \_ overrides: div(10, 2) = 5}

\NormalTok{\# Thread{-}last is useful for list{-}consuming functions:}
\NormalTok{fn first\_n \{ @\_[0..$\_[{-}1]{-}1] \}              \# first\_n(list, n)}
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{} (1,2,3,4,5) first\_n(3) p                 \# first\_n(1,2,3,4,5, 3) = (1,2,3)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Spellings: \texttt{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}},
\texttt{-\textgreater{}\textgreater{}}. The explicit \texttt{\_}
placeholder always takes precedence.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\textasciitilde{}s\textgreater{}}}{\textasciitilde s\textgreater{}}}\label{s}

Per-item streaming thread macro --- each stage runs as an independent
worker thread connected to the next via a bounded channel, so items flow
through the pipeline concurrently without materializing intermediate
arrays.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}s\textgreater{} [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] map \{ \_ * 10 \} map \{ \_ + 1 \}}
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}s\textgreater{} @lines map \{ json\_decode \} grep \{ \_{-}\textgreater{}\{ok\} \} map \{ \_{-}\textgreater{}\{val\} \}}
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}s\textgreater{} gen \{ yield\_lines "huge.log" \} map \{ parse \} grep \{ /ERROR/ \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Use \texttt{\textasciitilde{}s\textgreater{}} when stages have differing
latencies (I/O + CPU mix) so faster workers can pull ahead. Output order
is non-deterministic. The macro\textquotesingle s final value is the
count of items the last stage emitted.
\texttt{\textasciitilde{}s\textgreater{}\textgreater{}} is the
thread-last variant.

See \texttt{\textasciitilde{}p\textgreater{}} for chunk-parallel (vs
per-item) execution and \texttt{par\_pipeline} for the verbose builder
form.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\textasciitilde{}p\textgreater{}}}{\textasciitilde p\textgreater{}}}\label{p}

Chunk-parallel thread macro --- splits the input into chunks, runs the
entire pipeline on each chunk in parallel via rayon, and auto-merges
chunk results. Equivalent to
\texttt{par\_reduce\ \{\ stage1\ \textbar{}\textgreater{}\ stage2\ \textbar{}\textgreater{}\ ...\ \}\ SRC}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}p\textgreater{} c("**.rs") letters freq                              \# histogram across all .rs}
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}p\textgreater{} @big map \{ \_ * 2 \} sum                               \# parallel map+sum}
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}p\textgreater{} $huge letters freq ||\textgreater{} values |\textgreater{} sum                 \# boundary: terse}
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}p\textgreater{} $huge letters freq |then| values |\textgreater{} sum              \# boundary: english}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The auto-merger picks a strategy by inspecting the first
chunk\textquotesingle s result type: hash-of-numbers merges key-wise
with \texttt{+} (canonical histogram), numeric scalars sum, arrays
concat, strings concat. Use \texttt{\textbar{}\textbar{}\textgreater{}}
or \texttt{\textbar{}then\textbar{}} to switch back to sequential
\texttt{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{}} mode for stages that operate on
the merged whole. \texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} also terminates a
\texttt{\textasciitilde{}p\textgreater{}} macro.
\texttt{\textasciitilde{}p\textgreater{}\textgreater{}} is the
thread-last variant.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\textasciitilde{}d\textgreater{}}}{\textasciitilde d\textgreater{}}}\label{d}

Distributed thread macro --- same chunk-block semantics as
\texttt{\textasciitilde{}p\textgreater{}} but ships the pipeline to a
remote stryke cluster rather than running locally. Syntax:
\texttt{\textasciitilde{}d\textgreater{}\ on\ \$cluster\ SOURCE\ stage1\ stage2\ ...}.
The \texttt{on\ EXPR} slot is mandatory --- \texttt{EXPR} must evaluate
to a value built by \texttt{cluster(...)} (see \texttt{cluster}
builtin).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $c = cluster(["host1", "host2", "host3:8022"])}
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}d\textgreater{} on $c c("**.parquet") map \{ parse\_pq \} map \{ transform \} collect}
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}d\textgreater{} on $c @huge\_urls map \{ fetch \} grep \{ \_{-}\textgreater{}\{status\} == 200 \} |\textgreater{} sum\_bytes}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Unlike \texttt{\textasciitilde{}p\textgreater{}}, captures from the
enclosing scope are restricted: \texttt{mysync}/atomic captures are
rejected (the value can\textquotesingle t cross the wire safely). Errors
on remote workers are surfaced as a single aggregated error.
\texttt{\textasciitilde{}d\textgreater{}\textgreater{}} is the
thread-last variant --- threaded value injected as the last argument of
each stage.

See \texttt{cluster} for cluster construction,
\texttt{\textasciitilde{}p\textgreater{}} for local chunk-parallel,
\texttt{pmap\_on} for the verbose builder form.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{par}}{par}}\label{par}

Generic parallel-chunk evaluator. Splits the input list into chunks,
runs \texttt{BLOCK} on each chunk in parallel with the chunk bound to
\texttt{\_} / \texttt{\_0}, and flattens chunk outputs into a single
list. Callable in three shapes:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# 1. Top{-}level prefix form — yields a flat list}
\NormalTok{par \{ BLOCK \} LIST}
\NormalTok{my @r = par \{ letters \} "Hello, World 123"      \# H e l l o W o r l d}
\NormalTok{my @s = par \{ map \{ \_ ** 2 \} \_ \} @huge           \# parallel squares}

\NormalTok{\# 2. Postfix{-}statement form — side{-}effecting, no return binding}
\NormalTok{\{ BLOCK \} par LIST                               \# runs BLOCK per chunk}
\NormalTok{\{ log\_chunk(\_) \} par @huge}

\NormalTok{\# 3. As a \textasciigrave{}\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{}\textasciigrave{} / \textasciigrave{}\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textasciigrave{} thread{-}macro stage}
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{} "abc def" par \{ letters \}                     \# a b c d e f}
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{} @huge par \{ map \{ \_ ** 2 \} |\textgreater{} sum \} |\textgreater{} sum p  \# parallel sum{-}of{-}squares}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Each chunk gets a fresh sub-VM with the chunk bound to \texttt{\_} and
\texttt{\_0}. List-returning ops keep their values; the runtime
auto-flattens. Use \texttt{par\_reduce} when you need an explicit merge
step rather than flat concat.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{par\_reduce}}{par\_reduce}}\label{par_reduce}

Chunk-extract-merge thread-stage. The single-block form runs
\texttt{extract} per chunk and auto-merges results by type
(hash\textless num\textgreater→key-wise add, number→sum, array→concat,
string→concat). The two-block form binds \texttt{\$a}/\texttt{\$b} in
the explicit reducer.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{} "abc def abc" par\_reduce \{ letters |\textgreater{} freq \}      \# canonical histogram merge}
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{} @nums par\_reduce \{ sum \}                          \# parallel sum}
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{} "abcdefgh" par\_reduce \{ length(\_) \} \{ $a + $b \}   \# explicit pairwise reducer}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Below the chunk threshold the runtime falls back to single-chunk eval;
the result must still match the bare extract block\textquotesingle s
output. Multi-chunk paths require the merger (auto or explicit) to be
associative and commutative for correct results.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\textbar{}\textbar{}\textgreater{}}}{\textbar\textbar\textgreater{}}}\label{section-2}

Boundary marker that terminates the chunk-parallel section of a
\texttt{\textasciitilde{}p\textgreater{}} macro and switches the merged
result into a sequential \texttt{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{}}
continuation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}p\textgreater{} "abc def abc" letters freq ||\textgreater{} values |\textgreater{} sum     \# 9}
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}p\textgreater{} "abc def abc" letters freq |then| values |\textgreater{} sum  \# 9 — readable form}
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}p\textgreater{} @data map \{ \_ * 2 \} sum ||\textgreater{} sqrt p               \# parallel sum, sequential sqrt}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Both spellings are byte-equivalent;
\texttt{\textbar{}\textbar{}\textgreater{}} is terse,
\texttt{\textbar{}then\textbar{}} reads in English. Use either when you
need a post-merge stage that operates on the whole reduced value rather
than per-chunk. \texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} also terminates a
\texttt{\textasciitilde{}p\textgreater{}} macro.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pipeline}}{pipeline}}\label{pipeline}

\texttt{pipeline(@list)} --- wrap a list (or iterator) in a lazy
pipeline object supporting chained \texttt{-\textgreater{}map},
\texttt{-\textgreater{}filter}, \texttt{-\textgreater{}take},
\texttt{-\textgreater{}skip}, \texttt{-\textgreater{}flat\_map},
\texttt{-\textgreater{}tap}, and other transforms that execute zero work
until a terminal method is called.

No intermediate lists are allocated between stages --- each element
flows through the full chain one at a time, making pipelines
memory-efficient even on very large or infinite inputs. Terminal methods
include \texttt{-\textgreater{}collect} (materialize to list),
\texttt{-\textgreater{}reduce\ \{\ ...\ \}} (fold),
\texttt{-\textgreater{}for\_each\ \{\ ...\ \}} (side-effect iteration),
and \texttt{-\textgreater{}count}. Pipelines compose naturally with
stryke\textquotesingle s \texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} operator: you
can feed \texttt{pipeline(...)} output into further
\texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} stages or vice versa. Use
\texttt{pipeline} when you want explicit method-chaining style rather
than the flat \texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} pipe syntax --- both
compile to the same lazy evaluation engine.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @out = pipeline(@data)}
\NormalTok{  {-}\textgreater{}filter \{ \_ \textgreater{} 0 \}}
\NormalTok{  {-}\textgreater{}map \{ \_ * 2 \}}
\NormalTok{  {-}\textgreater{}take(10)}
\NormalTok{  {-}\textgreater{}collect}

\NormalTok{pipeline(1:1\_000\_000)}
\NormalTok{  {-}\textgreater{}filter \{ \_ \% 3 == 0 \}}
\NormalTok{  {-}\textgreater{}map \{ \_ ** 2 \}}
\NormalTok{  {-}\textgreater{}take(5)}
\NormalTok{  {-}\textgreater{}for\_each \{ p \_ \}                \# 9 36 81 144 225}

\NormalTok{my $sum = pipeline(@scores)}
\NormalTok{  {-}\textgreater{}filter \{ \_ \textgreater{}= 60 \}}
\NormalTok{  {-}\textgreater{}reduce \{ \_ + \_1 \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{par\_pipeline}}{par\_pipeline}}\label{par_pipeline}

\texttt{par\_pipeline(source\ =\textgreater{}\ \textbackslash{}@data,\ stages\ =\textgreater{}\ {[}...{]},\ workers\ =\textgreater{}\ N)}
--- build a multi-stage parallel pipeline where each
stage\textquotesingle s map/filter block runs concurrently across N
worker threads.

Unlike \texttt{pmap} which parallelizes a single transform,
\texttt{par\_pipeline} lets you define a sequence of named stages ---
each with its own block --- that execute in parallel while preserving
input order in the final output. Internally, stryke partitions the
source into chunks, distributes them across workers, and pipelines the
stages so that stage 2 can begin processing a chunk as soon as stage 1
finishes it, overlapping computation across stages. This is ideal for
multi-step ETL workloads where each step is CPU-bound and the data
volume is large. The \texttt{workers} parameter defaults to the number
of logical CPUs.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @results = par\_pipeline(}
\NormalTok{    source  =\textgreater{} \textbackslash{}@raw\_records,}
\NormalTok{    stages  =\textgreater{} [}
\NormalTok{        \{ name =\textgreater{} "parse",     map =\textgreater{} fn \{ json\_decode \} \},}
\NormalTok{        \{ name =\textgreater{} "transform", map =\textgreater{} fn \{ enrich \} \},}
\NormalTok{        \{ name =\textgreater{} "validate",  filter =\textgreater{} fn \{ \_{-}\textgreater{}\{valid\} \} \},}
\NormalTok{    ],}
\NormalTok{    workers =\textgreater{} 8,}
\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{par\_pipeline\_stream}}{par\_pipeline\_stream}}\label{par_pipeline_stream}

\texttt{par\_pipeline\_stream(source\ =\textgreater{}\ ...,\ stages\ =\textgreater{}\ {[}...{]},\ workers\ =\textgreater{}\ N)}
--- streaming variant of \texttt{par\_pipeline} that connects stages via
bounded \texttt{pchannel} queues instead of materializing intermediate
arrays.

Each stage runs as an independent pool of workers, pulling from an input
channel and pushing to an output channel. This gives true pipelined
parallelism: stage 1 workers produce items while stage 2 workers consume
them concurrently, bounded by channel capacity to prevent memory blowup.
The streaming design makes this suitable for infinite or very large data
sources (file streams, network feeds) where materializing the full
dataset between stages is impractical. Results are emitted in arrival
order by default; pass \texttt{ordered\ =\textgreater{}\ 1} to reorder
them to match input order at the cost of buffering.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{par\_pipeline\_stream(}
\NormalTok{    source  =\textgreater{} fn \{ while (my $line = \textless{}STDIN\textgreater{}) \{ yield $line \} \},}
\NormalTok{    stages  =\textgreater{} [}
\NormalTok{        \{ name =\textgreater{} "parse", map =\textgreater{} fn \{ json\_decode \} \},}
\NormalTok{        \{ name =\textgreater{} "score", map =\textgreater{} fn \{ compute\_score \} \},}
\NormalTok{    ],}
\NormalTok{    workers =\textgreater{} 4,}
\NormalTok{    on\_item =\textgreater{} fn \{ p \_ \},           \# process results as they arrive}
\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{collect}}{collect}}\label{collect}

\texttt{collect\ ITERATOR} --- materialize a lazy iterator or pipeline
into a concrete list.

Pipeline stages in stryke are lazy: chaining
\texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}\ map\ \{...\}\ \textbar{}\textgreater{}\ grep\ \{...\}}
builds up a deferred computation without consuming any elements. Calling
\texttt{collect} forces evaluation and returns all results as a regular
Perl list. This is the standard way to terminate a lazy chain when you
need the full result in an array. Without \texttt{collect}, the iterator
is consumed element-by-element by \texttt{e}, \texttt{take}, or other
streaming sinks.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @out = range(1,5) |\textgreater{} map \{ \_ * 2 \} |\textgreater{} collect}
\NormalTok{gen \{ yield \_ for 1:3 \} |\textgreater{} collect |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{my @data = stdin |\textgreater{} grep /INFO/ |\textgreater{} collect}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-streaming-iterators}
\chapter{Streaming Iterators}\label{streaming-iterators}

24 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{maps}}{maps}}\label{maps}

\texttt{maps\ \{\ BLOCK\ \}\ LIST} --- the lazy, streaming counterpart
of \texttt{map}. Instead of materializing the entire output list,
\texttt{maps} returns a pull iterator that evaluates the block on demand
as downstream consumers request values. This makes it ideal for
\texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} pipeline chains, especially when
combined with \texttt{take}, \texttt{greps}, or \texttt{collect}. Use
\texttt{maps} over \texttt{map} when working with large ranges, infinite
sequences, or when you want to short-circuit processing early with
\texttt{take}. Memory usage is constant regardless of input size.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{1:10 |\textgreater{} maps \{ \_ * 3 \} |\textgreater{} take 4 |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{\# 3 6 9 12}
\NormalTok{1:1\_000\_000 |\textgreater{} maps \{ \_ ** 2 \} |\textgreater{} greps \{ \_ \textgreater{} 100 \} |\textgreater{} take 3 |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{greps}}{greps}}\label{greps}

\texttt{greps\ \{\ BLOCK\ \}\ LIST} --- the lazy, streaming counterpart
of \texttt{grep}. Returns a pull iterator that only evaluates the
predicate as elements are requested downstream. This is the preferred
filtering function in \texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} pipelines
because it avoids materializing intermediate lists. Combine with
\texttt{take} to short-circuit early, or with \texttt{maps} and
\texttt{collect} for full lazy pipelines. The block receives \texttt{\_}
just like \texttt{grep}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{1:100 |\textgreater{} greps \{ \_ \% 7 == 0 \} |\textgreater{} take 3 |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{\# 7 14 21}
\NormalTok{@lines |\textgreater{} greps \{ /ERROR/ \} |\textgreater{} maps \{ tm \} |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{filter}}{filter}}\label{filter}

\texttt{filter} (alias \texttt{fi}) \texttt{\{\ BLOCK\ \}\ LIST} ---
stryke-native lazy filter that returns a pull iterator, functionally
identical to \texttt{greps} but named for familiarity with Rust/Ruby/JS
conventions. Use \texttt{filter} or \texttt{greps} interchangeably in
\texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} chains; both are streaming and both
set \texttt{\_} in the block. The result must be consumed with
\texttt{collect}, \texttt{e\ p}, \texttt{foreach}, or another terminal.
Prefer \texttt{filter} when writing stryke-idiomatic code; prefer
\texttt{grep}/\texttt{greps} when porting from Perl.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @big = 1:1000 |\textgreater{} filter \{ \_ \textgreater{} 990 \} |\textgreater{} collect}
\NormalTok{@big |\textgreater{} e p   \# 991 992 993 994 995 996 997 998 999 1000}
\NormalTok{1:50 |\textgreater{} fi \{ \_ \% 2 \} |\textgreater{} take 5 |\textgreater{} e p   \# 1 3 5 7 9}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tap}}{tap}}\label{tap}

\texttt{tap\ \{\ BLOCK\ \}\ LIST} --- execute a side-effecting block for
each element, then pass the element through unchanged.

The return value of the block is ignored; the original element is always
forwarded to the next pipeline stage. This makes \texttt{tap} ideal for
injecting logging, debugging, or metrics collection into the middle of a
pipeline without altering the data flow. It is fully streaming and
preserves element order.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{1:5 |\textgreater{} tap \{ log\_debug "saw: $\_" \} |\textgreater{} map \{ \_ * 2 \} |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{@files |\textgreater{} tap \{ p "processing: $\_" \} |\textgreater{} map slurp |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{peek}}{peek}}\label{peek}

\texttt{peek\ ITERATOR} --- inspect the next element of an iterator
without consuming it.

The peeked value is buffered internally so that the next call to
\texttt{-\textgreater{}next} or pipeline pull returns the same element.
This is useful for lookahead parsing, conditional branching on the next
value, or implementing \texttt{take\_while}-style logic manually.
Calling \texttt{peek} multiple times without advancing the iterator
returns the same value each time. Works with any stryke iterator
including \texttt{gen}, \texttt{range}, \texttt{stdin}, and pipeline
results.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $g = gen \{ yield \_ for 1:5 \}}
\NormalTok{p peek $g                             \# 1 (not consumed)}
\NormalTok{p $g{-}\textgreater{}next                            \# 1}
\NormalTok{p peek $g                             \# 2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tee}}{tee}}\label{tee}

\texttt{tee\ FILE,\ ITERATOR} --- write each element to a file as a side
effect while passing it through the pipeline.

Every element that flows through \texttt{tee} is appended as a line to
the specified file, and the element itself continues downstream
unchanged. The file is opened once on first element and closed when the
iterator is exhausted. This is the stryke equivalent of the Unix
\texttt{tee} command, useful for auditing or logging intermediate
pipeline results to disk.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{1:10 |\textgreater{} tee "/tmp/log.txt" |\textgreater{} map \{ \_ * 2 \} |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{stdin |\textgreater{} tee "/tmp/raw.log" |\textgreater{} grep /ERROR/ |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{take}}{take}}\label{take}

\texttt{take\ N,\ LIST} / \texttt{head\ N,\ LIST} /
\texttt{hd\ N,\ LIST} --- return at most the first N elements.

In streaming mode, \texttt{take} pulls exactly N elements from the
upstream iterator and then stops, so it short-circuits infinite or very
large sources efficiently. This makes it safe to write
\texttt{range(1,\ 1\_000\_000)\ \textbar{}\textgreater{}\ take\ 5}
without allocating a million-element list. If the source has fewer than
N elements, all of them are returned. The \texttt{head} and \texttt{hd}
aliases mirror Unix \texttt{head} semantics.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{1:100 |\textgreater{} take 5 |\textgreater{} e p               \# 1 2 3 4 5}
\NormalTok{my @top = hd 3, @sorted}
\NormalTok{stdin |\textgreater{} hd 10 |\textgreater{} e p                 \# first 10 lines}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tail}}{tail}}\label{tail}

\texttt{tail\ N,\ @list} --- returns the last N elements of the list. If
N exceeds the list length, the entire list is returned. The alias
\texttt{tl} is also available. This is the complement of
\texttt{head}/\texttt{take}. In stryke, it works both as a function call
and in \texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} pipelines.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @t = tail 2, 1:5}
\NormalTok{@t |\textgreater{} e p   \# 4 5}
\NormalTok{1:100 |\textgreater{} tl 3 |\textgreater{} e p  \# 98 99 100}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{drop}}{drop}}\label{drop}

\texttt{drop\ N,\ LIST} / \texttt{skip\ N,\ LIST} /
\texttt{drp\ N,\ LIST} --- skip the first N elements and return the
rest.

This operation is fully streaming: when used in a pipeline, the first N
elements are consumed and discarded without buffering, and all
subsequent elements flow through to the next stage. If the list contains
fewer than N elements, the result is empty. The \texttt{skip} and
\texttt{drp} aliases exist for readability in different contexts --- all
three compile to the same internal op.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{1:10 |\textgreater{} drop 3 |\textgreater{} e p                \# 4 5 6 7 8 9 10}
\NormalTok{my @rest = drp 2, @data}
\NormalTok{stdin |\textgreater{} skip 1 |\textgreater{} e p                \# skip header line}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{take\_while}}{take\_while}}\label{take_while}

\texttt{take\_while\ \{\ COND\ \}\ LIST} --- emit leading elements while
the predicate returns true, then stop.

The block receives each element as \texttt{\_}. Elements are emitted as
long as the predicate holds; the moment it returns false, the pipeline
terminates immediately without consuming further input. This
short-circuit behavior makes \texttt{take\_while} efficient on infinite
iterators and large streams. It is the complement of
\texttt{drop\_while}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{1:10 |\textgreater{} take\_while \{ \_ \textless{} 5 \} |\textgreater{} e p \# 1 2 3 4}
\NormalTok{stdin |\textgreater{} take\_while \{ !/\^{}END/ \} |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{drop\_while}}{drop\_while}}\label{drop_while}

\texttt{drop\_while\ \{\ COND\ \}\ LIST} --- skip leading elements while
the predicate returns true, then emit everything after.

The block receives each element as \texttt{\_}. Once the predicate
returns false for the first time, that element and all remaining
elements pass through unconditionally --- the predicate is never
consulted again. This is streaming: elements are tested one at a time
without buffering. Useful for skipping headers, preamble, or sorted
prefixes in data streams.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{1:10 |\textgreater{} drop\_while \{ \_ \textless{} 5 \} |\textgreater{} e p \# 5 6 7 8 9 10}
\NormalTok{@log |\textgreater{} drop\_while \{ /\^{} \#/ \} |\textgreater{} e p    \# skip comment header}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{reject}}{reject}}\label{reject}

\texttt{reject\ \{\ BLOCK\ \}\ LIST} --- stryke-native streaming inverse
of \texttt{filter}/\texttt{greps}. It keeps only the elements for which
the block returns false. This reads more naturally than
\texttt{filter\ \{\ !(...)\ \}} when the condition describes what you
want to exclude rather than what you want to keep. Like \texttt{filter}
and \texttt{greps}, it returns a lazy iterator suitable for
\texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} chains. The block receives \texttt{\_}
as the current element.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{1:10 |\textgreater{} reject \{ \_ \% 3 == 0 \} |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{\# 1 2 4 5 7 8 10}
\NormalTok{@files |\textgreater{} reject \{ /\textbackslash{}.bak$/ \} |\textgreater{} e p   \# skip backups}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{compact}}{compact}}\label{compact}

\texttt{compact} (alias \texttt{cpt}) --- stryke-native streaming
operator that removes \texttt{undef} and empty-string values from a list
or iterator. This is a common data-cleaning step when dealing with
parsed input, optional fields, or split results that produce empty
segments. It is equivalent to
\texttt{greps\ \{\ defined(\_)\ \&\&\ \_\ ne\ ""\ \}} but more concise
and faster because the check is inlined in Rust. Numeric zero and the
string \texttt{"0"} are preserved since they are defined and non-empty.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @raw = (1, undef, "", 2, undef, 3)}
\NormalTok{@raw |\textgreater{} compact |\textgreater{} e p   \# 1 2 3}
\NormalTok{split(/,/, "a,,b,,,c") |\textgreater{} cpt |\textgreater{} e p   \# a b c}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{concat}}{concat}}\label{concat}

\texttt{concat} (alias \texttt{chain}) --- stryke-native streaming
operator that concatenates multiple lists or iterators into a single
sequential iterator. Pass array references and they will be yielded in
order without copying. This is useful for merging data from multiple
sources into a unified pipeline. The operation is lazy: each source is
drained in turn, so memory usage stays proportional to the largest
single element, not the total.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @a = 1:3}
\NormalTok{my @b = 7:9}
\NormalTok{concat(\textbackslash{}@a, \textbackslash{}@b) |\textgreater{} e p   \# 1 2 3 7 8 9}
\NormalTok{my @c = ("x")}
\NormalTok{concat(\textbackslash{}@a, \textbackslash{}@b, \textbackslash{}@c) |\textgreater{} maps \{ uc \} |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{enumerate}}{enumerate}}\label{enumerate}

\texttt{enumerate\ ITERATOR} / \texttt{en\ ITERATOR} --- yield
\texttt{{[}\$index,\ \$item{]}} pairs from a streaming iterator.

Each element is wrapped as \texttt{{[}\$index,\ \$item{]}} where the
index starts at 0 and increments for each element. Unlike
\texttt{with\_index} which returns \texttt{{[}item,\ index{]}},
\texttt{enumerate} uses the Rust/Python convention of
\texttt{{[}index,\ item{]}}. This is a streaming operation: the index
counter is maintained lazily as elements flow through the pipeline.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{stdin |\textgreater{} en |\textgreater{} e \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}[0]: \_{-}\textgreater{}[1]" \}}
\NormalTok{1:5 |\textgreater{} en |\textgreater{} e \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}[0]: \_{-}\textgreater{}[1]" \}}
\NormalTok{@lines |\textgreater{} en |\textgreater{} grep \{ \_{-}\textgreater{}[0] \textless{} 10 \} |\textgreater{} e \{ p \_{-}\textgreater{}[1] \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{chunk}}{chunk}}\label{chunk}

\texttt{chunk\ N,\ ITERATOR} / \texttt{chk\ N,\ ITERATOR} --- group
elements into N-sized arrayrefs as they stream through.

Elements are buffered until N are collected, then the group is emitted
as a single arrayref. The final chunk may contain fewer than N elements
if the source is not evenly divisible. This is fully streaming: only one
chunk is held in memory at a time, making it safe for large or infinite
iterators. Use \texttt{chunk} for batching work (e.g., bulk database
inserts) or formatting output into rows.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{1:9 |\textgreater{} chk 3 |\textgreater{} e \{ p join ",", @\_ \}}
\NormalTok{\# 1,2,3  4,5,6  7,8,9}
\NormalTok{stdin |\textgreater{} chk 100 |\textgreater{} e \{ bulk\_insert(@\_) \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dedup}}{dedup}}\label{dedup}

\texttt{dedup\ ITERATOR} / \texttt{dup\ ITERATOR} --- drop consecutive
duplicate elements from a stream.

Only adjacent duplicates are removed: if the same value appears later
after an intervening different value, it is emitted again. Comparison is
string-based by default. This is a streaming operation that holds only
the previous element in memory, so it works on infinite iterators. For
global deduplication across the entire stream, use \texttt{distinct}
instead.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{1,1,2,2,3,1,1 |\textgreater{} dedup |\textgreater{} e p        \# 1 2 3 1}
\NormalTok{@sorted |\textgreater{} dedup |\textgreater{} e p              \# like uniq(1)}
\NormalTok{stdin |\textgreater{} dedup |\textgreater{} e p                 \# collapse repeated lines}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{distinct}}{distinct}}\label{distinct}

\texttt{distinct\ LIST} --- remove duplicate elements, preserving
first-occurrence order (alias for \texttt{uniq}).

Each element is compared as a string; the first time a value is seen it
is emitted, and all subsequent occurrences are silently dropped. When
used in a pipeline the deduplication state is maintained across streamed
chunks, so \texttt{distinct} works correctly on lazy iterators and
generators. This is a stryke built-in backed by a hash set internally,
so it runs in O(n) time regardless of list size.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @u = distinct(3,1,2,1,3)           \# (3,1,2)}
\NormalTok{1,2,2,3,3,3 |\textgreater{} distinct |\textgreater{} e p        \# 1 2 3}
\NormalTok{stdin |\textgreater{} distinct |\textgreater{} e p              \# unique lines}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{flatten}}{flatten}}\label{flatten}

\texttt{flatten\ LIST} / \texttt{fl\ LIST} --- recursively flatten
nested arrayrefs into a single flat list.

Flatten walks every element: scalars pass through unchanged, arrayrefs
are opened and their contents are recursively flattened, so arbitrarily
deep nesting is handled in one call. In pipeline mode
(\texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}\ fl}) it streams element-by-element,
so you can chain it with \texttt{map}, \texttt{grep}, or \texttt{take}
without materializing the entire intermediate list. The \texttt{fl}
alias keeps pipeline chains concise.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @flat = flatten([1,[2,3]],[4])     \# (1,2,3,4)}
\NormalTok{[1,[2,[3,4]]] |\textgreater{} fl |\textgreater{} e p            \# 1 2 3 4}
\NormalTok{@nested |\textgreater{} fl |\textgreater{} grep \{ \_ \textgreater{} 0 \} |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{with\_index}}{with\_index}}\label{with_index}

\texttt{with\_index\ LIST} / \texttt{wi\ LIST} --- pair each element
with its 0-based index as \texttt{{[}\$item,\ \$index{]}}.

Each element is wrapped in a two-element arrayref where
\texttt{\_-\textgreater{}{[}0{]}} is the original value and
\texttt{\_-\textgreater{}{[}1{]}} is its position. This is useful when
you need positional information during a map or grep without maintaining
a manual counter. Note the order is \texttt{{[}item,\ index{]}}, which
differs from \texttt{enumerate} which yields
\texttt{{[}index,\ item{]}}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{qw(a b c) |\textgreater{} wi |\textgreater{} e \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}[1]: \_{-}\textgreater{}[0]" \}}
\NormalTok{\# 0: a  1: b  2: c}
\NormalTok{@data |\textgreater{} wi |\textgreater{} grep \{ \_{-}\textgreater{}[1] \% 2 == 0 \} |\textgreater{} e \{ p \_{-}\textgreater{}[0] \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{first\_or}}{first\_or}}\label{first_or}

\texttt{first\_or\ DEFAULT,\ LIST} --- return the first element of the
list, or DEFAULT if the list is empty.

This is a streaming terminal: it pulls exactly one element from the
upstream iterator and returns it, or returns the default value if the
iterator is exhausted. It never buffers the entire list. This is
especially useful at the end of a \texttt{grep} or \texttt{map} pipeline
where you need a safe fallback instead of \texttt{undef} when no match
is found.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $v = first\_or 0, @maybe\_empty}
\NormalTok{my $x = grep \{ \_ \textgreater{} 99 \} @nums |\textgreater{} first\_or {-}1}
\NormalTok{stdin |\textgreater{} grep /\^{}ERROR/ |\textgreater{} first\_or "(none)" |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{range}}{range}}\label{range}

\texttt{range(START,\ END\ {[},\ STEP{]})} --- create a lazy integer
iterator from START to END with an optional step.

The range is inclusive on both ends. When START \textgreater{} END and
no step is given, stryke automatically counts downward. An explicit STEP
controls the increment and must be negative when counting down, or the
range will be empty. The iterator is fully lazy: no list is allocated,
and elements are generated on demand, making
\texttt{range(1,\ 1\_000\_000)} as cheap to create as
\texttt{range(1,\ 5)}. Combine with \texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} to
feed into streaming pipelines.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{range(1, 5) |\textgreater{} e p                    \# 1 2 3 4 5}
\NormalTok{range(5, 1) |\textgreater{} e p                    \# 5 4 3 2 1}
\NormalTok{range(0, 10, 2) |\textgreater{} e p                \# 0 2 4 6 8 10}
\NormalTok{range(10, 0, {-}2) |\textgreater{} e p               \# 10 8 6 4 2 0}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stdin}}{stdin}}\label{stdin}

\texttt{stdin} --- return a streaming iterator over lines read from
standard input.

Each call to the iterator reads one line from STDIN, strips the trailing
newline, and yields it. The iterator terminates at EOF. Because it is
lazy, combining \texttt{stdin} with \texttt{take}, \texttt{grep}, or
\texttt{first\_or} processes only as many lines as needed --- the rest
of STDIN is left unconsumed. This is the idiomatic stryke way to build
Unix-style filters.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{stdin |\textgreater{} grep /error/i |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{stdin |\textgreater{} take 5 |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{stdin |\textgreater{} en |\textgreater{} e \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}[0]: \_{-}\textgreater{}[1]" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{nth}}{nth}}\label{nth}

\texttt{nth\ N,\ LIST} --- return the Nth element using 0-based
indexing.

When used in a pipeline, \texttt{nth} consumes and discards the first N
elements, returns the next one, and stops --- so it short-circuits on
infinite iterators. On a plain list, it is equivalent to
\texttt{\$list{[}N{]}} but works as a function call for pipeline
composition. Returns \texttt{undef} if the list has fewer than N+1
elements.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $third = nth 2, @data}
\NormalTok{1:10 |\textgreater{} nth 4 |\textgreater{} p                   \# 5}
\NormalTok{stdin |\textgreater{} nth 0 |\textgreater{} p                   \# first line}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-list-operations}
\chapter{List Operations}\label{list-operations}

62 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{map}}{map}}\label{map}

\texttt{map\ BLOCK\ LIST} --- evaluates the block for each element of
the list, setting \texttt{\_} to the current element, and returns a new
list of all the block\textquotesingle s return values. This is the eager
version: it consumes the entire input list and produces the entire
output list before returning. Use \texttt{map} when you need the full
result array or when the input is small. For large or infinite
sequences, prefer \texttt{maps} (the streaming variant). The block can
return zero, one, or multiple values per element, making \texttt{map}
useful for both transformation and flattening. Use
\texttt{\{\ \textbar{}\$var\textbar{}\ body\ \}} to name the block
parameter instead of \texttt{\_}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @sq = map \{ \_ ** 2 \} 1:5}
\NormalTok{@sq |\textgreater{} e p   \# 1 4 9 16 25}
\NormalTok{map \{ |$n| $n * $n \}, 1:5   \# named param}
\NormalTok{my @pairs = map \{ (\_, \_ * 10) \} 1:3}
\NormalTok{@pairs |\textgreater{} e p   \# 1 10 2 20 3 30}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{grep}}{grep}}\label{grep}

\texttt{grep\ \{\ BLOCK\ \}\ LIST} --- filters the list, returning only
elements for which the block evaluates to true. The current element is
available as \texttt{\_}. This is the eager version: it processes the
entire list and returns a new list. It is Perl-compatible and works
exactly like Perl\textquotesingle s builtin \texttt{grep}. For
streaming/lazy filtering in \texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} pipelines,
use \texttt{greps} or \texttt{filter} instead. Use
\texttt{\{\ \textbar{}\$var\textbar{}\ body\ \}} to name the block
parameter instead of \texttt{\_}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @evens = grep \{ \_ \% 2 == 0 \} 1:10}
\NormalTok{@evens |\textgreater{} e p   \# 2 4 6 8 10}
\NormalTok{grep \{ |$x| $x \textgreater{} 3 \}, 1:6   \# named param}
\NormalTok{my @long = grep \{ len \textgreater{} 3 \} @words}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sort}}{sort}}\label{sort}

\texttt{sort\ {[}BLOCK{]}\ LIST} --- returns a new list sorted in
ascending order. Without a block, \texttt{sort} compares elements as
strings (lexicographic). Pass a comparator block; the two candidates are
\texttt{\_} and \texttt{\_1}. Use \texttt{\textless{}=\textgreater{}}
for numeric and \texttt{cmp} for string comparison. The sort is stable
in stryke, meaning equal elements preserve their original relative
order. For descending order, reverse the operands in the comparator. Use
\texttt{\{\ \textbar{}\$x,\ \$y\textbar{}\ body\ \}} to name the two
comparator params. Works naturally in \texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}}
pipelines.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @nums = (3, 1, 4, 1, 5)}
\NormalTok{my @asc = sort \{ \_ \textless{}=\textgreater{} \_1 \} @nums}
\NormalTok{@asc |\textgreater{} e p   \# 1 1 3 4 5}
\NormalTok{sort \{ |$x, $y| $y \textless{}=\textgreater{} $x \}, @nums   \# named params}
\NormalTok{my @desc = sort \{ \_1 \textless{}=\textgreater{} \_ \} @nums}
\NormalTok{@desc |\textgreater{} e p  \# 5 4 3 1 1}
\NormalTok{@nums |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} e p   \# string sort in pipeline}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{reverse}}{reverse}}\label{reverse}

\texttt{reverse\ LIST} --- in list context, return the elements in
reverse order; in scalar context, reverse the characters of a string.

The dual nature of \texttt{reverse} is context-dependent:
\texttt{reverse\ @array} flips element order, while
\texttt{scalar\ reverse\ \$string} (or just \texttt{reverse\ \$string}
in scalar context) reverses character order. In stryke, the string form
is Unicode-aware, correctly reversing multi-byte characters rather than
individual bytes. In \texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} pipelines, use
the \texttt{t\ rev} shorthand for a concise streaming string reverse.
\texttt{reverse} does not modify the original --- it always returns a
new list or string.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p reverse "hello"       \# olleh}
\NormalTok{my @r = reverse 1:5   \# (5,4,3,2,1)}
\NormalTok{@r |\textgreater{} e p               \# 5 4 3 2 1}
\NormalTok{"abc" |\textgreater{} t rev |\textgreater{} p     \# cba}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{reduce}}{reduce}}\label{reduce}

\texttt{reduce\ \{\ \_\ OP\ \_1\ \}\ @list} --- performs a sequential
left fold over the list. The first two elements are \texttt{\_} and
\texttt{\_1}; each iteration\textquotesingle s result becomes the next
\texttt{\_}. Use
\texttt{\{\ \textbar{}\$acc,\ \$val\textbar{}\ body\ \}} to name the two
params. Returns \texttt{undef} for an empty list and the single element
for a one-element list. For a fold with an explicit initial value, see
\texttt{fold}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $fac = reduce \{ \_ * \_1 \} 1:6}
\NormalTok{p $fac   \# 720}
\NormalTok{reduce \{ |$acc, $val| $acc + $val \}, 1:10   \# 55 (named params)}
\NormalTok{my $longest = reduce \{ length(\_) \textgreater{} length(\_1) ? \_ : \_1 \} @words}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{fold}}{fold}}\label{fold}

\texttt{fold\ \{\ \_\ OP\ \_1\ \}\ INIT,\ @list} --- left fold with an
explicit initial accumulator value. The initial value is the first
\texttt{\_}, and each list element is \texttt{\_1}. Unlike
\texttt{reduce}, \texttt{fold} never returns \texttt{undef} for an empty
list --- it returns the initial value instead. Use \texttt{fold} when
you need a guaranteed starting point for the accumulation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $total = fold \{ \_ + \_1 \} 100, 1:5}
\NormalTok{p $total   \# 115}
\NormalTok{my $csv = fold \{ \_ . "," . \_1 \} "", @fields}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{reductions}}{reductions}}\label{reductions}
