\texttt{reductions\ \{\ \_\ OP\ \_1\ \}\ @list} --- returns the running
(cumulative) results of a left fold, also known as a scan or prefix-sum.
Each element of the output is the accumulator state after processing the
corresponding input element. The output list has the same length as the
input.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @pfx = reductions \{ \_ + \_1 \} 1:4}
\NormalTok{@pfx |\textgreater{} e p   \# 1 3 6 10}
\NormalTok{1:5 |\textgreater{} reductions \{ \_ * \_1 \} |\textgreater{} e p  \# 1 2 6 24 120}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{all}}{all}}\label{all}

\texttt{all\ \{\ COND\ \}\ @list} --- returns true (1) if every element
in the list satisfies the predicate, false ("") otherwise. The block
receives each element as \texttt{\_} and should return a boolean.
Short-circuits on the first failing element, so it never evaluates more
than necessary. Works with \texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} pipelines
and accepts bare lists or array variables.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @nums = 2, 4, 6, 8}
\NormalTok{p all \{ \_ \% 2 == 0 \} @nums   \# 1}
\NormalTok{1:100 |\textgreater{} all \{ \_ \textgreater{} 0 \} |\textgreater{} p  \# 1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{any}}{any}}\label{any}

\texttt{any\ \{\ COND\ \}\ @list} --- returns true (1) if at least one
element satisfies the predicate, false ("") if none do. The block
receives each element as \texttt{\_}. Short-circuits on the first match,
making it efficient even on large lists. \texttt{any} is a stryke global
builtin and can be used in \texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} pipelines.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @vals = 1, 3, 5, 8}
\NormalTok{p any \{ \_ \textgreater{} 7 \} @vals   \# 1}
\NormalTok{1:1000 |\textgreater{} any \{ \_ == 42 \} |\textgreater{} p  \# 1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{none}}{none}}\label{none}

\texttt{none\ \{\ COND\ \}\ @list} --- returns true (1) if no element
satisfies the predicate, false ("") if any element matches. Logically
equivalent to \texttt{!any\ \{\ COND\ \}\ @list} but reads more
naturally in guard clauses. Short-circuits on the first match. Useful
for validation checks where you want to assert the absence of a
condition.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @words = ("cat", "dog", "bird")}
\NormalTok{p none \{ /z/ \} @words   \# 1}
\NormalTok{p "all non{-}negative" if none \{ \_ \textless{} 0 \} @vals}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{first}}{first}}\label{first}

\texttt{first\ \{\ COND\ \}\ @list} --- returns the first element for
which the block returns true, or \texttt{undef} if no element matches.
The alias \texttt{fst} is also available. Short-circuits immediately
upon finding a match, so only the minimum number of elements are tested.
Ideal for searching sorted or unsorted lists when you need a single
result.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $f = first \{ \_ \textgreater{} 10 \} 3, 7, 12, 20}
\NormalTok{p $f   \# 12}
\NormalTok{1:1\_000\_000 |\textgreater{} first \{ \_ \% 9999 == 0 \} |\textgreater{} p  \# 9999}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{min}}{min}}\label{min}

\texttt{min\ @list} --- returns the numerically smallest value from a
list. Compares all elements using numeric
(\texttt{\textless{}=\textgreater{}}) comparison, so stringy values are
coerced to numbers. Returns \texttt{undef} for an empty list. In stryke,
\texttt{min} is a global built-in (no import needed) and works directly
in \texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} pipelines.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p min(5, 3, 9, 1)   \# 1}
\NormalTok{my @temps = (72.1, 68.5, 74.3)}
\NormalTok{@temps |\textgreater{} min |\textgreater{} p  \# 68.5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{max}}{max}}\label{max}

\texttt{max\ @list} --- returns the numerically largest value from a
list. Compares all elements using numeric
(\texttt{\textless{}=\textgreater{}}) comparison. Returns \texttt{undef}
for an empty list. Like \texttt{min}, this is a stryke built-in
available without imports and works in \texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}}
pipelines. Combine with \texttt{map} to extract max values from complex
structures.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p max(5, 3, 9, 1)   \# 9}
\NormalTok{1:100 |\textgreater{} map \{ \_ ** 2 \} |\textgreater{} max |\textgreater{} p  \# 10000}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sum}}{sum}}\label{sum}

\texttt{sum\ @list} returns the numeric sum of all elements. Returns
\texttt{undef} for an empty list. \texttt{sum0} is identical except it
returns \texttt{0} for an empty list, which avoids the need for a
fallback \texttt{//\ 0} guard. Both are stryke built-ins that work in
\texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} pipelines. Use \texttt{sum0} in
contexts where an empty input is expected and you need a safe numeric
default.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p sum(1:100)    \# 5050}
\NormalTok{p sum0()          \# 0}
\NormalTok{@prices |\textgreater{} map \{ \_{-}\textgreater{}\{amount\} \} |\textgreater{} sum0 |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{product}}{product}}\label{product}

\texttt{product\ @list} --- returns the product of all numeric elements
in the list. Returns \texttt{undef} for an empty list. Useful for
computing factorials, combinatoric products, and compound multipliers.
In stryke this is a built-in that composes naturally with
\texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} pipelines and \texttt{range}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p product(1:5)   \# 120}
\NormalTok{range(1, 10) |\textgreater{} product |\textgreater{} p  \# 3628800}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mean}}{mean}}\label{mean}

\texttt{mean\ @list} --- returns the arithmetic mean (average) of a
numeric list. Computed as \texttt{sum\ /\ count} in a single pass.
Returns \texttt{undef} for an empty list. The result is always a
floating-point value even if all inputs are integers. Combine with
\texttt{map} to compute averages over extracted fields.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mean(2, 4, 6, 8)   \# 5}
\NormalTok{@students |\textgreater{} map \{ \_{-}\textgreater{}\{score\} \} |\textgreater{} mean |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{median}}{median}}\label{median}

\texttt{median\ @list} --- returns the median value of a numeric list.
For odd-length lists, this is the middle element after sorting. For
even-length lists, it is the arithmetic mean of the two middle elements.
The input list does not need to be pre-sorted. Returns \texttt{undef}
for an empty list.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p median(1, 3, 5, 7, 9)   \# 5}
\NormalTok{p median(1, 3, 5, 7)      \# 4}
\NormalTok{1:100 |\textgreater{} median |\textgreater{} p     \# 50.5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mode}}{mode}}\label{mode}

\texttt{mode\ @list} --- returns the most frequently occurring value in
the list. If there is a tie, the value that appears first in the list
wins. Returns \texttt{undef} for an empty list. Works with both numeric
and string values --- comparison is done by stringification. Useful for
finding the dominant category in a dataset.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mode(1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3)   \# 3}
\NormalTok{my @logs = qw(INFO WARN INFO ERROR INFO)}
\NormalTok{p mode(@logs)  \# INFO}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stddev}}{stddev}}\label{stddev}

\texttt{stddev\ @list} (alias \texttt{std}) --- returns the population
standard deviation of a numeric list. This is the square root of the
population variance, measuring how spread out values are from the mean.
Uses N (not N-1) in the denominator, so it computes the population
statistic rather than the sample statistic. Returns \texttt{undef} for
an empty list.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p stddev(2, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 7, 9)   \# 2}
\NormalTok{1:10 |\textgreater{} std |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{variance}}{variance}}\label{variance}

\texttt{variance\ @list} --- returns the population variance of a
numeric list, computed as the mean of the squared deviations from the
mean. Like \texttt{stddev}, this uses N (not N-1) as the divisor. The
variance is \texttt{stddev\ **\ 2}. Returns \texttt{undef} for an empty
list. Useful for statistical analysis and as a building block for
higher-order stats.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p variance(2, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 7, 9)   \# 4}
\NormalTok{my @samples = map \{ rand(100) \} 1:1000}
\NormalTok{p variance(@samples)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sample}}{sample}}\label{sample}

\texttt{sample\ N,\ @list} --- returns a random sample of N elements
drawn without replacement from the list. The returned elements are in
random order. If N exceeds the list length, the entire list is returned
(shuffled). Uses a Fisher-Yates partial shuffle internally for
efficiency. Each call produces a different result due to random
selection.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @pick = sample 3, 1:100}
\NormalTok{@pick |\textgreater{} e p   \# 3 random values}
\NormalTok{my @test\_cases = sample 10, @all\_cases}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{shuffle}}{shuffle}}\label{shuffle}

\texttt{shuffle\ @list} --- returns a new list with all elements in
random order using a Fisher-Yates shuffle. The original list is not
modified. Every permutation is equally likely. In stryke this is a
global built-in; no import is needed. The alias \texttt{shuf} is also
available. Commonly used with \texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} and
\texttt{take} to draw random subsets.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @deck = shuffle 1:52}
\NormalTok{@deck |\textgreater{} take 5 |\textgreater{} e p   \# 5 random cards}
\NormalTok{my @randomized = shuffle @questions}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{uniq}}{uniq}}\label{uniq}

\texttt{uniq\ @list} --- removes duplicate elements from a list,
preserving the order of first occurrence. Comparison is done by string
equality. The alias \texttt{uq} is also available. This is eager (not
streaming) and returns a new list. For streaming deduplication in
\texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} pipelines, use \texttt{distinct}. For
type-specific comparison, see \texttt{uniqnum}, \texttt{uniqstr}, and
\texttt{uniqint}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @u = uniq 1, 2, 2, 3, 1, 3}
\NormalTok{@u |\textgreater{} e p   \# 1 2 3}
\NormalTok{my @hosts = uniq @all\_hosts}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{uniqint}}{uniqint}}\label{uniqint}

\texttt{uniqint\ @list} --- removes duplicate elements comparing values
as integers. Each element is truncated to its integer part before
comparison, so \texttt{1.1} and \texttt{1.9} are considered equal (both
become \texttt{1}). The first occurrence is kept. This is useful when
you have floating-point data but care only about the integer portion for
uniqueness.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @u = uniqint 1, 1.1, 1.9, 2}
\NormalTok{@u |\textgreater{} e p   \# 1 2}
\NormalTok{my @distinct\_ids = uniqint @raw\_ids}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{uniqnum}}{uniqnum}}\label{uniqnum}

\texttt{uniqnum\ @list} --- removes duplicate elements comparing values
as floating-point numbers. Unlike \texttt{uniq} (which compares as
strings), \texttt{uniqnum} treats \texttt{1.0} and \texttt{1.00} as
equal because they have the same numeric value. The first occurrence of
each numeric value is preserved. Use this when your data contains
numbers that may have different string representations.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @u = uniqnum 1.0, 1.00, 2.5, 2.50}
\NormalTok{@u |\textgreater{} e p   \# 1 2.5}
\NormalTok{my @prices = uniqnum @all\_prices}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{uniqstr}}{uniqstr}}\label{uniqstr}

\texttt{uniqstr\ @list} --- removes duplicate elements comparing values
strictly as strings. This is the same comparison as \texttt{uniq} but
makes the intent explicit. Numeric values \texttt{1} and \texttt{1.0}
are considered different because their string representations differ.
Use \texttt{uniqstr} when you want to be explicit that string semantics
are intended.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @u = uniqstr "a", "b", "a", "c"}
\NormalTok{@u |\textgreater{} e p   \# a b c}
\NormalTok{my @tags = uniqstr @all\_tags}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{zip}}{zip}}\label{zip}

\texttt{zip(\textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b,\ ...)} --- combines
multiple arrays element-wise into a list of arrayrefs. Each output
arrayref contains one element from each input array at the corresponding
index. Accepts two or more array references. The alias \texttt{zp} is
also available. By default, \texttt{zip} pads shorter arrays with
\texttt{undef} (equivalent to \texttt{zip\_longest}). For truncating
behavior, use \texttt{zip\_shortest}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @a = 1:3}
\NormalTok{my @b = ("a","b","c")}
\NormalTok{zip(\textbackslash{}@a, \textbackslash{}@b) |\textgreater{} e p   \# [1,a] [2,b] [3,c]}
\NormalTok{my @matrix = zip(\textbackslash{}@xs, \textbackslash{}@ys, \textbackslash{}@zs)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{zip\_longest}}{zip\_longest}}\label{zip_longest}

\texttt{zip\_longest(\textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b,\ ...)} ---
combines arrays element-wise, padding shorter arrays with \texttt{undef}
to match the longest. This is the explicit version of the default
\texttt{zip} behavior. Every input array contributes exactly one element
per output tuple; missing elements become \texttt{undef}. Useful when
you need to process all data from unequal-length sources.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @a = 1:3}
\NormalTok{my @b = ("x")}
\NormalTok{zip\_longest(\textbackslash{}@a, \textbackslash{}@b) |\textgreater{} e p   \# [1,x] [2,undef] [3,undef]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{zip\_shortest}}{zip\_shortest}}\label{zip_shortest}

\texttt{zip\_shortest(\textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b,\ ...)}
--- combines arrays element-wise, stopping at the shortest input array.
No \texttt{undef} padding is produced; the output length equals the
minimum input length. Use this when you only want complete tuples and
extra trailing elements should be discarded.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @a = 1:5}
\NormalTok{my @b = ("x","y")}
\NormalTok{zip\_shortest(\textbackslash{}@a, \textbackslash{}@b) |\textgreater{} e p   \# [1,x] [2,y]}
\NormalTok{my @paired = zip\_shortest(\textbackslash{}@keys, \textbackslash{}@values)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{chunked}}{chunked}}\label{chunked}

\texttt{chunked\ N,\ @list} --- splits a list into non-overlapping
chunks of N elements each. Each chunk is an arrayref. The final chunk
may contain fewer than N elements if the list length is not evenly
divisible. The alias \texttt{chk} is also available. In stryke,
\texttt{chunked} is eager and returns a list of arrayrefs; for streaming
chunk behavior in pipelines, use \texttt{chunk}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @ch = chunked 3, 1:7}
\NormalTok{@ch |\textgreater{} e p   \# [1,2,3] [4,5,6] [7]}
\NormalTok{1:12 |\textgreater{} chunked 4 |\textgreater{} e \{ p join ",", @\_ \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{windowed}}{windowed}}\label{windowed}

\texttt{windowed\ N,\ @list} --- returns a sliding window of N
consecutive elements over the list. Each window is an arrayref. The
output contains \texttt{len\ -\ N\ +\ 1} windows. Unlike
\texttt{chunked}, windows overlap: each successive window advances by
one element. The alias \texttt{win} is also available. Useful for
computing moving averages, detecting patterns in sequences, or n-gram
extraction.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @w = windowed 3, 1:5}
\NormalTok{@w |\textgreater{} e p   \# [1,2,3] [2,3,4] [3,4,5]}
\NormalTok{my @deltas = windowed(2, @vals) |\textgreater{} map \{ \_{-}\textgreater{}[1] {-} \_{-}\textgreater{}[0] \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pairs}}{pairs}}\label{pairs}

\texttt{pairs\ @list} --- takes a flat list and groups consecutive
elements into pairs, returning a list of two-element arrayrefs
\texttt{({[}\$k,\ \$v{]},\ ...)}. The input list must have an even
number of elements. Each pair can be accessed via array indexing
(\texttt{\_-\textgreater{}{[}0{]}}, \texttt{\_-\textgreater{}{[}1{]}}).
This is the inverse of \texttt{unpairs} and is commonly used to iterate
over hash-like flat lists in a structured way.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @p = pairs "a", 1, "b", 2}
\NormalTok{@p |\textgreater{} e \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}[0]=\_{-}\textgreater{}[1]" \}   \# a=1 b=2}
\NormalTok{my @entries = pairs \%hash}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{unpairs}}{unpairs}}\label{unpairs}

\texttt{unpairs\ @list\_of\_pairs} --- flattens a list of two-element
arrayrefs back into a flat key-value list. This is the inverse of
\texttt{pairs}. Each arrayref \texttt{{[}\$k,\ \$v{]}} becomes two
consecutive elements in the output. Useful for converting structured
pair data back into a format suitable for hash assignment or flat list
processing.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @flat = unpairs ["a",1], ["b",2]}
\NormalTok{@flat |\textgreater{} e p   \# a 1 b 2}
\NormalTok{my \%h = unpairs @filtered\_pairs}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pairkeys}}{pairkeys}}\label{pairkeys}

\texttt{pairkeys\ @list} --- extracts the keys (even-indexed elements)
from a flat pairlist. Given a list like
\texttt{("a",\ 1,\ "b",\ 2,\ "c",\ 3)}, returns
\texttt{("a",\ "b",\ "c")}. This is equivalent to
\texttt{map\ \{\ \_-\textgreater{}{[}0{]}\ \}\ pairs\ @list} but more
concise and efficient. Useful for extracting just the key side of a
key-value flat list without constructing intermediate pair objects.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @k = pairkeys "a", 1, "b", 2, "c", 3}
\NormalTok{@k |\textgreater{} e p   \# a b c}
\NormalTok{my @config\_pairs = (host =\textgreater{} "localhost", port =\textgreater{} 8080)}
\NormalTok{my @names = pairkeys @config\_pairs}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pairvalues}}{pairvalues}}\label{pairvalues}

\texttt{pairvalues\ @list} --- extracts the values (odd-indexed
elements) from a flat pairlist. Given \texttt{("a",\ 1,\ "b",\ 2)},
returns \texttt{(1,\ 2)}. This is equivalent to
\texttt{map\ \{\ \_-\textgreater{}{[}1{]}\ \}\ pairs\ @list} but more
concise. Pair it with \texttt{pairkeys} to split a flat key-value list
into separate key and value arrays.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @v = pairvalues "a", 1, "b", 2}
\NormalTok{@v |\textgreater{} e p   \# 1 2}
\NormalTok{my @defaults = (timeout =\textgreater{} 30, retries =\textgreater{} 3)}
\NormalTok{my @settings = pairvalues @defaults}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pairmap}}{pairmap}}\label{pairmap}

\texttt{pairmap\ BLOCK\ @list} --- maps over consecutive pairs in a flat
list, passing the key as \texttt{\_} and the value as \texttt{\_1}. The
block can return any number of elements. This is the pair-aware
equivalent of \texttt{map} and is ideal for transforming hash-like flat
lists. The result is a flat list of whatever the block returns.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @out = pairmap \{ \_ . "=" . \_1 \} "a", 1, "b", 2}
\NormalTok{@out |\textgreater{} e p   \# a=1 b=2}
\NormalTok{my @cfg\_pairs = (host =\textgreater{} "x", port =\textgreater{} 80)}
\NormalTok{my @upper = pairmap \{ uc(\_), \_1 \} @cfg\_pairs}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pairgrep}}{pairgrep}}\label{pairgrep}

\texttt{pairgrep\ \{\ BLOCK\ \}\ @list} --- filters consecutive pairs
from a flat list, keeping only those where the block returns true. The
key is \texttt{\_} and the value is \texttt{\_1}. Returns a flat list of
the matching key-value pairs. This is the pair-aware equivalent of
\texttt{grep} and is useful for filtering hash-like data by both key and
value simultaneously.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @big = pairgrep \{ \_1 \textgreater{} 5 \} "a", 3, "b", 9, "c", 1}
\NormalTok{@big |\textgreater{} e p   \# b 9}
\NormalTok{my @alert\_pairs = (info =\textgreater{} 1, critical\_a =\textgreater{} 9, critical\_b =\textgreater{} 7)}
\NormalTok{my @important = pairgrep \{ \_ =\textasciitilde{} /\^{}critical/ \} @alert\_pairs}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pairfirst}}{pairfirst}}\label{pairfirst}

\texttt{pairfirst\ \{\ BLOCK\ \}\ @list} --- returns the first pair from
a flat list where the block returns true, as a two-element list
\texttt{(\$key,\ \$value)}. The key is \texttt{\_} and the value is
\texttt{\_1}. Short-circuits on the first match. Returns an empty list
if no pair matches. This is the pair-aware equivalent of \texttt{first}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @hit = pairfirst \{ \_1 \textgreater{} 5 \} "x", 2, "y", 8}
\NormalTok{p "@hit"   \# y 8}
\NormalTok{my @flags = (info =\textgreater{} 1, debug =\textgreater{} 7, trace =\textgreater{} 0)}
\NormalTok{my ($k, $v) = pairfirst \{ \_ eq "debug" \} @flags}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mesh}}{mesh}}\label{mesh}

\texttt{mesh(\textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b,\ ...)} ---
interleaves multiple arrays into a flat list rather than arrayrefs.
While \texttt{zip} returns \texttt{({[}1,"a"{]},\ {[}2,"b"{]})},
\texttt{mesh} returns \texttt{(1,\ "a",\ 2,\ "b")}. This makes it ideal
for constructing hashes from parallel key and value arrays. The result
is a flat list suitable for direct hash assignment.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @k = ("a","b")}
\NormalTok{my @v = (1,2)}
\NormalTok{my \%h = mesh(\textbackslash{}@k, \textbackslash{}@v)}
\NormalTok{p $h\{a\}   \# 1}
\NormalTok{my \%lookup = mesh(\textbackslash{}@ids, \textbackslash{}@names)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mesh\_longest}}{mesh\_longest}}\label{mesh_longest}

\texttt{mesh\_longest(\textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b,\ ...)}
--- interleaves arrays into a flat list, padding shorter arrays with
\texttt{undef} to match the longest. Like \texttt{mesh}, the output is
flat (not arrayrefs). Missing elements become \texttt{undef} in the
output sequence. Use this when building a flat interleaved list from
arrays of unequal length where you need every element represented.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @a = 1:3}
\NormalTok{my @b = ("x")}
\NormalTok{my @r = mesh\_longest(\textbackslash{}@a, \textbackslash{}@b)}
\NormalTok{@r |\textgreater{} e p   \# 1 x 2 undef 3 undef}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mesh\_shortest}}{mesh\_shortest}}\label{mesh_shortest}

\texttt{mesh\_shortest(\textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b,\ ...)}
--- interleaves arrays into a flat list, stopping at the shortest input
array. No \texttt{undef} padding is produced; trailing elements from
longer arrays are silently dropped. Use this when you only want complete
interleaved groups and partial data should be discarded.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @a = 1:3}
\NormalTok{my @b = ("x","y")}
\NormalTok{my @r = mesh\_shortest(\textbackslash{}@a, \textbackslash{}@b)}
\NormalTok{@r |\textgreater{} e p   \# 1 x 2 y}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{partition}}{partition}}\label{partition}

\texttt{partition} --- operation \texttt{partition}. Alias for
\texttt{take\_while}. Category: *functional / iterator*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p partition $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{frequencies}}{frequencies}}\label{frequencies}

\texttt{frequencies} (aliases \texttt{freq}, \texttt{frq}) counts how
many times each distinct element appears in a list and returns a hashref
mapping each value to its count. This is the stryke equivalent of a
histogram or counter --- useful for analyzing log files, counting word
occurrences, tallying categorical data, or finding duplicates. The input
list is flattened, so you can pass arrays directly. The returned hashref
can be fed into \texttt{dd} for inspection or \texttt{to\_json} for
serialization.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @words = qw(apple banana apple cherry banana apple)}
\NormalTok{my $counts = freq @words}
\NormalTok{p $counts   \# \{apple =\textgreater{} 3, banana =\textgreater{} 2, cherry =\textgreater{} 1\}}
\NormalTok{rl("access.log") |\textgreater{} map \{ /\^{}(\textbackslash{}S+)/ \&\& $1 \} |\textgreater{} freq |\textgreater{} dd}
\NormalTok{my @rolls = map \{ 1 + int(rand 6) \} 1:1000}
\NormalTok{frq(@rolls) |\textgreater{} to\_json |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tally}}{tally}}\label{tally}

\texttt{tally} counts how many times each distinct element appears in a
list and returns a hash ref mapping element → count. This is the same as
Ruby\textquotesingle s \texttt{Enumerable\#tally} or
Python\textquotesingle s \texttt{Counter}. Similar to
\texttt{frequencies} but follows the Ruby naming convention.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $t = tally("a", "b", "a", "c", "a", "b")}
\NormalTok{p $t{-}\textgreater{}\{a\}   \# 3}
\NormalTok{p $t{-}\textgreater{}\{b\}   \# 2}
\NormalTok{qw(red blue red green blue red) |\textgreater{} tally |\textgreater{} dd}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{interleave}}{interleave}}\label{interleave}

\texttt{interleave} (alias \texttt{il}) merges two or more arrays by
alternating their elements: first element of each array, then second
element of each, and so on. If the arrays have different lengths,
shorter arrays contribute \texttt{undef} for their missing positions.
This is useful for building key-value pair lists from separate key and
value arrays, creating round-robin schedules, or weaving parallel data
streams together.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @keys = qw(name age city)}
\NormalTok{my @vals = ("Alice", 30, "NYC")}
\NormalTok{my @pairs = il \textbackslash{}@keys, \textbackslash{}@vals}
\NormalTok{p @pairs   \# name, Alice, age, 30, city, NYC}
\NormalTok{my \%h = il \textbackslash{}@keys, \textbackslash{}@vals}
\NormalTok{p $h\{name\} \# Alice}
\NormalTok{my @rgb = il [255,0,0], [0,255,0], [0,0,255]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pluck}}{pluck}}\label{pluck}

\texttt{pluck\ KEY,\ LIST\_OF\_HASHREFS} --- extract a single key from
each hashref in the list.

For each element, \texttt{pluck} dereferences it as a hashref and
returns the value at the given key. Elements where the key is missing
yield \texttt{undef}. This is a streaming operation: in a pipeline, each
hashref is processed and the extracted value is emitted immediately. It
is the stryke equivalent of
\texttt{map\ \{\ \_-\textgreater{}\{KEY\}\ \}} but more readable and
optimized internally.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{@users |\textgreater{} pluck "name" |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{my @ids = pluck "id", @records}
\NormalTok{@rows |\textgreater{} pluck "email" |\textgreater{} distinct |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{grep\_v}}{grep\_v}}\label{grep_v}

\texttt{grep\_v\ PATTERN,\ LIST} --- inverse grep: reject elements that
match the pattern, keep the rest.

This is the complement of \texttt{grep} --- any element where the
pattern matches is dropped, and non-matching elements pass through. It
accepts a regex, a string, or a code block as the pattern. In streaming
mode, each element is tested and either forwarded or discarded without
buffering. This is a stryke built-in that avoids the awkward
\texttt{grep\ \{\ !/pattern/\ \}} double-negation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{@words |\textgreater{} grep\_v /\^{} \#/ |\textgreater{} e p          \# drop comments}
\NormalTok{my @clean = grep\_v qr/tmp/, @files}
\NormalTok{stdin |\textgreater{} grep\_v /\^{}\textbackslash{}s*$/ |\textgreater{} e p        \# drop blank lines}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{select\_keys}}{select\_keys}}\label{select_keys}

\texttt{select\_keys} --- operation \texttt{select\ keys}. Category:
*pipeline / string helpers*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p select\_keys $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{clamp}}{clamp}}\label{clamp}

\texttt{clamp} (alias \texttt{clp}) constrains each value in a list to
lie within a specified minimum and maximum range. Values below the
minimum are raised to it; values above the maximum are lowered to it;
values already in range pass through unchanged. This is essential for
sanitizing user input, bounding computed values before display, or
enforcing physical constraints in simulations. When given a single
scalar, it returns a single clamped value.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @scores = (105, {-}3, 42, 99, 200)}
\NormalTok{my @clamped = clp 0, 100, @scores}
\NormalTok{p @clamped   \# 100, 0, 42, 99, 100}
\NormalTok{my $val = clp 0, 255, $input   \# bound to byte range}
\NormalTok{my @pct = map \{ clp 0.0, 1.0, \_ \} @raw\_ratios}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{normalize}}{normalize}}\label{normalize}

\texttt{normalize} (alias \texttt{nrm}) rescales a list of numeric
values so that the minimum maps to 0 and the maximum maps to 1, using
min-max normalization. This is a standard preprocessing step for machine
learning features, data visualization (mapping values to color gradients
or bar heights), and statistical analysis. If all values are identical,
the result is a list of zeros to avoid division by zero. The output
preserves the relative ordering of the input.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @temps = (32, 68, 100, 212)}
\NormalTok{my @normed = nrm @temps}
\NormalTok{p @normed   \# 0, 0.2, 0.377..., 1}
\NormalTok{my @pixels = nrm @raw\_intensities}
\NormalTok{@pixels |\textgreater{} map \{ int(\_ * 255) \} |\textgreater{} e p  \# scale to 0{-}255}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{compose}}{compose}}\label{compose}

\texttt{compose} (alias \texttt{comp}) creates a right-to-left function
composition. Given
\texttt{compose(\textbackslash{}\&f,\ \textbackslash{}\&g)}, calling the
result with \texttt{x} computes \texttt{f(g(x))}. Chain any number of
functions --- they apply from right to left (last argument first). This
is the standard mathematical function composition found in Haskell,
Clojure, and Ramda. The returned code ref can be stored, passed around,
or used in pipelines.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $double = fn \{ \_0 * 2 \}}
\NormalTok{my $inc    = fn \{ \_0 + 1 \}}
\NormalTok{my $f = compose($inc, $double)}
\NormalTok{p $f{-}\textgreater{}(5)   \# 11  (double 5 → 10, inc 10 → 11)}

\NormalTok{my $pipeline = compose(}
\NormalTok{    fn \{ join ",", @\{\_0\} \},}
\NormalTok{    fn \{ [sort @\{\_0\}] \},}
\NormalTok{    fn \{ [grep \{ \_ \textgreater{} 2 \} @\{\_0\}] \},}
\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{p $pipeline{-}\textgreater{}([3,1,4,1,5])  \# 3,4,5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{partial}}{partial}}\label{partial}

\texttt{partial} returns a partially applied function --- the bound
arguments are prepended to any arguments supplied at call time.
\texttt{partial(\textbackslash{}\&f,\ @bound)-\textgreater{}(x)} is
equivalent to \texttt{f(@bound,\ x)}. This is the standard partial
application from functional programming, useful for creating specialized
versions of general functions without closures.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $add = fn \{ \_0 + \_1 \}}
\NormalTok{my $add5 = partial($add, 5)}
\NormalTok{p $add5{-}\textgreater{}(3)   \# 8}

\NormalTok{my $log = fn \{ p "[\#\{\_0\}] \#\{\_1\}" \}}
\NormalTok{my $warn\_log = partial($log, "WARN")}
\NormalTok{$warn\_log{-}\textgreater{}("disk full")   \# [WARN] disk full}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{curry}}{curry}}\label{curry}

\texttt{curry} auto-curries a function with a given arity. The curried
function accumulates arguments across calls and invokes the original
only when enough have been collected.
\texttt{curry(\textbackslash{}\&f,\ N)-\textgreater{}(a)-\textgreater{}(b)}
calls \texttt{f(a,\ b)} when N=2. If all arguments are supplied at once,
it calls immediately.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $add = curry(fn \{ \_0 + \_1 \}, 2)}
\NormalTok{my $add5 = $add{-}\textgreater{}(5)}
\NormalTok{p $add5{-}\textgreater{}(3)       \# 8}
\NormalTok{p $add{-}\textgreater{}(10, 20)   \# 30  (enough args — calls immediately)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{memoize}}{memoize}}\label{memoize}

\texttt{memoize} (alias \texttt{memo}) wraps a function so that repeated
calls with the same arguments return a cached result instead of
re-executing the function. Arguments are stringified and joined as the
cache key. This is essential for expensive pure functions like recursive
algorithms, API lookups with stable results, or any computation where
the same inputs always produce the same output.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $fib = memoize(fn \{}
\NormalTok{    my $n = \_0}
\NormalTok{    $n \textless{} 2 ? $n : $fib{-}\textgreater{}($n{-}1) + $fib{-}\textgreater{}($n{-}2)}
\NormalTok{\})}
\NormalTok{p $fib{-}\textgreater{}(30)   \# instant (without memoize: \textasciitilde{}1B calls)}

\NormalTok{my $fetch\_user = memo(fn \{ fetch\_json("https://api/users/\#\{\_0\}") \})}
\NormalTok{$fetch\_user{-}\textgreater{}(42)   \# hits API}
\NormalTok{$fetch\_user{-}\textgreater{}(42)   \# returns cached}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{once}}{once}}\label{once}

\texttt{once} wraps a function so it is called at most once. The first
invocation executes the function and caches the result; all subsequent
calls return the cached value without re-executing. This is ideal for
lazy initialization, one-time setup, or singleton patterns.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $init = once(fn \{ p "initializing..."}
\NormalTok{    42 \})}
\NormalTok{p $init{-}\textgreater{}()   \# prints "initializing..." → 42}
\NormalTok{p $init{-}\textgreater{}()   \# 42 (no print — cached)}
\NormalTok{p $init{-}\textgreater{}()   \# 42 (still cached)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{constantly}}{constantly}}\label{constantly}

\texttt{constantly} (alias \texttt{const}) returns a function that
ignores all arguments and always returns the given value. Useful as a
default callback, a stub in higher-order function pipelines, or anywhere
a function is required but a fixed value suffices.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $zero = constantly(0)}
\NormalTok{p $zero{-}\textgreater{}("anything")   \# 0}
\NormalTok{my @defaults = map \{ constantly(0){-}\textgreater{}() \} 1:5   \# [0,0,0,0,0]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{complement}}{complement}}\label{complement}

\texttt{complement} (alias \texttt{compl}) wraps a predicate function
and returns a new function that negates its boolean result.
\texttt{complement(\textbackslash{}\&even?)-\textgreater{}(3)} returns
true. This is the functional equivalent of \texttt{!f(x)} without
creating a closure.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $even = fn \{ \_0 \% 2 == 0 \}}
\NormalTok{my $odd = complement($even)}
\NormalTok{p $odd{-}\textgreater{}(3)   \# 1}
\NormalTok{p $odd{-}\textgreater{}(4)   \# 0}
\NormalTok{1:10 |\textgreater{} grep \{ complement($even){-}\textgreater{}(\_) \} |\textgreater{} e p  \# 1 3 5 7 9}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{juxt}}{juxt}}\label{juxt}

\texttt{juxt} (juxtapose) takes multiple functions and returns a new
function that calls each one with the same arguments and collects the
results into an array. This is useful for computing multiple derived
values from the same input in a single pass.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $stats = juxt(fn \{ min @\_ \}, fn \{ max @\_ \}, fn \{ avg @\_ \})}
\NormalTok{my @r = $stats{-}\textgreater{}(3, 1, 4, 1, 5)}
\NormalTok{p "@r"   \# 1 5 2.8}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{fnil}}{fnil}}\label{fnil}

\texttt{fnil} wraps a function so that any \texttt{undef} arguments are
replaced with the given defaults before the function is called. This
eliminates repetitive \texttt{//\ \$default} patterns inside function
bodies.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $greet = fnil(fn \{ "Hello, \#\{\_0\}!" \}, "World")}
\NormalTok{p $greet{-}\textgreater{}(undef)    \# Hello, World!}
\NormalTok{p $greet{-}\textgreater{}("Alice")  \# Hello, Alice!}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{deep\_clone}}{deep\_clone}}\label{deep_clone}

\texttt{deep\_clone} (alias \texttt{dclone}) performs a recursive deep
copy of a nested data structure. Array refs, hash refs, and scalar refs
are cloned recursively so that the result shares no references with the
original. Modifications to the clone never affect the source. This is
the stryke equivalent of JavaScript\textquotesingle s
\texttt{structuredClone} or Perl\textquotesingle s
\texttt{Storable::dclone}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $orig = \{users =\textgreater{} [\{name =\textgreater{} "Alice"\}], meta =\textgreater{} \{v =\textgreater{} 1\}\}}
\NormalTok{my $copy = deep\_clone($orig)}
\NormalTok{$copy{-}\textgreater{}\{users\}[0]\{name\} = "Bob"}
\NormalTok{p $orig{-}\textgreater{}\{users\}[0]\{name\}   \# Alice (unchanged)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{deep\_merge}}{deep\_merge}}\label{deep_merge}

\texttt{deep\_merge} (alias \texttt{dmerge}) recursively merges two hash
references. When both sides have a hash ref for the same key, they are
merged recursively; otherwise the right-hand value wins. This is the
standard deep merge from Lodash, Ruby\textquotesingle s
\texttt{deep\_merge}, and config-file overlay patterns. Returns a new
hash ref --- neither input is modified.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $defaults = \{db =\textgreater{} \{host =\textgreater{} "localhost", port =\textgreater{} 5432\}, debug =\textgreater{} 0\}}
\NormalTok{my $overrides = \{db =\textgreater{} \{port =\textgreater{} 3306\}, debug =\textgreater{} 1\}}
\NormalTok{my $cfg = deep\_merge($defaults, $overrides)}
\NormalTok{p $cfg{-}\textgreater{}\{db\}\{host\}   \# localhost (from defaults)}
\NormalTok{p $cfg{-}\textgreater{}\{db\}\{port\}   \# 3306 (overridden)}
\NormalTok{p $cfg{-}\textgreater{}\{debug\}      \# 1 (overridden)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{deep\_equal}}{deep\_equal}}\label{deep_equal}

\texttt{deep\_equal} (alias \texttt{deq}) performs structural equality
comparison of two values, recursively descending into array refs, hash
refs, and scalar refs. Returns 1 if the structures are identical, 0
otherwise. This is the stryke equivalent of Node\textquotesingle s
\texttt{assert.deepStrictEqual}, Lodash \texttt{isEqual}, or
Python\textquotesingle s \texttt{==} on nested dicts/lists.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p deep\_equal([1, \{a =\textgreater{} 2\}], [1, \{a =\textgreater{} 2\}])   \# 1}
\NormalTok{p deep\_equal([1, \{a =\textgreater{} 2\}], [1, \{a =\textgreater{} 3\}])   \# 0}
\NormalTok{p deq(\{x =\textgreater{} [1,2]\}, \{x =\textgreater{} [1,2]\})            \# 1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-operators}
\chapter{Operators}\label{operators}

66 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{-\textgreater{}}}{-\textgreater{}}}\label{section-3}

\textbf{Arrow operator} --- method invocation on objects/classes and
dereference of refs. The single most-used operator in Perl OOP and any
code touching references.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# Method call (object or class):}
\NormalTok{my $p = Point{-}\textgreater{}new(x =\textgreater{} 1, y =\textgreater{} 2)}
\NormalTok{p $p{-}\textgreater{}x                                    \# 1}
\NormalTok{p Math::Utils{-}\textgreater{}compute(@args)}

\NormalTok{\# Hash{-}ref dereference:}
\NormalTok{my $cfg = \{ host =\textgreater{} "localhost", port =\textgreater{} 5432 \}}
\NormalTok{p $cfg{-}\textgreater{}\{host\}}
\NormalTok{$cfg{-}\textgreater{}\{port\} = 6543}

\NormalTok{\# Array{-}ref dereference:}
\NormalTok{my $rows = [[1,2,3], [4,5,6]]}
\NormalTok{p $rows{-}\textgreater{}[1][2]                            \# 6 (chained — implicit {-}\textgreater{})}
\NormalTok{my $val = $rows{-}\textgreater{}[0]{-}\textgreater{}[2]                  \# explicit form: 3}

\NormalTok{\# Code{-}ref invocation:}
\NormalTok{my $f = fn ($x) \{ $x * 2 \}}
\NormalTok{p $f{-}\textgreater{}(21)                                 \# 42}

\NormalTok{\# Postfix{-}deref (modern):}
\NormalTok{my @copy = $rows{-}\textgreater{}@*                       \# all elements}
\NormalTok{my \%env  = \%ENV{-}\textgreater{}\%*                        \# entire hash via slice}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Chained arrow elision} --- between subscripts
(\texttt{{[}..{]}}, \texttt{\{..\}}), \texttt{-\textgreater{}} is
optional: \texttt{\$rows-\textgreater{}{[}1{]}{[}2{]}} ≡
\texttt{\$rows-\textgreater{}{[}1{]}-\textgreater{}{[}2{]}}. The first
arrow after a scalar variable is *required*; intermediate arrows can be
omitted for chained subscripting only.

See also: \texttt{-\textgreater{}\textgreater{}} /
\texttt{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}} (thread-last
macros --- distinct operator), \texttt{bless}, \texttt{class},
\texttt{new}, \texttt{ref}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{new}}{new}}\label{new}

\texttt{new} --- the default constructor automatically provided for
every \texttt{class} and \texttt{struct}. Called as
\texttt{ClassName-\textgreater{}new(field1\ =\textgreater{}\ v1,\ field2\ =\textgreater{}\ v2,\ ...)}.
Returns a blessed object whose attribute storage is initialized from the
named arguments. If the class defines a \texttt{BUILD} method,
\texttt{new} runs it *after* attribute initialization but *before*
returning, so \texttt{BUILD} can validate, derive fields, or attach
external resources. A class never overrides \texttt{new} itself in
stryke --- to customize construction, define \texttt{BUILD} (post-init
hook) instead.

Implementation: \texttt{vm\_helper.rs:10686} --- when
\texttt{-\textgreater{}new} is called and no user-defined \texttt{new}
exists, the runtime dispatches to \texttt{builtin\_new} which allocates
the object, populates fields from the arglist, then calls \texttt{BUILD}
via the C3 MRO if present.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{class Point \{}
\NormalTok{    has \textquotesingle{}x\textquotesingle{} =\textgreater{} (is =\textgreater{} \textquotesingle{}ro\textquotesingle{}, default =\textgreater{} 0)}
\NormalTok{    has \textquotesingle{}y\textquotesingle{} =\textgreater{} (is =\textgreater{} \textquotesingle{}ro\textquotesingle{}, default =\textgreater{} 0)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{my $p = Point{-}\textgreater{}new(x =\textgreater{} 1.5, y =\textgreater{} 2.0)}
\NormalTok{p $p{-}\textgreater{}x          \# 1.5}

\NormalTok{class Connection \{}
\NormalTok{    has \textquotesingle{}host\textquotesingle{} =\textgreater{} (is =\textgreater{} \textquotesingle{}ro\textquotesingle{}, required =\textgreater{} 1)}
\NormalTok{    has \textquotesingle{}port\textquotesingle{} =\textgreater{} (is =\textgreater{} \textquotesingle{}ro\textquotesingle{}, default =\textgreater{} 5432)}
\NormalTok{    fn BUILD($self, $args) \{}
\NormalTok{        die "host required" unless $self{-}\textgreater{}host}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{my $c = Connection{-}\textgreater{}new(host =\textgreater{} "db.example.com")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{BUILD} (post-init hook), \texttt{DESTROY}
(destructor), \texttt{class}, \texttt{struct}, \texttt{bless},
\texttt{@ISA}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$}}{\$}}\label{section-4}

\textbf{Scalar sigil.} \texttt{\$name} accesses a scalar variable.
\texttt{\$ref} holds references. \texttt{\$h\{key\}} accesses one hash
value; \texttt{\$arr{[}idx{]}} accesses one array element (sigil
reflects the *value being accessed*, not the container). For
postfix-deref: \texttt{\$ref-\textgreater{}\$*} (in stryke contexts that
accept it).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $x = 42                    \# scalar declaration}
\NormalTok{my @arr = (1, 2, 3)}
\NormalTok{p $arr[0]                     \# 1 — scalar context on @arr}
\NormalTok{my \%h = (k =\textgreater{} "v")}
\NormalTok{p $h\{k\}                       \# one value}
\NormalTok{my $ref = \textbackslash{}@arr               \# reference, scalar holds it}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{@} (array sigil), \texttt{\%} (hash sigil),
\texttt{\textbackslash{}} (reference), \texttt{-\textgreater{}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{@}}{@}}\label{section-5}

\textbf{Array sigil.} \texttt{@name} accesses an entire array.
\texttt{@h\{@keys\}} is a hash slice (multiple values by key list).
\texttt{@a{[}@idx{]}} is an array slice. \texttt{@\_} is the implicit
sub argument array. Stringified in double quotes by joining elements
with \texttt{\$"} (space by default).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @nums = (1, 2, 3)}
\NormalTok{p scalar @nums            \# 3 — length in scalar context}
\NormalTok{p "@nums"                  \# 1 2 3 — joined with $"}
\NormalTok{my @slice = @arr[1..3]    \# array slice}
\NormalTok{my @vals  = @h\{qw(a b c)\}  \# hash slice}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\$} (scalar sigil), \texttt{\%} (hash sigil),
\texttt{@\_}, \texttt{\$"}, postfix-deref \texttt{-\textgreater{}@*}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%}}{\%}}\label{section-6}

\textbf{Hash sigil / modulo operator} --- same character, two meanings
disambiguated by context.

\textbf{As sigil} (preceding a name): \texttt{\%h} accesses the whole
hash. \texttt{\%ENV} is the env hash,
\texttt{\%h\ =\ (k1\ =\textgreater{}\ v1,\ k2\ =\textgreater{}\ v2)}
declares one, \texttt{keys\ \%h} / \texttt{values\ \%h} /
\texttt{each\ \%h} iterate. The sigil reflects the *whole hash*; one
value is \texttt{\$h\{k\}} (scalar).

\textbf{As operator} (between expressions): \texttt{\$x\ \%\ \$y} is
integer modulo (remainder); result takes the sign of the right operand.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%cfg = (host =\textgreater{} "localhost", port =\textgreater{} 5432)}
\NormalTok{p keys \%cfg               \# hash iteration}
\NormalTok{p 10 \% 3                  \# 1 — modulo}
\NormalTok{p {-}7 \% 3                  \# 2 — Perl semantics: sign matches RHS}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\$} (scalar), \texttt{@} (array), \texttt{\%=},
\texttt{\%ENV}, \texttt{\%SIG}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{+}}{+}}\label{section-7}

\textbf{Addition / numeric coercion.} \texttt{\$x\ +\ \$y} adds two
numbers. Operands are coerced to numeric context --- strings parse as
\texttt{\textbackslash{}d+(\textbackslash{}.\textbackslash{}d+)?} until
a non-numeric character, so \texttt{"5\ apples"\ +\ 3\ ==\ 8}. Use
\texttt{.} (dot) for string concatenation; using \texttt{+} on
intended-strings is a common bug since the strings parse to 0 silently
under \texttt{no\ warnings}. As a unary prefix, \texttt{+EXPR} forces
numeric context without changing value.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p 2 + 3                \# 5}
\NormalTok{p "10x" + 5            \# 15 — leading digits parse}
\NormalTok{p "x10" + 5            \# 5  — leading non{-}digit → 0}
\NormalTok{my $sum = sum(1:100)   \# 5050}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{-}, \texttt{*}, \texttt{/}, \texttt{+=} (compound
assign), \texttt{.} (string concat).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{-}}{-}}\label{section-8}

\textbf{Subtraction / unary minus / file-test operator.} Binary:
\texttt{\$x\ -\ \$y} subtracts numbers (same coercion rules as
\texttt{+}). Unary: \texttt{-EXPR} negates. Special: \texttt{-X\ FILE}
where \texttt{X} is a single letter is a file-test operator (\texttt{-e}
exists, \texttt{-f} regular file, \texttt{-d} directory, \texttt{-r}
readable, \texttt{-w} writable, \texttt{-x} executable, \texttt{-s} size
in bytes, \texttt{-M} age in days, etc.).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p 10 {-} 3              \# 7}
\NormalTok{p {-}5 * 2               \# {-}10}
\NormalTok{if ({-}e $path) \{ p "exists" \}}
\NormalTok{if ({-}f $path \&\& {-}r \_) \{ p "readable file" \}   \# \_ reuses last stat}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{+}, \texttt{-=}, file-test reference.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{*}}{*}}\label{section-9}

\textbf{Multiplication / glob deref.} Binary: \texttt{\$x\ *\ \$y}
multiplies numbers. In expression context (\texttt{*name}), references
the typeglob for that name --- rarely used directly; assigning
\texttt{*alias\ =\ \textbackslash{}\&func} installs an alias for the
sub.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p 6 * 7              \# 42}
\NormalTok{my $area = $w * $h}
\NormalTok{*shortcut = \textbackslash{}\&long\_function\_name   \# sub alias}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{*=}, \texttt{**} (exponent), \texttt{x} (string/list
repeat --- NOT multiplication).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{/}}{/}}\label{section-10}

\textbf{Division / regex delimiter.} Binary: \texttt{\$x\ /\ \$y}
divides as floats. Stryke uses true division ---
\texttt{5\ /\ 2\ ==\ 2.5}, not 2. Use \texttt{int(\$x\ /\ \$y)} or
\texttt{intdiv(\$x,\ \$y)} for integer division. In expression context,
\texttt{/PATTERN/} opens a regex --- context-dependent: after an
operator or at statement start, \texttt{/} parses as regex; after a
variable or value, it\textquotesingle s division.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p 10 / 4               \# 2.5}
\NormalTok{p int(10 / 4)          \# 2}
\NormalTok{if ($s =\textasciitilde{} /\^{}\textbackslash{}d+$/) \{ p "all digits" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{*}, \texttt{\%} (modulo), \texttt{/=}, regex syntax,
\texttt{intdiv}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{**}}{**}}\label{section-11}

\textbf{Exponentiation.} \texttt{\$x\ **\ \$y} raises \texttt{\$x} to
the \texttt{\$y}-th power. Floating-point throughout ---
\texttt{2\ **\ 10\ ==\ 1024}, \texttt{2\ **\ 0.5\ ==\ 1.414...}.
Right-associative: \texttt{2\ **\ 3\ **\ 2\ ==\ 2\ **\ 9\ ==\ 512}.
Negative bases with fractional exponents return NaN.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p 2 ** 10              \# 1024}
\NormalTok{p 2 ** 0.5             \# 1.4142135623731}
\NormalTok{p 2 ** 3 ** 2          \# 512 (right{-}assoc)}
\NormalTok{my $sq = $r ** 2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{**=}, \texttt{sqrt}, \texttt{exp}, \texttt{log}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{++}}{++}}\label{section-12}

\textbf{Increment.} Postfix \texttt{\$x++} returns the value and *then*
increments; prefix \texttt{++\$x} increments first and returns the new
value. Works on integers, floats, and \textbf{magic strings}
(\texttt{"aa"++\ ==\ "ab"}, \texttt{"az"++\ ==\ "ba"},
\texttt{"Az"++\ ==\ "Ba"}). String increment follows
Perl\textquotesingle s letter-roll rules --- preserves case, rolls
letters with carry into the next position.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $n = 5}
\NormalTok{p $n++                  \# 5  (post: returns old)}
\NormalTok{p $n                    \# 6}
\NormalTok{p ++$n                  \# 7  (pre: returns new)}

\NormalTok{my $s = "Az"}
\NormalTok{$s++; p $s              \# Ba — magic string increment}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{-\/-}, atomic \texttt{++} on \texttt{mysync} shared
scalars (always atomic).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{-\/-}}{-\/-}}\label{section-13}

\textbf{Decrement.} Postfix \texttt{\$x-\/-} returns the value and
*then* decrements; prefix \texttt{-\/-\$x} decrements first.
Numeric-only --- there is no magic-string *decrement* in Perl. On
non-numeric strings, \texttt{-\/-} coerces to 0 first then decrements.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $n = 5}
\NormalTok{p $n{-}{-}                  \# 5}
\NormalTok{p $n                    \# 4}
\NormalTok{p {-}{-}$n                  \# 3}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{++}, atomic \texttt{-\/-} on \texttt{mysync} shared
scalars.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{.}}{.}}\label{section-14}

\textbf{String concatenation.} \texttt{\$x\ .\ \$y} glues two strings
together. Both operands are coerced to string context. For multi-arg
joins prefer \texttt{join\ "",\ @parts} (faster, less typing); for
building large strings prefer interpolation or \texttt{sprintf}. The
\texttt{.} operator does \textbf{not} insert separators ---
\texttt{"hello"\ .\ "world"\ eq\ "helloworld"}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $name = "world"}
\NormalTok{p "hello " . $name . "!"        \# hello world!}
\NormalTok{my $full = $first . " " . $last}
\NormalTok{my $line = sprintf "\%{-}20s \%d", $name, $count   \# often clearer}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{.=} (append assign), \texttt{x} (repeat),
\texttt{join}, \texttt{sprintf}, interpolation.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{x}}{x}}\label{x}

\textbf{Repeat operator.} \texttt{STR\ x\ N} repeats a string N times;
\texttt{(LIST)\ x\ N} repeats a list. The context of the LHS decides:
scalar LHS → string repeat; list LHS (must be in parens) → list repeat.
N is truncated to integer; negative or zero N produces empty.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p "=" x 20                        \# ====================}
\NormalTok{p "ab" x 3                        \# ababab}
\NormalTok{my @z = (0) x 10                  \# ten zeros}
\NormalTok{my @grid = ([0,0,0]) x 5           \# five distinct rows (each is a new arrayref via shallow copy)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{.} (concat), \texttt{x=} (compound), \texttt{repeat}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{==}}{==}}\label{section-15}

\textbf{Numeric equality.} Coerces both operands to numbers and
compares. Use \texttt{eq} for strings --- \texttt{"10"\ ==\ "10.0"} is
true, but \texttt{"10"\ eq\ "10.0"} is false. Float comparison with
\texttt{==} is dangerous due to rounding; for tolerances use
\texttt{abs(\$x\ -\ \$y)\ \textless{}\ \$epsilon}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p 5 == 5.0                \# 1}
\NormalTok{p "10" == 10              \# 1 (numeric coercion)}
\NormalTok{p "foo" == "bar"          \# 1 (both → 0)  — common bug, use eq!}
\NormalTok{if ($n == 0) \{ ... \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{!=}, \texttt{\textless{}=\textgreater{}}, \texttt{eq}
(string equality), \texttt{near\_eq} builtin for float tolerance.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{!=}}{!=}}\label{section-16}

\textbf{Numeric inequality.} Inverse of \texttt{==}. Use \texttt{ne} for
strings.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p 5 != 6              \# 1}
\NormalTok{while ($i != $target) \{ ... \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{==}, \texttt{ne}, \texttt{\textless{}=\textgreater{}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\textless{}}}{\textless{}}}\label{section-17}

\textbf{Numeric less-than.} Coerces to numbers. Use \texttt{lt} for
strings.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p 3 \textless{} 5                \# 1}
\NormalTok{if ($age \textless{} 18) \{ ... \}}
\NormalTok{my @small = grep \{ \_ \textless{} 10 \} @nums}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Stryke supports \textbf{chained comparisons} Raku-style:
\texttt{1\ \textless{}\ \$x\ \textless{}\ 10} ≡
\texttt{(1\ \textless{}\ \$x)\ \&\&\ (\$x\ \textless{}\ 10)}. See
\texttt{chained\_comparison}.

See also: \texttt{\textgreater{}}, \texttt{\textless{}=},
\texttt{\textless{}=\textgreater{}}, \texttt{lt}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\textgreater{}}}{\textgreater{}}}\label{section-18}

\textbf{Numeric greater-than.} Coerces to numbers. Use \texttt{gt} for
strings.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p 7 \textgreater{} 5               \# 1}
\NormalTok{my @big = grep \{ \_ \textgreater{} 100 \} @vals}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Also the redirect operator in \texttt{open}
(\texttt{open\ my\ \$fh,\ \textquotesingle{}\textgreater{}\textquotesingle{},\ \$path})
and the diamond closer (\texttt{\textless{}\$fh\textgreater{}}).

See also: \texttt{\textless{}}, \texttt{\textgreater{}=},
\texttt{\textless{}=\textgreater{}}, \texttt{gt},
\texttt{chained\_comparison}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\textless{}=}}{\textless=}}\label{section-19}

\textbf{Numeric less-than-or-equal.} Use \texttt{le} for strings.
Participates in chained comparisons:
\texttt{0\ \textless{}=\ \$i\ \textless{}\ @arr}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{if (0 \textless{}= $i \textless{} scalar @arr) \{ p $arr[$i] \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\textless{}}, \texttt{\textgreater{}=},
\texttt{\textless{}=\textgreater{}}, \texttt{le},
\texttt{chained\_comparison}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\textgreater{}=}}{\textgreater=}}\label{section-20}

\textbf{Numeric greater-than-or-equal.} Use \texttt{ge} for strings.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{return if $count \textgreater{}= $max}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\textgreater{}}, \texttt{\textless{}=},
\texttt{\textless{}=\textgreater{}}, \texttt{ge},
\texttt{chained\_comparison}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\textless{}=\textgreater{}}}{\textless=\textgreater{}}}\label{section-21}

\textbf{Spaceship operator} --- numeric three-way compare. Returns -1,
0, or 1. The standard \texttt{sort} comparator for numeric sort.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @sorted = sort \{ $a \textless{}=\textgreater{} $b \} @nums        \# ascending}
\NormalTok{my @desc   = sort \{ $b \textless{}=\textgreater{} $a \} @nums         \# descending}
\NormalTok{p 5 \textless{}=\textgreater{} 10                                     \# {-}1}
\NormalTok{p 10 \textless{}=\textgreater{} 10                                    \# 0}
\NormalTok{p 15 \textless{}=\textgreater{} 10                                    \# 1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{cmp} (string version), \texttt{sort}, \texttt{\$a},
\texttt{\$b}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{eq}}{eq}}\label{eq}

\textbf{String equality.} Compares operands as strings.
\texttt{"10"\ eq\ "10.0"} is false. The string-context counterpart of
\texttt{==}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{if ($cmd eq "start") \{ ... \}}
\NormalTok{p "abc" eq "abc"                \# 1}
\NormalTok{p "abc" eq "ABC"                \# "" (case matters)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{ne}, \texttt{cmp}, \texttt{==} (numeric),
\texttt{lc}/\texttt{uc} for case-insensitive compare.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ne}}{ne}}\label{ne}

\textbf{String inequality.} Inverse of \texttt{eq}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{next if $line ne ""}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{eq}, \texttt{cmp}, \texttt{!=} (numeric).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lt}}{lt}}\label{lt}

\textbf{String less-than} --- lexicographic comparison. Use
\texttt{\textless{}} for numeric.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p "apple" lt "banana"           \# 1}
\NormalTok{my @sorted = sort \{ $a lt $b ? {-}1 : 1 \} @words}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{gt}, \texttt{le}, \texttt{cmp}, \texttt{\textless{}}
(numeric), \texttt{chained\_comparison}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{le}}{le}}\label{le}

\textbf{String less-than-or-equal} --- lexicographic. Use
\texttt{\textless{}=} for numeric. Participates in chained comparisons.

See also: \texttt{lt}, \texttt{ge}, \texttt{cmp}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gt}}{gt}}\label{gt}

\textbf{String greater-than} --- lexicographic.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p "banana" gt "apple"           \# 1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{lt}, \texttt{ge}, \texttt{cmp},
\texttt{\textgreater{}} (numeric).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ge}}{ge}}\label{ge}

\textbf{String greater-than-or-equal} --- lexicographic. Use
\texttt{\textgreater{}=} for numeric.

See also: \texttt{gt}, \texttt{le}, \texttt{cmp}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cmp}}{cmp}}\label{cmp}

\textbf{String three-way compare} --- returns -1, 0, or 1. The standard
\texttt{sort} comparator for string sort.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @sorted = sort \{ $a cmp $b \} @words      \# case{-}sensitive}
\NormalTok{my @ci     = sort \{ lc($a) cmp lc($b) \} @words   \# case{-}insensitive}
\NormalTok{p "alice" cmp "bob"            \# {-}1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\textless{}=\textgreater{}} (numeric three-way),
\texttt{eq}, \texttt{lt}, \texttt{gt}, \texttt{sort}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\&\&}}{\&\&}}\label{section-22}

\textbf{Logical AND} (high-precedence). Short-circuit: returns the
\textbf{first falsy operand}, or the \textbf{last operand if all
truthy}. Stryke (like Perl) doesn\textquotesingle t return boolean ---
it returns the actual value, which makes idioms like
\texttt{\$default\ =\ \$val\ \&\&\ transform(\$val)} work.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{if ($x \textgreater{} 0 \&\& $x \textless{} 100) \{ ... \}}
\NormalTok{my $first = $x \&\& $y \&\& $c       \# first truthy or last falsy}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Lower-precedence variant \texttt{and} binds looser than \texttt{=} so
use it for control flow: \texttt{open\ \$fh,\ \$f\ and\ read\_loop()}.
See also: \texttt{\textbar{}\textbar{}}, \texttt{//}, \texttt{and},
\texttt{!}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\textbar{}\textbar{}}}{\textbar\textbar{}}}\label{section-23}

\textbf{Logical OR} (high-precedence). Short-circuit: returns the
\textbf{first truthy operand}, or the \textbf{last operand if all
falsy}. The classic Perl default idiom:
\texttt{my\ \$x\ =\ \$arg\ \textbar{}\textbar{}\ "default"} --- but
watch out: \texttt{0}, \texttt{""}, \texttt{"0"}, and \texttt{undef} are
all falsy, so \texttt{\textbar{}\textbar{}} falls through for legitimate
zero values. Use \texttt{//} (defined-or) when only \texttt{undef}
should trigger the fallback.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $name = $user || "guest"}
\NormalTok{my $val  = $count // 0          \# better: only undef triggers fallback}
\NormalTok{open $fh, $f or die $!           \# \textasciigrave{}or\textasciigrave{} is the low{-}precedence form}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\&\&}, \texttt{//}, \texttt{or}, \texttt{defined}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{//}}{//}}\label{section-24}

\textbf{Defined-or operator.} Returns the LHS if it is defined (even if
\texttt{0} or \texttt{""}), else the RHS. Distinct from
\texttt{\textbar{}\textbar{}} which treats all falsy values as "use the
fallback". Use \texttt{//} whenever you have a numeric default or want
to distinguish absent values from explicit zero.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $count = $opts\{count\} // 10        \# 10 only if missing — 0 is allowed}
\NormalTok{my $tag = $row{-}\textgreater{}\{label\} // "untagged"}
\NormalTok{return $cache\{$key\} //= compute($key)  \# //= : assign only if undef}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\textbar{}\textbar{}}, \texttt{defined}, \texttt{//=}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{!}}{!}}\label{section-25}

\textbf{Logical NOT} (high-precedence). Returns 1 (true) if the operand
is falsy, \texttt{""} (false) if truthy. Lower-precedence variant
\texttt{not} reads English-y; prefer \texttt{!} in expressions,
\texttt{not} in conditions.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{if (!$ok) \{ die "failed" \}}
\NormalTok{return unless not @items   \# avoid double{-}negative — prefer \textasciigrave{}unless @items\textasciigrave{}}
\NormalTok{my $inverse = !!$truthy    \# !! coerces to canonical 1 / ""}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{not}, \texttt{unless}, \texttt{\&\&},
\texttt{\textbar{}\textbar{}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{and}}{and}}\label{and}

\textbf{Low-precedence logical AND.} Same short-circuit behaviour as
\texttt{\&\&} but binds looser than \texttt{=}, \texttt{,}, and most
other operators. Use for control-flow expressions where
you\textquotesingle d otherwise need parens:
\texttt{\$ok\ =\ read\_file(\$path)\ and\ parse(...)} --- but note this
assigns to \texttt{\$ok} ONLY the return of \texttt{read\_file} (because
\texttt{=} binds tighter than \texttt{and}). For value-binding use
\texttt{\&\&}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{open $fh, \textquotesingle{}\textless{}\textquotesingle{}, $f and read\_loop($fh)   \# control flow}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\&\&}, \texttt{or}, \texttt{not}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{or}}{or}}\label{or}

\textbf{Low-precedence logical OR.} Same short-circuit behaviour as
\texttt{\textbar{}\textbar{}} but binds looser than \texttt{=}. The
canonical idiom for \texttt{open\ ...\ or\ die}:
\texttt{open\ my\ \$fh,\ \$path\ or\ die\ "open:\ \$!"}. The whole
\texttt{open} expression is evaluated, then \texttt{or\ die} runs only
if open returned false.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{open my $fh, \textquotesingle{}\textless{}\textquotesingle{}, $f or die "open: $!"}
\NormalTok{my $val = read\_line() or last           \# WRONG: assigns the read, not the fallback}
\NormalTok{my $val = read\_line() || last           \# WRONG too — falsy values fall through}
\NormalTok{\# Use defined{-}or for value defaults:}
\NormalTok{my $val = read\_line() // last}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\textbar{}\textbar{}}, \texttt{//}, \texttt{and}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{not}}{not}}\label{not}

Low-precedence logical negation --- returns true if the expression is
false, and false if it is true. Functionally identical to \texttt{!} but
binds looser than almost everything, so \texttt{not\ \$x\ ==\ \$y} is
\texttt{not(\$x\ ==\ \$y)} rather than \texttt{(!\$x)\ ==\ \$y}. Useful
for readable boolean conditions.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{if (not defined $val) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "val is undef"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{my @missing = grep \{ not {-}e \_ \} @files}
\NormalTok{@missing |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{p not 0    \# 1}
\NormalTok{p not 1    \# (empty string)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{xor}}{xor}}\label{xor}

\textbf{Low-precedence logical XOR.} Returns true exactly when one
operand is truthy and the other is falsy. No short-circuit (both sides
always evaluated). Rarely used; if you find yourself wanting
\texttt{xor} consider \texttt{(\$x\ ?\ 1\ :\ 0)\ !=\ (\$y\ ?\ 1\ :\ 0)}
for clarity.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{if ($admin xor $guest) \{ p "exactly one role" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\&\&}, \texttt{\textbar{}\textbar{}}, \texttt{\^{}}
(bitwise xor).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{=\textasciitilde{}}}{=\textasciitilde{}}}\label{section-26}

\textbf{Regex bind} --- applies the right-side
regex/transliteration/substitution to the left-side scalar. Returns
success/match data in scalar context, captured groups in list context.
Without \texttt{=\textasciitilde{}}, regex ops default to operating on
\texttt{\$\_}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{if ($line =\textasciitilde{} /\^{}\textbackslash{}s*\#/) \{ ... \}                    \# match}
\NormalTok{my ($host) = $url =\textasciitilde{} m\{\^{}https?://([\^{}/]+)\}        \# extract}
\NormalTok{$text =\textasciitilde{} s/foo/bar/g                              \# substitute}
\NormalTok{$str =\textasciitilde{} tr/a{-}z/A{-}Z/                                \# transliterate}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{!\textasciitilde{}}, \texttt{m//}, \texttt{s///},
\texttt{tr///}, named captures
(\texttt{(?\textless{}name\textgreater{}...)}, \texttt{\%+}).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{!\textasciitilde{}}}{!\textasciitilde{}}}\label{section-27}

\textbf{Negated regex bind.} True if the regex does NOT match.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{next if $line !\textasciitilde{} /\^{}\textbackslash{}s*\textbackslash{}w/      \# skip lines that don\textquotesingle{}t start with a word}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{=\textasciitilde{}}, \texttt{m//}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{..}}{..}}\label{section-28}

\textbf{Range operator.} In list context, generates an inclusive integer
or magic-string sequence: \texttt{1..5\ ==\ (1,2,3,4,5)}. In
scalar/boolean context (rare --- only in \texttt{while}/\texttt{if}),
acts as a flip-flop state machine.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{for (1..10) \{ p \_ \}}
\NormalTok{my @letters = (\textquotesingle{}a\textquotesingle{}..\textquotesingle{}z\textquotesingle{})        \# magic string range}
\NormalTok{my @rev = reverse 1..100}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Stryke convention: prefer \texttt{1:5} (colon-range, fully equivalent)
since it doesn\textquotesingle t visually clash with method/property
access chains.

See also: \texttt{...} (three-dot range), \texttt{:} colon range,
flip-flop in \texttt{while}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{...}}{...}}\label{section-29}

\textbf{Exclusive range / yada-yada.} As an operator: \texttt{1...5} is
the *exclusive* range (does not include the endpoint in some contexts)
and the *flip-flop* operator with slightly different reset semantics
than \texttt{..}. As a statement: \texttt{...} alone (the "yada-yada")
is a placeholder that compiles fine but dies at runtime --- useful for
sketching APIs.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fn not\_yet\_implemented \{ ... \}       \# yada{-}yada — dies if called}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{..}, yada-yada placeholder.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{=\textgreater{}}}{=\textgreater{}}}\label{section-30}

\textbf{Fat comma} --- variant of \texttt{,} that auto-quotes the left
operand if it looks like a bareword identifier. The canonical syntax for
hash entries and named arguments since it self-documents which side is
the key.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%cfg = (}
\NormalTok{    host   =\textgreater{} "localhost",     \# "host" auto{-}quoted}
\NormalTok{    port   =\textgreater{} 5432,}
\NormalTok{    debug  =\textgreater{} 1,}
\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{open my $fh, \textquotesingle{}\textless{}\textquotesingle{}, $path or die}
\NormalTok{Class{-}\textgreater{}new(name =\textgreater{} "alice", age =\textgreater{} 30)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

With strict syntax, \texttt{,} and \texttt{=\textgreater{}} are
semantically equivalent EXCEPT for the auto-quoting.
\texttt{1\ =\textgreater{}\ 2} is the same as \texttt{1\ ,\ 2}; the
magic only fires on bareword LHS.

See also: \texttt{,}, hash declaration, named parameters.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{,}}{,}}\label{section-31}

\textbf{Comma operator / list separator.} In list context, separates
list elements. In scalar context (right-to-left), evaluates each
expression for side effects and returns the rightmost (like C). The
fat-comma \texttt{=\textgreater{}} is a variant that auto-quotes
bareword LHS.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @x = (1, 2, 3)}
\NormalTok{my \%h = (a =\textgreater{} 1, b =\textgreater{} 2)}
\NormalTok{for (my $i = 0, my $j = 10; $i \textless{} 5; $i++, $j{-}{-}) \{ ... \}   \# scalar context}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{=\textgreater{}}, list operators (\texttt{push},
\texttt{unshift}, \ldots).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{?:}}{?:}}\label{section-32}

\textbf{Ternary conditional} ---
\texttt{COND\ ?\ IF\_TRUE\ :\ IF\_FALSE}. Right-associative for
chaining. Returns one of the two branches as an expression value, unlike
\texttt{if}/\texttt{else} which is a statement. Stryke also accepts the
\texttt{if}/\texttt{else} block form as an expression:
\texttt{my\ \$x\ =\ do\ \{\ if\ (cond)\ \{\ 1\ \}\ else\ \{\ 2\ \}\ \}}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $sign = $n \textgreater{} 0 ? "+" : $n \textless{} 0 ? "{-}" : "0"}
\NormalTok{p $count == 1 ? "$count item" : "$count items"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Binds looser than most operators; use parens around complex conditions
for readability. See also: \texttt{if}/\texttt{else}, \texttt{match}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\textbackslash{}}}{\textbackslash{}}}\label{section-33}

\textbf{Reference operator} --- takes a reference to a variable, sub, or
value. \texttt{\textbackslash{}\$scalar},
\texttt{\textbackslash{}@array}, \texttt{\textbackslash{}\%hash},
\texttt{\textbackslash{}\&sub}, \texttt{\textbackslash{}(...)}.
References are scalars; deref with \texttt{\$\$ref} / \texttt{@\$ref} /
\texttt{\%\$ref} / \texttt{\&\$ref} /
\texttt{\$ref-\textgreater{}{[}i{]}} /
\texttt{\$ref-\textgreater{}\{k\}} / \texttt{\$ref-\textgreater{}(...)}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @nums = (1, 2, 3)}
\NormalTok{my $aref = \textbackslash{}@nums}
\NormalTok{p $$aref[0]                  \# 1 (sigil{-}deref)}
\NormalTok{p $aref{-}\textgreater{}[0]                  \# 1 (arrow{-}deref — preferred)}
\NormalTok{p join ",", @$aref            \# 1,2,3}

\NormalTok{my $code = \textbackslash{}\&my\_function}
\NormalTok{$code{-}\textgreater{}(42)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Also \texttt{\textbackslash{}(LIST)} returns a list of references ---
\texttt{\textbackslash{}(@a,\ @b)} gives one ref per element, not a
single ref to the list.

See also: \texttt{-\textgreater{}}, \texttt{ref}, deref syntax,
\texttt{bless}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\&}}{\&}}\label{section-34}

\textbf{Bitwise AND} (and prototype/sub-deref). On numbers,
\texttt{\$x\ \&\ \$y} computes the bitwise AND. As a sigil prefix
\texttt{\&name} calls a sub (in non-bareword contexts) or takes a
coderef. With \texttt{\textbackslash{}} makes a coderef:
\texttt{\textbackslash{}\&sub}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p 0b1100 \& 0b1010            \# 8 (0b1000)}
\NormalTok{my $mask = $flags \& 0xFF}
\NormalTok{my $code = \textbackslash{}\&my\_sub          \# coderef}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\textbar{}}, \texttt{\^{}},
\texttt{\textasciitilde{}}, \texttt{\&\&}, \texttt{\textbackslash{}\&}
(coderef).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\textbar{}}}{\textbar{}}}\label{section-35}

\textbf{Bitwise OR.} \texttt{\$x\ \textbar{}\ \$y} ORs the bits. Also a
regex alternation inside \texttt{/.../}. As a stream operator
\texttt{\textbar{}\textbar{}} is logical OR,
\texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} is pipe-forward.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p 0b1100 | 0b1010           \# 14 (0b1110)}
\NormalTok{my $flags = OPEN\_READ | OPEN\_NONBLOCK}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\&}, \texttt{\^{}}, \texttt{\textasciitilde{}},
\texttt{\textbar{}\textbar{}}, \texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}}, regex
alternation.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\^{}}}{\^{}}}\label{section-36}

\textbf{Bitwise XOR.} \texttt{\$x\ \^{}\ \$y} XORs the bits.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p 0b1100 \^{} 0b1010           \# 6 (0b0110)}
\NormalTok{my $toggled = $bits \^{} $mask}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\&}, \texttt{\textbar{}}, \texttt{\textasciitilde{}},
\texttt{xor} (logical).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\textasciitilde{}}}{\textasciitilde{}}}\label{section-37}

\textbf{Bitwise NOT} (unary). Flips every bit; works on integers. Also
the prefix of stryke thread macros
(\texttt{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{}},
\texttt{\textasciitilde{}p\textgreater{}},
\texttt{\textasciitilde{}d\textgreater{}}, etc.) --- those are distinct
multi-char operators, not \texttt{\textasciitilde{}} followed by
something.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p \textasciitilde{}0                        \# {-}1 (all bits set on signed int)}
\NormalTok{p \textasciitilde{}0xFF                     \# …higher bits set}
\NormalTok{my $inverted = \textasciitilde{}$mask \& 0xFFFF}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\&}, \texttt{\textbar{}}, \texttt{\^{}}, \texttt{!}
(logical NOT), \texttt{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{}} /
\texttt{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}} /
\texttt{\textasciitilde{}p\textgreater{}} (distinct ops).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\textless{}\textless{}}}{\textless\textless{}}}\label{section-38}

\textbf{Left bit-shift / heredoc.} As
\texttt{\$x\ \textless{}\textless{}\ \$y}, shifts bits left by
\texttt{\$y} positions. As \texttt{\textless{}\textless{}TAG}, opens a
heredoc (multi-line string literal terminated by \texttt{TAG} at start
of line).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p 1 \textless{}\textless{} 10                    \# 1024}
\NormalTok{my $kb = $bytes \textless{}\textless{} 10}
\NormalTok{my $sql = \textless{}\textless{}END}
\NormalTok{    SELECT *}
\NormalTok{    FROM users}
\NormalTok{END}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\textgreater{}\textgreater{}},
\texttt{\textless{}\textless{}=}, heredoc syntax.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\textgreater{}\textgreater{}}}{\textgreater\textgreater{}}}\label{section-39}

\textbf{Right bit-shift / file redirect.} As
\texttt{\$x\ \textgreater{}\textgreater{}\ \$y}, shifts bits right by
\texttt{\$y} positions. As a mode in \texttt{open},
\texttt{\textgreater{}\textgreater{}} opens for append.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p 1024 \textgreater{}\textgreater{} 2                  \# 256}
\NormalTok{my $exit\_code = $? \textgreater{}\textgreater{} 8       \# extract exit code from $?}
\NormalTok{open my $fh, \textquotesingle{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textquotesingle{}, \textquotesingle{}log.txt\textquotesingle{} or die}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\textless{}\textless{}},
\texttt{\textgreater{}\textgreater{}=}, \texttt{open} modes.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{=}}{=}}\label{section-40}

\textbf{Assignment.} Returns the assigned value (so chained
\texttt{\$x\ =\ \$y\ =\ 5} works). Context matters:
\texttt{my\ @a\ =\ \$scalar} copies the scalar\textquotesingle s value
into a one-element list; \texttt{my\ @a\ =\ @b} does list copy;
\texttt{my\ \$s\ =\ @a} gives the array\textquotesingle s length in
scalar context. The single most-used operator after \texttt{;}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $x = 42}
\NormalTok{my ($x, $y, $c) = (1, 2, 3)        \# list{-}destructure}
\NormalTok{my ($first, @rest) = split /,/}
\NormalTok{($x, $y) = ($y, $x)                  \# swap}
