\NormalTok{printf STDERR "error \%d: \%s\textbackslash{}n", $code, $msg}
\NormalTok{printf "\%08.2f\textbackslash{}n", 3.14159   \# 00003.14}
\NormalTok{printf "\%s has \%d item\%s\textbackslash{}n", $user, $n, $n == 1 ? "" : "s"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sprintf}}{sprintf}}\label{sprintf}

Return a formatted string without printing it, using the same C-style
format specifiers as \texttt{printf}. This is the go-to function for
building formatted strings for later use --- constructing log messages,
building padded table columns, converting numbers to hex or binary
representations, or assembling strings that will be passed to other
functions. In stryke, \texttt{sprintf} is often combined with the pipe
operator:
\texttt{\$val\ \textbar{}\textgreater{}\ t\ \{\ sprintf\ "0x\%04x",\ \_\ \}\ \textbar{}\textgreater{}\ p}.
The return value is always a string. All format specifiers from
\texttt{printf} apply here.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $hex = sprintf "0x\%04x", 255}
\NormalTok{my $padded = sprintf "\%08d", $id}
\NormalTok{my $msg = sprintf "\%{-}20s: \%s", $key, $value}
\NormalTok{my @rows = map \{ sprintf "\%3d. \%s", \_, $names[\_] \} 0..$ \#names}
\NormalTok{@rows |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{slurp}}{slurp}}\label{slurp}

Read an entire file into memory as a \textbf{byte string} (Perl-default
semantics --- strings are bytes), or --- when the path is a glob pattern
--- concatenate the bytes of every matching regular file. Aliases:
\texttt{sl}, \texttt{cat}, \texttt{c}. Dies if the file is missing or
unreadable.

\textbf{Binary-safe.} The result is a byte buffer that stringifies via
UTF-8-or-Latin-1 decoding, so \texttt{eq}/regex/substr still match on
text content, but \texttt{length()} reports the byte count and writing
back via \texttt{spew} round-trips the exact bytes. Use directly on
binary files (images, archives, etc.) --- no separate
\texttt{read\_bytes}/\texttt{slurp\_raw} call needed.
\texttt{read\_bytes}/\texttt{slurp\_raw} remain available as
explicit-intent aliases.

When the path contains glob metacharacters (\texttt{*} \texttt{?}
\texttt{{[}} \texttt{**}) or a trailing zsh qualifier suffix
\texttt{(...)}, it is expanded by the zshrs glob engine and the results
are concatenated. \textbf{\texttt{slurp} hard-fails if the qualifier
yields any non-regular file} --- \texttt{slurp\ "**(/)"} errors
\texttt{slurp:\ not\ a\ regular\ file:\ ./sub} because slurping a
directory is meaningless. To list directory paths, use
\texttt{glob\ "**(/)"}. See \texttt{glob} for the full qualifier
reference.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $text = slurp("config.yaml")}
\NormalTok{my $png = slurp("logo.png")         \# binary{-}safe; bytes preserved}
\NormalTok{spew("copy.png", $png)              \# round{-}trips exactly}
\NormalTok{my $all = c("**(.)")                \# every regular file recursively, concatenated bytes}
\NormalTok{my $logs = c("logs/*.log")          \# all logs in one go}
\NormalTok{my $json = sl("data.json")}
\NormalTok{my $data = decode\_json($json)}
\NormalTok{"README.md" |\textgreater{} sl |\textgreater{} length |\textgreater{} p    \# byte count (Perl{-}default)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Per-file hash sibling:} see \texttt{swallow}/\texttt{swa} ---
same glob engine but returns
\texttt{\{\ canonical\_abspath\ =\textgreater{}\ bytes\ \}} instead of
concatenating.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{slurp\_raw}}{slurp\_raw}}\label{slurp_raw}

Read an entire file into memory as raw bytes without any encoding
interpretation. Unlike \texttt{slurp}, which returns a decoded UTF-8
string, \texttt{read\_bytes} preserves the exact byte content --- useful
for binary files like images, compressed archives, or protocol buffers.
The alias \texttt{slurp\_raw} emphasizes the raw nature.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $png = read\_bytes("logo.png")}
\NormalTok{p length($png)                        \# byte count}
\NormalTok{my $gz = slurp\_raw("data.gz")}
\NormalTok{my $text = gunzip($gz)}
\NormalTok{p $text}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{swallow}}{swallow}}\label{swallow}

\texttt{swallow\ PATTERN} --- expand a zsh-style glob and return a hash
\texttt{\{\ canonical\_abspath\ =\textgreater{}\ raw\_bytes\ \}}.
Aliases: \texttt{swa}. The per-file body never decodes, so text and
binary files round-trip uniformly --- wrap with
\texttt{decode\_utf8(...)} when you want a string.

\textbf{Symmetry table:}

\textbar{} \textbar{} concat / single value \textbar{} per-file hash
\textbar{} streaming iterator \textbar{}
\textbar-\/-\/-\textbar-\/-\/-\textbar-\/-\/-\textbar-\/-\/-\textbar{}
\textbar{} text (UTF-8) \textbar{} \texttt{slurp} / \texttt{sl} /
\texttt{cat} / \texttt{c} \textbar{} --- \textbar{} --- \textbar{}
\textbar{} bytes (raw) \textbar{} \texttt{read\_bytes} /
\texttt{slurp\_raw} / \texttt{rb} \textbar{} \textbf{\texttt{swallow} /
\texttt{swa}} \textbar{} \textbf{\texttt{ingest} / \texttt{ing}}
\textbar{}

Uses the \textbf{same} zshrs glob engine as \texttt{glob} and
\texttt{slurp}, so every qualifier (\texttt{(N)}, \texttt{(.)},
\texttt{(/)}, \texttt{(L+1024)}, \texttt{(om{[}1{]})}, \texttt{(f644)},
\ldots) is supported. Hash keys are absolute paths produced by
\texttt{fs::canonicalize}, which flattens symlinks to their real targets
--- a symlink farm collapses to the underlying files.

\textbf{Hard-fails} on a non-regular-file match the same way
\texttt{slurp} does (\texttt{swallow:\ not\ a\ regular\ file:\ ./sub}).
Opt into silence with the \texttt{(N)} null-glob qualifier
(\texttt{swallow\ "missing*(N)"} returns an empty hash).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%src = swallow("src/**/*.rs")               \# every Rust file, raw bytes}
\NormalTok{swallow("**(.)") |\textgreater{} keys |\textgreater{} p                  \# every regular file\textquotesingle{}s abs path}
\NormalTok{my \%imgs = swa("assets/**/*.\{png,jpg\}")        \# binary files work uniformly}
\NormalTok{for my ($path, $bytes) (swallow "*.log") \{}
\NormalTok{    p "$path: ", length($bytes), " bytes"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{my \%recent = swallow("**(.om[1,5])")           \# 5 newest files (zsh qualifier)}
\NormalTok{my \%safe = swallow("missing*(N)")              \# empty hash, no error}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

For giant trees where every file\textquotesingle s bytes
don\textquotesingle t fit in RAM at once, use \texttt{ingest} --- it
streams \texttt{{[}path,\ bytes{]}} one file at a time instead of
materialising the full hash.

See also: \texttt{ingest} (streaming variant), \texttt{slurp} (concat
text), \texttt{read\_bytes} (single-file bytes), \texttt{glob} (paths
only), \texttt{glob\_par} (parallel paths).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ingest}}{ingest}}\label{ingest}

\texttt{ingest\ PATTERN} --- streaming variant of \texttt{swallow}.
Returns a lazy iterator that yields
\texttt{{[}canonical\_abspath,\ raw\_bytes{]}} one file at a time.
Aliases: \texttt{ing}. Only one file\textquotesingle s bytes are
resident at any moment --- usable on directory trees whose total
contents won\textquotesingle t fit in memory.

\textbf{Pipeline shape}: path discovery + stat + canonicalize run
eagerly up front (so qualifier support is identical to
\texttt{glob}/\texttt{slurp}/\texttt{swallow} and non-regular matches
hard-fail immediately). File content reads are deferred to each
\texttt{next\_item()} call.

\textbf{Iteration models}:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \texttt{for\ my\ (\$path,\ \$bytes)\ (ingest\ "…")\ \{\ …\ \}} ---
  streams (the foreach loop pulls one pair at a time from the iterator,
  never materialising a list).
\item
  \texttt{ingest\ "…"\ \textbar{}\textgreater{}\ e\ \{\ …\ \}} ---
  streams through stryke\textquotesingle s pipe terminals.
\item
  \texttt{my\ \$it\ =\ ingest\ "…";\ while\ (my\ \$pair\ =\ \$it-\textgreater{}next-\textgreater{}{[}0{]})\ \{\ …\ \}}
  --- explicit driving via the standard iterator
  \texttt{-\textgreater{}next} protocol.
\end{itemize}

Hard-fails on non-regular matches up-front
(\texttt{ingest:\ not\ a\ regular\ file:\ ./sub}). Opt into silence with
the \texttt{(N)} null-glob qualifier.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# Hash every file in a tree without ever loading two files\textquotesingle{} bytes at once.}
\NormalTok{use Digest::SHA qw(sha256\_hex);}
\NormalTok{for my ($path, $bytes) (ingest "data/**/*.bin") \{}
\NormalTok{    p "$path  ", sha256\_hex($bytes)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{\# Streaming pipe — counts total bytes without materialising any file twice.}
\NormalTok{my $total = 0;}
\NormalTok{for my ($p, $b) (ingest "logs/**/*.log") \{ $total += length($b) \}}
\NormalTok{p "$total bytes across ", scalar(glob "logs/**/*.log"), " files"}

\NormalTok{\# Explicit driving + early termination.}
\NormalTok{my $it = ingest "src/**/*.rs";}
\NormalTok{while (my $pair = $it{-}\textgreater{}next{-}\textgreater{}[0]) \{}
\NormalTok{    my ($path, $bytes) = @$pair;}
\NormalTok{    last if length($bytes) \textgreater{} 100\_000;}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{swallow} (eager hash variant), \texttt{slurp}
(concat), \texttt{read\_bytes} (single-file).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{burp}}{burp}}\label{burp}

\texttt{burp\ HASH} --- inverse of \texttt{swallow}. Walk a
\texttt{\{\ path\ =\textgreater{}\ content\ \}} hash, write each entry
to disk (parent directories created automatically), and return the
integer count of files written.

Values that are byte buffers (\texttt{StrykeValue::bytes} --- the type
returned by \texttt{swallow}/\texttt{read\_bytes}/\texttt{ingest}) write
raw bytes. Any other scalar stringifies through \texttt{to\_string()}
first (matches \texttt{spew}/\texttt{spurt} conventions). Pass the hash
by reference: \texttt{burp\ \textbackslash{}\%h} or
\texttt{burp\ \{\ ...\ \}} (inline hashref). The bare \texttt{burp\ \%h}
form is not supported --- the parser binds it as a scalar, not a hash.

\textbf{Hard-fails on the first I/O error}, so a half-applied burp
surfaces immediately instead of silently dropping entries. Empty hash
returns \texttt{0} without touching the filesystem.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# Canonical swallow → modify → burp round{-}trip}
\NormalTok{my \%src = swallow "src/**/*.rs";}
\NormalTok{for my $path (keys \%src) \{}
\NormalTok{    $src\{$path\} = uc $src\{$path\};       \# bytes are mutable scalars in stryke}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{my $n = burp \textbackslash{}\%src;}
\NormalTok{p "$n files updated";}

\NormalTok{\# Snapshot{-}restore: copy an entire tree under a new root.}
\NormalTok{my \%tree = swallow "old/**/*";}
\NormalTok{my \%dest = map \{ my $k = $\_; $k =\textasciitilde{} s\{\^{}/abs/old\}\{/abs/new\}; ($k =\textgreater{} $tree\{$\_\}) \} keys \%tree;}
\NormalTok{burp \%dest;                              \# parent dirs in /abs/new/... created on the fly}

\NormalTok{\# Project scaffold from inline literals.}
\NormalTok{burp \{}
\NormalTok{    "./out/README.md"   =\textgreater{} "\# Hello\textbackslash{}n",}
\NormalTok{    "./out/src/main.rs" =\textgreater{} "fn main() \{ println!(\textbackslash{}"hi\textbackslash{}"); \}\textbackslash{}n",}
\NormalTok{\};}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{swallow} (read side), \texttt{spew}/\texttt{spurt}
(single-file write), \texttt{ingest} (streaming read).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{input}}{input}}\label{input}

Slurp all of stdin (or a filehandle) as one string.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $all = input          \# slurp stdin}
\NormalTok{my $fh\_data = input($fh) \# slurp filehandle}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{read\_lines}}{read\_lines}}\label{read_lines}

Read a file and return its contents as a list of lines with trailing
newlines stripped. This is the idiomatic way to slurp a file
line-by-line in stryke without manually opening a filehandle. The short
alias \texttt{rl} keeps one-liners concise. If the file does not exist,
the program dies with an error message.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @lines = rl("data.txt")}
\NormalTok{p scalar @lines               \# line count}
\NormalTok{@lines |\textgreater{} grep /ERROR/ |\textgreater{} e p \# print error lines}
\NormalTok{my $first = (rl "config.ini")[0]$1}

\NormalTok{Note: returns an empty list for an empty file.}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{append\_file}}{append\_file}}\label{append_file}

Append a string to the end of a file, creating it if it does not exist.
This is the safe way to add content without overwriting --- useful for
log files, CSV accumulation, or incremental output. The short alias
\texttt{af} is convenient in pipelines. The file is opened, written, and
closed atomically per call.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{af("log.txt", "started at " . datetime\_utc() . "\textbackslash{}n")}
\NormalTok{1:5 |\textgreater{} e \{ af("nums.txt", "$\_\textbackslash{}n") \}}
\NormalTok{my @data = ("a","b","c")}
\NormalTok{@data |\textgreater{} e \{ af "out.txt", "$\_\textbackslash{}n" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{to\_file}}{to\_file}}\label{to_file}

Write a string to a file, truncating any existing content. Unlike
\texttt{append\_file}, this replaces the file entirely. Returns the
written content so it can be used in a pipeline --- write to disk and
continue processing in one expression. Creates the file if it does not
exist.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $csv = "name,age\textbackslash{}nAlice,30\textbackslash{}nBob,25"}
\NormalTok{$csv |\textgreater{} to\_file("people.csv") |\textgreater{} p}
\NormalTok{to\_file("empty.txt", "")  \# truncate a file}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Note: the return-value-for-piping behavior distinguishes this from a
plain write.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{write}}{write}}\label{write}

Output a formatted record to a filehandle using a \texttt{format}
declaration. \texttt{write} looks up the format associated with the
current (or specified) filehandle, evaluates the
format\textquotesingle s picture lines against the current variables,
and outputs the result. This is Perl\textquotesingle s original
report-generation mechanism, predating modules like \texttt{Text::Table}
and template engines. In stryke, \texttt{write} and \texttt{format} are
supported for backward compatibility but are rarely used in new code ---
\texttt{printf}/\texttt{sprintf} or template strings are more flexible.
The format name defaults to the filehandle name (e.g., format
\texttt{STDOUT} is used by \texttt{write\ STDOUT}).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{format STDOUT =}
\NormalTok{@\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{} @\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{$name,       $score}
\NormalTok{.}

\NormalTok{my ($name, $score) = ("alice", 42)}
\NormalTok{write   \# outputs: alice           42}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{write\_file}}{write\_file}}\label{write_file}

Write a string to a file, creating it if it does not exist or truncating
it if it does. This is the complement of \texttt{slurp} --- together
they form a read/write pair for whole-file operations. The short alias
\texttt{wf} is convenient for one-liners. The file is opened, written,
and closed in a single call.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{spurt("hello.txt", "Hello, world!\textbackslash{}n")}
\NormalTok{wf("nums.txt", join("\textbackslash{}n", 1:10))}
\NormalTok{"generated content" |\textgreater{} wf("out.txt")}
\NormalTok{my $data = slurp("in.txt")}
\NormalTok{wf("copy.txt", $data)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{write\_json}}{write\_json}}\label{write_json}

Serialize a stryke data structure (hash ref or array ref) as
pretty-printed JSON and write it to a file. Creates or overwrites the
target file. The short alias \texttt{wj} pairs with \texttt{rj} for
round-trip JSON workflows. Useful for persisting configuration, caching
API responses, or generating fixture data.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%data = (name =\textgreater{} "Alice", scores =\textgreater{} [98, 87, 95])}
\NormalTok{wj("out.json", \textbackslash{}\%data)}
\NormalTok{my $back = rj("out.json")}
\NormalTok{p $back{-}\textgreater{}\{name\}   \# Alice}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{read\_json}}{read\_json}}\label{read_json}

Read a JSON file from disk and parse it into a stryke data structure
(hash ref or array ref). The short alias \texttt{rj} keeps JSON-config
one-liners terse. Dies if the file does not exist or contains malformed
JSON. This is the complement of \texttt{write\_json}/\texttt{wj}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $cfg = rj("config.json")}
\NormalTok{p $cfg{-}\textgreater{}\{database\}\{host\}}
\NormalTok{my @items = @\{ rj("list.json") \}}
\NormalTok{@items |\textgreater{} e \{ p \_{-}\textgreater{}\{name\} \}$1}

\NormalTok{Note: numeric strings remain strings; use \textasciigrave{}+0\textasciigrave{} to coerce if needed.}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tempfile}}{tempfile}}\label{tempfile}

Create a temporary file in the system temp directory and return its
absolute path as a string. The file is created with a unique name and
exists on disk immediately. Use \texttt{tf} as a short alias for quick
scratch files in one-liners. The caller is responsible for cleanup,
though OS temp-directory reaping will eventually reclaim it.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $tmp = tf()}
\NormalTok{to\_file($tmp, "scratch data\textbackslash{}n")}
\NormalTok{p rl($tmp)           \# scratch data}
\NormalTok{my @all = map \{ tf() \} 1:3  \# three temp files}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tempdir}}{tempdir}}\label{tempdir}

Create a temporary directory in the system temp directory and return its
absolute path. The directory is created with a unique name and is ready
for use immediately. The short alias \texttt{tdr} mirrors \texttt{tf}
for files. Useful for isolating multi-file operations like test
fixtures, build artifacts, or staged output.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $dir = tdr()}
\NormalTok{to\_file("$dir/a.txt", "hello")}
\NormalTok{to\_file("$dir/b.txt", "world")}
\NormalTok{my @files = glob("$dir/*.txt")}
\NormalTok{p scalar @files   \# 2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{binmode}}{binmode}}\label{binmode}

Set the I/O layer on a filehandle, controlling how bytes are translated
during reads and writes. Without a layer argument,
\texttt{binmode\ \$fh} switches the handle to raw binary mode (no CRLF
translation on Windows, no encoding transforms). With a layer,
\texttt{binmode\ \$fh,\ \textquotesingle{}:utf8\textquotesingle{}}
enables UTF-8 decoding,
\texttt{binmode\ \$fh,\ \textquotesingle{}:raw\textquotesingle{}} strips
all layers for pure byte I/O, and
\texttt{binmode\ \$fh,\ \textquotesingle{}:encoding(ISO-8859-1)\textquotesingle{}}
sets a specific encoding. In stryke, encoding layers can also be
specified directly in \texttt{open}:
\texttt{open\ my\ \$fh,\ \textquotesingle{}\textless{}:utf8\textquotesingle{},\ \$path}.
Call \texttt{binmode} before any I/O on the handle --- calling it
mid-stream may produce garbled output. For binary file processing
(images, archives, network protocols), always use \texttt{:raw}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{open my $fh, \textquotesingle{}\textless{}\textquotesingle{}, $path or die $!}
\NormalTok{binmode $fh, \textquotesingle{}:utf8\textquotesingle{}}
\NormalTok{my @lines = \textless{}$fh\textgreater{}}

\NormalTok{open my $bin, \textquotesingle{}\textless{}\textquotesingle{}, $img\_path or die $!}
\NormalTok{binmode $bin, \textquotesingle{}:raw\textquotesingle{}}
\NormalTok{read $bin, my $header, 8}
\NormalTok{p sprintf "magic: \%s", unpack("H*", $header)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{fileno}}{fileno}}\label{fileno}

Return the underlying OS file descriptor number for a filehandle, or
\texttt{undef} if the handle is not connected to a real file descriptor
(e.g., tied handles, in-memory handles opened to scalar refs). File
descriptor numbers are small non-negative integers managed by the OS
kernel: 0 is STDIN, 1 is STDOUT, 2 is STDERR. This function is mainly
useful for interfacing with system calls that require raw fd numbers,
checking whether two handles share the same underlying fd, or passing
descriptors to child processes. In stryke, \texttt{fileno} is rarely
needed in everyday code but is essential for low-level I/O multiplexing
and process management.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $fd = fileno STDOUT}
\NormalTok{p "stdout fd: $fd"   \# 1}

\NormalTok{if (defined fileno $fh) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "handle is backed by fd " . fileno($fh)}
\NormalTok{\} else \{}
\NormalTok{    p "not a real file descriptor"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{flock}}{flock}}\label{flock}

Advisory file locking for coordinating access between processes.
\texttt{flock\ \$fh,\ OPERATION} where OPERATION is \texttt{LOCK\_SH}
(shared/read lock), \texttt{LOCK\_EX} (exclusive/write lock), or
\texttt{LOCK\_UN} (unlock). Add \texttt{LOCK\_NB} for non-blocking mode:
\texttt{LOCK\_EX\ \textbar{}\ LOCK\_NB} returns false immediately if the
lock is held rather than waiting. Import constants from \texttt{Fcntl}.
Advisory locks are cooperative --- they only work if all processes
accessing the file use \texttt{flock}. In stryke, \texttt{flock} is the
standard mechanism for safe concurrent file access in multi-process
scripts, cron jobs, and daemons. Always unlock explicitly or let the
handle close (which releases the lock). Gotcha: \texttt{flock} does not
work on NFS on many systems.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{use Fcntl \textquotesingle{}:flock\textquotesingle{}}
\NormalTok{open my $fh, \textquotesingle{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textquotesingle{}, \textquotesingle{}shared.log\textquotesingle{} or die $!}
\NormalTok{flock $fh, LOCK\_EX or die "lock: $!"}
\NormalTok{print $fh tm "pid $$ wrote this\textbackslash{}n"}
\NormalTok{flock $fh, LOCK\_UN}
\NormalTok{close $fh}

\NormalTok{unless (flock $fh, LOCK\_EX | LOCK\_NB) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "file is locked by another process"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getc}}{getc}}\label{getc}

Read a single character from a filehandle (default \texttt{STDIN}).
Returns \texttt{undef} at EOF. The character returned respects the
handle\textquotesingle s encoding layer --- under \texttt{:utf8},
\texttt{getc} returns a full Unicode character which may be multiple
bytes on disk. This function is useful for interactive single-key input,
parsing binary formats one byte at a time, or implementing
character-level tokenizers. In stryke, \texttt{getc} blocks until a
character is available on the handle. For terminal input, note that most
terminals are line-buffered by default, so \texttt{getc\ STDIN}
won\textquotesingle t return until the user presses Enter unless you put
the terminal into raw mode.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $ch = getc STDIN}
\NormalTok{p "you pressed: $ch"}

\NormalTok{open my $fh, \textquotesingle{}\textless{}:utf8\textquotesingle{}, $path or die $!}
\NormalTok{while (defined(my $c = getc $fh)) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "char: $c (ord " . ord($c) . ")"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{select}}{select}}\label{select}

In its one-argument form, \texttt{select\ HANDLE} sets the default
output handle for \texttt{print}, \texttt{say}, and \texttt{write},
returning the previously selected handle. This is useful for temporarily
redirecting output --- for example, sending diagnostics to STDERR while
a report goes to a file. In its four-argument form,
\texttt{select\ RBITS,\ WBITS,\ EBITS,\ TIMEOUT} performs POSIX
\texttt{select(2)} I/O multiplexing, waiting for one or more file
descriptors to become ready for reading, writing, or to report
exceptions. The four-argument form is low-level and rarely used directly
in stryke --- prefer \texttt{IO::Select} or async I/O patterns for
multiplexing. \texttt{select} with \texttt{\$\textbar{}} is also the
classic way to enable autoflush on a handle.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $old = select STDERR}
\NormalTok{p "this goes to stderr"}
\NormalTok{select $old}

\NormalTok{\# Enable autoflush on STDOUT}
\NormalTok{select STDOUT; $| = 1}
\NormalTok{print "immediately flushed"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{truncate}}{truncate}}\label{truncate}

Truncate a file to a specified byte length. Accepts either a filehandle
or a filename as the first argument, and the desired length as the
second. \texttt{truncate\ \$fh,\ 0} empties the file entirely --- a
common pattern when rewriting a file in place.
\texttt{truncate\ \$fh,\ \$len} discards everything beyond byte
\texttt{\$len}. Returns true on success, false on failure (check
\texttt{\$!} for the error). In stryke, truncate works on any seekable
filehandle. When using truncate to rewrite a file, remember to also
\texttt{seek\ \$fh,\ 0,\ 0} to rewind the write position --- truncating
does not move the file pointer. Gotcha: truncating a file opened
read-only will fail.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{open my $fh, \textquotesingle{}+\textless{}\textquotesingle{}, \textquotesingle{}data.txt\textquotesingle{} or die $!}
\NormalTok{truncate $fh, 0       \# empty the file}
\NormalTok{seek $fh, 0, 0        \# rewind to start}
\NormalTok{print $fh "fresh content\textbackslash{}n"}
\NormalTok{close $fh}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sysopen}}{sysopen}}\label{sysopen}

Low-level open using POSIX flags for precise control over how a file is
opened. The signature is
\texttt{sysopen\ FH,\ FILENAME,\ FLAGS\ {[},\ PERMS{]}}. Flags are
bitwise-OR combinations from \texttt{Fcntl}: \texttt{O\_RDONLY},
\texttt{O\_WRONLY}, \texttt{O\_RDWR}, \texttt{O\_CREAT},
\texttt{O\_EXCL}, \texttt{O\_TRUNC}, \texttt{O\_APPEND},
\texttt{O\_NONBLOCK}, and others. The optional PERMS argument (e.g.,
\texttt{0644}) sets the file mode when \texttt{O\_CREAT} creates a new
file, subject to the process umask. \texttt{sysopen} is the right tool
when you need \texttt{O\_EXCL} for atomic file creation (lock files,
temp files), \texttt{O\_NONBLOCK} for non-blocking I/O, or other flags
that \texttt{open} cannot express. In stryke, prefer three-argument
\texttt{open} for routine file access and reserve \texttt{sysopen} for
cases requiring specific POSIX semantics.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{use Fcntl}
\NormalTok{\# Atomic create — fails if file already exists}
\NormalTok{sysopen my $lock, \textquotesingle{}/tmp/my.lock\textquotesingle{}, O\_WRONLY|O\_CREAT|O\_EXCL, 0644}
\NormalTok{    or die "already running: $!"}
\NormalTok{print $lock $$}

\NormalTok{sysopen my $log, \textquotesingle{}app.log\textquotesingle{}, O\_WRONLY|O\_APPEND|O\_CREAT, 0644}
\NormalTok{    or die "open log: $!"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sysread}}{sysread}}\label{sysread}

Low-level unbuffered read directly from a file descriptor, bypassing
PerlIO buffering layers. The signature is
\texttt{sysread\ FH,\ SCALAR,\ LENGTH\ {[},\ OFFSET{]}}. Returns the
number of bytes read, 0 at EOF, or \texttt{undef} on error. Unlike
\texttt{read}, \texttt{sysread} issues a single \texttt{read(2)} system
call and may return fewer bytes than requested (short read). This is the
right choice for sockets, pipes, and non-blocking I/O where you need
precise control over how many system calls occur and cannot tolerate
buffering. In stryke, never mix \texttt{sysread}/\texttt{syswrite} with
buffered I/O (\texttt{print}, \texttt{read},
\texttt{\textless{}\$fh\textgreater{}}) on the same handle --- the
buffered and unbuffered positions will diverge and produce corrupted
reads.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{open my $fh, \textquotesingle{}\textless{}:raw\textquotesingle{}, $path or die $!}
\NormalTok{my $buf = \textquotesingle{}\textquotesingle{}}
\NormalTok{while (my $n = sysread $fh, $buf, 4096, length($buf)) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "read $n bytes (total: " . length($buf) . ")"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{close $fh}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{syswrite}}{syswrite}}\label{syswrite}

Low-level unbuffered write directly to a file descriptor, bypassing
PerlIO buffering layers. The signature is
\texttt{syswrite\ FH,\ SCALAR\ {[},\ LENGTH\ {[},\ OFFSET{]}{]}}.
Returns the number of bytes actually written, which may be less than
requested on non-blocking handles or when writing to pipes/sockets
(short write). Returns \texttt{undef} on error. Like \texttt{sysread},
this issues a single \texttt{write(2)} system call and must not be mixed
with buffered I/O on the same handle. In stryke, \texttt{syswrite} is
essential for socket programming, IPC, and performance-critical binary
output where you need to avoid double-buffering. Always check the return
value and handle short writes in a loop for robust code.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $data = "hello, world"}
\NormalTok{my $n = syswrite $fh, $data}
\NormalTok{p "wrote $n bytes"}

\NormalTok{\# Robust write loop for sockets}
\NormalTok{my $off = 0}
\NormalTok{while ($off \textless{} length $data) \{}
\NormalTok{    my $written = syswrite $fh, $data, length($data) {-} $off, $off}
\NormalTok{    die "syswrite: $!" unless defined $written}
\NormalTok{    $off += $written}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sysseek}}{sysseek}}\label{sysseek}

Low-level seek on a file descriptor, bypassing PerlIO buffering. The
signature is \texttt{sysseek\ FH,\ POSITION,\ WHENCE} with the same
WHENCE values as \texttt{seek} (0=start, 1=current, 2=end). Returns the
new position as a true value, or \texttt{undef} on failure. Unlike
\texttt{seek}, \texttt{sysseek} does not flush PerlIO buffers --- it
operates directly on the underlying OS file descriptor. Use
\texttt{sysseek} when working with \texttt{sysread}/\texttt{syswrite}
for consistent positioning. In stryke, \texttt{sysseek} with
\texttt{SEEK\_CUR} and position 0 is an idiom for querying the current
fd position without moving:
\texttt{my\ \$pos\ =\ sysseek\ \$fh,\ 0,\ 1}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{sysseek $fh, 0, 0   \# rewind to start}
\NormalTok{sysread $fh, my $buf, 512}

\NormalTok{my $pos = sysseek $fh, 0, 1}
\NormalTok{p "fd position: $pos"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{format}}{format}}\label{format}

Declare a picture-format template for generating fixed-width text
reports. The syntax is \texttt{format\ NAME\ =\ ...\ .} where each line
alternates between picture lines (containing field placeholders) and
argument lines (listing the variables to fill in). Placeholders include
\texttt{@\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}} (left-align),
\texttt{@\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}}
(right-align), \texttt{@\textbar{}\textbar{}\textbar{}\textbar{}}
(center), \texttt{@\#\#\#.\#\#} (numeric with decimal), and \texttt{@*}
(multiline block fill). The format ends with a lone \texttt{.} on its
own line. In stryke, formats are a legacy feature preserved for
compatibility with Perl 5 code --- for new reports, prefer
\texttt{printf}/\texttt{sprintf} for simple alignment or a templating
module for complex layouts. Formats interact with the special variables
\texttt{\$\textasciitilde{}} (current format name), \texttt{\$\^{}}
(header format), and \texttt{\$=} (lines per page).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{format REPORT =}
\NormalTok{@\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{} @\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{} @ \#\#\#.\#\#}
\NormalTok{$item,            $qty,  $price}
\NormalTok{.}

\NormalTok{my ($item, $qty, $price) = ("Widget", 100, 9.99)}
\NormalTok{my $old = select REPORT}
\NormalTok{$\textasciitilde{} = \textquotesingle{}REPORT\textquotesingle{}}
\NormalTok{write REPORT}
\NormalTok{select $old}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{formline}}{formline}}\label{formline}

Format a line of output according to a picture template, appending the
result to the \texttt{\$\^{}A} (format accumulator) variable. This is
the low-level engine behind Perl\textquotesingle s
\texttt{format}/\texttt{write} report-generation system. Template
characters like \texttt{@\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}}
(left-justify), \texttt{@\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}}
(right-justify), and \texttt{@\#\#\#.\#\#} (numeric) control field
placement.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{$\^{}A = ""}
\NormalTok{formline("@\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{} @\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textbackslash{}n", "Name", "Score")}
\NormalTok{formline("@\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{} @\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textbackslash{}n", "Alice", 98)}
\NormalTok{formline("@\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{} @\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textbackslash{}n", "Bob", 85)}
\NormalTok{p $\^{}A}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-strings}
\chapter{Strings}\label{strings}

26 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{chomp}}{chomp}}\label{chomp}

\texttt{chomp\ STRING} --- remove the trailing record separator (usually
\texttt{\textbackslash{}n}) from a string in place and return the number
of characters removed.

\texttt{chomp} is the idiomatic way to strip newlines after reading
input; unlike \texttt{chop}, it only removes the value of \texttt{\$/}
(the input record separator), so it is safe to call on strings that do
not end with a newline --- it simply does nothing. You can also
\texttt{chomp} an entire array to strip every element at once. In
stryke, \texttt{chomp} operates on UTF-8 strings and respects multi-byte
\texttt{\$/} values. Prefer \texttt{chomp} over \texttt{chop} in
virtually all input-processing code; \texttt{chop} is only for when you
truly need to remove an arbitrary trailing character regardless of what
it is.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $line = \textless{}STDIN\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{chomp $line}
\NormalTok{p $line}
\NormalTok{chomp(my @lines = \textless{}$fh\textgreater{})  \# strip all at once}
\NormalTok{p scalar @lines}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{chop}}{chop}}\label{chop}

\texttt{chop\ STRING} --- remove and return the last character of a
string, modifying the string in place.

Unlike \texttt{chomp}, \texttt{chop} unconditionally removes whatever
the final character is --- newline, letter, digit, or even a multi-byte
UTF-8 codepoint in stryke. The return value is the removed character.
This makes \texttt{chop} useful for peeling off known trailing
delimiters or building parsers that consume input
character-by-character, but dangerous for general newline removal
because it will silently eat a real character if the string does not end
with \texttt{\textbackslash{}n}. When called on an array, \texttt{chop}
removes the last character of every element and returns the last one
removed.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $s = "hello!"}
\NormalTok{my $last = chop $s  \# $last = "!", $s = "hello"}
\NormalTok{p $s}
\NormalTok{my @words = ("foo\textbackslash{}n", "bar\textbackslash{}n")}
\NormalTok{chop @words  \# strips trailing newline from each}
\NormalTok{@words |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{length}}{length}}\label{length}

\texttt{length\ STRING} --- return the \textbf{byte} length of a string.
Perl 5 builtin; pairs with \texttt{index} / \texttt{rindex} /
\texttt{substr} (all byte-based) so positions and lengths share a
coordinate system.

\texttt{length\ "hello"} is 5; \texttt{length\ "─"} is 3 (the
box-drawing char takes 3 bytes in UTF-8). For codepoint count, use
\texttt{len} (stryke extension), which mirrors \texttt{{[}i{]}} /
\texttt{{[}a:b{]}} slice semantics.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p length "hello"      \# 5}
\NormalTok{p length "─"          \# 3 (bytes)}
\NormalTok{p len "─"             \# 1 (codepoint)}
\NormalTok{p length "\textbackslash{}x\{1F600\}"  \# 4 (bytes — emoji is 4{-}byte UTF{-}8)}
\NormalTok{p len "\textbackslash{}x\{1F600\}"     \# 1 (codepoint)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{substr}}{substr}}\label{substr}

\texttt{substr\ STRING,\ OFFSET\ {[},\ LENGTH\ {[},\ REPLACEMENT{]}{]}}
--- extract or replace a substring.

\texttt{substr} is stryke\textquotesingle s Swiss-army knife for
positional string manipulation. With two arguments it extracts from
OFFSET to end; with three it extracts LENGTH characters; with four it
replaces that range with REPLACEMENT and returns the original extracted
portion. Negative OFFSET counts from the end of the string
(\texttt{substr\ \$s,\ -3} gives the last three characters). In stryke,
offsets are UTF-8 character positions, making it safe for multi-byte
text. \texttt{substr} as an lvalue
(\texttt{substr(\$s,\ 0,\ 1)\ =\ "X"}) is also supported for in-place
mutation. Prefer \texttt{substr} over regex when you know exact
positions --- it is faster and clearer.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $s = "hello world"}
\NormalTok{p substr $s, 0, 5              \# hello}
\NormalTok{p substr $s, {-}5                \# world}
\NormalTok{substr $s, 6, 5, "stryke"     \# $s = "hello stryke"}
\NormalTok{p $s}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{index}}{index}}\label{index}

\texttt{index\ STRING,\ SUBSTRING\ {[},\ POSITION{]}} --- return the
zero-based \textbf{byte} position of the first occurrence of SUBSTRING
within STRING, or -1 if not found. Pairs with \texttt{substr} and
\texttt{length} (both byte-based) for Perl 5 compat.

The optional POSITION argument lets you start the search at a given
offset, which is essential for scanning forward through a string in a
loop (call \texttt{index} repeatedly, advancing POSITION past each hit).
For multi-byte strings where you want \textbf{codepoint} positions
paired with \texttt{{[}i{]}} / \texttt{{[}a:b{]}} slice operators, use
\texttt{cindex} (stryke extension) instead --- \texttt{index} is
byte-indexed because \texttt{length} and \texttt{substr} are, and
changing it would break Perl 5 binary-protocol code.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $i = index "hello world", "world"  \# 6}
\NormalTok{p $i}
\NormalTok{my $s = "a.b.c.d"}
\NormalTok{my $pos = 0}
\NormalTok{while (($pos = index $s, ".", $pos) != {-}1) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "dot at $pos"}
\NormalTok{    $pos++}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rindex}}{rindex}}\label{rindex}

\texttt{rindex\ STRING,\ SUBSTRING\ {[},\ POSITION{]}} --- return the
zero-based \textbf{byte} position of the last occurrence of SUBSTRING
within STRING, searching backward from POSITION (or the end). Mirror of
\texttt{index}; pairs with \texttt{substr} and \texttt{length} for Perl
5 compat.

\texttt{rindex} searches from right to left, making it ideal for
extracting file extensions, final path components, or the last delimiter
in a string. The optional POSITION argument caps how far right the
search begins --- characters after POSITION are ignored. Returns -1 when
the substring is not found. For codepoint positions paired with
\texttt{{[}{]}} slice operators, use \texttt{crindex}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $path = "foo/bar/baz.tar.gz"}
\NormalTok{my $i = rindex $path, "/"     \# 7}
\NormalTok{p substr $path, $i + 1        \# baz.tar.gz}
\NormalTok{my $ext = rindex $path, "."   \# 15}
\NormalTok{p substr $path, $ext + 1      \# gz}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{split}}{split}}\label{split}

\texttt{split\ /PATTERN/,\ STRING\ {[},\ LIMIT{]}} --- divide STRING
into a list of substrings by splitting on each occurrence of PATTERN.

\texttt{split} is one of the most-used string functions. The PATTERN is
a regex, so you can split on character classes, alternations, or
lookaheads. A LIMIT caps the number of returned fields; the final field
contains the unsplit remainder. The special pattern \texttt{"\ "} (a
single space string, not regex) mimics \texttt{awk}-style splitting: it
strips leading whitespace and splits on runs of whitespace. In stryke,
\texttt{split} integrates with \texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}}
pipelines, accepting piped-in strings for ergonomic one-liners. Trailing
empty fields are removed by default; pass -1 as LIMIT to preserve them.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @parts = split /,/, "a,b,c"}
\NormalTok{"one:two:three" |\textgreater{} split /:/ |\textgreater{} e p   \# one two three}
\NormalTok{my ($user, $domain) = split /@/, $email, 2}
\NormalTok{p "$user at $domain"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{join}}{join}}\label{join}

\texttt{join\ SEPARATOR,\ LIST} --- concatenate all elements of LIST
into a single string, placing SEPARATOR between each pair of adjacent
elements.

\texttt{join} is the inverse of \texttt{split}. It stringifies each
element before joining, so mixing numbers and strings is fine. An empty
separator (\texttt{join\ "",\ @list}) concatenates without gaps. In
stryke, \texttt{join} works naturally with
\texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} pipelines: a range or filtered list
can be piped directly into \texttt{join} with a separator argument. This
is the standard way to build CSV lines, path strings, or human-readable
lists from arrays. \texttt{join} never adds a trailing separator --- if
you need one, append it yourself.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $csv = join ",", @fields}
\NormalTok{1:5 |\textgreater{} join "{-}" |\textgreater{} p   \# 1{-}2{-}3{-}4{-}5}
\NormalTok{my @parts = ("usr", "local", "bin")}
\NormalTok{p join "/", "", @parts   \# /usr/local/bin}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{uc}}{uc}}\label{uc}

\texttt{uc\ STRING} --- return a fully uppercased copy of the string.

\texttt{uc} performs Unicode-aware uppercasing in stryke, correctly
handling multi-byte characters and locale-sensitive transformations. It
returns a new string without modifying the original. Use \texttt{uc} for
normalizing strings before comparison, formatting headers, or
transforming pipeline output. In stryke
\texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} chains, combine with \texttt{maps} or
\texttt{t} for streaming uppercase transforms. For uppercasing only the
first character (e.g., sentence capitalization), use \texttt{ucfirst}
instead.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p uc "hello"  \# HELLO}
\NormalTok{@words |\textgreater{} maps \{ uc \} |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{"whisper" |\textgreater{} t uc |\textgreater{} p   \# WHISPER}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lc}}{lc}}\label{lc}

\texttt{lc\ STRING} --- return a fully lowercased copy of the string.

\texttt{lc} performs Unicode-aware lowercasing in stryke, so
\texttt{lc\ "\textbackslash{}x\{C4\}"} (capital A with diaeresis)
correctly returns the lowercase form. It does not modify the original
string --- it returns a new one. This is the standard way to normalize
strings for case-insensitive comparison:
\texttt{if\ (lc\ \$x\ eq\ lc\ \$y)}. In stryke
\texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} pipelines, wrap \texttt{lc} with
\texttt{t} to apply it as a streaming transform. For lowercasing only
the first character, use \texttt{lcfirst} instead.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p lc "HELLO"               \# hello}
\NormalTok{"SHOUT" |\textgreater{} t lc |\textgreater{} t rev |\textgreater{} p  \# tuohs}
\NormalTok{my @norm = map lc, @words}
\NormalTok{@norm |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ucfirst}}{ucfirst}}\label{ucfirst}

\texttt{ucfirst\ STRING} --- return a copy of the string with only the
first character uppercased, leaving the rest unchanged.

This is the standard way to capitalize the first letter of a word for
display, title-casing, or converting camelCase to PascalCase. In stryke,
\texttt{ucfirst} is Unicode-aware and correctly handles multi-byte
leading characters. It returns a new string and does not modify the
original. For full uppercasing, use \texttt{uc}. A common idiom is
\texttt{ucfirst\ lc\ \$word} to normalize a word to "Title" form.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ucfirst "hello"            \# Hello}
\NormalTok{p ucfirst lc "hELLO"         \# Hello}
\NormalTok{my @titled = map \{ ucfirst lc \} @raw}
\NormalTok{@titled |\textgreater{} join " " |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lcfirst}}{lcfirst}}\label{lcfirst}

\texttt{lcfirst\ STRING} --- return a copy of the string with only the
first character lowercased, leaving the rest unchanged.

This is useful for converting PascalCase identifiers to camelCase, or
for formatting output where only the initial letter matters. In stryke,
\texttt{lcfirst} is Unicode-aware, so it handles accented capitals and
multi-byte first characters correctly. Like \texttt{lc}, it returns a
new string rather than modifying in place. If you need the entire string
lowercased, use \texttt{lc} instead.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p lcfirst "Hello"    \# hello}
\NormalTok{p lcfirst "XMLParser" \# xMLParser}
\NormalTok{my @camel = map lcfirst, @PascalNames}
\NormalTok{@camel |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{chr}}{chr}}\label{chr}

\texttt{chr\ NUMBER} --- return the character represented by the given
ASCII or Unicode code point.

\texttt{chr} is the inverse of \texttt{ord}:
\texttt{chr(ord(\$c))\ eq\ \$c} always holds. It accepts any
non-negative integer and returns a single-character string. In stryke,
values above 127 produce valid UTF-8 characters, so
\texttt{chr\ 0x1F600} gives you a smiley emoji with no special encoding
gymnastics. For string literals, stryke supports all standard escapes:
\texttt{\textbackslash{}x\{hex\}}, \texttt{\textbackslash{}u\{hex\}},
\texttt{\textbackslash{}o\{oct\}}, \texttt{\textbackslash{}NNN} (octal),
\texttt{\textbackslash{}cX} (control),
\texttt{\textbackslash{}N\{U+hex\}},
\texttt{\textbackslash{}N\{UNICODE\ NAME\}}, plus case modifiers
\texttt{\textbackslash{}U..\textbackslash{}E},
\texttt{\textbackslash{}L..\textbackslash{}E},
\texttt{\textbackslash{}u}, \texttt{\textbackslash{}l},
\texttt{\textbackslash{}Q..\textbackslash{}E}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p chr 65       \# A}
\NormalTok{p chr 0x1F600  \# smiley emoji}
\NormalTok{p "\textbackslash{}u\{0301\}"   \# combining acute accent}
\NormalTok{p "\textbackslash{}N\{SNOWMAN\}" \# ☃}
\NormalTok{my @abc = map \{ chr(\_ + 64) \} 1:26}
\NormalTok{@abc |\textgreater{} join "" |\textgreater{} p  \# ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ord}}{ord}}\label{ord}

\texttt{ord\ STRING} --- return the numeric (Unicode code point) value
of the first character of the string.

\texttt{ord} is the inverse of \texttt{chr}:
\texttt{ord(chr(\$n))\ ==\ \$n} always holds. When passed a
multi-character string, only the first character is examined --- the
rest are ignored. In stryke, \texttt{ord} returns the full Unicode code
point, not just 0-255, so \texttt{ord\ "\textbackslash{}u\{1F600\}"}
returns 128512. This is useful for character classification, building
lookup tables, or implementing custom encodings. For ASCII checks,
\texttt{ord(\$c)\ \textgreater{}=\ 32\ \&\&\ ord(\$c)\ \textless{}=\ 126}
tests printability.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ord "A"          \# 65}
\NormalTok{p ord "\textbackslash{}n"         \# 10}
\NormalTok{p ord "\textbackslash{}u\{1F600\}"  \# 128512}
\NormalTok{my @codes = map ord, split //, "hello"}
\NormalTok{@codes |\textgreater{} e p      \# 104 101 108 108 111}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hex}}{hex}}\label{hex}

\texttt{hex\ STRING} --- interpret a hexadecimal string and return its
numeric value.

The leading \texttt{0x} prefix is optional: both \texttt{hex\ "ff"} and
\texttt{hex\ "0xff"} return 255. The string is case-insensitive, so
\texttt{hex\ "DeAdBeEf"} works fine. If the string contains non-hex
characters, stryke warns and converts up to the first invalid character.
This is the standard way to parse hex-encoded values from config files,
color codes, or protocol dumps. For the reverse operation (number to hex
string), use \texttt{sprintf\ "\%x"}. Note that \texttt{hex} always
returns a number, never a string --- arithmetic is immediate.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $n = hex "deadbeef"}
\NormalTok{printf "0x\%x = \%d\textbackslash{}n", $n, $n}
\NormalTok{p hex "ff"   \# 255}
\NormalTok{"cafe" |\textgreater{} t \{ hex \} |\textgreater{} p   \# 51966}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{oct}}{oct}}\label{oct}

\texttt{oct\ STRING} --- interpret an octal, hexadecimal, or binary
string and return its numeric value.

\texttt{oct} is the multi-base cousin of \texttt{hex}: it auto-detects
the base from the prefix. Strings starting with \texttt{0b} are binary,
\texttt{0x} are hex, and bare digits or \texttt{0}-prefixed digits are
octal. This makes it the go-to for parsing Unix file permissions
(\texttt{oct\ "0755"} gives 493), binary literals, or any string where
the radix is embedded in the value. In stryke, \texttt{oct} handles
arbitrarily large integers via the same big-number pathway as other
arithmetic. A common gotcha: \texttt{oct\ "8"} warns because 8 is not a
valid octal digit --- use \texttt{hex} or a bare numeric literal
instead.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p oct "0755"    \# 493}
\NormalTok{p oct "0b1010"  \# 10}
\NormalTok{p oct "0xff"    \# 255}
\NormalTok{my $perms = oct "644"}
\NormalTok{p sprintf "\%04o", $perms  \# 0644}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{quotemeta}}{quotemeta}}\label{quotemeta}

\texttt{quotemeta\ STRING} --- escape all non-alphanumeric characters
with backslashes, returning a string safe for interpolation into a
regex.

This is essential when building dynamic regexes from user input: without
\texttt{quotemeta}, characters like \texttt{.}, \texttt{*}, \texttt{(},
\texttt{)}, \texttt{{[}}, and \texttt{\textbackslash{}} would be
interpreted as regex metacharacters, leading to either broken patterns
or security vulnerabilities (ReDoS). The equivalent inline syntax is
\texttt{\textbackslash{}Q..\textbackslash{}E} inside a regex. In stryke,
\texttt{quotemeta} handles the full Unicode range, escaping any
character that is not \texttt{{[}A-Za-z0-9\_{]}}. Use it liberally
whenever you splice user-provided strings into patterns.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $input = "file (1).txt"}
\NormalTok{my $safe = quotemeta $input}
\NormalTok{p $safe  \# file\textbackslash{} \textbackslash{}(1\textbackslash{})\textbackslash{}.txt}
\NormalTok{my $found = ("file (1).txt" =\textasciitilde{} /\^{}$safe$/)}
\NormalTok{p $found  \# 1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{trim}}{trim}}\label{trim}

\texttt{trim\ STRING} or \texttt{trim\ LIST} --- strip leading and
trailing ASCII whitespace.

When given a single string, \texttt{trim} returns the stripped result.
When given a list or used in a pipeline, it operates in streaming mode,
trimming each element individually as it flows through. Whitespace
includes spaces, tabs, carriage returns, and newlines. The \texttt{tm}
alias is convenient in pipeline chains where brevity matters.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{" hello " |\textgreater{} tm |\textgreater{} p                  \# "hello"}
\NormalTok{@raw |\textgreater{} tm |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{slurp("data.csv") |\textgreater{} ln |\textgreater{} tm |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lines}}{lines}}\label{lines}

\texttt{lines\ STRING} / \texttt{ln\ STRING} --- split a string on
newline boundaries, returning a streaming iterator of lines.

Each line is yielded without the trailing newline character. When piped
from \texttt{slurp}, this gives you a lazy line-by-line view of a file
without loading all lines into memory at once. Both
\texttt{\textbackslash{}n} and
\texttt{\textbackslash{}r\textbackslash{}n} line endings are handled.
The \texttt{ln} alias keeps pipelines compact.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{slurp("data.txt") |\textgreater{} lines |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{my @rows = lines $multiline\_str}
\NormalTok{$body |\textgreater{} ln |\textgreater{} grep /TODO/ |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{words}}{words}}\label{words}

\texttt{words} (alias \texttt{wd}) splits a string on whitespace
boundaries and returns the resulting list of words. It handles leading,
trailing, and consecutive whitespace gracefully --- unlike a naive
\texttt{split\ /\ /}, it never produces empty strings. This is the
idiomatic way to tokenize a line of text in stryke, and the short alias
\texttt{wd} keeps pipelines compact. It is equivalent to
Perl\textquotesingle s
\texttt{split\ \textquotesingle{}\ \textquotesingle{}} behavior.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @w = wd "  hello   world  "}
\NormalTok{p @w       \# hello, world}
\NormalTok{my $line = "  foo  bar  baz  "}
\NormalTok{my $count = scalar wd $line}
\NormalTok{p $count   \# 3}
\NormalTok{rl("data.txt") |\textgreater{} map \{ scalar wd \_ \} |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{chars}}{chars}}\label{chars}

\texttt{chars\ STRING} / \texttt{ch\ STRING} --- split a string into
individual characters, returning a streaming iterator.

Each Unicode grapheme cluster is yielded as a separate string element.
This is useful for character-level processing such as frequency
counting, transliteration, or building character n-grams. In pipeline
mode the characters stream one at a time, so you can chain with
\texttt{take}, \texttt{grep}, or \texttt{with\_index} without
materializing the full character array.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{"hello" |\textgreater{} chars |\textgreater{} e p               \# h e l l o}
\NormalTok{my @c = chars "abc"}
\NormalTok{"emoji: \textbackslash{}x\{1F600\}" |\textgreater{} ch |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{snake\_case}}{snake\_case}}\label{snake_case}

\texttt{snake\_case} (alias \texttt{sc}) converts a string from any
common casing convention --- camelCase, PascalCase, kebab-case, or mixed
--- into snake\_case, where words are lowercase and separated by
underscores. This is the standard naming convention for Perl and Rust
variables and function names. Consecutive uppercase letters in acronyms
are handled intelligently (e.g., \texttt{parseHTTPResponse} becomes
\texttt{parse\_http\_response}).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p sc "camelCase"          \# camel\_case}
\NormalTok{p sc "PascalCase"         \# pascal\_case}
\NormalTok{p sc "kebab{-}case"         \# kebab\_case}
\NormalTok{p sc "parseHTTPResponse"  \# parse\_http\_response}
\NormalTok{my @methods = qw(getUserName setEmailAddr)}
\NormalTok{@methods |\textgreater{} map sc |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{camel\_case}}{camel\_case}}\label{camel_case}

\texttt{camel\_case} (alias \texttt{cc}) converts a string from any
casing convention into camelCase, where the first word is lowercase and
subsequent words are capitalized with no separators. This is the
standard naming convention for JavaScript variables and Java methods.
The function handles underscores, hyphens, and spaces as word boundaries
and strips them during conversion.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cc "snake\_case"         \# snakeCase}
\NormalTok{p cc "kebab{-}case"         \# kebabCase}
\NormalTok{p cc "PascalCase"         \# pascalCase}
\NormalTok{p cc "hello world"        \# helloWorld}
\NormalTok{my @cols = qw(first\_name last\_name email\_addr)}
\NormalTok{@cols |\textgreater{} map cc |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kebab\_case}}{kebab\_case}}\label{kebab_case}

\texttt{kebab\_case} (alias \texttt{kc}) converts a string from any
casing convention into kebab-case, where words are lowercase and
separated by hyphens. This is the standard naming convention for CSS
classes, URL slugs, and CLI flag names. Like \texttt{snake\_case}, it
intelligently handles acronyms and mixed-case input. The function treats
underscores, spaces, and case transitions as word boundaries.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p kc "camelCase"          \# camel{-}case}
\NormalTok{p kc "PascalCase"         \# pascal{-}case}
\NormalTok{p kc "snake\_case"         \# snake{-}case}
\NormalTok{p kc "parseHTTPResponse"  \# parse{-}http{-}response}
\NormalTok{my $slug = kc $title      \# URL{-}safe slug}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{study}}{study}}\label{study}

\texttt{study\ STRING} --- hint to the regex engine that the given
string will be matched against multiple patterns, allowing it to build
an internal lookup table for faster matching.

In classic Perl, \texttt{study} builds a linked list of character
positions so subsequent regex matches can skip impossible starting
points. In practice, modern regex engines (including
stryke\textquotesingle s Rust-based engine) already perform these
optimizations internally, so \texttt{study} is effectively a no-op in
stryke --- calling it is harmless but provides no measurable speedup. It
exists for compatibility with Perl code that uses it. You only need
\texttt{study} when porting legacy scripts that call it; do not add it
to new stryke code expecting a performance benefit.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{study $text   \# no{-}op in stryke, kept for compatibility}
\NormalTok{my @hits = grep \{ /$pattern/ \} @lines}
\NormalTok{p scalar @hits}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pos}}{pos}}\label{pos}

\texttt{pos\ SCALAR} --- gets or sets the position where the next
\texttt{m//g} (global match) will start searching in the given string.
After a successful \texttt{m//g} match, \texttt{pos} returns the offset
just past the end of the match. You can assign to \texttt{pos(\$s)} to
manually reposition the search. If the last \texttt{m//g} failed,
\texttt{pos} resets to \texttt{undef}. This is essential for writing
lexers or tokenizers that consume a string incrementally with
\texttt{\textbackslash{}G}-anchored patterns. In stryke, \texttt{pos}
tracks per-scalar state just like Perl.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $s = "abcabc"}
\NormalTok{while ($s =\textasciitilde{} /a/g) \{ p pos($s) \}}
\NormalTok{\# 1 4}
\NormalTok{pos($s) = 0   \# reset to scan again}
\NormalTok{p pos($s)     \# 0}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-arrays-hashes}
\chapter{Arrays \& Hashes}\label{arrays-hashes}

20 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{push}}{push}}\label{push}

\texttt{push\ @array,\ LIST} --- appends one or more elements to the end
of an array and returns the new length. This is the primary way to grow
arrays in stryke and works identically to Perl\textquotesingle s
builtin. You can push scalars, lists, or even the result of a pipeline.
In stryke, \texttt{push} is O(1) amortized thanks to the underlying Rust
\texttt{Vec}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @q}
\NormalTok{push @q, 1:3}
\NormalTok{push @q, "four", "five"}
\NormalTok{p scalar @q   \# 5}
\NormalTok{@q |\textgreater{} e p     \# 1 2 3 four five}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Returns the new element count, so
\texttt{my\ \$len\ =\ push\ @arr,\ \$val;} is valid.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pop}}{pop}}\label{pop}

\texttt{pop\ @array} --- removes and returns the last element of an
array, or \texttt{undef} if the array is empty. This is the complement
of \texttt{push} and together they give you classic stack (LIFO)
semantics. In stryke the operation is O(1) because the underlying Rust
\texttt{Vec::pop} simply decrements the length. When called without an
argument inside a subroutine, \texttt{pop} operates on \texttt{@\_}; at
file scope it operates on \texttt{@ARGV}, matching standard Perl
behavior.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @stk = 1:5}
\NormalTok{my $top = pop @stk}
\NormalTok{p $top          \# 5}
\NormalTok{p scalar @stk   \# 4}
\NormalTok{@stk |\textgreater{} e p     \# 1 2 3 4}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{shift}}{shift}}\label{shift}

\texttt{shift\ @array} --- removes and returns the first element of an
array, sliding all remaining elements down by one index. Like
\texttt{pop}, it returns \texttt{undef} on an empty array. Without an
explicit argument it defaults to \texttt{@\_} inside subroutines and
\texttt{@ARGV} at file scope. Because every element must be moved,
\texttt{shift} is O(n); if you only need LIFO access, prefer
\texttt{push}/\texttt{pop}. Use \texttt{shift} when processing argument
lists or implementing FIFO queues.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @args = @ARGV}
\NormalTok{my $cmd = shift @args}
\NormalTok{p $cmd}
\NormalTok{@args |\textgreater{} e p   \# remaining arguments}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{unshift}}{unshift}}\label{unshift}

\texttt{unshift\ @array,\ LIST} --- prepends one or more elements to the
beginning of an array and returns the new length. It is the counterpart
of \texttt{push} for the front of the array. Like \texttt{shift}, this
is O(n) because existing elements must be moved to make room. When you
need to build an array in reverse order or maintain a priority queue
with newest items first, \texttt{unshift} is the idiomatic choice. You
can pass multiple values and they will appear in the same order they
were given.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @log = ("b", "c")}
\NormalTok{unshift @log, "a"}
\NormalTok{@log |\textgreater{} e p   \# a b c}
\NormalTok{my $len = unshift @log, "x", "y"}
\NormalTok{p $len        \# 5}
\NormalTok{@log |\textgreater{} e p   \# x y a b c}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{splice}}{splice}}\label{splice}

\texttt{splice\ @array,\ OFFSET\ {[},\ LENGTH\ {[},\ LIST{]}{]}} --- the
Swiss-army knife for array mutation. It can insert, remove, or replace
elements at any position in a single call. With just an offset it
removes everything from that point to the end. With offset and length it
removes that many elements. With a replacement list it inserts those
elements in place of the removed ones. The return value is the list of
removed elements, which is useful for saving what you cut. In stryke
this compiles down to Rust\textquotesingle s \texttt{Vec::splice} and
\texttt{Vec::drain}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @a = 1:5}
\NormalTok{my @removed = splice @a, 1, 2, 8, 9}
\NormalTok{@a |\textgreater{} e p        \# 1 8 9 4 5}
\NormalTok{@removed |\textgreater{} e p  \# 2 3}
\NormalTok{splice @a, 2     \# remove from index 2 onward}
\NormalTok{@a |\textgreater{} e p        \# 1 8}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{splice\_last}}{splice\_last}}\label{splice_last}

\texttt{splice\_last\ @array,\ OFFSET\ {[},\ LENGTH\ {[},\ LIST{]}{]}}
--- same shape as \texttt{splice}, but returns the \textbf{last} removed
element instead of the full removed list (or \texttt{undef} if nothing
was removed). The stryke spelling of Perl\textquotesingle s
\texttt{scalar\ splice(@a,\ off,\ n)} for \texttt{-\/-no-interop} mode
where \texttt{scalar} is rejected at parse time. The array is still
mutated in place, exactly like \texttt{splice}. Aliases:
\texttt{splice1}, \texttt{spl\_last}. Desugars to
\texttt{tail(splice(...))} so any list-context property of
\texttt{splice} is preserved.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @a = 10:50:10}
\NormalTok{my $last = splice\_last @a, 1, 2   \# removes (20, 30), returns 30}
\NormalTok{@a |\textgreater{} e p                          \# 10 40 50}
\NormalTok{my $popped = splice\_last @b, 0, 0  \# removes nothing, returns undef}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{keys}}{keys}}\label{keys}

\texttt{keys\ \%hash} --- returns the list of all keys in a hash in no
particular order. When called on an array, it returns the list of valid
indices (0 to \texttt{\$\#array}). In scalar context, \texttt{keys}
returns the number of keys. Calling \texttt{keys} resets the
\texttt{each} iterator on that hash, which is the standard way to
restart iteration. In stryke, this calls Rust\textquotesingle s
\texttt{HashMap::keys()} and collects into a \texttt{Vec}. For sorted
output, chain with \texttt{sort} via the pipe operator.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%env = (HOME =\textgreater{} "/root", USER =\textgreater{} "me")}
\NormalTok{keys(\%env) |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} e p   \# HOME USER}
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%env           \# 2}
\NormalTok{my @a = (10, 20, 30)}
\NormalTok{keys(@a) |\textgreater{} e p              \# 0 1 2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{values}}{values}}\label{values}

\texttt{values\ \%hash} --- returns the list of all values in a hash in
no particular order (matching the order of \texttt{keys} for the same
hash state). When called on an array, it returns the array elements
themselves. In scalar context, returns the count of values. Like
\texttt{keys}, calling \texttt{values} resets the \texttt{each}
iterator. In stryke this maps to Rust\textquotesingle s
\texttt{HashMap::values()}. Combine with \texttt{sum}, \texttt{sort}, or
pipeline operators for common aggregation patterns.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%scores = (alice =\textgreater{} 90, bob =\textgreater{} 85)}
\NormalTok{p sum(values \%scores)   \# 175}
\NormalTok{values(\%scores) |\textgreater{} sort \{ \_1 \textless{}=\textgreater{} \_ \} |\textgreater{} e p   \# 90 85}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{each}}{each}}\label{each}

\texttt{each\ \%hash} --- returns the next (key, value) pair from a hash
as a two-element list, or an empty list when the iterator is exhausted.
Each hash has its own internal iterator, which is reset when you call
\texttt{keys} or \texttt{values} on the same hash. This is
memory-efficient for large hashes because it does not build the full key
list. Gotcha: do not add or delete keys while iterating with
\texttt{each}; that can cause skipped or duplicated entries. In stryke,
the iteration order is non-deterministic (Rust \texttt{HashMap} order).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%h = (a =\textgreater{} 1, b =\textgreater{} 2, c =\textgreater{} 3)}
\NormalTok{while (my ($k, $v) = each \%h) \{ p "$k=$v" \}}
\NormalTok{\# output order varies: a=1 b=2 c=3}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{delete}}{delete}}\label{delete}

\texttt{delete\ EXPR} --- removes a key-value pair from a hash or an
element from an array and returns the removed value (or \texttt{undef}
if it did not exist). For hashes this is the only way to truly remove a
key; assigning \texttt{undef} to \texttt{\$h\{key\}} leaves the key in
place. For arrays, \texttt{delete} sets the element to \texttt{undef}
but does not shift indices (use \texttt{splice} if you need to close the
gap). In stryke, hash deletion maps to Rust\textquotesingle s
\texttt{HashMap::remove}. You can delete multiple keys at once with a
hash slice: \texttt{delete\ @h\{@keys\}}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%h = (x =\textgreater{} 1, y =\textgreater{} 2, z =\textgreater{} 3)}
\NormalTok{my $old = delete $h\{x\}}
\NormalTok{p $old   \# 1}
\NormalTok{p exists $h\{x\} ? "yes" : "no"   \# no}
\NormalTok{delete @h\{"y", "z"\}   \# delete multiple keys}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{exists}}{exists}}\label{exists}

\texttt{exists\ EXPR} --- tests whether a specific key is present in a
hash or an index is present in an array, regardless of whether the
associated value is \texttt{undef}. This is different from
\texttt{defined}: a key can exist but hold \texttt{undef}. Use
\texttt{exists} to check hash membership before accessing a value to
avoid autovivification side effects. In stryke, \texttt{exists} on a
hash compiles to Rust\textquotesingle s \texttt{HashMap::contains\_key},
and on an array it checks bounds. You can also use it with nested
structures: \texttt{exists\ \$h\{a\}\{b\}} only checks the final level.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%h = (a =\textgreater{} 1, b =\textgreater{} undef)}
\NormalTok{p exists $h\{b\} ? "yes" : "no"   \# yes (key present, value undef)}
\NormalTok{p exists $h\{c\} ? "yes" : "no"   \# no}
\NormalTok{my @a = (10, 20, 30)}
\NormalTok{p exists $x[5] ? "yes" : "no"   \# no}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{scalar}}{scalar}}\label{scalar}

\texttt{scalar\ EXPR} --- forces the expression into scalar context. The
most common use is \texttt{scalar\ @array} to get the element count
instead of the list of elements. In stryke, scalar context on an array
returns its length as a Rust \texttt{usize}. You can also use
\texttt{scalar} on a hash to get the number of key-value pairs, or on a
function call to force it to return a single value. This is essential
when you want a count inside string interpolation or as a function
argument where list context would be ambiguous.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @items = ("a", "b", "c")}
\NormalTok{p scalar @items   \# 3}
\NormalTok{p "count: " . scalar @items   \# count: 3}
\NormalTok{my \%h = (x =\textgreater{} 1, y =\textgreater{} 2)}
\NormalTok{p scalar \%h       \# 2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{defined}}{defined}}\label{defined}

\texttt{defined\ EXPR} --- returns true if the expression has a value
that is not \texttt{undef}. This is the canonical way to distinguish
between "no value" and "a value that happens to be false" (such as
\texttt{0}, \texttt{""}, or \texttt{"0"}). Use \texttt{defined} before
dereferencing optional values or checking return codes from functions
that return \texttt{undef} on failure. In stryke, \texttt{defined}
compiles to a Rust \texttt{Option::is\_some()} check internally. Note
that \texttt{defined} on an aggregate (hash or array) is deprecated in
Perl 5 and is a no-op in stryke.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $x = undef}
\NormalTok{p defined($x) ? "yes" : "no"   \# no}
\NormalTok{$x = 0}
\NormalTok{p defined($x) ? "yes" : "no"   \# yes}
\NormalTok{my $val = fn\_that\_may\_fail()}
\NormalTok{p $val if defined $val}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{undef}}{undef}}\label{undef}

\texttt{undef} --- the undefined value, representing the absence of a
value. As a function, \texttt{undef\ \$var} explicitly undefines a
variable, freeing any value it held. \texttt{undef} is falsy in boolean
context and triggers "use of uninitialized value" warnings under
\texttt{use\ warnings}. In stryke, \texttt{undef} maps to
Rust\textquotesingle s \texttt{None} in an \texttt{Option} type
internally. Use \texttt{undef} to reset variables, signal missing return
values, or clear hash entries without deleting the key.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $x = 42}
\NormalTok{undef $x}
\NormalTok{p defined($x) ? "def" : "undef"   \# undef}
\NormalTok{fn maybe \{ return undef if !@\_}
\NormalTok{    return $\_[0] \}}
\NormalTok{p defined(maybe()) ? "got" : "nothing"   \# nothing}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ref}}{ref}}\label{ref}

\texttt{ref\ EXPR} --- returns a string indicating the reference type of
the value, or an empty string if it is not a reference. Common return
values are \texttt{SCALAR}, \texttt{ARRAY}, \texttt{HASH},
\texttt{CODE}, \texttt{REF}, and \texttt{Regexp}. For blessed objects it
returns the class name. Use \texttt{ref} to dispatch on data type or
validate arguments in polymorphic functions. In stryke, \texttt{ref}
inspects the Rust enum variant of the underlying value. Note that
\texttt{ref} does not recurse; it only tells you the top-level type.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $r = [1, 2, 3]}
\NormalTok{p ref($r)       \# ARRAY}
\NormalTok{p ref(\textbackslash{}\%ENV)    \# HASH}
\NormalTok{p ref(\textbackslash{}\&main)   \# CODE}
\NormalTok{p ref(42)       \# (empty string)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bless}}{bless}}\label{bless}

Associate a reference with a package name, turning it into an object of
that class. The blessed reference can then have methods called on it via
\texttt{-\textgreater{}}. The second argument defaults to the current
package. This is the foundation of Perl\textquotesingle s object system.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fn new($class, \%args) \{}
\NormalTok{    bless \{ \%args \}, $class}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{my $obj = new("Dog", name =\textgreater{} "Rex", breed =\textgreater{} "Lab")}
\NormalTok{p ref($obj)          \# Dog}
\NormalTok{p $obj{-}\textgreater{}\{name\}       \# Rex}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tie}}{tie}}\label{tie}

Bind a variable to an implementing class so that all accesses (read,
write, delete, etc.) are intercepted by methods on that class. This is
Perl\textquotesingle s mechanism for transparent object-backed variables
--- tied hashes can be backed by a database, tied scalars can validate
on assignment, etc. Use \texttt{untie} to remove the binding.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{tie my \%db, \textquotesingle{}DB\_File\textquotesingle{}, \textquotesingle{}cache.db\textquotesingle{}}
\NormalTok{$db\{key\} = "value"         \# writes to disk}
\NormalTok{p $db\{key\}                  \# reads from disk}
\NormalTok{untie \%db}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{prototype}}{prototype}}\label{prototype}

Return the prototype string of a named function, or \texttt{undef} if
the function has no prototype. Prototypes control how arguments are
parsed at compile time --- they influence context and reference-passing
behavior. Useful for introspection and metaprogramming.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p prototype("CORE::push")     \# \textbackslash{}@@}
\NormalTok{p prototype("CORE::map")      \# \&@}
\NormalTok{fn greet($name) \{ p "hi $name" \}}
\NormalTok{p prototype(\textbackslash{}\&greet)          \# undef (signatures, no proto)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{wantarray}}{wantarray}}\label{wantarray}

\texttt{wantarray()} --- returns true if the current subroutine was
called in list context, false in scalar context, and \texttt{undef} in
void context. This lets a function adapt its return value to the
caller\textquotesingle s expectations. A common pattern is returning a
list in list context and a count or reference in scalar context. In
stryke, use \texttt{fn} to define subroutines and \texttt{wantarray()}
inside them just like in Perl. Note that \texttt{wantarray} only
reflects the immediate call site, not nested contexts.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fn ctx \{ wantarray() ? "list" : "scalar" \}}
\NormalTok{my @r = ctx()}
\NormalTok{p $r[0]   \# list}
\NormalTok{my $r = ctx()}
\NormalTok{p $r      \# scalar}
\NormalTok{fn flexible \{ wantarray() ? (1, 2, 3) : 3 \}}
\NormalTok{my @all = flexible()}
\NormalTok{p scalar @all   \# 3}
\NormalTok{my $cnt = flexible()}
\NormalTok{p $cnt          \# 3}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{caller}}{caller}}\label{caller}

\texttt{caller\ {[}LEVEL{]}} --- returns information about the calling
subroutine\textquotesingle s context. In list context it returns
\texttt{(package,\ filename,\ line)} for the given call-stack level
(default 0, the immediate caller). With higher levels you can walk up
the call stack for debugging or generating stack traces. In scalar
context, \texttt{caller} returns just the package name. In stryke,
\texttt{caller} works with \texttt{fn}-defined subroutines and
integrates with the runtime\textquotesingle s frame tracking. This is
invaluable for building custom error reporters or trace utilities.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fn here \{}
\NormalTok{    my ($pkg, $f, $ln) = caller()}
\NormalTok{    p "$f:$ln"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{here()   \# prints current file:line}
\NormalTok{fn deep \{}
\NormalTok{    my ($pkg, $f, $ln) = caller(1)}
\NormalTok{    p "grandparent: $f:$ln"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-control-flow}
\chapter{Control Flow}\label{control-flow}

16 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{if}}{if}}\label{if}

The fundamental conditional construct. Evaluates its condition in
boolean context and executes the block if true. stryke supports both the
block form \texttt{if\ (COND)\ \{\ BODY\ \}} and the postfix form
\texttt{EXPR\ if\ COND}, which is idiomatic for single-statement guards.
The condition can be any expression --- numbers (0 is false), strings
(empty and \texttt{"0"} are false), undef (false), and references
(always true). Postfix \texttt{if} cannot have
\texttt{elsif}/\texttt{else} --- use the block form for multi-branch
logic.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $n = 42}
\NormalTok{if ($n \textgreater{} 0) \{ p "positive" \}}
\NormalTok{p "big" if $n \textgreater{} 10          \# postfix — clean one{-}liner}
\NormalTok{my $label = "even" if $n \% 2 == 0}
\NormalTok{p "got: $n" if defined $n   \# guard against undef}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{elsif}}{elsif}}\label{elsif}

Chain additional conditions after an \texttt{if} block without nesting.
Each \texttt{elsif} is tested in order; the first one whose condition is
true has its block executed, and the rest are skipped. There is no limit
on the number of \texttt{elsif} branches. In stryke, prefer
\texttt{match} for complex multi-branch dispatch since it supports
pattern matching, destructuring, and guards --- but \texttt{elsif}
remains the right tool for simple linear condition chains. Note: it is
\texttt{elsif}, not \texttt{elseif} or \texttt{else\ if} --- the latter
is a syntax error.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fn classify($n) \{}
\NormalTok{    if    ($n \textless{} 0)   \{ "negative" \}}
\NormalTok{    elsif ($n == 0)  \{ "zero" \}}
