useful in math-heavy pipelines. The aliases \texttt{cb} and
\texttt{cube} are interchangeable.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cubed(3)          \# 27}
\NormalTok{p cb(10)            \# 1000}
\NormalTok{1:4 |\textgreater{} map cb |\textgreater{} e p  \# 1 8 27 64}
\NormalTok{my $vol = cb($side)             \# volume of a cube}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{expt}}{expt}}\label{expt}

Raise a base to an arbitrary exponent and return the result. This is the
function form of the \texttt{**} operator. Accepts integer and
floating-point exponents, including negative values for reciprocals and
fractional values for roots.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p expt(2, 10)       \# 1024}
\NormalTok{p expt(27, 1/3)     \# 3.0 (cube root)}
\NormalTok{p expt(10, {-}2)      \# 0.01}
\NormalTok{1:8 |\textgreater{} map \{ expt(2, \_) \} |\textgreater{} e p  \# 2 4 8 16 32 64 128 256}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{exp}}{exp}}\label{exp}

Return Euler\textquotesingle s number *e* raised to the given power.
\texttt{exp(0)} is \texttt{1}, \texttt{exp(1)} is approximately
\texttt{2.71828}. This is the inverse of \texttt{log}. Useful for
exponential growth/decay calculations, probability distributions, and
converting between logarithmic and linear scales.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p exp(1)            \# 2.71828182845905}
\NormalTok{p exp(0)            \# 1}
\NormalTok{my $growth = $initial * exp($rate * $time)}
\NormalTok{1:5 |\textgreater{} map exp |\textgreater{} e \{ p sprintf("\%.4f", \_) \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{log}}{log}}\label{log}

Return the natural (base-*e*) logarithm of a positive number. Dies if
the argument is zero or negative. For base-10 logarithms, divide by
\texttt{log(10)}. For base-2, divide by \texttt{log(2)}. This is the
inverse of \texttt{exp}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p log(exp(1))       \# 1.0}
\NormalTok{p log(100) / log(10)  \# 2.0 (log base 10)}
\NormalTok{my $bits = log($n) / log(2)  \# log base 2}
\NormalTok{1:5 |\textgreater{} map log |\textgreater{} e \{ p sprintf("\%.3f", \_) \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sin}}{sin}}\label{sin}

Return the sine of an angle given in radians. The result ranges from
\texttt{-1} to \texttt{1}. For degrees, convert first:
\texttt{sin(\$deg\ *\ 3.14159265\ /\ 180)}. Use \texttt{atan2} to go in
the reverse direction.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p sin(0)               \# 0}
\NormalTok{p sin(3.14159265 / 2)  \# 1.0}
\NormalTok{my @wave = map \{ sin(\_ * 0.1) \} 0:62}
\NormalTok{@wave |\textgreater{} e \{ p sprintf("\%6.3f", \_) \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cos}}{cos}}\label{cos}

Return the cosine of an angle given in radians. The result ranges from
\texttt{-1} to \texttt{1}. \texttt{cos(0)} is \texttt{1}. Like
\texttt{sin}, convert degrees to radians before calling.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cos(0)                 \# 1}
\NormalTok{p cos(3.14159265)        \# {-}1.0}
\NormalTok{my $x = $radius * cos($theta)}
\NormalTok{my $y = $radius * sin($theta)}
\NormalTok{p "($x, $y)"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{atan2}}{atan2}}\label{atan2}

Return the arctangent of \texttt{Y/X} in radians, using the signs of
both arguments to determine the correct quadrant. The result ranges from
\texttt{-pi} to \texttt{pi}. This is the standard way to compute angles
from Cartesian coordinates and is more robust than \texttt{atan(Y/X)}
because it handles \texttt{X=0} correctly.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $pi = atan2(0, {-}1)       \# 3.14159265...}
\NormalTok{p atan2(1, 1)               \# 0.785... (pi/4)}
\NormalTok{my $angle = atan2($dy, $dx)}
\NormalTok{p sprintf("\%.1f degrees", $angle * 180 / $pi)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rand}}{rand}}\label{rand}

Return a pseudo-random floating-point number in the range
\texttt{{[}0,\ N)}. If N is omitted it defaults to \texttt{1}. The
result is never exactly equal to N. For integer results, combine with
\texttt{int}. Seed the generator with \texttt{srand} for reproducible
sequences.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p rand()                \# e.g. 0.7342...}
\NormalTok{p int(rand(100))        \# random int 0:99}
\NormalTok{my @deck = 1:52}
\NormalTok{my @shuffled = sort \{ rand() \textless{}=\textgreater{} rand() \} @deck  \# poor shuffle}
\NormalTok{my $coin = rand() \textless{} 0.5 ? "heads" : "tails"}
\NormalTok{p $coin}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{srand}}{srand}}\label{srand}

Seed the pseudo-random number generator used by \texttt{rand}. Calling
\texttt{srand} with a specific value produces a reproducible sequence,
which is useful for testing. Without arguments, Perl seeds from a
platform-specific entropy source. You rarely need to call this
explicitly --- Perl auto-seeds on first use of \texttt{rand}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{srand(42)                   \# reproducible sequence}
\NormalTok{p int(rand(100))            \# always the same value}
\NormalTok{srand(42)}
\NormalTok{p int(rand(100))            \# same value again}
\NormalTok{srand()                     \# re{-}seed from entropy}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-file-system}
\chapter{File System}\label{file-system}

35 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{basename}}{basename}}\label{basename}

Extract the filename component from a path, stripping all leading
directory segments. The short alias \texttt{bn} keeps one-liner
pipelines terse. If an optional suffix argument is provided, that suffix
is also stripped from the result, which is handy for removing
extensions.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p basename("/usr/local/bin/stryke")        \# stryke}
\NormalTok{p bn("/tmp/data.csv", ".csv")           \# data}
\NormalTok{"/etc/nginx/nginx.conf" |\textgreater{} bn |\textgreater{} p      \# nginx.conf}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dirname}}{dirname}}\label{dirname}

Return the directory portion of a path, stripping the final filename
component. The short alias \texttt{dn} mirrors \texttt{bn}. This is a
pure string operation --- it does not touch the filesystem, so it works
on paths that do not exist yet. Useful for deriving output directories
from input file paths.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p dirname("/usr/local/bin/stryke")          \# /usr/local/bin}
\NormalTok{p dn("/tmp/data.csv")                   \# /tmp}
\NormalTok{my $dir = dn($0)                        \# directory of current script}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{fileparse}}{fileparse}}\label{fileparse}

Split a path into its three logical components: the filename, the
directory prefix, and a suffix that matches one of the supplied
patterns. This mirrors Perl\textquotesingle s
\texttt{File::Basename::fileparse} and is the most flexible path
decomposition available. When no suffix patterns are given the suffix is
empty.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($name, $dir, $sfx) = fileparse("/home/user/report.txt", qr/\textbackslash{}.txt/)}
\NormalTok{p "$dir | $name | $sfx"  \# /home/user/ | report | .txt}
\NormalTok{my ($n, $d) = fileparse("./lib/Foo/Bar.pm")}
\NormalTok{p "$d$n"                 \# ./lib/Foo/Bar.pm}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{realpath}}{realpath}}\label{realpath}

Resolve a path to its absolute canonical form by following all symbolic
links and eliminating \texttt{.} and \texttt{..} segments. Unlike
\texttt{canonpath}, this hits the filesystem and will die if any
component does not exist. The short alias \texttt{rp} is convenient in
pipelines. Use this when you need a guaranteed unique path for
deduplication or comparison.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p realpath(".")                      \# /home/user/project}
\NormalTok{p rp("../sibling")                   \# /home/user/sibling}
\NormalTok{my $canon = rp($0)                  \# absolute path of current script}
\NormalTok{"." |\textgreater{} rp |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{canonpath}}{canonpath}}\label{canonpath}

Clean up a file path by collapsing redundant separators, resolving
\texttt{.} and \texttt{..} segments, and normalizing trailing slashes
--- all without touching the filesystem. Unlike \texttt{realpath}, this
is a purely lexical operation so it works on paths that do not exist.
Use it to normalize user-supplied paths before comparison or storage.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p canonpath("/usr/./local/../local/bin/")   \# /usr/local/bin}
\NormalTok{p canonpath("a/b/../c")                     \# a/c}
\NormalTok{my $clean = canonpath($ENV\{HOME\} . "/./docs/../docs")}
\NormalTok{p $clean}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getcwd}}{getcwd}}\label{getcwd}

Return the current working directory as an absolute path string. This
calls the underlying OS \texttt{getcwd} function, so it always reflects
the real directory even if the process changed directories via
\texttt{chdir}. The alias \texttt{pwd} matches the familiar shell
command. Often used to save and restore directory context around
\texttt{chdir} calls.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $orig = pwd()}
\NormalTok{chdir("/tmp")}
\NormalTok{p pwd()      \# /tmp}
\NormalTok{chdir($orig)}
\NormalTok{p getcwd()   \# back to original}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cd}}{cd}}\label{cd}

Set the interpreter working directory used to resolve \textbf{relative}
paths for Stryke builtins (\texttt{slurp}, \texttt{spurt},
\texttt{open}, \texttt{glob}, \texttt{stat}, \texttt{rename},
\texttt{ls}, \texttt{-e}, \ldots). Does \textbf{not} call the OS
\texttt{chdir} by itself --- \texttt{getcwd}/\texttt{pwd} stay unchanged
until you call \texttt{chdir}. A successful \texttt{chdir} \textbf{does}
refresh this logical base from the new OS cwd. Relative \texttt{cd}
targets are resolved against the current logical directory first. With
no arguments, uses \texttt{\$HOME} / \texttt{\%USERPROFILE\%} when set.
Returns \texttt{1} on success, \texttt{0} on failure and sets
\texttt{\$!}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{cd("/tmp")}
\NormalTok{spurt("x.txt", "hi")       \# writes /tmp/x.txt}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{which}}{which}}\label{which}

Search the \texttt{PATH} environment variable for the first executable
matching the given command name and return its absolute path, or
\texttt{undef} if not found. This is the programmatic equivalent of the
shell \texttt{which} command. Useful for checking tool availability
before calling \texttt{system} or \texttt{exec}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $gcc = which("gcc") // die "gcc not found"}
\NormalTok{p $gcc                       \# /usr/bin/gcc}
\NormalTok{if (which("rg")) \{}
\NormalTok{    system("rg pattern file")}
\NormalTok{\} else \{}
\NormalTok{    system("grep pattern file")}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{glob}}{glob}}\label{glob}

Expand a zsh-style glob pattern against the filesystem and return a list
of matching paths. World-first: stryke supports \textbf{every} zsh glob
qualifier from \texttt{man\ zshexpn} \_Filename Generation
\textgreater{} Glob Qualifiers\_ (no other scripting language does
this). Backed by \texttt{zsh::glob} from zshrs --- single source of
truth, zero stryke-side reimplementation. Patterns: \texttt{*},
\texttt{?}, \texttt{{[}abc{]}}, \texttt{\{a,b\}}, \texttt{**} recursive,
plus the \texttt{(...)} qualifier suffix.

\subsection{Qualifier reference (full
set)}\label{qualifier-reference-full-set}

\textbf{File type:} \texttt{(/)} dir · \texttt{(.)} regular file ·
\texttt{(@)} symlink · \texttt{(=)} socket · \texttt{(p)} FIFO ·
\texttt{(\%b)} block dev · \texttt{(\%c)} char dev · \texttt{(\%)} any
device · \texttt{(*)} executable file.

\textbf{Permission:} \texttt{(r)} \texttt{(w)} \texttt{(x)} EUID r/w/x ·
\texttt{(R)} \texttt{(W)} \texttt{(X)} world · \texttt{(A)} \texttt{(I)}
\texttt{(E)} group · \texttt{(s)} setuid · \texttt{(S)} setgid ·
\texttt{(t)} sticky · \texttt{(f\textless{}bits\textgreater{})} exact
mode-bit match (e.g. \texttt{(f644)}).

\textbf{Ownership / device:} \texttt{(U)} owned by EUID · \texttt{(G)}
owned by EGID · \texttt{(u\textless{}N\textgreater{})} uid N ·
\texttt{(g\textless{}N\textgreater{})} gid N ·
\texttt{(d\textless{}N\textgreater{})} match by device number.

\textbf{Numeric} (each accepts \texttt{+N} greater, \texttt{-N} less,
\texttt{N} equal): \texttt{(L{[}unit{]}±N)} size ---
\texttt{p}/\texttt{k}/\texttt{m}/\texttt{g}/\texttt{t} units, default
bytes · \texttt{(l±N)} link count · \texttt{(a{[}unit{]}±N)} atime ·
\texttt{(m{[}unit{]}±N)} mtime · \texttt{(c{[}unit{]}±N)} ctime --- time
units \texttt{s}/\texttt{m}/\texttt{h}/\texttt{d}/\texttt{w}/\texttt{M}.

\textbf{Sort:} \texttt{(on)} \texttt{(oL)} \texttt{(ol)} \texttt{(oa)}
\texttt{(om)} \texttt{(oc)} \texttt{(od)} ascending; capital \texttt{O*}
descending; \texttt{(oN)} no sort.

\textbf{Selection:} \texttt{({[}N{]})} Nth match · \texttt{({[}N,M{]})}
slice. Combine with sort: \texttt{(om{[}1{]})} newest single match.

\textbf{Flags:} \texttt{(N)} NULL\_GLOB --- empty list on no match ·
\texttt{(D)} include dotfiles · \texttt{(F)} non-empty directory ·
\texttt{(M)} mark-dirs (append \texttt{/}) · \texttt{(T)} list-types
(append type char) · \texttt{(n)} numeric sort.

\textbf{Eval / functions:}
\texttt{(e\textquotesingle{}CMD\textquotesingle{})} shell-eval predicate
· \texttt{(+func)} function-as-test (predicate sub).

\textbf{Join words:} \texttt{(P…)} prefix words · \texttt{(Q…)} postfix
words around each match.

\textbf{Colon modifiers:} \texttt{(:s/PAT/REPL/)} sed substitution on
each path · \texttt{(:e)} extension · \texttt{(:r)} strip extension ·
\texttt{(:t)} tail · \texttt{(:h)} head · all of zsh\textquotesingle s
history-style modifiers.

\textbf{Combinators:} prefix \texttt{\^{}} negates · prefix \texttt{-}
toggles symlink-following · \texttt{,} separates OR alternatives ·
chained qualifiers AND.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @scripts = glob("*.pl")}
\NormalTok{my @all\_rs = glob("src/**/*.rs")}
\NormalTok{my @dirs = glob("**(/)")           \# directories only, recursive}
\NormalTok{my @big = glob("**(L+1024)")       \# files larger than 1024 bytes}
\NormalTok{my @recent = glob("**(om[1])")     \# most recently modified, take 1}
\NormalTok{my @safe = glob("missing*(N)")     \# null{-}glob, never errors}
\NormalTok{my @mode = glob("**(f644)")        \# files with exactly mode 644}
\NormalTok{my @stems = glob("*.txt(:r)")      \# all .txt files, extension stripped}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{glob\_match}}{glob\_match}}\label{glob_match}

Test whether a string matches a shell-style glob pattern. Returns true
(1) on match, false (empty string) otherwise. Supports \texttt{*},
\texttt{?}, \texttt{{[}abc{]}}, and \texttt{\{a,b\}}. This is a pure
string match --- it does not read the filesystem, so it works for
filtering lists of paths you already have. Note: zsh glob qualifiers
like \texttt{(/)} or \texttt{(.)} are stat-based filters that only apply
at filesystem-expansion time
(\texttt{glob}/\texttt{glob\_par}/\texttt{slurp}); \texttt{glob\_match}
ignores any trailing qualifier.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p glob\_match("*.pl", "script.pl")        \# 1}
\NormalTok{p glob\_match("*.pl", "script.py")        \# (empty)}
\NormalTok{my @perl = grep \{ glob\_match("*.\{pl,pm\}", \_) \} @files}
\NormalTok{@perl |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{copy}}{copy}}\label{copy}

Copy a file from a source path to a destination path. The destination
can be a file path or a directory (in which case the source filename is
preserved). Dies on failure. This is the programmatic equivalent of
\texttt{cp} and avoids shelling out. Metadata such as permissions is
preserved where possible.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{copy("src/config.yaml", "/tmp/config.yaml")}
\NormalTok{copy("report.pdf", "/backup/")}
\NormalTok{my $tmp = tf()}
\NormalTok{copy($0, $tmp)  \# back up the current script}
\NormalTok{p slurp($tmp)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{move}}{move}}\label{move}

Move or rename a file from source to destination. If the source and
destination are on the same filesystem this is an atomic rename;
otherwise it falls back to copy-then-delete. Dies on failure. The short
alias \texttt{mv} mirrors the shell command.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{move("draft.txt", "final.txt")         \# rename in place}
\NormalTok{mv("output.csv", "/archive/output.csv") \# move across dirs}
\NormalTok{my $tmp = tf()}
\NormalTok{spurt($tmp, "data")}
\NormalTok{mv($tmp, "data.txt")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rename}}{rename}}\label{rename}

Rename a file or directory from an old name to a new name. This is an
atomic operation on the same filesystem. If the destination already
exists it is silently replaced. Dies on failure. Unlike
\texttt{move}/\texttt{mv}, this does not fall back to copy-then-delete
across filesystems.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{rename("draft.md", "final.md")}
\NormalTok{rename("output", "output\_v2")          \# works on dirs too}
\NormalTok{my $bak = "config.yaml.bak"}
\NormalTok{rename("config.yaml", $bak)}
\NormalTok{spurt("config.yaml", $new\_config)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{unlink}}{unlink}}\label{unlink}

Delete one or more files from the filesystem. Returns the number of
files successfully removed. Does not remove directories --- use
\texttt{rmdir} for those. Dies on permission errors. Accepts a list of
paths, making it convenient for batch cleanup.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{unlink("temp.log")}
\NormalTok{my $n = unlink("a.tmp", "b.tmp", "c.tmp")}
\NormalTok{p "removed $n files"}
\NormalTok{my @old = glob("*.bak")}
\NormalTok{unlink(@old)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mkdir}}{mkdir}}\label{mkdir}

Create a directory at the given path. An optional second argument
specifies the permission mode as an octal number (default \texttt{0777},
modified by the current \texttt{umask}). Dies if the directory cannot be
created. Only creates one level --- use \texttt{make\_path} or shell out
for recursive creation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{mkdir("output")}
\NormalTok{mkdir("/tmp/secure", 0700)}
\NormalTok{my $dir = tdr() . "/sub"}
\NormalTok{mkdir($dir)}
\NormalTok{p {-}d $dir    \# 1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rmdir}}{rmdir}}\label{rmdir}

Remove an empty directory. Dies if the directory does not exist, is not
empty, or cannot be removed due to permissions. This only removes a
single directory --- it will not recursively delete contents. Remove
files with \texttt{unlink} first, then call \texttt{rmdir}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{mkdir("scratch")}
\NormalTok{rmdir("scratch")}
\NormalTok{p {-}d "scratch"   \# (empty, dir is gone)}
\NormalTok{unlink("tmp/file.txt")}
\NormalTok{rmdir("tmp")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{chmod}}{chmod}}\label{chmod}

Change the permission bits of one or more files. The mode is specified
as an octal number. Returns the number of files successfully changed.
Does not follow symlinks on some platforms. Use \texttt{stat} to read
the current mode before modifying.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{chmod(0755, "script.pl")}
\NormalTok{chmod(0644, "config.yaml", "data.json")}
\NormalTok{my $n = chmod(0600, glob("*.key"))}
\NormalTok{p "secured $n key files"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{chown}}{chown}}\label{chown}

Change the owner and group of one or more files, specified as numeric
UID and GID. Pass \texttt{-1} for either to leave it unchanged. Returns
the number of files successfully changed. Typically requires root
privileges.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{chown(1000, 1000, "app.log")}
\NormalTok{chown({-}1, 100, "shared.txt")     \# change group only}
\NormalTok{my $uid = (getpwnam("deploy"))[2]}
\NormalTok{chown($uid, {-}1, "release.tar")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{chdir}}{chdir}}\label{chdir}

Change the current working directory of the process. On success, also
refreshes the interpreter logical directory used for relative Stryke
builtins (same as after a successful \texttt{cd} to that path). Returns
\texttt{0}/\texttt{1} (does not throw). Pair with
\texttt{getcwd}/\texttt{pwd} to save and restore the original directory.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $orig = pwd()}
\NormalTok{chdir("/tmp")}
\NormalTok{spurt("scratch.txt", "hello")}
\NormalTok{chdir($orig)                  \# return to original}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stat}}{stat}}\label{stat}

Return a 13-element list of file status information for a path or
filehandle:
\texttt{(\$dev,\ \$ino,\ \$mode,\ \$nlink,\ \$uid,\ \$gid,\ \$rdev,\ \$size,\ \$atime,\ \$mtime,\ \$ctime,\ \$blksize,\ \$blocks)}.
This calls the POSIX \texttt{stat} system call. Use it to check file
size, modification time, permissions, and other metadata without reading
the file.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @st = stat("data.bin")}
\NormalTok{p "size: $st[7] bytes"}
\NormalTok{p "modified: " . datetime\_from\_epoch($st[9])}
\NormalTok{my ($mode) = (stat($0))[2]}
\NormalTok{p sprintf("perms: \%04o", $mode \& 07777)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{link}}{link}}\label{link}

Create a hard link --- a new directory entry pointing to the same
underlying inode as the original file. Both names are indistinguishable
and share the same data; removing one does not affect the other. Hard
links cannot cross filesystem boundaries and typically cannot link
directories.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{link("data.csv", "data\_backup.csv")}
\NormalTok{my @st1 = stat("data.csv")}
\NormalTok{my @st2 = stat("data\_backup.csv")}
\NormalTok{p $st1[1] == $st2[1]   \# 1 — same inode}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{symlink}}{symlink}}\label{symlink}

Create a symbolic (soft) link that points to a target path. Unlike hard
links, symlinks can cross filesystems and can point to directories. The
link stores the target as a string, so it can dangle if the target is
later removed. Use \texttt{readlink} to inspect where a symlink points.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{symlink("/usr/local/bin/stryke", "pe\_link")}
\NormalTok{p readlink("pe\_link")       \# /usr/local/bin/stryke}
\NormalTok{p {-}l "pe\_link"              \# 1 (is a symlink)}
\NormalTok{symlink("../lib", "lib\_link")  \# relative target}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{readlink}}{readlink}}\label{readlink}

Return the target path that a symbolic link points to, without following
the link further. Returns \texttt{undef} if the path is not a symlink.
This is useful for inspecting symlink chains, verifying link targets, or
resolving one level of indirection at a time.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{symlink("real.conf", "link.conf")}
\NormalTok{my $target = readlink("link.conf")}
\NormalTok{p $target                    \# real.conf}
\NormalTok{if (defined readlink($path)) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "$path is a symlink"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{utime}}{utime}}\label{utime}

Set the access time and modification time of one or more files. Times
are specified as epoch seconds. Pass \texttt{undef} for either time to
set it to the current time. Returns the number of files successfully
updated. Useful for cache invalidation, build systems, or preserving
timestamps after transformations.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{utime(time(), time(), "output.txt")     \# touch to now}
\NormalTok{my $epoch = 1700000000}
\NormalTok{utime($epoch, $epoch, @files)           \# backdate files}
\NormalTok{utime(undef, undef, "marker.flag")      \# equivalent of touch}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{umask}}{umask}}\label{umask}

Get or set the file creation mask, which controls the default
permissions for newly created files and directories. When called with an
argument, sets the new mask and returns the previous one. When called
without arguments, returns the current mask. The mask is subtracted from
the requested permissions in \texttt{mkdir}, \texttt{open}, etc.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $old = umask(0077)         \# restrict: owner{-}only}
\NormalTok{mkdir("private")               \# created with 0700}
\NormalTok{umask($old)                    \# restore previous mask}
\NormalTok{p sprintf("\%04o", umask())     \# print current mask}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{uname}}{uname}}\label{uname}

Return system identification as a five-element list:
\texttt{(\$sysname,\ \$nodename,\ \$release,\ \$version,\ \$machine)}.
This calls the POSIX \texttt{uname} system call and is useful for
platform-specific logic, logging system info, or generating diagnostic
reports without shelling out.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($sys, $node, $rel, $ver, $arch) = uname()}
\NormalTok{p "$sys $rel ($arch)"           \# Linux 6.1.0 (x86\_64)}
\NormalTok{if ($sys eq "Darwin") \{}
\NormalTok{    p "running on macOS"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{log\_info("host: $node, kernel: $rel")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gethostname}}{gethostname}}\label{gethostname}

Return the hostname of the current machine as a string. This calls the
POSIX \texttt{gethostname} system call. The short alias \texttt{hn} is
useful in log prefixes, temp-file naming, or distributed-system
identifiers where you need to tag output by machine.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gethostname()                          \# myhost.local}
\NormalTok{my $log\_prefix = hn() . ":" . $$        \# myhost.local:12345}
\NormalTok{log\_info("running on " . hn())}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{opendir}}{opendir}}\label{opendir}

Open a directory handle for reading its entries. Returns a directory
handle that can be passed to \texttt{readdir}, \texttt{seekdir},
\texttt{telldir}, \texttt{rewinddir}, and \texttt{closedir}. Dies if the
directory does not exist or cannot be opened. For most use cases
\texttt{glob} or \texttt{readdir} with a path is simpler.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{opendir(my $dh, "/tmp") or die "cannot open: $!"}
\NormalTok{my @entries = readdir($dh)}
\NormalTok{closedir($dh)}
\NormalTok{@entries |\textgreater{} grep \{ \_ !\textasciitilde{} /\^{}\textbackslash{}./ \} |\textgreater{} e p  \# skip dotfiles}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{readdir}}{readdir}}\label{readdir}

Read entries from a directory handle opened with \texttt{opendir}. In
list context, returns all remaining entries. In scalar context, returns
the next single entry or \texttt{undef} when exhausted. Entries include
\texttt{.} and \texttt{..} so you typically filter them out.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{opendir(my $dh, ".") or die $!}
\NormalTok{while (my $entry = readdir($dh)) \{}
\NormalTok{    next if $entry =\textasciitilde{} /\^{}\textbackslash{}./}
\NormalTok{    p $entry}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{closedir($dh)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{closedir}}{closedir}}\label{closedir}

Close a directory handle previously opened with \texttt{opendir},
releasing the underlying OS resource. While directory handles are closed
automatically when they go out of scope, explicit \texttt{closedir} is
good practice in long-running programs or loops that open many
directories.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{opendir(my $dh, "/var/log") or die $!}
\NormalTok{my @logs = readdir($dh)}
\NormalTok{closedir($dh)}
\NormalTok{@logs |\textgreater{} grep \{ /\textbackslash{}.log$/ \} |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{seekdir}}{seekdir}}\label{seekdir}

Set the current position in a directory handle to a location previously
obtained from \texttt{telldir}. This allows you to revisit directory
entries without closing and reopening the handle. Rarely needed in
practice, but useful for multi-pass directory scanning.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{opendir(my $dh, ".") or die $!}
\NormalTok{my $pos = telldir($dh)}
\NormalTok{my @first\_pass = readdir($dh)}
\NormalTok{seekdir($dh, $pos)              \# rewind to saved position}
\NormalTok{my @second\_pass = readdir($dh)}
\NormalTok{closedir($dh)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{telldir}}{telldir}}\label{telldir}

Return the current read position within a directory handle as an opaque
integer. The returned value can be passed to \texttt{seekdir} to return
to that position later. This is the directory-handle equivalent of
\texttt{tell} for file handles.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{opendir(my $dh, "/tmp") or die $!}
\NormalTok{readdir($dh)                  \# skip .}
\NormalTok{readdir($dh)                  \# skip ..}
\NormalTok{my $pos = telldir($dh)        \# save position after . and ..}
\NormalTok{my @real = readdir($dh)}
\NormalTok{seekdir($dh, $pos)            \# go back}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rewinddir}}{rewinddir}}\label{rewinddir}

Reset a directory handle back to the beginning so that the next
\texttt{readdir} returns the first entry again. This is equivalent to
closing and reopening the directory but more efficient. Useful when you
need to iterate a directory multiple times.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{opendir(my $dh, "src") or die $!}
\NormalTok{my $count = scalar readdir($dh)}
\NormalTok{rewinddir($dh)}
\NormalTok{my @entries = readdir($dh)}
\NormalTok{closedir($dh)}
\NormalTok{p "$count entries"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{du}}{du}}\label{du}

\texttt{du} (alias \texttt{dir\_size}) --- compute the total size of a
directory tree in bytes, recursively walking all files.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $bytes = du("/usr/local")}
\NormalTok{p "$bytes bytes"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{du\_tree}}{du\_tree}}\label{du_tree}

\texttt{du\_tree} (alias \texttt{dir\_sizes}) --- return directory sizes
as an array of hashrefs \texttt{\{path,\ size\}}, sorted descending by
size. Each entry is an immediate child directory.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @dirs = du\_tree("/usr/local")}
\NormalTok{for (@dirs) \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}\{path\}: \_{-}\textgreater{}\{size\}" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-process}
\chapter{Process}\label{process}

21 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{system}}{system}}\label{system}

Execute a command in a subshell and wait for it to complete, returning
the exit status. A return value of \texttt{0} means success; non-zero
indicates failure. The exit status is encoded as
\texttt{\$?\ \textgreater{}\textgreater{}\ 8} for the actual exit code.
Use backticks or \texttt{capture} if you need the
command\textquotesingle s output.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $rc = system("make", "test")}
\NormalTok{p "exit code: " . ($rc \textgreater{}\textgreater{} 8)}
\NormalTok{system("cp data.csv /backup/") == 0 or die "copy failed"}
\NormalTok{if (system("which rg \textgreater{}/dev/null 2\textgreater{}\&1") == 0) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "ripgrep is installed"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{exec}}{exec}}\label{exec}

Replace the current process image entirely with a new command. This
never returns on success --- the new program takes over. If
\texttt{exec} fails (command not found, permission denied) it returns
false and execution continues. Use \texttt{system} instead if you want
to run a command and keep the current process alive.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{exec("ls", "{-}la", "/tmp") or die "exec failed: $!"}
\NormalTok{\# code here only runs if exec fails}

\NormalTok{\# common fork+exec pattern}
\NormalTok{if (fork() == 0) \{}
\NormalTok{    exec("worker", "{-}{-}daemon")}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{fork}}{fork}}\label{fork}

Fork the current process, creating a child that is an exact copy of the
parent. Returns the child\textquotesingle s PID to the parent process
and \texttt{0} to the child, allowing each side to branch. Returns
\texttt{undef} on failure. Always pair with
\texttt{wait}/\texttt{waitpid} to reap the child and avoid zombies.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $pid = fork()}
\NormalTok{if ($pid == 0) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "child $$"}
\NormalTok{    exit(0)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{p "parent $$, child is $pid"}
\NormalTok{waitpid($pid, 0)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{wait}}{wait}}\label{wait}

Wait for any child process to terminate and return its PID. The exit
status is stored in \texttt{\$?}. If there are no child processes,
returns \texttt{-1}. This is the simplest reaping function --- use
\texttt{waitpid} when you need to wait for a specific child or use
non-blocking flags.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{for (1:3) \{}
\NormalTok{    fork() or do \{ sleep(1)}
\NormalTok{    exit(0) \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{while ((my $pid = wait()) != {-}1) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "child $pid exited with " . ($? \textgreater{}\textgreater{} 8)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{waitpid}}{waitpid}}\label{waitpid}

Wait for a specific child process identified by PID to change state. The
flags argument controls behavior --- use \texttt{0} for blocking wait,
or \texttt{WNOHANG} for non-blocking (returns \texttt{0} if child is
still running). Returns the PID on success, \texttt{-1} if the child
does not exist. Exit status is in \texttt{\$?}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $pid = fork() // die "fork: $!"}
\NormalTok{if ($pid == 0) \{ sleep(2)}
\NormalTok{    exit(42) \}}
\NormalTok{waitpid($pid, 0)}
\NormalTok{p "child exited: " . ($? \textgreater{}\textgreater{} 8)   \# 42}

\NormalTok{\# non{-}blocking poll}
\NormalTok{while (waitpid($pid, WNOHANG) == 0) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "still running..."}
\NormalTok{    sleep(1)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kill}}{kill}}\label{kill}

Send a signal to one or more processes by PID. The signal can be
specified as a number (\texttt{9}) or a name (\texttt{"TERM"}). Sending
signal \texttt{0} tests whether the process exists without actually
sending anything. Returns the number of processes successfully signaled.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{kill("TERM", $child\_pid)}
\NormalTok{kill(9, @worker\_pids)                \# SIGKILL}
\NormalTok{if (kill(0, $pid)) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "process $pid is alive"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{my $n = kill("HUP", @daemons)}
\NormalTok{p "signaled $n processes"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{exit}}{exit}}\label{exit}

Terminate the program immediately with the given exit status code. An
exit code of \texttt{0} conventionally means success; any non-zero value
indicates an error. \texttt{END} blocks and object destructors are still
run. Use \texttt{POSIX::\_exit} to skip cleanup entirely.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{exit(0)                 \# success}
\NormalTok{exit(1) if $error       \# failure}

\NormalTok{\# conditional exit in a pipeline}
\NormalTok{my $ok = system("make test")}
\NormalTok{exit($ok \textgreater{}\textgreater{} 8) if $ok}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getlogin}}{getlogin}}\label{getlogin}

Return the login name of the user who owns the current terminal session.
This reads from the system\textquotesingle s utmp/utmpx database and may
return \texttt{undef} for processes without a controlling terminal (cron
jobs, daemons). For a more reliable alternative, use
\texttt{getpwuid(\$\textless{})} which looks up the effective UID.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $user = getlogin() // (getpwuid($\textless{}))[0]}
\NormalTok{p "running as $user"}
\NormalTok{log\_info("session started by " . getlogin())}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getpwnam}}{getpwnam}}\label{getpwnam}

Look up user account information by username string. Returns the same
9-element list as \texttt{getpwuid}:
\texttt{(\$name,\ \$passwd,\ \$uid,\ \$gid,\ \$quota,\ \$comment,\ \$gcos,\ \$dir,\ \$shell)}.
Returns empty list if the user does not exist. Useful for resolving a
username to a UID before calling \texttt{chown}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @info = getpwnam("deploy")}
\NormalTok{p "uid=$info[2] home=$info[7]"}
\NormalTok{my $uid = (getpwnam("www{-}data"))[2]}
\NormalTok{chown($uid, {-}1, "public/index.html")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getpwuid}}{getpwuid}}\label{getpwuid}

Look up user account information by numeric UID. Returns
\texttt{(\$name,\ \$passwd,\ \$uid,\ \$gid,\ \$quota,\ \$comment,\ \$gcos,\ \$dir,\ \$shell)}
on success, or empty list if the UID does not exist. This is the
reliable way to map a UID to a username and home directory.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($name, undef, undef, undef, undef, undef, undef, $home) = getpwuid($\textless{})}
\NormalTok{p "user: $name, home: $home"}
\NormalTok{my $root\_shell = (getpwuid(0))[8]}
\NormalTok{p $root\_shell   \# /bin/bash or /bin/zsh}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getpwent}}{getpwent}}\label{getpwent}

Read the next entry from the system password database, iterating through
all user accounts. Returns
\texttt{(\$name,\ \$passwd,\ \$uid,\ \$gid,\ \$quota,\ \$comment,\ \$gcos,\ \$dir,\ \$shell)}
for each user, or empty list when exhausted. Call \texttt{setpwent} to
rewind and \texttt{endpwent} to close.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{while (my @pw = getpwent()) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "$pw[0]: uid=$pw[2] home=$pw[7]"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{endpwent()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getgrgid}}{getgrgid}}\label{getgrgid}

Look up group information by numeric GID. Returns
\texttt{(\$name,\ \$passwd,\ \$gid,\ \$members)} where members is a
space-separated string of usernames belonging to the group. Returns
empty list if the GID does not exist.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($name, undef, undef, $members) = getgrgid(0)}
\NormalTok{p "group $name: $members"}
\NormalTok{my $gname = (getgrgid((stat("file.txt"))[5]))[0]}
\NormalTok{p "file group: $gname"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getgrnam}}{getgrnam}}\label{getgrnam}

Look up group information by group name string. Returns
\texttt{(\$name,\ \$passwd,\ \$gid,\ \$members)}. Useful for resolving a
group name to a GID before calling \texttt{chown}, or for checking group
membership.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($name, undef, $gid, $members) = getgrnam("staff")}
\NormalTok{p "gid=$gid members=$members"}
\NormalTok{chown({-}1, $gid, "shared\_dir")}
\NormalTok{if ((getgrnam("admin"))[3] =\textasciitilde{} /\textbackslash{}b$user\textbackslash{}b/) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "$user is an admin"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getgrent}}{getgrent}}\label{getgrent}

Read the next entry from the system group database, iterating through
all groups. Returns \texttt{(\$name,\ \$passwd,\ \$gid,\ \$members)} for
each group, or empty list when exhausted. The members field is a
space-separated string of usernames. Call \texttt{endgrent} when done.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{while (my @gr = getgrent()) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "$gr[0]: gid=$gr[2] members=$gr[3]"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{endgrent()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getppid}}{getppid}}\label{getppid}

Return the process ID of the parent process. This is useful for
detecting whether the process has been orphaned (parent PID becomes 1 on
Unix when the original parent exits), or for logging the process
hierarchy. Always returns a valid PID.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p "my pid: $$, parent: " . getppid()}
\NormalTok{if (getppid() == 1) \{}
\NormalTok{    log\_warn("parent process has exited, we are orphaned")}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getpgrp}}{getpgrp}}\label{getpgrp}

Return the process group ID of the current process (or of the specified
PID). Processes in the same group receive signals together --- for
example, Ctrl-C sends SIGINT to the entire foreground process group. Use
\texttt{setpgrp} to move a process into a different group.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p "process group: " . getpgrp()}
\NormalTok{my $pg = getpgrp($$)}
\NormalTok{p $pg == $$ ? "group leader" : "group member"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{setpgrp}}{setpgrp}}\label{setpgrp}

Set the process group ID of a process. Call \texttt{setpgrp(0,\ 0)} to
make the current process a new group leader, which is useful for
daemonization or isolating a subprocess from the
terminal\textquotesingle s signal group. Takes \texttt{(PID,\ PGID)} ---
use \texttt{0} for the current process.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{setpgrp(0, 0)   \# become process group leader}
\NormalTok{if (fork() == 0) \{}
\NormalTok{    setpgrp(0, 0)  \# child gets its own group}
\NormalTok{    exec("worker")}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{getpriority}}{getpriority}}\label{getpriority}

Get the scheduling priority (nice value) of a process, process group, or
user. The \texttt{which} argument selects the target type:
\texttt{PRIO\_PROCESS}, \texttt{PRIO\_PGRP}, or \texttt{PRIO\_USER}.
Lower values mean higher priority. The default nice value is \texttt{0};
range is typically \texttt{-20} to \texttt{19}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $nice = getpriority(0, $$)    \# PRIO\_PROCESS, current PID}
\NormalTok{p "nice value: $nice"}
\NormalTok{my $user\_prio = getpriority(2, $\textless{})  \# PRIO\_USER, current user}
\NormalTok{p "user priority: $user\_prio"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{setpriority}}{setpriority}}\label{setpriority}

Set the scheduling priority (nice value) of a process, process group, or
user. Lowering the nice value (higher priority) typically requires root
privileges. Raising it (lower priority) is always allowed. Use this to
deprioritize background batch work or boost latency-sensitive tasks.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{setpriority(0, $$, 10)   \# lower priority for batch work}
\NormalTok{if (fork() == 0) \{}
\NormalTok{    setpriority(0, 0, 19)  \# lowest priority for child}
\NormalTok{    exec("batch{-}job")}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{syscall}}{syscall}}\label{syscall}

\texttt{syscall(NUM\ {[},\ ARG\ ...{]})} --- raw \texttt{syscall(2)}
entry point. Calls the kernel with the platform-specific syscall number
\texttt{NUM} and up to six arguments. Integer args pass as
\texttt{c\_long}; string args pass as pointer-to-bytes (so
\texttt{read}-style scratch buffers work). Returns the kernel return
value (or \texttt{-1} on error).

Syscall numbers are \textbf{not normalized across platforms} --- pass
the exact number for the host kernel (see
\texttt{\textless{}sys/syscall.h\textgreater{}} for the \texttt{SYS\_*}
constants). Wrong numbers can crash the process; this is an escape
hatch, not a portability layer. Use the wrapper builtins
(\texttt{getpid}, \texttt{kill}, \texttt{fork}, \texttt{system}, file
ops) for portable code.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# macOS aarch64: SYS\_getpid = 20}
\NormalTok{my $pid = syscall(20)}
\NormalTok{p $pid == $$ ? "matches \textbackslash{}$\textbackslash{}$" : "mismatch"}

\NormalTok{\# Linux x86\_64: SYS\_getpid = 39}
\NormalTok{\# my $pid = syscall(39)}

\NormalTok{\# Linux: SYS\_sync = 162 — flush filesystem caches.}
\NormalTok{\# syscall(162)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

On non-Unix platforms (Windows) \texttt{syscall} returns \texttt{-1} and
is otherwise inert.

See also: \texttt{getpid}, \texttt{getppid}, \texttt{kill},
\texttt{fork}, \texttt{system}, \texttt{\$!}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{process\_list}}{process\_list}}\label{process_list}

\texttt{process\_list} (aliases \texttt{ps}, \texttt{procs}) --- list
running processes as an array of hashrefs with
\texttt{\{pid,\ name,\ uid\}} keys.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @procs = ps()}
\NormalTok{for (@procs) \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}\{pid\} \_{-}\textgreater{}\{name\}" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-pack-binary}
\chapter{Pack / Binary}\label{pack-binary}

4 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pack}}{pack}}\label{pack}

Convert a list of values into a binary string according to a template.
Each template character specifies how one value is encoded: \texttt{N}
for 32-bit big-endian unsigned, \texttt{n} for 16-bit, \texttt{a} for
raw bytes, \texttt{Z} for null-terminated string, etc. This is essential
for constructing binary protocols, file formats, and \texttt{sockaddr}
structures.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $bin = pack("NnA4", 0xDEADBEEF, 8080, "test")}
\NormalTok{p length($bin)                \# 10 bytes}
\NormalTok{my $header = pack("A8 N N", "MAGIC01\textbackslash{}0", 1, 42)}
\NormalTok{spurt("data.bin", $header)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{unpack}}{unpack}}\label{unpack}

Decode a binary string into a list of values according to a template,
performing the inverse of \texttt{pack}. The template characters must
match how the data was packed. Use this for parsing binary file formats,
network protocol headers, or any structured binary data.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($magic, $version, $count) = unpack("A8 N N", $header)}
\NormalTok{p "v$version, $count records"}
\NormalTok{my ($port, $addr) = unpack("n a4", $sockaddr)}
\NormalTok{p inet\_ntoa($addr) . ":$port"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{unpack\_first}}{unpack\_first}}\label{unpack_first}

\texttt{unpack\_first(FMT,\ STR)} --- decode the binary string and
return the \textbf{first} decoded element (or \texttt{undef} if the
format yields nothing). The stryke spelling of Perl\textquotesingle s
\texttt{scalar\ unpack(FMT,\ STR)} for \texttt{-\/-no-interop} mode
where \texttt{scalar} is rejected at parse time. Aliases:
\texttt{unpack1}, \texttt{up1}. Equivalent to
\texttt{head(unpack(FMT,\ STR))} but spelled as a single verb.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $first\_byte = unpack\_first("C", $bytes)}
\NormalTok{my $port       = unpack1("n", $sockaddr)}
\NormalTok{my $magic\_word = up1("N", $header)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec}}{vec}}\label{vec}

Treat a string as a bit vector and get or set individual elements at a
specified bit width. The first argument is the string, the second is the
element offset, and the third is the bit width (1, 2, 4, 8, 16, or 32).
As an lvalue, \texttt{vec} modifies the string in place. Useful for
compact boolean arrays and bitmap manipulation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $bits = ""}
\NormalTok{vec($bits, 0, 1) = 1   \# set bit 0}
\NormalTok{vec($bits, 7, 1) = 1   \# set bit 7}
\NormalTok{p vec($bits, 0, 1)     \# 1}
\NormalTok{p vec($bits, 3, 1)     \# 0}
\NormalTok{p unpack("B8", $bits)  \# 10000001}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-logging}
\chapter{Logging}\label{logging}

7 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{log\_info}}{log\_info}}\label{log_info}

Log a message at INFO level to stderr with a timestamp prefix. INFO is
the default visible level and is appropriate for normal operational
messages --- startup notices, progress milestones, summary statistics.
Messages are suppressed if the current log level is set higher than
INFO.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{log\_info("server started on port $port")}
\NormalTok{my @rows = rl("data.csv")}
\NormalTok{log\_info("loaded " . scalar(@rows) . " rows")}
\NormalTok{1:5 |\textgreater{} e \{ log\_info("processing item $\_") \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{log\_warn}}{log\_warn}}\label{log_warn}

Log a message at WARN level to stderr. Warnings indicate unexpected but
recoverable situations --- missing optional config, deprecated usage,
slow operations. WARN messages appear at the default log level and are
visually distinct from INFO in structured log output.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{log\_warn("config file not found, using defaults")}
\NormalTok{log\_warn("query took $\{elapsed\}s, exceeds threshold")}
\NormalTok{unless ({-}e $path) \{}
\NormalTok{    log\_warn("$path missing, skipping")}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{log\_error}}{log\_error}}\label{log_error}

Log a message at ERROR level to stderr. Use this for failures that
prevent an operation from completing but do not necessarily terminate
the program --- failed network requests, invalid input, permission
errors. ERROR is always visible regardless of log level.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{log\_error("failed to connect to $host: $!")}
\NormalTok{eval \{ rj("bad.json") \}}
\NormalTok{log\_error("parse failed: $@") if $@}
\NormalTok{log\_error("missing required field \textquotesingle{}name\textquotesingle{}")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{log\_debug}}{log\_debug}}\label{log_debug}

Log a message at DEBUG level to stderr. Debug messages are hidden by
default and only appear when the log level is lowered to DEBUG or TRACE
via \texttt{log\_level}. Use for detailed internal state that helps
during development --- variable dumps, branch decisions, intermediate
values.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{log\_level("debug")}
\NormalTok{log\_debug("cache key: $key")}
\NormalTok{my $result = compute($x)}
\NormalTok{log\_debug("compute($x) =\textgreater{} $result")}
\NormalTok{@items |\textgreater{} e \{ log\_debug("item: $\_") \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{log\_trace}}{log\_trace}}\label{log_trace}

Log a message at TRACE level to stderr. This is the most verbose level,
producing very fine-grained output --- loop iterations, function
entry/exit, raw payloads. Only visible when \texttt{log\_level("trace")}
is set. Use sparingly in production code; primarily for deep debugging
sessions.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{log\_level("trace")}
\NormalTok{fn process($x) \{}
\NormalTok{    log\_trace("entering process($x)")}
\NormalTok{    my $r = $x * 2}
\NormalTok{    log\_trace("leaving process =\textgreater{} $r")}
\NormalTok{    $r}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{1:3 |\textgreater{} map process |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{log\_json}}{log\_json}}\label{log_json}

Emit a structured JSON log line to stderr containing the message plus
any additional key-value metadata. This is designed for
machine-parseable logging pipelines --- centralized log collectors,
JSON-based monitoring, or \texttt{jq}-friendly output. Each call emits
exactly one JSON object per line.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{log\_json("request", method =\textgreater{} "GET", path =\textgreater{} "/api")}
\NormalTok{log\_json("metric", name =\textgreater{} "latency\_ms", value =\textgreater{} 42)}
\NormalTok{log\_json("error", msg =\textgreater{} $@, file =\textgreater{} $0)$1}

\NormalTok{Note: all values are serialized as JSON strings.}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{log\_level}}{log\_level}}\label{log_level}

Get or set the current minimum log level. When called with no arguments,
returns the current level as a string. When called with a level name,
sets it for all subsequent log calls. Valid levels from most to least
verbose: \texttt{trace}, \texttt{debug}, \texttt{info}, \texttt{warn},
\texttt{error}. The default level is \texttt{info}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p log\_level()         \# info}
\NormalTok{log\_level("debug")    \# enable debug output}
\NormalTok{log\_debug("now visible")}
\NormalTok{log\_level("error")    \# suppress everything below error}
\NormalTok{log\_info("hidden")    \# not printed}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-charts-svg}
\chapter{Charts (SVG)}\label{charts-svg}

19 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{scatter\_svg}}{scatter\_svg}}\label{scatter_svg}

\texttt{scatter\_svg} (alias \texttt{scatter\_plot}) --- generate an SVG
scatter plot. Args: xs, ys {[}, title{]}. Dark theme, auto-scaled axes.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{scatter\_svg([1,2,3,4], [1,4,9,16], "Squares") |\textgreater{} to\_file("scatter.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{line\_svg}}{line\_svg}}\label{line_svg}

\texttt{line\_svg} (alias \texttt{line\_plot}) --- generate an SVG line
plot. Args: xs, ys {[}, title{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @x = map \{ \_ * 0.1 \} 0:100}
\NormalTok{my @y = map \{ sin(\_) \} @x}
\NormalTok{line\_svg(\textbackslash{}@x, \textbackslash{}@y, "Sine") |\textgreater{} to\_file("sine.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{plot\_svg}}{plot\_svg}}\label{plot_svg}

\texttt{plot\_svg} --- SVG line plot with auto X axis (0..n-1). Args: ys
{[}, title{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{plot\_svg([map \{ \_ ** 2 \} 0:50], "Parabola") |\textgreater{} to\_file("plot.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hist\_svg}}{hist\_svg}}\label{hist_svg}

\texttt{hist\_svg} (alias \texttt{histogram\_svg}) --- SVG histogram
with auto-binning (sqrt rule). Args: data {[}, bins, title{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{rnorm(1000) |\textgreater{} hist\_svg(30, "Normal") |\textgreater{} to\_file("hist.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{boxplot\_svg}}{boxplot\_svg}}\label{boxplot_svg}

\texttt{boxplot\_svg} (alias \texttt{box\_plot}) --- SVG box-and-whisker
plot with IQR whiskers and outlier detection. Args: groups {[},
title{]}. Groups is array of arrays.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{boxplot\_svg([[1,2,3,4,5], [3,4,5,6,20]], "Compare") |\textgreater{} to\_file("box.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{bar\_svg}}{bar\_svg}}\label{bar_svg}

\texttt{bar\_svg} (alias \texttt{barchart\_svg}) --- SVG bar chart with
labeled bars and value annotations. Args: labels, values {[}, title{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{bar\_svg(["Rust","Go","C++"], [45,30,25], "Languages") |\textgreater{} to\_file("bar.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pie\_svg}}{pie\_svg}}\label{pie_svg}

\texttt{pie\_svg} (alias \texttt{pie\_chart}) --- SVG pie chart with
percentage labels. Args: labels, values {[}, title{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{pie\_svg(["A","B","C"], [50,30,20]) |\textgreater{} to\_file("pie.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{heatmap\_svg}}{heatmap\_svg}}\label{heatmap_svg}

\texttt{heatmap\_svg} (alias \texttt{heatmap}) --- SVG heatmap with
blue-cyan-yellow-red colormap. Args: matrix {[}, title{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{heatmap\_svg(cor\_mat($data), "Correlation") |\textgreater{} to\_file("heat.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{donut\_svg}}{donut\_svg}}\label{donut_svg}

\texttt{donut\_svg} (alias \texttt{donut}) --- SVG donut chart with
percentage labels. Accepts labels+values or a hashref.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{donut\_svg(["A","B","C"], [50,30,20], "Share") |\textgreater{} to\_file("donut.svg")}
\NormalTok{\{Rust =\textgreater{} 45, Go =\textgreater{} 30\} |\textgreater{} donut("Languages") |\textgreater{} to\_file("donut.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{area\_svg}}{area\_svg}}\label{area_svg}

\texttt{area\_svg} (alias \texttt{area\_chart}) --- SVG filled area
chart. Args: xs, ys {[}, title{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @x = 0:20}
\NormalTok{my @y = map \{ sin(\_ * 0.3) \} @x}
\NormalTok{area\_svg(\textbackslash{}@x, \textbackslash{}@y, "Wave") |\textgreater{} to\_file("area.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hbar\_svg}}{hbar\_svg}}\label{hbar_svg}

\texttt{hbar\_svg} (alias \texttt{hbar}) --- SVG horizontal bar chart.
Accepts labels+values or a hashref.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{hbar\_svg(["Rust","Go","C++"], [45,30,25], "Speed") |\textgreater{} to\_file("hbar.svg")}
\NormalTok{\{alpha =\textgreater{} 10, beta =\textgreater{} 20\} |\textgreater{} hbar |\textgreater{} to\_file("hbar.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{radar\_svg}}{radar\_svg}}\label{radar_svg}

\texttt{radar\_svg} (aliases \texttt{radar}, \texttt{spider}) --- SVG
radar/spider chart. Args: labels, values {[}, title{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{radar\_svg(["Atk","Def","Spd","HP","MP"], [8,6,9,5,7], "Stats") |\textgreater{} to\_file("radar.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{candlestick\_svg}}{candlestick\_svg}}\label{candlestick_svg}

\texttt{candlestick\_svg} (aliases \texttt{candlestick}, \texttt{ohlc})
--- SVG candlestick OHLC chart. Args: array of {[}open,high,low,close{]}
{[}, title{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{candlestick\_svg([[100,110,95,105],[105,115,100,112]], "Stock") |\textgreater{} to\_file("ohlc.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{violin\_svg}}{violin\_svg}}\label{violin_svg}

\texttt{violin\_svg} (alias \texttt{violin}) --- SVG violin plot from
array of arrays showing distribution shape.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{violin\_svg([rnorm(200), rnorm(200, 2)], "Distributions") |\textgreater{} to\_file("violin.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cor\_heatmap}}{cor\_heatmap}}\label{cor_heatmap}

\texttt{cor\_heatmap} (alias \texttt{cor\_matrix\_svg}) --- SVG
correlation matrix heatmap. Computes pairwise correlations and renders
as heatmap.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{cor\_heatmap([rnorm(100), rnorm(100), rnorm(100)], "Correlations") |\textgreater{} to\_file("cor.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stacked\_bar\_svg}}{stacked\_bar\_svg}}\label{stacked_bar_svg}

\texttt{stacked\_bar\_svg} (alias \texttt{stacked\_bar}) --- SVG stacked
bar chart. Args: labels, series (array of arrays) {[}, title{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{stacked\_bar\_svg(["Q1","Q2","Q3"], [[10,20,30],[5,15,25]], "Revenue") |\textgreater{} to\_file("stacked.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{wordcloud\_svg}}{wordcloud\_svg}}\label{wordcloud_svg}

\texttt{wordcloud\_svg} (aliases \texttt{wordcloud}, \texttt{wcloud})
--- SVG word cloud from frequency hashref. Word size scales with
frequency.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\{rust =\textgreater{} 50, go =\textgreater{} 30, python =\textgreater{} 40\} |\textgreater{} wordcloud("Languages") |\textgreater{} to\_file("cloud.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{treemap\_svg}}{treemap\_svg}}\label{treemap_svg}

\texttt{treemap\_svg} (alias \texttt{treemap}) --- SVG treemap from
frequency hashref. Area proportional to values.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\{src =\textgreater{} 500, tests =\textgreater{} 200, docs =\textgreater{} 100\} |\textgreater{} treemap("Codebase") |\textgreater{} to\_file("tree.svg")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{preview}}{preview}}\label{preview}

\texttt{preview} (alias \texttt{pvw}) --- wrap SVG/HTML content in a
cyberpunk-styled page and open in the default browser.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{scatter\_svg([1,2,3], [1,4,9]) |\textgreater{} preview}
\NormalTok{bar\_svg(["A","B"], [10,20]) |\textgreater{} pvw}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-audio}
\chapter{Audio}\label{audio}

4 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{audio\_convert}}{audio\_convert}}\label{audio_convert}

\texttt{audio\_convert} (alias \texttt{aconv}) --- convert audio files
between WAV, FLAC, AIFF, and MP3 formats.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{audio\_convert("song.flac", "song.mp3")}
\NormalTok{aconv("input.wav", "output.mp3")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{audio\_info}}{audio\_info}}\label{audio_info}

\texttt{audio\_info} (alias \texttt{ainfo}) --- get audio file metadata
(duration, sample rate, channels, format) as a hashref.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $info = ainfo("song.mp3")}
\NormalTok{p $info{-}\textgreater{}\{duration\}      \# seconds}
\NormalTok{p $info{-}\textgreater{}\{sample\_rate\}   \# e.g. 44100}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{id3\_read}}{id3\_read}}\label{id3_read}

\texttt{id3\_read} (alias \texttt{id3}) --- read ID3 tags from an MP3
file as a hashref (title, artist, album, year, etc.).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $tags = id3("song.mp3")}
\NormalTok{p "$tags{-}\textgreater{}\{artist\} {-} $tags{-}\textgreater{}\{title\}"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{id3\_write}}{id3\_write}}\label{id3_write}

\texttt{id3\_write} (alias \texttt{id3w}) --- write ID3 tags to an MP3
file from a hashref.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{id3w("song.mp3", \{title =\textgreater{} "New Title", artist =\textgreater{} "Artist", year =\textgreater{} "2026"\})}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-network-utilities}
\chapter{Network Utilities}\label{network-utilities}

43 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kick}}{kick}}\label{kick}

\texttt{kick(\$host,\ \$port\ {[},\ \$timeout\_ms{]})} --- TCP liveness
probe. Returns \texttt{1} if a TCP connect to \texttt{\$host:\$port}
succeeds within the timeout, \texttt{0} otherwise. Default timeout
1000ms (matches \texttt{nc\ -zw1} convention).

Resolves \texttt{\$host} via the OS resolver --- accepts hostnames, IPv4
/ IPv6 literals, \texttt{localhost}. When DNS returns multiple
addresses, each is tried in turn until one succeeds OR all fail within
the timeout window.

Does not raise on failure --- wrong host, closed port, refused
connection, DNS failure, and timeout all return \texttt{0}, so callers
can write boolean-style code:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{if (kick("db.prod", 5432, 500)) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "postgres is up"}
\NormalTok{\} else \{}
\NormalTok{    warn "postgres unreachable"}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{\# Quick service{-}mesh health sweep:}
\NormalTok{for my $pair ((["web", 80], ["api", 8080], ["db", 5432])) \{}
\NormalTok{    printf "  \%{-}6s :\%d  \%s\textbackslash{}n", $pair{-}\textgreater{}[0], $pair{-}\textgreater{}[1],}
\NormalTok{        kick($pair{-}\textgreater{}[0], $pair{-}\textgreater{}[1]) ? "up" : "down"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

This is a \textbf{convenience builtin} --- the underlying capability
(TCP connect with timeout) is in every language\textquotesingle s
stdlib. Stryke ships it as a bare builtin so script-level service probes
don\textquotesingle t need a socket import.

See also: \texttt{punch} (UDP send), \texttt{net\_port\_open} (older
alias-style probe).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tcp\_probe}}{tcp\_probe}}\label{tcp_probe}

\texttt{tcp\_probe(\$host,\ \$port\ {[},\ \$timeout\_ms=1000{]})} ---
TCP connect probe returning RTT, not just bool. Returns
\texttt{\{\ alive,\ latency\_ms\ \}}. Use \texttt{kick} for fast 1/0
sweeps, \texttt{tcp\_probe} when you want latency, \texttt{tcp\_banner}
for service fingerprinting.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $r = tcp\_probe("db.prod", 5432, 500)}
\NormalTok{printf "postgres up — \%dms\textbackslash{}n", $r{-}\textgreater{}\{latency\_ms\} if $r{-}\textgreater{}\{alive\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{kick}, \texttt{tcp\_banner}, \texttt{whois\_query}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tcp\_banner}}{tcp\_banner}}\label{tcp_banner}

\texttt{tcp\_banner(\$host,\ \$port\ {[},\ \$timeout\_ms=1000,\ \$max\_bytes=512{]})}
--- connect + read first \texttt{\$max\_bytes} bytes the server sends.
Classic service-fingerprint pattern.

Returns \texttt{\{\ alive,\ latency\_ms,\ banner\ \}}. Servers that
don\textquotesingle t greet (HTTP) return
\texttt{banner\ =\textgreater{}\ ""} with \texttt{alive=1}. Read timeout
hardcoded to 200ms after connect.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $r = tcp\_banner("web1", 22)}
\NormalTok{printf "SSH \%s\textbackslash{}n", $1 if $r{-}\textgreater{}\{alive\} \&\& $r{-}\textgreater{}\{banner\} =\textasciitilde{} /\^{}SSH{-}(\textbackslash{}S+)/}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{tcp\_probe}, \texttt{kick}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{whois\_query}}{whois\_query}}\label{whois_query}

\texttt{whois\_query(\$domain\ {[},\ \$server="whois.iana.org",\ \$timeout\_ms=5000{]})}
--- RFC 3912 WHOIS query. Connects \texttt{\$server:43}, sends
\texttt{\$domain\textbackslash{}r\textbackslash{}n}, reads response
until close. Returns string, \texttt{undef} on failure.

Default server is IANA\textquotesingle s referral --- chase the
\texttt{Refer:} line for definitive registry results. 256 KB response
cap.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $iana = whois\_query("example.com")}
\NormalTok{my ($registry) = $iana =\textasciitilde{} /\^{}refer:\textbackslash{}s+(\textbackslash{}S+)/m}
\NormalTok{my $detail = whois\_query("example.com", $registry // "whois.verisign{-}grs.com")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Common registries: \texttt{.com}/\texttt{.net} →
\texttt{whois.verisign-grs.com}, \texttt{.io} → \texttt{whois.nic.io},
IP/ASN → \texttt{whois.arin.net} / \texttt{whois.ripe.net} /
\texttt{whois.apnic.net}.

See also: \texttt{tcp\_probe}, \texttt{tcp\_banner}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{udp\_send}}{udp\_send}}\label{udp_send}

\texttt{udp\_send(\$host,\ \$port,\ \$payload\ {[},\ \$retries=1,\ \$interval\_ms=20{]})}
--- send \texttt{\$payload} as a UDP datagram to \texttt{\$host:\$port}.
Returns the number of datagrams successfully sent (0 = total failure,
\texttt{\$retries} = full success).

\texttt{\$payload} can be a string (sent as UTF-8 bytes) or a byte
buffer (sent as-is). When \texttt{\$retries\ \textgreater{}\ 1}, the
same datagram is sent that many times at \texttt{\$interval\_ms} apart
--- useful for Wake-on-LAN magic packets (commonly sent 3× for
reliability), NAT keepalives, and best-effort IoT discovery broadcasts.
Broadcast addresses (\texttt{255.255.255.255}, subnet broadcasts) are
enabled automatically.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# Wake{-}on{-}LAN: 6× 0xFF + target MAC × 16 = 102{-}byte magic packet on UDP/9.}
\NormalTok{my $mac = "aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff"}
\NormalTok{my @bytes = (0xff) x 6}
\NormalTok{push @bytes, (map \{ hex $\_ \} split /:/, $mac) for 1:16}
\NormalTok{my $packet = pack("C*", @bytes)}
\NormalTok{udp\_send("255.255.255.255", 9, $packet, 3)  \# send 3× for reliability}

\NormalTok{\# NAT keepalive.}
\NormalTok{udp\_send("203.0.113.45", 51820, "", 5, 100)  \# 5 shots × 100ms apart}

\NormalTok{\# IoT discovery (SSDP).}
\NormalTok{udp\_send("239.255.255.250", 1900, $ssdp\_query)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

For peer-to-peer NAT traversal where both endpoints are behind NAT and
need STUN discovery + bidirectional flow establishment, see
\texttt{punch} (forthcoming).

See also: \texttt{kick} (TCP probe).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{udp\_open}}{udp\_open}}\label{udp_open}

\texttt{udp\_open({[}\$bind\_host="0.0.0.0",\ \$bind\_port=0{]})} ---
bind a UDP socket and return an opaque integer handle (0 on bind
failure). The socket lives in a process-global pool until
\texttt{udp\_close(\$id)}.

\texttt{\$bind\_port\ =\ 0} lets the kernel assign an ephemeral port ---
the normal case. Passing a fixed port is useful when you want a stable
inbound address.

Returned handle plugs into the persistent-socket family:
\texttt{udp\_send\_to}, \texttt{udp\_recv}, \texttt{udp\_close},
\texttt{stun}, \texttt{punch}. The same handle MUST be used across a
STUN-discover → punch → peer-to-peer-traffic flow because the NAT
mapping the STUN response reveals is tied to that
socket\textquotesingle s \texttt{(local\_ip,\ local\_port)} --- using a
different socket invalidates the mapping.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $sock = udp\_open()                          \# ephemeral inbound}
\NormalTok{my $info = stun($sock)                          \# discover public ip:port}
\NormalTok{udp\_send\_to($sock, $peer\_ip, $peer\_port, "hi")  \# send via same socket}
\NormalTok{my $reply = udp\_recv($sock, 1000)               \# receive on same socket}
\NormalTok{udp\_close($sock)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{udp\_send} (fire-and-forget one-shot), \texttt{stun},
\texttt{punch}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{udp\_send\_to}}{udp\_send\_to}}\label{udp_send_to}

\texttt{udp\_send\_to(\$id,\ \$host,\ \$port,\ \$payload)} --- send
\texttt{\$payload} to \texttt{\$host:\$port} via the pool socket
\texttt{\$id}. Returns bytes sent on success, \texttt{0} on any failure
(unknown id, DNS failure, send error).

\texttt{\$payload} is sent verbatim --- pass strings as UTF-8 or byte
buffers as-is. For broadcast (\texttt{255.255.255.255}, subnet
broadcasts) the socket\textquotesingle s SO\_BROADCAST is already
enabled by \texttt{udp\_open}.

This is the \textbf{persistent-socket} counterpart to \texttt{udp\_send}
--- use this when you need to send from a specific local port (e.g., one
whose NAT mapping was discovered via STUN). Use \texttt{udp\_send} for
fire-and-forget sends where the local port doesn\textquotesingle t
matter.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $sock = udp\_open("0.0.0.0", 51820)}
\NormalTok{udp\_send\_to($sock, "203.0.113.45", 51820, $wg\_keepalive\_pkt)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{udp\_open}, \texttt{udp\_recv}, \texttt{udp\_send}
(one-shot variant).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{udp\_recv}}{udp\_recv}}\label{udp_recv}

\texttt{udp\_recv(\$id\ {[},\ \$timeout\_ms=1000{]})} --- read one
datagram from socket \texttt{\$id}. Returns the payload as a UTF-8
string if valid, otherwise as raw bytes the caller can \texttt{unpack}.
Returns \texttt{undef} on timeout, unknown id, or recv error.

Default timeout is 1000 ms. Pass \texttt{0} to block indefinitely
(useful for daemon-style listeners). The blocked thread is the calling
stryke task; for concurrent recv loops, wrap in
\texttt{spawn\ \{\ ...\ \}}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $sock = udp\_open("0.0.0.0", 5683)        \# CoAP port for IoT inbound}
\NormalTok{while (defined(my $payload = udp\_recv($sock, 0))) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "received " . length($payload) . " bytes"}
\NormalTok{    handle($payload)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{udp\_open}, \texttt{udp\_send\_to}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{udp\_recv\_from}}{udp\_recv\_from}}\label{udp_recv_from}

\texttt{udp\_recv\_from(\$id\ {[},\ \$timeout\_ms=1000{]})} --- read one
datagram from socket \texttt{\$id} and surface the source address
alongside the payload. Returns a hashref:

\begin{verbatim}
{ payload  => "hello",          # string if UTF-8 valid, else bytes
  src_ip   => "203.0.113.45",
  src_port => 51234 }
\end{verbatim}

Or \texttt{undef} on timeout / unknown id / recv error. Default timeout
1000 ms; \texttt{0} blocks indefinitely.

\texttt{src\_ip} / \texttt{src\_port} are exactly what the kernel
reported on \texttt{recvfrom(2)} --- for a peer behind NAT this is the
peer\textquotesingle s PUBLIC address as seen from this side, not their
private LAN address. That\textquotesingle s the address you want to pair
with \texttt{udp\_send\_to(\$id,\ \$src\_ip,\ \$src\_port,\ \$reply)} to
answer back through the same NAT pinhole the peer punched open.

This is the bidirectional-chat counterpart to \texttt{udp\_recv} --- use
this when you need to reply to whoever sent the datagram. Use
\texttt{udp\_recv} when you just want the payload and
don\textquotesingle t care about the source.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $sock = udp\_open("0.0.0.0", 5683)}
\NormalTok{while (defined(my $msg = udp\_recv\_from($sock, 0))) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "from $msg{-}\textgreater{}\{src\_ip\}:$msg{-}\textgreater{}\{src\_port\}: $msg{-}\textgreater{}\{payload\}"}
\NormalTok{    udp\_send\_to($sock, $msg{-}\textgreater{}\{src\_ip\}, $msg{-}\textgreater{}\{src\_port\}, "ack")}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{udp\_recv} (payload-only variant), \texttt{udp\_open},
\texttt{udp\_send\_to}, \texttt{punch}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{udp\_close}}{udp\_close}}\label{udp_close}

\texttt{udp\_close(\$id)} --- release the socket handle \texttt{\$id}
from the pool. Returns \texttt{1} if the handle was present, \texttt{0}
if unknown. Idempotent --- calling on an already-closed handle is safe.

Long-running scripts should close sockets they no longer need to bound
the pool size and free the underlying file descriptor. Process exit also
releases all sockets, so short-lived scripts can skip the close.

See also: \texttt{udp\_open}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stun}}{stun}}\label{stun}

\texttt{stun(\$id\ {[},\ \$stun\_host="stun.l.google.com",\ \$stun\_port=19302,\ \$timeout\_ms=2000{]})}
--- query a STUN server via socket \texttt{\$id} and return the public
\texttt{(ip,\ port)} the server reports observing the request from.
Returns:

\begin{verbatim}
{ public_ip => "203.0.113.45", public_port => 51234 }
\end{verbatim}

Or \texttt{undef} on timeout / parse failure / unknown id. The STUN
protocol (RFC 8489) is implemented in-tree --- no external crate
dependency.

\textbf{Critical}: subsequent \texttt{punch(\$id,\ ...)} and
\texttt{udp\_send\_to(\$id,\ ...)} calls MUST use the same socket
\texttt{\$id}. The NAT mapping the STUN response reveals is tied to that
socket\textquotesingle s local \texttt{(ip,\ port)}; using a different
socket gets you a different public port (or none at all) and the punch
will fail.

Default STUN server is \texttt{stun.l.google.com:19302} ---
Google\textquotesingle s public, free, and reliable. Other public
options: \texttt{stun.cloudflare.com:3478},
\texttt{stun.nextcloud.com:443}. Hosted internally for privacy-sensitive
deployments.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $sock = udp\_open()}
\NormalTok{if (defined(my $info = stun($sock))) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "I\textquotesingle{}m at $info{-}\textgreater{}\{public\_ip\}:$info{-}\textgreater{}\{public\_port\}"}
\NormalTok{    \# Now tell the peer this address via signaling (out{-}of{-}band — email,}
\NormalTok{    \# paste, shared file, IRC, anything that gets bytes from A to B).}
\NormalTok{    \# The peer does the same, then both call punch() pointing at each}
\NormalTok{    \# other\textquotesingle{}s discovered ip:port.}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{punch}, \texttt{udp\_open}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stun\_classify}}{stun\_classify}}\label{stun_classify}

\texttt{stun\_classify(\$id\ {[},\ \$opts{]})} --- query multiple STUN
servers via the SAME socket and classify the local NAT based on whether
the reported public ports match across servers. Returns a hashref:

\begin{verbatim}
{ nat_type     => "cone" | "symmetric" | "unknown",
  public_ip    => "203.0.113.45" | undef,
  queried      => 3,
  succeeded    => 3,
  observations => [
    { server => "stun.l.google.com:19302", ok => 1,
      public_ip => "203.0.113.45", public_port => 51234 },
    { server => "stun.cloudflare.com:3478", ok => 1,
      public_ip => "203.0.113.45", public_port => 51234 },
    ... ] }
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{\texttt{nat\_type} interpretation}:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \texttt{"cone"} --- every responding server reported the SAME port →
  NAT uses one public mapping per source socket regardless of
  destination. \texttt{punch} will work to any peer.
\item
  \texttt{"symmetric"} --- servers reported DIFFERENT ports → NAT
  allocates a fresh public port per destination. \texttt{punch} will
  FAIL because the port the peer punches at (learned from STUN server X)
  won\textquotesingle t be the port your traffic to the peer uses. The
  only recovery is a TURN relay (not yet shipped).
\item
  \texttt{"unknown"} --- fewer than 2 servers responded →
  can\textquotesingle t classify.
\end{itemize}

\texttt{\$opts} (hashref, all optional):

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \texttt{servers} (arrayref of \texttt{{[}"host",\ port{]}} pairs) ---
  defaults to a diverse set: Google, Cloudflare, Nextcloud STUN.
  Diversity matters --- some symmetric NATs bucket by destination /24,
  so same-provider servers can falsely produce matching ports. Three
  different ASNs catches more cases.
\item
  \texttt{timeout\_ms} (default 2000) --- per-server timeout.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Recommended pattern} --- classify BEFORE calling \texttt{punch},
bail early on symmetric to give the user a useful error rather than a
30s \texttt{punch} timeout:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $sock = udp\_open()}
\NormalTok{my $nat = stun\_classify($sock)}
\NormalTok{printf "NAT type: \%s (succeeded \%d/\%d)\textbackslash{}n",}
\NormalTok{    $nat{-}\textgreater{}\{nat\_type\}, $nat{-}\textgreater{}\{succeeded\}, $nat{-}\textgreater{}\{queried\}}
\NormalTok{if ($nat{-}\textgreater{}\{nat\_type\} eq "symmetric") \{}
\NormalTok{    die "behind symmetric NAT — hole{-}punching impossible without TURN relay"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{if ($nat{-}\textgreater{}\{nat\_type\} eq "unknown") \{}
\NormalTok{    warn "only $nat{-}\textgreater{}\{succeeded\} STUN servers reached — proceeding cautiously"}
\NormalTok{\}}
