\NormalTok{\# ... continue with stun() + signaling + punch() ...}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{stun} (single-server query), \texttt{punch}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{punch}}{punch}}\label{punch}

\texttt{punch(\$id,\ \$peer\_ip,\ \$peer\_port\ {[},\ \$opts{]})} ---
UDP NAT hole-punching state machine. Bombards
\texttt{\$peer\_ip:\$peer\_port} via socket \texttt{\$id} at
\texttt{\$opts\{interval\_ms\}} apart, listens for any inbound datagram,
returns when bidirectional flow is established OR the timeout fires.

\texttt{\$opts} (hashref, all keys optional):

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \texttt{timeout\_ms} (default 5000) --- wall-clock punch budget
\item
  \texttt{interval\_ms} (default 50) --- gap between bombards
\item
  \texttt{payload} (default \texttt{"stryke-punch"}) --- probe payload
  sent each round
\end{itemize}

Returns:

\begin{verbatim}
{ established => 1,         # 0 on timeout
  latency_ms  => 380,       # time to first reply
  bombards    => 8,         # count of datagrams we sent
  peer_msg    => "hi from b",  # first received payload (or undef)
  peer_addr   => "203.0.113.45:51234" }   # source addr (or undef)
\end{verbatim}

\textbf{The full P2P flow}: both peers run \texttt{udp\_open} →
\texttt{stun} → exchange their discovered \texttt{(ip,\ port)} via an
out-of-band signaling channel (email, paste, shared file, anything) →
simultaneously call \texttt{punch} pointing at each
other\textquotesingle s discovered address. The first few bombards from
each side are dropped by the other\textquotesingle s NAT (no inbound
mapping yet); the act of sending establishes the mapping, and subsequent
packets get through.

\textbf{Real-world success rate is \textasciitilde70-80\%}. Failures
(with no language-level workaround short of allocating a TURN relay):

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Symmetric NATs --- some mobile carriers, some corporate firewalls ---
  assign a different public port per destination, so the port revealed
  by STUN ≠ the port the peer\textquotesingle s traffic actually arrives
  on
\item
  UDP-blocking firewalls --- drop all UDP outright
\item
  Misaligned timing --- if one peer punches 30s before the other, the
  first peer\textquotesingle s NAT mapping may time out before the
  second peer starts
\end{itemize}

For a guaranteed-delivery fallback you need TURN (relay every packet
through a public server) --- out of scope for stryke\textquotesingle s
builtin set; if you need it, run a coturn server and use
\texttt{udp\_send\_to} to relay manually.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# Side A:}
\NormalTok{my $sock = udp\_open()}
\NormalTok{my $me = stun($sock)}
\NormalTok{printf "Tell peer to: stryke peer.stk \%s \%d\textbackslash{}n",}
\NormalTok{    $me{-}\textgreater{}\{public\_ip\}, $me{-}\textgreater{}\{public\_port\}}
\NormalTok{\# ... peer signals back their (ip, port) via email/paste ...}
\NormalTok{my $peer\_ip   = "203.0.113.45"}
\NormalTok{my $peer\_port = 51234}
\NormalTok{my $r = punch($sock, $peer\_ip, $peer\_port, \{ timeout\_ms =\textgreater{} 10\_000 \})}
\NormalTok{if ($r{-}\textgreater{}\{established\}) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "connected after $r{-}\textgreater{}\{bombards\} bombards (" . $r{-}\textgreater{}\{latency\_ms\} . "ms)"}
\NormalTok{    p "first message from peer: $r{-}\textgreater{}\{peer\_msg\}"}
\NormalTok{    udp\_send\_to($sock, $peer\_ip, $peer\_port, "reply from A")}
\NormalTok{    p "reply: " . udp\_recv($sock, 5000)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{udp\_close($sock)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{stun}, \texttt{udp\_open}, \texttt{udp\_send\_to},
\texttt{udp\_recv}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{turn\_allocate}}{turn\_allocate}}\label{turn_allocate}

\texttt{turn\_allocate(\$id,\ \$server,\ \$port,\ \$username,\ \$password\ {[},\ \$timeout\_ms=3000{]})}
--- drive the TURN (RFC 8656) two-roundtrip allocation flow on socket
\texttt{\$id}. Returns a hashref on success, \texttt{undef} on auth /
network / parse failure.

\begin{verbatim}
{ relay_ip      => "203.0.113.99",  # the public address the TURN server allocated to us
  relay_port    => 49999,
  lifetime_secs => 600,               # how long the allocation lives before needing refresh
  realm         => "turn.example",    # server-supplied auth realm
  nonce_len     => 13 }               # length of opaque nonce stored in pool
\end{verbatim}

The allocation is registered in a process-global pool keyed by
\texttt{\$id} so subsequent \texttt{turn\_permission},
\texttt{turn\_send}, \texttt{turn\_recv}, \texttt{turn\_refresh} all
find it via the same socket id. \textbf{The socket must be the one you
intend to use for the full TURN session} --- TURN\textquotesingle s NAT
mapping is tied to the source (ip, port) of the original Allocate
request.

Protocol: client sends Allocate (no auth) → server responds 401 with
REALM + NONCE → client retries with USERNAME + REALM + NONCE +
MESSAGE-INTEGRITY (HMAC-SHA1 of MD5(user:realm:pass) over the message
bytes) → server replies success with XOR-RELAYED-ADDRESS.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $sock = udp\_open()}
\NormalTok{my $alloc = turn\_allocate($sock, "turn.example.com", 3478, "alice", "hunter2")}
\NormalTok{if (!defined $alloc) \{ die "TURN allocation failed" \}}
\NormalTok{printf "Relay address: \%s:\%d (lifetime \%ds)\textbackslash{}n",}
\NormalTok{    $alloc{-}\textgreater{}\{relay\_ip\}, $alloc{-}\textgreater{}\{relay\_port\}, $alloc{-}\textgreater{}\{lifetime\_secs\}}
\NormalTok{\# Share $alloc{-}\textgreater{}\{relay\_ip\}:$alloc{-}\textgreater{}\{relay\_port\} with the peer via signaling.}
\NormalTok{\# They install a permission for our relay address and send to it; the TURN}
\NormalTok{\# server forwards their packets to us as DATA{-}INDICATION wrappers.}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{turn\_permission}, \texttt{turn\_send},
\texttt{turn\_recv}, \texttt{turn\_refresh}, \texttt{punch} (direct
hole-punching, faster when it works), \texttt{stun\_classify} (detect
when TURN is necessary).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{turn\_permission}}{turn\_permission}}\label{turn_permission}

\texttt{turn\_permission(\$id,\ \$peer\_ip\ {[},\ \$timeout\_ms=2000{]})}
--- install a TURN CreatePermission for \texttt{\$peer\_ip} on the
allocation associated with socket \texttt{\$id}. Required before the
TURN server will accept SEND-INDICATIONs targeting that
peer\textquotesingle s address (per RFC 8656 §9 --- the server uses
permissions to prevent generic relay abuse).

Returns \texttt{1} on CreatePermission\_Success, \texttt{0} on failure
(no allocation, network error, server rejection).

Permissions last \textasciitilde5 minutes by default; call
\texttt{turn\_permission} again to refresh. Permission is keyed by peer
IP only --- any port from that IP is accepted, but a different IP
requires a separate permission.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $sock = udp\_open()}
\NormalTok{my $alloc = turn\_allocate($sock, $turn\_host, 3478, $user, $pass) or die}
\NormalTok{if (!turn\_permission($sock, "203.0.113.45")) \{}
\NormalTok{    die "could not install permission for peer"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{turn\_send($sock, "203.0.113.45", 51820, "hello via relay")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{turn\_allocate}, \texttt{turn\_send},
\texttt{turn\_recv}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{turn\_send}}{turn\_send}}\label{turn_send}

\texttt{turn\_send(\$id,\ \$peer\_ip,\ \$peer\_port,\ \$payload)} ---
wrap \texttt{\$payload} in a TURN SEND-INDICATION and ship via the
allocation associated with socket \texttt{\$id}. The TURN server
forwards the payload to \texttt{\$peer\_ip:\$peer\_port} from the relay
address.

Returns bytes written to the TURN server (NOT bytes confirmed at the
peer --- peer receipt is best-effort UDP; confirmation comes only when a
DATA-INDICATION reply arrives, surfaced via \texttt{turn\_recv}).

A permission for \texttt{\$peer\_ip} MUST be installed via
\texttt{turn\_permission} first, or the server silently drops the send.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{turn\_send($sock, $peer\_ip, $peer\_port, "control message")}
\NormalTok{turn\_send($sock, $peer\_ip, $peer\_port, pack("V", $frame\_number))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{turn\_allocate}, \texttt{turn\_permission},
\texttt{turn\_recv}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{turn\_recv}}{turn\_recv}}\label{turn_recv}

\texttt{turn\_recv(\$id\ {[},\ \$timeout\_ms=1000{]})} --- wait for the
next DATA-INDICATION on the TURN allocation\textquotesingle s socket.
Returns:

\begin{verbatim}
{ payload  => "reply from peer",   # string if UTF-8 valid, else bytes
  peer_ip  => "203.0.113.45",      # the peer's address as the TURN server saw it
  peer_port => 51820 }
\end{verbatim}

Or \texttt{undef} on timeout, if the next packet isn\textquotesingle t a
DATA-INDICATION (e.g. a late CreatePermission\_Success), or if no
allocation exists for \texttt{\$id}. Caller should call again to drain
non-data frames.

Default timeout 1000 ms; \texttt{0} blocks indefinitely.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{while (defined(my $msg = turn\_recv($sock, 0))) \{}
\NormalTok{    printf "from \%s:\%d: \%s\textbackslash{}n", $msg{-}\textgreater{}\{peer\_ip\}, $msg{-}\textgreater{}\{peer\_port\}, $msg{-}\textgreater{}\{payload\}}
\NormalTok{    turn\_send($sock, $msg{-}\textgreater{}\{peer\_ip\}, $msg{-}\textgreater{}\{peer\_port\}, "ack")}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{turn\_allocate}, \texttt{turn\_send},
\texttt{udp\_recv\_from} (direct-UDP counterpart).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{turn\_refresh}}{turn\_refresh}}\label{turn_refresh}

\texttt{turn\_refresh(\$id\ {[},\ \$lifetime\_secs=600,\ \$timeout\_ms=2000{]})}
--- send a Refresh request to extend the TURN
allocation\textquotesingle s lifetime. Pass \texttt{\$lifetime\_secs=0}
to release the allocation immediately.

Returns the new lifetime on success, \texttt{0} on failure. Call before
the previous \texttt{lifetime\_secs} elapses --- typical cadence is
refresh at \textasciitilde⅔ of the current lifetime so a single network
hiccup doesn\textquotesingle t drop the allocation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# Background keepalive: refresh every 5 minutes (allocation has 10{-}min lifetime).}
\NormalTok{spawn \{}
\NormalTok{    while (1) \{}
\NormalTok{        sleep 300}
\NormalTok{        last unless turn\_refresh($sock)}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Releasing on shutdown is good citizenship --- it frees the TURN
server\textquotesingle s relay port immediately instead of waiting for
the timeout:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{turn\_refresh($sock, 0)}
\NormalTok{udp\_close($sock)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{turn\_allocate}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{teleport}}{teleport}}\label{teleport}

\texttt{teleport(\$val,\ @receiver\_pids\ {[},\ \{\ hold\_ms\ =\textgreater{}\ 500\ \}{]})}
--- broadcast a value to N stryke processes via POSIX shared memory +
per-receiver UDS notification. Returns the count of receivers whose
notification landed (their UDS socket exists + accepted the notify).

Data-first signature so the prefix thread macro composes naturally:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{} $huge teleport(@worker\_pids)     \# prefix \textasciitilde{}\textgreater{}: threads $huge as arg 0}
\NormalTok{teleport($payload, $pid1, $pid2)    \# standalone, literal PIDs}
\NormalTok{teleport($payload, [@pids])         \# arrayref also flattens}
\NormalTok{teleport($payload, $pid, \{ hold\_ms =\textgreater{} 1500 \})   \# opts hash for hold window}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Receivers must be stryke processes running an \texttt{arrive()} loop.
Non-stryke processes (or stryke processes without an \texttt{arrive}
call) have no bound UDS socket and count as unreachable.

\texttt{hold\_ms} (default 500) is how long the sender holds the SHM
segment alive after notifying so receivers can \texttt{shm\_open} +
\texttt{mmap} + read. Receivers slower than \texttt{hold\_ms} miss the
window.

\textbf{Wire path}:
\texttt{StrykeValue\ →\ serde\_json::Value\ →\ JSON\ bytes\ →\ SHM\ segment\ named\ }/stryke\_tp\_PID\_SEQ\texttt{\ →\ 40-byte\ notify\ over\ UDS\ to\ }/tmp/stryke\_teleport\_PID.sock\texttt{\ per\ receiver}.
Receivers reverse this. Same lossy-but-portable shape as
\texttt{cluster} / \texttt{dist\_thread} IPC --- closures and blessed
objects don\textquotesingle t round-trip; scalars, arrays, hashes, and
nested combos do. JSON beats bincode here because bincode
can\textquotesingle t deserialize a free-standing serde\_json::Value
(Value needs \texttt{deserialize\_any}).

\textbf{Top-level convention}: arrays / hashes always arrive as refs
(\texttt{@a} → arrayref, \texttt{\%h} → hashref), matching
\texttt{decode\_json}. So \texttt{\$msg-\textgreater{}\{key\}} and
\texttt{\$msg-\textgreater{}{[}i{]}} Just Work without re-wrapping.

\textbf{World-first capability}: no other scripting language ships
zero-copy(ish) multi-target value teleportation as a single builtin.
Closest analogs (Python multiprocessing.shared\_memory, Perl
IPC::Shareable) require manual segment lifecycle + per-process attach
plumbing, single-target only, no notification primitive bundled in.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# Parent forks workers, then teleports a 100MB hashref to all of them.}
\NormalTok{my @worker\_pids = map \{ fork\_worker() \} 1:8}
\NormalTok{my $huge = build\_corpus()           \# 100MB hashref}
\NormalTok{teleport($huge, @worker\_pids, \{ hold\_ms =\textgreater{} 1500 \})}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{arrive}, \texttt{fork}, \texttt{cluster}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{arrive}}{arrive}}\label{arrive}

\texttt{arrive({[}\$timeout\_ms=5000{]})} --- block up to
\texttt{\$timeout\_ms} for the next \texttt{teleport} notification from
any sender, then read + return the value. Returns \texttt{undef} on
timeout, malformed notification, or stale SHM (sender unlinked before
this receiver got around to reading).

First call lazy-binds the per-process UDS socket at
\texttt{/tmp/stryke\_teleport\_\$\$.sock}; subsequent calls reuse it.
The socket file is best-effort cleaned up on bind (stale file from a
prior crashed instance gets unlinked).

Idiomatic receiver loop:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{while (1) \{}
\NormalTok{    my $msg = arrive(1000)}
\NormalTok{    last unless defined $msg}
\NormalTok{    process($msg)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Returned value is whatever the sender passed to
\texttt{teleport(\$val,\ ...)} after a JSON → bincode → SHM → bincode →
JSON → StrykeValue round-trip. Numbers, strings, arrays, hashes
round-trip exactly; coderefs and blessed objects do not.

See also: \texttt{teleport}, \texttt{cluster\_recv}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{turnbuckle}}{turnbuckle}}\label{turnbuckle}

\texttt{turnbuckle(\$peer\_pid\ {[},\ \{\ interval\_ms\ =\textgreater{}\ 50,\ timeout\_ms\ =\textgreater{}\ 200\ \}{]})}
--- open a 1:1 peer-pair keepalive against another stryke process.
Spawns a background thread that exchanges single-byte heartbeats with
the peer over symmetric UDS sockets:

\begin{verbatim}
this side binds:  /tmp/stryke_turnbuckle_<my_pid>_<peer_pid>.sock
this side sends:  /tmp/stryke_turnbuckle_<peer_pid>_<my_pid>.sock
\end{verbatim}

Returns a handle hashref
\texttt{\{\ \_tb\_id\ =\textgreater{}\ N,\ peer\_pid\ =\textgreater{}\ P\ \}}
for use with \texttt{tb\_alive} / \texttt{tb\_ping} /
\texttt{tb\_close}. Returns \texttt{undef} on bind failure or
non-positive \texttt{\$peer\_pid}.

Companion to \texttt{teleport} / \texttt{arrive}: where teleport is 1:N
value broadcast, turnbuckle is a 1:1 liveness primitive. Carries no
payload --- use teleport to send data through the pair.

\texttt{interval\_ms} (default 50) is how often the background thread
heartbeats; \texttt{timeout\_ms} (default 200) is the liveness window.
\texttt{timeout\_ms} is auto-bumped to \texttt{2\ ×\ interval\_ms} if
the caller specifies a tighter value, to absorb a single missed
heartbeat without flapping.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# Each side opens against the other.}
\NormalTok{my $tb = turnbuckle($peer\_pid, \{ interval\_ms =\textgreater{} 100, timeout\_ms =\textgreater{} 500 \})}
\NormalTok{while (tb\_alive($tb)) \{ do\_work() \}}
\NormalTok{tb\_close($tb)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{tb\_alive}, \texttt{tb\_ping}, \texttt{tb\_close},
\texttt{teleport}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tb\_alive}}{tb\_alive}}\label{tb_alive}

\texttt{tb\_alive(\$handle)} --- return 1 if the peer has heartbeated
within the configured \texttt{timeout\_ms} window, else 0. Non-blocking.
The handle is the hashref returned by \texttt{turnbuckle(\$peer\_pid)}.
Unknown handles (already-closed or never-opened) return 0.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $tb = turnbuckle($peer\_pid)}
\NormalTok{sleep\_ms(150)              \# let one heartbeat exchange happen}
\NormalTok{p tb\_alive($tb)            \# 1 if peer is up, 0 otherwise}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tb\_ping}}{tb\_ping}}\label{tb_ping}

\texttt{tb\_ping(\$handle)} --- send one extra heartbeat to the peer
right now, in addition to the background cadence. Returns 1 on send
success, 0 otherwise. Useful when you want to pre-warm the
peer\textquotesingle s \texttt{last\_heard} timestamp ahead of a
deadline rather than waiting for the next \texttt{interval\_ms} tick.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{tb\_ping($tb)               \# peer should see us alive within \textasciitilde{}1 RTT}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tb\_close}}{tb\_close}}\label{tb_close}

\texttt{tb\_close(\$handle)} --- tear down the pair: signal the
heartbeat thread to exit, join it, unbind the UDS socket file, drop the
registry entry. Returns 1 if the handle was known, 0 if it was already
closed or never opened. Idempotent.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{tb\_close($tb)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{weep}}{weep}}\label{weep}

\texttt{weep(@source,\ \$interval\_ms)} --- slow-trickle emitter. The
LAST argument is always \texttt{interval\_ms}; everything before it is
the source. Wraps the source into a streaming iterator that emits each
item paced at \texttt{\$interval\_ms} between emissions. The FIRST item
emits immediately; only the gap between successive items is throttled.
Zero interval = passthrough (no sleep).

\textbf{Call shapes}:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{weep(1:5, 100)              \# range flattens → 5 items, throttle 100ms}
\NormalTok{weep([1, 2, 3], 100)        \# arrayref → 3 items, throttle 100ms}
\NormalTok{weep(@list, 100)            \# @list flattens → N items, throttle 100ms}
\NormalTok{weep($iter, 100)            \# iterator (e.g. from another builtin) → use as{-}is}
\NormalTok{weep("a", "b", "c", 100)    \# 3 string items, throttle 100ms}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Single-arg form \texttt{weep(\$x)} is source-only (no throttle). To pass
a 1-item numeric source where the value would otherwise be parsed as the
interval, wrap it: \texttt{weep({[}42{]},\ 0)}.

Lazy by construction --- the sleep happens at each consumer pull, not
up-front. The throttle counts wall-clock time SINCE THE LAST EMISSION,
so if a downstream consumer is already slow, weep adds nothing.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# Drip every 100ms.}
\NormalTok{for my $item (weep([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], 100)) \{ p $item \}}

\NormalTok{\# Lazy chain — take(3) pulls only 3 items, paying for only 2 sleeps.}
\NormalTok{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{} 1:1000 weep(50) take(10) collect()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{take}, \texttt{map}, \texttt{tap},
\texttt{turnbuckle}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_interfaces}}{net\_interfaces}}\label{net_interfaces}

\texttt{net\_interfaces} (aliases \texttt{net\_ifs}, \texttt{ifconfig})
--- list all network interfaces with name, IPs, MAC, and status.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @ifs = net\_ifs()}
\NormalTok{for (@ifs) \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}\{name\}: \_{-}\textgreater{}\{ipv4\}" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_ipv4}}{net\_ipv4}}\label{net_ipv4}

\texttt{net\_ipv4} (aliases \texttt{myip}, \texttt{myip4}) --- return
the first non-loopback IPv4 address as a string.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p myip()   \# e.g. 192.168.1.42}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_ipv6}}{net\_ipv6}}\label{net_ipv6}

\texttt{net\_ipv6} (alias \texttt{myip6}) --- return the first
non-loopback IPv6 address as a string.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p myip6()   \# e.g. fe80::1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_mac}}{net\_mac}}\label{net_mac}

\texttt{net\_mac} (alias \texttt{mymac}) --- return the first
non-loopback MAC address.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mymac()   \# e.g. aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_public\_ip}}{net\_public\_ip}}\label{net_public_ip}

\texttt{net\_public\_ip} (aliases \texttt{pubip}, \texttt{extip}) ---
fetch your public IP address via HTTP.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p pubip()   \# e.g. 203.0.113.42}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_dns}}{net\_dns}}\label{net_dns}

\texttt{net\_dns} (aliases \texttt{dns\_resolve}, \texttt{resolve}) ---
resolve a hostname to IP addresses.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @ips = resolve("github.com")}
\NormalTok{p @ips}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_reverse\_dns}}{net\_reverse\_dns}}\label{net_reverse_dns}

\texttt{net\_reverse\_dns} (alias \texttt{rdns}) --- reverse DNS lookup
via UDP PTR query.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p rdns("8.8.8.8")   \# dns.google}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_ping}}{net\_ping}}\label{net_ping}

\texttt{net\_ping} (alias \texttt{ping}) --- TCP connect ping to
host:port. Returns array of RTT measurements in ms.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @rtt = ping("google.com", 443, 5)   \# 5 pings}
\NormalTok{p mean(@rtt)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_port\_open}}{net\_port\_open}}\label{net_port_open}

\texttt{net\_port\_open} (alias \texttt{port\_open}) --- check if a TCP
port is open on a host. Returns boolean.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p port\_open("localhost", 8080)   \# 1 or 0}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_ports\_scan}}{net\_ports\_scan}}\label{net_ports_scan}

\texttt{net\_ports\_scan} (aliases \texttt{port\_scan},
\texttt{portscan}) --- scan a range of TCP ports on a host. Returns list
of open ports.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @open = port\_scan("localhost", 80, 443)}
\NormalTok{p @open   \# e.g. 80, 443}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_latency}}{net\_latency}}\label{net_latency}

\texttt{net\_latency} (alias \texttt{tcplat}) --- measure TCP connect
latency to host:port in milliseconds.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p tcplat("google.com", 443)   \# e.g. 12.5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_download}}{net\_download}}\label{net_download}

\texttt{net\_download} (aliases \texttt{download}, \texttt{wget}) ---
download a URL to a local file via ureq.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{wget("https://example.com/data.csv", "data.csv")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_headers}}{net\_headers}}\label{net_headers}

\texttt{net\_headers} (alias \texttt{http\_headers}) --- fetch HTTP
response headers as a hashref.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $h = http\_headers("https://example.com")}
\NormalTok{p $h{-}\textgreater{}\{"content{-}type"\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_dns\_servers}}{net\_dns\_servers}}\label{net_dns_servers}

\texttt{net\_dns\_servers} (alias \texttt{dns\_servers}) --- return the
system\textquotesingle s configured DNS server addresses.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @dns = dns\_servers()}
\NormalTok{p @dns   \# e.g. 8.8.8.8, 8.8.4.4}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_gateway}}{net\_gateway}}\label{net_gateway}

\texttt{net\_gateway} (alias \texttt{gateway}) --- return the default
gateway IP address.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gateway()   \# e.g. 192.168.1.1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_whois}}{net\_whois}}\label{net_whois}

\texttt{net\_whois} (alias \texttt{whois}) --- WHOIS lookup via raw TCP
connection on port 43.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p whois("example.com")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{net\_hostname}}{net\_hostname}}\label{net_hostname}

\texttt{net\_hostname} --- return the system hostname.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p net\_hostname()   \# e.g. my{-}macbook.local}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{smtp\_send}}{smtp\_send}}\label{smtp_send}

\texttt{smtp\_send} (aliases \texttt{send\_email}, \texttt{email}) ---
send an email via SMTP with TLS. Takes a hashref with to, from, subject,
body, and SMTP connection fields.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{smtp\_send(\{to =\textgreater{} "bob@ex.com", from =\textgreater{} "me@ex.com", subject =\textgreater{} "Hi",}
\NormalTok{  body =\textgreater{} "Hello!", smtp\_host =\textgreater{} "smtp.ex.com", smtp\_port =\textgreater{} 587,}
\NormalTok{  smtp\_user =\textgreater{} "me@ex.com", smtp\_pass =\textgreater{} $pass\})}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-markup-web-scraping}
\chapter{Markup / Web Scraping}\label{markup-web-scraping}

4 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{html\_parse}}{html\_parse}}\label{html_parse}

\texttt{html\_parse} (alias \texttt{parse\_html}) --- parse an HTML
string into an array of element hashrefs with tag, text, and attrs.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @els = html\_parse(slurp("page.html"))}
\NormalTok{for (@els) \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}\{tag\}: \_{-}\textgreater{}\{text\}" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{css\_select}}{css\_select}}\label{css_select}

\texttt{css\_select} (aliases \texttt{css}, \texttt{qs},
\texttt{query\_selector}) --- query parsed HTML with a CSS selector.
Returns matching elements as hashrefs with tag, text, attrs, and html.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @links = css\_select(slurp("page.html"), "a.nav")}
\NormalTok{for (@links) \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}\{tag\} \_{-}\textgreater{}\{attrs\}\{href\}" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{xml\_parse}}{xml\_parse}}\label{xml_parse}

\texttt{xml\_parse} (alias \texttt{parse\_xml}) --- parse an XML string
into a nested hashref tree with tag, text, attrs, and children.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $root = xml\_parse(slurp("data.xml"))}
\NormalTok{p $root{-}\textgreater{}\{tag\}}
\NormalTok{for (@\{$root{-}\textgreater{}\{children\}\}) \{ p \_{-}\textgreater{}\{text\} \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{xpath}}{xpath}}\label{xpath}

\texttt{xpath} (alias \texttt{xml\_select}) --- query XML with
XPath-like expressions (//tag,
//tag{[}@attr=\textquotesingle val\textquotesingle{]}). Returns matching
nodes as hashrefs.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @items = xpath(slurp("feed.xml"), "//item")}
\NormalTok{for (@items) \{ p \_{-}\textgreater{}\{text\} \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-date-utilities}
\chapter{Date Utilities}\label{date-utilities}

4 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dateseq}}{dateseq}}\label{dateseq}

\texttt{dateseq} (alias \texttt{dseq}) --- generate a sequence of dates.
Args: start, end {[}, step{]}. Step defaults to 1 day.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @days = dseq("2026{-}01{-}01", "2026{-}01{-}07")}
\NormalTok{p @days}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dategrep}}{dategrep}}\label{dategrep}

\texttt{dategrep} (alias \texttt{dgrep}) --- filter a list of date
strings by a pattern or range.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @jan = dgrep("2026{-}01", @dates)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dateround}}{dateround}}\label{dateround}

\texttt{dateround} (alias \texttt{dround}) --- round date strings to a
unit (day, hour, month, etc.).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p dround("2026{-}04{-}19T14:35:22", "hour")   \# 2026{-}04{-}19T15:00:00}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{datesort}}{datesort}}\label{datesort}

\texttt{datesort} (alias \texttt{dsort}) --- sort date strings
chronologically.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @sorted = dsort("2026{-}03{-}01", "2026{-}01{-}15", "2026{-}02{-}10")}
\NormalTok{p @sorted}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-git}
\chapter{Git}\label{git}

10 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{git\_log}}{git\_log}}\label{git_log}

\texttt{git\_log} (alias \texttt{glog}) --- get the last N commits as an
array of hashrefs (hash, author, date, message).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @commits = glog(10)}
\NormalTok{for (@commits) \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}\{hash\} \_{-}\textgreater{}\{message\}" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{git\_status}}{git\_status}}\label{git_status}

\texttt{git\_status} (alias \texttt{gst}) --- get working tree status as
an array of hashrefs (path, status).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @st = gst()}
\NormalTok{for (@st) \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}\{status\} \_{-}\textgreater{}\{path\}" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{git\_diff}}{git\_diff}}\label{git_diff}

\texttt{git\_diff} (alias \texttt{gdiff}) --- get the current diff as a
string.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gdiff()}
\NormalTok{gdiff() |\textgreater{} lines |\textgreater{} grep /\^{}\textbackslash{}+/ |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{git\_branches}}{git\_branches}}\label{git_branches}

\texttt{git\_branches} (alias \texttt{gbr}) --- list all branches as an
array of strings.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @br = gbr()}
\NormalTok{p @br}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{git\_tags}}{git\_tags}}\label{git_tags}

\texttt{git\_tags} (alias \texttt{gtags}) --- list all tags as an array
of strings.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @tags = gtags()}
\NormalTok{p @tags}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{git\_blame}}{git\_blame}}\label{git_blame}

\texttt{git\_blame} (alias \texttt{gblame}) --- blame a file, returning
per-line annotation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gblame("src/main.rs")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{git\_authors}}{git\_authors}}\label{git_authors}

\texttt{git\_authors} (alias \texttt{gauthors}) --- list unique authors
sorted by commit count as hashrefs.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @authors = gauthors()}
\NormalTok{for (@authors) \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}\{name\}: \_{-}\textgreater{}\{count\}" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{git\_files}}{git\_files}}\label{git_files}

\texttt{git\_files} (alias \texttt{gfiles}) --- list all tracked files
in the repo.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @files = gfiles()}
\NormalTok{p scalar @files   \# total tracked files}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{git\_show}}{git\_show}}\label{git_show}

\texttt{git\_show} (alias \texttt{gshow}) --- show details of a commit
(message, diff, author).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gshow("HEAD")}
\NormalTok{p gshow("abc1234")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{git\_root}}{git\_root}}\label{git_root}

\texttt{git\_root} (alias \texttt{groot}) --- return the repository root
path.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p groot()   \# e.g. /home/user/project}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-system}
\chapter{System}\label{system-1}

13 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mounts}}{mounts}}\label{mounts}

\texttt{mounts} (aliases \texttt{disk\_mounts}, \texttt{filesystems})
--- list mounted filesystems with usage (total, used, available).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @m = mounts()}
\NormalTok{for (@m) \{ p "\_{-}\textgreater{}\{mount\}: \_{-}\textgreater{}\{used\}/\_{-}\textgreater{}\{total\}" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{thread\_count}}{thread\_count}}\label{thread_count}

\texttt{thread\_count} (alias \texttt{nthreads}) --- return the rayon
thread pool size.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p nthreads()   \# e.g. 8}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pool\_info}}{pool\_info}}\label{pool_info}

\texttt{pool\_info} (alias \texttt{par\_info}) --- thread-pool + host
metadata as a hashref. Useful for sizing parallel work, gating on
architecture, and reporting in benchmarks.

\textbf{Keys (always present):}

\textbar{} key \textbar{} type \textbar{} meaning \textbar{}
\textbar-\/-\/-\textbar-\/-\/-\textbar-\/-\/-\textbar{} \textbar{}
\texttt{cpus} \textbar{} Int \textbar{} logical-CPU count from
\texttt{available\_parallelism} (hyperthreads counted) \textbar{}
\textbar{} \texttt{rayon\_threads} \textbar{} Int \textbar{} current
rayon worker-pool size (what \texttt{pmap} / \texttt{pfor} /
\texttt{pgrep} use) \textbar{} \textbar{} \texttt{arch} \textbar{} Str
\textbar{} build target architecture: \texttt{aarch64},
\texttt{x86\_64}, \texttt{riscv64}, \ldots{} \textbar{} \textbar{}
\texttt{os} \textbar{} Str \textbar{} build target OS: \texttt{macos},
\texttt{linux}, \texttt{freebsd}, \texttt{windows}, \ldots{} \textbar{}

\textbf{macOS-only keys} (populated via \texttt{sysctlbyname} on Apple
Silicon / Intel Macs):

\textbar{} key \textbar{} type \textbar{} meaning \textbar{}
\textbar-\/-\/-\textbar-\/-\/-\textbar-\/-\/-\textbar{} \textbar{}
\texttt{perf\_cores} \textbar{} Int \textbar{} performance-core count
(\texttt{hw.perflevel0.physicalcpu}) \textbar{} \textbar{}
\texttt{eff\_cores} \textbar{} Int \textbar{} efficiency-core count
(\texttt{hw.perflevel1.physicalcpu}) \textbar{}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $info = pool\_info()}
\NormalTok{p $info{-}\textgreater{}\{cpus\}            \# 12 (e.g. M3 Pro)}
\NormalTok{p $info{-}\textgreater{}\{rayon\_threads\}   \# 12}
\NormalTok{p $info{-}\textgreater{}\{arch\}            \# "aarch64"}
\NormalTok{p $info{-}\textgreater{}\{os\}              \# "macos"}
\NormalTok{p $info{-}\textgreater{}\{perf\_cores\}      \# 6     (Apple Silicon only)}
\NormalTok{p $info{-}\textgreater{}\{eff\_cores\}       \# 4     (Apple Silicon only)}

\NormalTok{\# Sizing parallel work to perf cores when available:}
\NormalTok{my $n = $info{-}\textgreater{}\{perf\_cores\} // $info{-}\textgreater{}\{rayon\_threads\}}
\NormalTok{@inputs |\textgreater{} pmap \{ heavy(\_) \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{thread\_count}, \texttt{par\_bench}, \texttt{pmap},
\texttt{pfor}, \texttt{pgrep}, \texttt{set\_pool\_size}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{par\_bench}}{par\_bench}}\label{par_bench}

\texttt{par\_bench} (alias \texttt{pbench}) --- run a parallel
throughput benchmark and return results.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = pbench(1000000)}
\NormalTok{p $result{-}\textgreater{}\{ops\_per\_sec\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{to\_pdf}}{to\_pdf}}\label{to_pdf}

\texttt{to\_pdf} --- generate a PDF from text, SVG, or structured data.
Returns raw PDF bytes.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{"Hello World" |\textgreater{} to\_pdf |\textgreater{} to\_file("out.pdf")}
\NormalTok{scatter\_svg([1,2,3], [1,4,9]) |\textgreater{} to\_pdf |\textgreater{} to\_file("plot.pdf")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pdf\_text}}{pdf\_text}}\label{pdf_text}

\texttt{pdf\_text} (aliases \texttt{pdf\_read}, \texttt{pdf\_extract})
--- extract all text content from a PDF file and return it as a string.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $text = pdf\_text("report.pdf")}
\NormalTok{p $text |\textgreater{} lines |\textgreater{} cnt   \# number of lines}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pdf\_pages}}{pdf\_pages}}\label{pdf_pages}

\texttt{pdf\_pages} --- return the number of pages in a PDF file.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p pdf\_pages("report.pdf")   \# e.g. 42}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{jq}}{jq}}\label{jq}

\texttt{jq} --- alias for \texttt{json\_jq}. Query JSON data with
jq-style expressions.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $data = json\_decode(slurp("data.json"))}
\NormalTok{p jq($data, ".items[].name")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_cpu}}{stress\_cpu}}\label{stress_cpu}

\texttt{stress\_cpu} (alias \texttt{scpu}) --- CPU stress test: run
SHA256 hashing in a tight loop for the specified duration (default 1
second). Returns number of hashes computed. Use for load testing and
thermal validation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $hashes = stress\_cpu(10)   \# 10 seconds of CPU stress}
\NormalTok{p "Computed $hashes hashes"}

\NormalTok{\# Distributed across cluster}
\NormalTok{my $c = cluster(["node1:8", "node2:8"])}
\NormalTok{1:16 |\textgreater{} pmap\_on $c \{ stress\_cpu(60) \}   \# 60s stress on all workers}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_mem}}{stress\_mem}}\label{stress_mem}

\texttt{stress\_mem} (alias \texttt{smem}) --- Memory stress test:
allocate and touch the specified number of bytes (default 100MB).
Returns bytes allocated. Use for memory pressure testing and OOM
validation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $bytes = stress\_mem(1e9)   \# Allocate 1GB}
\NormalTok{p "Allocated $bytes bytes"}

\NormalTok{\# Memory pressure across cluster}
\NormalTok{1:16 |\textgreater{} pmap\_on $cluster \{ stress\_mem(4e9) \}   \# 4GB per worker}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_io}}{stress\_io}}\label{stress_io}

\texttt{stress\_io} (alias \texttt{sio}) --- IO stress test: write and
read temp files in the specified directory (default /tmp) for N
iterations (default 100). Returns total bytes written. Use for storage
IOPS testing.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $bytes = stress\_io("/tmp", 1000)   \# 1000 iterations}
\NormalTok{p "Wrote $bytes bytes"}

\NormalTok{\# IO stress across cluster}
\NormalTok{1:16 |\textgreater{} pmap\_on $cluster \{ stress\_io("/data", 500) \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_test}}{stress\_test}}\label{stress_test}

\texttt{stress\_test} (alias \texttt{st}) --- Combined CPU/memory/IO
stress test. If cluster provided, distributes across all workers.
Returns hashref with stats: \{cpu\_hashes, mem\_bytes, io\_bytes,
workers, duration\}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# Local stress test (10 seconds)}
\NormalTok{my $r = stress\_test(10)}
\NormalTok{p "CPU: $r{-}\textgreater{}\{cpu\_hashes\}, Workers: $r{-}\textgreater{}\{workers\}"}

\NormalTok{\# Distributed stress test}
\NormalTok{my $c = cluster(["node1:8", "node2:8", "node3:8"])}
\NormalTok{my $r = stress\_test($c, 60)   \# 60s across 24 workers}
\NormalTok{p "Hashes: $r{-}\textgreater{}\{cpu\_hashes\}, Duration: $r{-}\textgreater{}\{duration\}s"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{heat}}{heat}}\label{heat}

\texttt{heat} --- Maximum thermal stress. Pins ALL cores to 100\% TDP.
The hottest function in any programming language. Use for infrastructure
burn-in, cooling validation, and power testing.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{heat(60)     \# 60 seconds of maximum heat}
\NormalTok{heat()       \# default 60 seconds}

\NormalTok{\# Output:}
\NormalTok{\# 🔥 HEAT: Pinning N cores to 100\% TDP for 60s}
\NormalTok{\# 🔥 HEAT: 3,116,320,000 hashes in 60.00s (51.9M/s across N cores)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-testing}
\chapter{Testing}\label{testing}

15 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_eq}}{assert\_eq}}\label{assert_eq}

\texttt{assert\_eq} (alias \texttt{aeq}) --- assert that two values are
equal as strings. Takes A, B, and an optional message. Fails the test
with a diff if A ne B.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_eq $got, $expected, "username matches"}
\NormalTok{aeq length(@arr), 3}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_ne}}{assert\_ne}}\label{assert_ne}

\texttt{assert\_ne} (alias \texttt{ane}) --- assert that two values are
not equal as strings. Takes A, B, and an optional message. Fails if A eq
B.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_ne $token, "", "token must not be empty"}
\NormalTok{ane $x, $y}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_ok}}{assert\_ok}}\label{assert_ok}

\texttt{assert\_ok} (alias \texttt{aok}) --- assert that a value is
truthy (defined and non-zero/non-empty). Fails if the value is falsy or
undef.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_ok $result, "fetch returned data"}
\NormalTok{aok $user{-}\textgreater{}\{active\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_err}}{assert\_err}}\label{assert_err}

\texttt{assert\_err} --- assert that a value is falsy or undef. The
inverse of \texttt{assert\_ok}. Useful for verifying error conditions or
absent values.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_err $deleted\_user, "user should be gone"}
\NormalTok{assert\_err 0}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_true}}{assert\_true}}\label{assert_true}

\texttt{assert\_true} (alias \texttt{atrue}) --- alias for
\texttt{assert\_ok}. Assert that a value is truthy.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_true $connected, "should be connected"}
\NormalTok{atrue $flag}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_false}}{assert\_false}}\label{assert_false}

\texttt{assert\_false} (alias \texttt{afalse}) --- alias for
\texttt{assert\_err}. Assert that a value is falsy or undef.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_false $error, "no error expected"}
\NormalTok{afalse $disabled}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_gt}}{assert\_gt}}\label{assert_gt}

\texttt{assert\_gt} --- assert that the first numeric value is strictly
greater than the second. Fails with the actual values on mismatch.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_gt $elapsed, 0, "elapsed must be positive"}
\NormalTok{assert\_gt scalar(@results), 10}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_lt}}{assert\_lt}}\label{assert_lt}

\texttt{assert\_lt} --- assert that the first numeric value is strictly
less than the second.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_lt $latency, 100, "latency under 100ms"}
\NormalTok{assert\_lt $errors, $threshold}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_ge}}{assert\_ge}}\label{assert_ge}

\texttt{assert\_ge} --- assert that the first numeric value is greater
than or equal to the second.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_ge $count, 1, "at least one result"}
\NormalTok{assert\_ge $version, 2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_le}}{assert\_le}}\label{assert_le}

\texttt{assert\_le} --- assert that the first numeric value is less than
or equal to the second.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_le $memory, $limit, "within memory budget"}
\NormalTok{assert\_le length($buf), 4096}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_match}}{assert\_match}}\label{assert_match}

\texttt{assert\_match} (alias \texttt{amatch}) --- assert that a string
matches a regex pattern. Fails with the actual string and pattern on
mismatch.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_match $email, qr/\textbackslash{}@/, "must contain @"}
\NormalTok{amatch $line, qr/\^{}OK/}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_contains}}{assert\_contains}}\label{assert_contains}

\texttt{assert\_contains} (alias \texttt{acontains}) --- assert that a
string contains a given substring. Fails with both values on mismatch.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_contains $html, "\textless{}title\textgreater{}", "page has title"}
\NormalTok{acontains $log, "SUCCESS"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_near}}{assert\_near}}\label{assert_near}

\texttt{assert\_near} (alias \texttt{anear}) --- assert that two floats
are approximately equal within an epsilon tolerance (default 1e-9).
Essential for floating-point comparisons.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_near 0.1 + 0.2, 0.3, 1e{-}10, "float add"}
\NormalTok{anear $pi, 3.14159, 1e{-}5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{assert\_dies}}{assert\_dies}}\label{assert_dies}

\texttt{assert\_dies} (alias \texttt{adies}) --- assert that a block
throws an error. Passes if the block dies, fails if it returns normally.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{assert\_dies \{ die "boom" \} "should throw"}
\NormalTok{adies \{ 1 / 0 \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{test\_run}}{test\_run}}\label{test_run}

\texttt{test\_run} (alias \texttt{run\_tests}) --- print a test summary
with pass/fail counts and exit with code 1 if any test failed. Call at
the end of a test file to report results.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# ... assertions above ...}
\NormalTok{test\_run   \# prints summary, exits 1 on failure}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-test-runner-worker-pool}
\chapter{Test Runner (worker pool)}\label{test-runner-worker-pool}

4 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{test}}{test}}\label{test}

\texttt{test(FILE\_OR\_DIR,\ ...)} --- run every \texttt{test\_*.stk} /
\texttt{t\_*.stk} under each path through the \textbf{worker pool}. Same
engine as \texttt{s\ t} on the CLI: pre-forks N persistent stryke
\texttt{-\/-test-worker} processes, dispatches paths via JSON over
stdin/stdout, each worker \texttt{fork()}s a grandchild per test for
full isolation, no per-test dyld cost. Returns the exit code (0 on
all-pass, 1 on any failure). Optional trailing hashref selects mode:
\texttt{\{\ fork\ =\textgreater{}\ 1\ \}} for the legacy
\texttt{posix\_spawn}-per-test path,
\texttt{\{\ inproc\ =\textgreater{}\ 1\ \}} for single-process
VM-per-test (fastest, hermetic only),
\texttt{\{\ quiet\ =\textgreater{}\ 1\ \}},
\texttt{\{\ no\_interop\ =\textgreater{}\ 1\ \}}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{test("t/")                                \# default: worker pool}
\NormalTok{test(["t/", "examples/"], \{ quiet =\textgreater{} 1 \})}
\NormalTok{test("t/test\_foo.stk", \{ fork =\textgreater{} 1 \})     \# opt back into per{-}test fork}
\NormalTok{my $rc = test(@dirs)                      \# array flattens; rc = 0/1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{test\_no\_interop}}{test\_no\_interop}}\label{test_no_interop}

\texttt{test\_no\_interop(FILE\_OR\_DIR,\ ...)} (alias
\texttt{test\_ni}) --- same as \texttt{test}, but pins each worker
thread\textquotesingle s TLS no-interop flag for the duration of the
test (\texttt{set\_no\_interop\_mode\_tls}). Sibling \texttt{pmaps}
workers don\textquotesingle t race on a shared atomic. Forwards
\texttt{-\/-no-interop} to every spawned grandchild so each test runs
under stryke\textquotesingle s bot-firewall mode (rejects Perl-isms,
forces stryke idioms).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{test\_no\_interop("t/")}
\NormalTok{test\_ni(["t/a", "t/b"], \{ quiet =\textgreater{} 1 \})}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{check}}{check}}\label{check}

\texttt{check(FILE,\ ...)} --- in-process equivalent of
\texttt{stryke\ check\ FILE...}. Parses, compiles, and lints each path
without executing it. Returns the exit code (0 on success, 1 on errors).
Optional trailing hashref: \texttt{\{\ quiet\ =\textgreater{}\ 1\ \}},
\texttt{\{\ json\ =\textgreater{}\ 1\ \}},
\texttt{\{\ no\_interop\ =\textgreater{}\ 1\ \}}.

Faster than \texttt{system\ "stryke\ check\ \$f"} because it skips the
binary fork and reuses the parent process\textquotesingle s
parser/compiler. The lint pass is \textbf{not} a strict-vars enforcer
--- undefined-variable errors only fire on files that themselves do
\texttt{use\ strict;}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $rc = check("foo.stk")                    \# 0 = ok, 1 = errors}
\NormalTok{check(\textbackslash{}@files, \{ quiet =\textgreater{} 1 \})               \# arrayref + opts}
\NormalTok{for my $f (glob("src/*.stk")) \{ check($f) or warn "$f failed" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{check\_no\_interop}}{check\_no\_interop}}\label{check_no_interop}

\texttt{check\_no\_interop(FILE,\ ...)} (alias \texttt{check\_ni}) ---
same as \texttt{check}, but pins \textbf{this thread} to
\texttt{-\/-no-interop} for the duration of the check via an RAII guard
on the per-thread no-interop flag. Sibling threads are unaffected, so
the builtin is safe to call from \texttt{pmaps} workers in parallel ---
no global-atomic race.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{check\_no\_interop("foo.stk")                  \# rejects sub/say/$x/$y}
\NormalTok{pmaps \{ check\_ni(\_, \{ quiet =\textgreater{} 1 \}) \} @files \# parallel{-}safe, no race}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-ai-primitives-docs-ai-primitives-md}
\chapter{AI primitives
(docs/AI\_PRIMITIVES.md)}\label{ai-primitives-docsai_primitives.md}

79 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai}}{ai}}\label{ai}

\texttt{ai\ \$prompt,\ opts...} --- single-shot LLM call (or auto-routes
to the agent loop when
\texttt{tools\ =\textgreater{}\ {[}...{]}}/\texttt{tool\ fn}s are in
scope, vision when
\texttt{image\ =\textgreater{}\ \$url\textbar{}\$path}, structured
output when \texttt{schema\ =\textgreater{}\ +\{...\}}, document input
when \texttt{pdf\ =\textgreater{}\ \$path}).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $r = ai "summarize this", $document}
\NormalTok{my $r = ai "weather in SF"   \# auto{-}uses any \textasciigrave{}tool fn\textasciigrave{} defined in scope}
\NormalTok{my $r = ai "describe", image =\textgreater{} "/photo.jpg"}
\NormalTok{my $r = ai "extract user", schema =\textgreater{} +\{ name =\textgreater{} "string", age =\textgreater{} "int" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Providers: anthropic (default), openai. Mock with
\texttt{ai\_mock\_install}. Cost via \texttt{ai\_cost}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stream\_prompt}}{stream\_prompt}}\label{stream_prompt}

\texttt{stream\_prompt\ \$prompt,\ on\_chunk\ =\textgreater{}\ sub\ \{\ ...\ \}}
--- Anthropic SSE streaming. With \texttt{on\_chunk}, fires once per
text-delta. Without, returns a \texttt{PerlIterator}-backed handle:
\texttt{for\ my\ \$chunk\ in\ stream\_prompt("...")\ \{\ print\ \$chunk\ \}}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $state = +\{ buf =\textgreater{} "" \};}
\NormalTok{stream\_prompt("write a haiku",}
\NormalTok{    on\_chunk =\textgreater{} sub \{ $state{-}\textgreater{}\{buf\} .= \_0; print \_0 \});}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{chat}}{chat}}\label{chat}

\texttt{chat\ \textbackslash{}@messages,\ opts...} --- explicit
message-list call. Each entry is
\texttt{+\{role\ =\textgreater{}\ "user"\textbar{}"assistant"\textbar{}"system",\ content\ =\textgreater{}\ ...\}}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{chat([}
\NormalTok{    +\{ role =\textgreater{} "system", content =\textgreater{} "Be concise" \},}
\NormalTok{    +\{ role =\textgreater{} "user",   content =\textgreater{} "hi" \},}
\NormalTok{])}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

For multi-turn with auto-tracking, use \texttt{ai\_session\_*} instead.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{embed}}{embed}}\label{embed}

\texttt{embed\ \$text} or \texttt{embed\ \textbackslash{}@texts} ---
vector embedding via Voyage AI (default) or OpenAI. Returns arrayref of
floats (single text) or arrayref-of-arrayref (batch). Routes via
\texttt{ai\_routing\_set("embed",\ ...)}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @vec = @\{ embed("hello world") \}}
\NormalTok{my @vecs = @\{ embed(["a", "b", "c"]) \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tokens\_of}}{tokens\_of}}\label{tokens_of}

\texttt{tokens\_of} --- operation \texttt{tokens\ of}. Category: *ai
primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p tokens\_of $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_estimate}}{ai\_estimate}}\label{ai_estimate}

\texttt{ai\_estimate} --- operation \texttt{ai\ estimate}. Category: *ai
primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ai\_estimate $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_cost}}{ai\_cost}}\label{ai_cost}

\texttt{ai\_cost()} --- running spend / token / cache stats hashref:
\texttt{+\{usd,\ input\_tokens,\ output\_tokens,\ embed\_tokens,\ cache\_creation\_tokens,\ cache\_read\_tokens,\ cache\_hits,\ cache\_misses\}}.

\texttt{ai\_config\_get} --- operation \texttt{ai\ config\ get}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_config\_set}}{ai\_config\_set}}\label{ai_config_set}

\texttt{ai\_config\_set} --- operation \texttt{ai\ config\ set}.
Category: *ai primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ai\_config\_set $x}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_routing\_get}}{ai\_routing\_get}}\label{ai_routing_get}

\texttt{ai\_routing\_get} --- operation \texttt{ai\ routing\ get}.
Category: *ai primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]

\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_routing\_set}}{ai\_routing\_set}}\label{ai_routing_set}

\texttt{ai\_routing\_set} --- operation \texttt{ai\ routing\ set}.
Category: *ai primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ai\_routing\_set $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_register\_tool}}{ai\_register\_tool}}\label{ai_register_tool}

\texttt{ai\_register\_tool(\$name,\ \$description,\ +\{params\},\ sub\ \{\ ...\ \})}
--- register a tool that the bare \texttt{ai(\$prompt)} call
auto-attaches to its agent loop. Idempotent re-register on same name.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{ai\_register\_tool("weather", "Get weather",}
\NormalTok{    +\{ city =\textgreater{} "string" \},}
\NormalTok{    sub \{ fetch "https://api.weather.com/" . uri\_encode(\_0{-}\textgreater{}\{city\}) \});}
\NormalTok{ai\_clear\_tools();}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

For parser-level sugar, use \texttt{tool\ fn} instead.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_unregister\_tool}}{ai\_unregister\_tool}}\label{ai_unregister_tool}

\texttt{ai\_unregister\_tool} --- operation
\texttt{ai\ unregister\ tool}. Category: *ai primitives
(docs/ai\_primitives.md)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ai\_unregister\_tool $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_clear\_tools}}{ai\_clear\_tools}}\label{ai_clear_tools}

\texttt{ai\_clear\_tools} --- operation \texttt{ai\ clear\ tools}.
Category: *ai primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ai\_clear\_tools $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_tools\_list}}{ai\_tools\_list}}\label{ai_tools_list}

\texttt{ai\_tools\_list} --- operation \texttt{ai\ tools\ list}.
Category: *ai primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ai\_tools\_list $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_filter}}{ai\_filter}}\label{ai_filter}

\texttt{ai\_filter\ \textbackslash{}@items,\ "criterion"} --- keep items
the model judges as matching the criterion. Single batched LLM call;
cheap by construction.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_map}}{ai\_map}}\label{ai_map}

\texttt{ai\_map\ \textbackslash{}@items,\ "instruction"} --- apply
instruction to each item, single batched LLM call. Returns arrayref of
strings.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_classify}}{ai\_classify}}\label{ai_classify}

\texttt{ai\_classify\ \textbackslash{}@items,\ "hint",\ into\ =\textgreater{}\ {[}"a",\ "b",\ "c"{]}}
--- assign one of the labels to each item.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_match}}{ai\_match}}\label{ai_match}

\texttt{ai\_match} --- operation \texttt{ai\ match}. Category: *ai
primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ai\_match $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_sort}}{ai\_sort}}\label{ai_sort}

\texttt{ai\_sort} --- operation \texttt{ai\ sort}. Category: *ai
primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ai\_sort $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_dedupe}}{ai\_dedupe}}\label{ai_dedupe}

\texttt{ai\_dedupe} --- operation \texttt{ai\ dedupe}. Category: *ai
primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ai\_dedupe $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_extract}}{ai\_extract}}\label{ai_extract}

\texttt{ai\_extract\ "prompt",\ schema\ =\textgreater{}\ +\{\ name\ =\textgreater{}\ "string",\ age\ =\textgreater{}\ "int"\ \}}
--- structured JSON output; returns a typed hashref.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_summarize}}{ai\_summarize}}\label{ai_summarize}

\texttt{ai\_summarize\ \$text,\ words\ =\textgreater{}\ 50} ---
convenience wrapper that asks the model for a concise summary at target
length.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_translate}}{ai\_translate}}\label{ai_translate}

\texttt{ai\_translate\ \$text,\ to\ =\textgreater{}\ "Spanish"} ---
convenience wrapper for translation.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_template}}{ai\_template}}\label{ai_template}

\texttt{ai\_template\ "hi\ \{name\}",\ name\ =\textgreater{}\ "world"}
--- pure string substitution. \texttt{\{\{} escapes to a literal
\texttt{\{}. Missing keys pass through unchanged.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_transcribe}}{ai\_transcribe}}\label{ai_transcribe}

\texttt{ai\_transcribe\ "audio.mp3",\ model\ =\textgreater{}\ "whisper-1",\ language\ =\textgreater{}\ "en"}
--- OpenAI Whisper transcription. Multipart upload to
\texttt{/v1/audio/transcriptions}. Returns the transcript string.
Defaults to \texttt{whisper-1}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_speak}}{ai\_speak}}\label{ai_speak}

\texttt{ai\_speak\ \$text,\ voice\ =\textgreater{}\ "alloy",\ model\ =\textgreater{}\ "tts-1",\ format\ =\textgreater{}\ "mp3",\ output\ =\textgreater{}\ "out.mp3"}
--- OpenAI TTS. Returns raw audio bytes; pass
\texttt{output\ =\textgreater{}} to also write to disk. Voices: alloy,
echo, fable, onyx, nova, shimmer.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_image}}{ai\_image}}\label{ai_image}

\texttt{ai\_image\ \$prompt,\ model\ =\textgreater{}\ "dall-e-3",\ size\ =\textgreater{}\ "1024x1024",\ quality\ =\textgreater{}\ "hd",\ output\ =\textgreater{}\ "out.png",\ n\ =\textgreater{}\ 1}
--- image generation (OpenAI Images). Returns raw PNG bytes for n=1, or
arrayref of bytes for n\textgreater1. Models: \texttt{dall-e-3},
\texttt{gpt-image-1}. \texttt{output} writes to disk (with
\texttt{\_1}/\texttt{\_2}/... suffix for n\textgreater1).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_image\_edit}}{ai\_image\_edit}}\label{ai_image_edit}

\texttt{ai\_image\_edit\ \$prompt,\ image\ =\textgreater{}\ "in.png",\ mask\ =\textgreater{}\ "mask.png",\ output\ =\textgreater{}\ "out.png"}
--- image-to-image edit via OpenAI \texttt{/v1/images/edits}. Optional
mask is a PNG with transparent regions marking where to edit. Returns
PNG bytes (or arrayref for \texttt{n\textgreater{}1}).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_image\_variation}}{ai\_image\_variation}}\label{ai_image_variation}

\texttt{ai\_image\_variation\ image\ =\textgreater{}\ "in.png",\ n\ =\textgreater{}\ 4,\ size\ =\textgreater{}\ "1024x1024"}
--- generate variations of an existing image (OpenAI
\texttt{/v1/images/variations}, DALL-E 2). Returns PNG bytes for
\texttt{n=1} or arrayref for \texttt{n\textgreater{}1}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_models}}{ai\_models}}\label{ai_models}

\texttt{ai\_models(\$provider)} → arrayref of model IDs from the live
catalog. Providers: \texttt{openai}, \texttt{anthropic},
\texttt{ollama}, \texttt{gemini}. Useful for tooling that surfaces the
available models without hardcoding lists.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_citations}}{ai\_citations}}\label{ai_citations}

\texttt{ai\_citations()} --- arrayref of citations from the most recent
Anthropic call (when \texttt{citations\ =\textgreater{}\ 1} was passed).
Each entry is a hashref with \texttt{text} (cited string),
\texttt{title}, \texttt{start}/\texttt{end} char offsets or
\texttt{start\_page}/\texttt{end\_page} page numbers, and
\texttt{document\_index}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_dashboard}}{ai\_dashboard}}\label{ai_dashboard}

\texttt{ai\_dashboard()} → ANSI-colored multi-line summary (cost, tokens
in/out, embed tokens, prompt-cache write/read, result-cache hit ratio).
Drop at the end of a script or during a session:
\texttt{print\ ai\_dashboard()}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_pricing}}{ai\_pricing}}\label{ai_pricing}

\texttt{ai\_pricing("claude-opus-4-7")} → hashref
\texttt{+\{\ model,\ input,\ output,\ input\_per\_1m,\ output\_per\_1m\ \}}
with the per-1K-token pricing the runtime uses for cost tracking. Useful
for pre-flight cost estimates.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_describe}}{ai\_describe}}\label{ai_describe}

\texttt{ai\_describe("image.png",\ style\ =\textgreater{}\ "concise")}
--- vision wrapper that asks the model to describe an image. Styles:
\texttt{concise} (one sentence), \texttt{detailed} (paragraph),
\texttt{alt} (HTML alt-text ≤125 chars), or any custom suffix.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_grounded}}{ai\_grounded}}\label{ai_grounded}

\texttt{ai\_grounded(\$prompt,\ documents\ =\textgreater{}\ {[}@paths\_or\_strings{]},\ titles\ =\textgreater{}\ {[}@titles{]})}
--- Anthropic-only multi-document grounding. Each entry can be a PDF
path, a text-file path, or an inline string. Citations are auto-enabled;
retrieve via \texttt{ai\_citations()} after the call.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_moderate}}{ai\_moderate}}\label{ai_moderate}

\texttt{ai\_moderate(\$text,\ model\ =\textgreater{}\ "omni-moderation-latest")}
→ \texttt{+\{\ flagged,\ categories,\ scores\ \}}. OpenAI content-safety
classifier. Free endpoint --- no token cost. Use it before sending
user-generated content to a generative model.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_chunk}}{ai\_chunk}}\label{ai_chunk}

\texttt{ai\_chunk(\$text,\ max\_tokens\ =\textgreater{}\ 500,\ overlap\ =\textgreater{}\ 50,\ by\ =\textgreater{}\ "tokens"\textbar{}"chars"\textbar{}"sentences")}
→ arrayref of strings. Pure local logic --- no API call. Sentence mode
splits on \texttt{.!?} and greedily packs.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_warm}}{ai\_warm}}\label{ai_warm}

\texttt{ai\_warm(model\ =\textgreater{}\ ...,\ provider\ =\textgreater{}\ ...)}
→ \texttt{+\{\ ok,\ latency\_ms,\ error,\ model,\ provider\ \}}. Sends a
1-token ping so auth/network failures surface at script start instead of
mid-flow.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_compare}}{ai\_compare}}\label{ai_compare}

\texttt{ai\_compare(\$x,\ \$y,\ criteria\ =\textgreater{}\ "factual\ accuracy",\ scale\ =\textgreater{}\ 5)}
→
\texttt{+\{\ winner,\ reason,\ scores\ =\textgreater{}\ +\{a,\ b\},\ raw\ \}}.
Single-call structured comparison. \texttt{winner} is \texttt{"a"},
\texttt{"b"}, or \texttt{"tie"}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_session\_export}}{ai\_session\_export}}\label{ai_session_export}

\texttt{ai\_session\_export(\$handle)} → JSON string capturing system,
model, provider, and full message log. Pair with
\texttt{ai\_session\_import(\$json)} to restore.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_session\_import}}{ai\_session\_import}}\label{ai_session_import}

\texttt{ai\_session\_import(\$json)} → fresh session handle hashref.
Inverse of \texttt{ai\_session\_export}. Allocates a new id and
populates the session from the JSON body.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_file\_upload}}{ai\_file\_upload}}\label{ai_file_upload}

\texttt{ai\_file\_upload("path",\ purpose\ =\textgreater{}\ "user\_data")}
→ metadata hashref with \texttt{id}, \texttt{bytes}, \texttt{filename},
\texttt{purpose}, \texttt{created\_at}. OpenAI Files API --- file\_id
can be referenced by Whisper/Vision/Batch/Assistants.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_file\_list}}{ai\_file\_list}}\label{ai_file_list}

\texttt{ai\_file\_list(purpose\ =\textgreater{}\ "...")} → arrayref of
file metadata hashrefs from \texttt{/v1/files}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_file\_get}}{ai\_file\_get}}\label{ai_file_get}

\texttt{ai\_file\_get(\$file\_id)} → metadata hashref for a single file.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_file\_delete}}{ai\_file\_delete}}\label{ai_file_delete}

\texttt{ai\_file\_delete(\$file\_id)} → 1 if deleted, 0 otherwise.
Removes the file from OpenAI\textquotesingle s storage.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_file\_anthropic\_upload}}{ai\_file\_anthropic\_upload}}\label{ai_file_anthropic_upload}

\texttt{ai\_file\_anthropic\_upload("path/to/file.pdf")} → metadata
hashref with \texttt{id}, \texttt{filename}, \texttt{mime\_type},
\texttt{size\_bytes}, \texttt{created\_at}. Anthropic\textquotesingle s
beta \texttt{/v1/files} endpoint --- file\_id can be referenced from
batch jobs and document blocks.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_file\_anthropic\_list}}{ai\_file\_anthropic\_list}}\label{ai_file_anthropic_list}

\texttt{ai\_file\_anthropic\_list()} → arrayref of file-metadata
hashrefs from Anthropic\textquotesingle s \texttt{/v1/files}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_file\_anthropic\_delete}}{ai\_file\_anthropic\_delete}}\label{ai_file_anthropic_delete}

\texttt{ai\_file\_anthropic\_delete(\$file\_id)} → 1 on success, 0
otherwise. Removes the file from Anthropic\textquotesingle s storage.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_session\_new}}{ai\_session\_new}}\label{ai_session_new}

\texttt{ai\_session\_new(system\ =\textgreater{}\ "...",\ model\ =\textgreater{}\ "...")}
--- start a multi-turn chat session. Use
\texttt{ai\_session\_send(\$s,\ "msg")} to advance,
\texttt{ai\_session\_history(\$s)} to inspect,
\texttt{ai\_session\_close(\$s)} to drop.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_session\_send}}{ai\_session\_send}}\label{ai_session_send}

\texttt{ai\_session\_send} --- send of \texttt{ai\ session}. Category:
*ai primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ai\_session\_send $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_session\_history}}{ai\_session\_history}}\label{ai_session_history}

\texttt{ai\_session\_history} --- operation
\texttt{ai\ session\ history}. Category: *ai primitives
(docs/ai\_primitives.md)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ai\_session\_history $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_session\_close}}{ai\_session\_close}}\label{ai_session_close}

\texttt{ai\_session\_close} --- close handle of \texttt{ai\ session}.
Category: *ai primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ai\_session\_close $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_session\_reset}}{ai\_session\_reset}}\label{ai_session_reset}

\texttt{ai\_session\_reset} --- operation \texttt{ai\ session\ reset}.
Category: *ai primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ai\_session\_reset $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_memory\_save}}{ai\_memory\_save}}\label{ai_memory_save}

\texttt{ai\_memory\_save("id",\ "content",\ \$metadata?,\ \$path?)} ---
embed + persist a doc in the sqlite-backed memory store.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_memory\_recall}}{ai\_memory\_recall}}\label{ai_memory_recall}

\texttt{ai\_memory\_recall("query",\ top\_k\ =\textgreater{}\ 5)} ---
re-embed query, return top-k matches by cosine similarity. Each entry:
\texttt{+\{id,\ content,\ score,\ metadata\}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_memory\_forget}}{ai\_memory\_forget}}\label{ai_memory_forget}

\texttt{ai\_memory\_forget} --- operation \texttt{ai\ memory\ forget}.
Category: *ai primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ai\_memory\_forget $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_memory\_count}}{ai\_memory\_count}}\label{ai_memory_count}

\begin{Highlighting}[]

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_budget}}{ai\_budget}}\label{ai_budget}

\texttt{ai\_budget(\$usd\_cap,\ sub\ \{\ ...\ \})} --- runs the block;
raises a runtime error if AI spend during the block exceeds
\texttt{\$usd\_cap}. Restores prior global cap on exit.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ai\_last\_thinking}}{ai\_last\_thinking}}\label{ai_last_thinking}

\texttt{ai\_last\_thinking} --- operation \texttt{ai\ last\ thinking}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec\_cosine}}{vec\_cosine}}\label{vec_cosine}

\texttt{vec\_cosine\ \textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b} --- cosine
similarity in \texttt{{[}-1,\ 1{]}}. Both args are arrayrefs of floats
(typical embedding shape).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec\_search}}{vec\_search}}\label{vec_search}

\texttt{vec\_search\ \textbackslash{}@query,\ \textbackslash{}@candidates,\ top\_k\ =\textgreater{}\ N}
--- return top-k matches as \texttt{+\{idx,\ score\}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec\_topk}}{vec\_topk}}\label{vec_topk}

\texttt{vec\_topk} --- operation \texttt{vec\ topk}. Category: *ai
primitives (docs/ai\_primitives.md)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p vec\_topk $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_search\_tool}}{web\_search\_tool}}\label{web_search_tool}

\texttt{web\_search\_tool()} --- built-in tool spec for
\texttt{tools\ =\textgreater{}\ {[}...{]}}. Brave Search via
\texttt{\$BRAVE\_SEARCH\_API\_KEY}, falls back to DuckDuckGo HTML
scrape.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{fetch\_url\_tool}}{fetch\_url\_tool}}\label{fetch_url_tool}

\texttt{fetch\_url\_tool()} --- built-in tool spec that fetches a URL
and returns text.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{read\_file\_tool}}{read\_file\_tool}}\label{read_file_tool}

\texttt{read\_file\_tool()} --- built-in tool spec that reads a file
from disk.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{run\_code\_tool}}{run\_code\_tool}}\label{run_code_tool}

\texttt{run\_code\_tool()} --- built-in tool spec that runs Python code
in a subprocess (10s timeout, returns stdout+stderr).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tool}}{tool}}\label{tool}

\texttt{tool\ fn\ NAME(\$p1:\ type,\ \$p2:\ type)\ -\textgreater{}\ Type\ "docstring"\ \{\ body\ \}}
--- declare an agent-callable tool. Param types become the JSON Schema.
Auto-registers via \texttt{ai\_register\_tool} so bare
\texttt{ai(\$prompt)} calls see it.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{tool fn weather($city: string) "Get current weather" \{}
\NormalTok{    fetch "https://api.weather.com/" . uri\_encode($city)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{tool fn calc($x: int, $y: int) {-}\textgreater{} int "Add two ints" \{ $x + $y \}}

\NormalTok{my $r = ai("what\textquotesingle{}s the weather in SF and 7+5?");}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-mcp-model-context-protocol}
\chapter{MCP (Model Context Protocol)}\label{mcp-model-context-protocol}

13 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_connect}}{mcp\_connect}}\label{mcp_connect}

\texttt{mcp\_connect\ "stdio:CMD\ ARGS"} or
\texttt{mcp\_connect\ "https://server/mcp"} --- connect to an MCP
server. Speaks JSON-RPC over stdio (subprocess) or streamable HTTP.
Returns a handle.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_tools}}{mcp\_tools}}\label{mcp_tools}

\texttt{mcp\_tools\ \$h} --- list the server\textquotesingle s tools.
Cached after first call.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_resources}}{mcp\_resources}}\label{mcp_resources}

\texttt{mcp\_resources} --- operation \texttt{mcp\ resources}. Category:
*mcp (model context protocol)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mcp\_resources $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_prompts}}{mcp\_prompts}}\label{mcp_prompts}

\texttt{mcp\_prompts} --- operation \texttt{mcp\ prompts}. Category:
*mcp (model context protocol)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mcp\_prompts $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_call}}{mcp\_call}}\label{mcp_call}

\texttt{mcp\_call\ \$h,\ "tool\_name",\ +\{...args\}} --- invoke a tool
on the connected server.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_resource}}{mcp\_resource}}\label{mcp_resource}

\texttt{mcp\_resource} --- operation \texttt{mcp\ resource}. Category:
*mcp (model context protocol)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mcp\_resource $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_prompt}}{mcp\_prompt}}\label{mcp_prompt}

\texttt{mcp\_prompt} --- operation \texttt{mcp\ prompt}. Category: *mcp
(model context protocol)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mcp\_prompt $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_close}}{mcp\_close}}\label{mcp_close}

\texttt{mcp\_close} --- close handle of \texttt{mcp}. Category: *mcp
(model context protocol)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mcp\_close $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_attach\_to\_ai}}{mcp\_attach\_to\_ai}}\label{mcp_attach_to_ai}

\texttt{mcp\_attach\_to\_ai\ \$h} --- register a connected MCP server so
subsequent bare \texttt{ai(\$prompt)} calls auto-include its tools.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_detach\_from\_ai}}{mcp\_detach\_from\_ai}}\label{mcp_detach_from_ai}

\texttt{mcp\_detach\_from\_ai} --- operation
\texttt{mcp\ detach\ from\ ai}. Category: *mcp (model context
protocol)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mcp\_detach\_from\_ai $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_attached}}{mcp\_attached}}\label{mcp_attached}

\texttt{mcp\_attached} --- operation \texttt{mcp\ attached}. Category:
*mcp (model context protocol)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mcp\_attached $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_server\_start}}{mcp\_server\_start}}\label{mcp_server_start}

\texttt{mcp\_server\_start\ "name",\ +\{\ tools\ =\textgreater{}\ {[}...{]}\ \}}
--- run a stdio JSON-RPC MCP server in-process. Pair with
\texttt{s\_web\ build} to ship a self-contained MCP server binary.

For parser-level sugar, use
\texttt{mcp\_server\ "name"\ \{\ tool\ foo(\$x)\ "..."\ \{...\}\ \}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mcp\_server}}{mcp\_server}}\label{mcp_server}

\texttt{mcp\_server\ "name"\ \{\ tool\ NAME(args)\ "doc"\ \{\ body\ \}\ ...\ \}}
--- declarative MCP server block. Compiles to
\texttt{mcp\_server\_start("name",\ +\{\ tools\ =\textgreater{}\ {[}...{]}\ \})}
with private helper fns for each tool body.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{mcp\_server "filesystem" \{}
\NormalTok{    tool read\_file($path: string) "Read file contents" \{ slurp $path \}}
\NormalTok{    tool list\_dir($path: string)  "List dir entries"  \{ join("\textbackslash{}n", readdir $path) \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-web-framework-stryke-web-readme-md}
\chapter{Web framework
(stryke\_web/README.md)}\label{web-framework-stryke_webreadme.md}

112 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_route}}{web\_route}}\label{web_route}

\texttt{web\_route\ "VERB\ /path",\ "controller\#action"} --- register a
route in the global router.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{web\_route "GET /posts", "posts\#index"}
\NormalTok{web\_route "POST /posts", "posts\#create"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_resources}}{web\_resources}}\label{web_resources}

\texttt{web\_resources\ "posts"} --- register the 7-route REST scaffold
(index/show/new/create/edit/update/destroy) for a resource.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_root}}{web\_root}}\label{web_root}

\texttt{web\_root\ "posts\#index"} --- shortcut for
\texttt{web\_route\ "GET\ /"}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_application\_config}}{web\_application\_config}}\label{web_application_config}

\texttt{web\_application\_config} --- operation
\texttt{web\ application\ config}. Category: *web framework
(docs/web\_framework.md)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p web\_application\_config $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_render}}{web\_render}}\label{web_render}

\texttt{web\_render\ html\ =\textgreater{}\ "..."} /
\texttt{text\ =\textgreater{}\ ...} /
\texttt{json\ =\textgreater{}\ ...} /
\texttt{template\ =\textgreater{}\ "posts/index",\ locals\ =\textgreater{}\ +\{...\}}
--- emit a response. Templates resolve \texttt{app/views/...html.erb},
run through the ERB engine, wrap in the application layout.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_render\_partial}}{web\_render\_partial}}\label{web_render_partial}

\texttt{web\_redirect\ "/path",\ 302} --- issue a 3xx redirect with
\texttt{Location} header.

SQLite connection. Required before any \texttt{web\_model\_*} call.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_db\_execute}}{web\_db\_execute}}\label{web_db_execute}

\texttt{web\_db\_execute} --- operation \texttt{web\ db\ execute}.

\begin{Highlighting}[]
