\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_jobs\_init}}{web\_jobs\_init}}\label{web_jobs_init}

\texttt{web\_jobs\_init()} --- create the SQLite \texttt{jobs} table if
it doesn\textquotesingle t exist. Idempotent.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_job\_enqueue}}{web\_job\_enqueue}}\label{web_job_enqueue}

\texttt{web\_job\_enqueue("name",\ +\{...args\},\ queue\ =\textgreater{}\ "...",\ priority\ =\textgreater{}\ N,\ max\_attempts\ =\textgreater{}\ N)}
→ job id (i64). Status starts as \texttt{pending}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_job\_dequeue}}{web\_job\_dequeue}}\label{web_job_dequeue}

\texttt{web\_job\_dequeue(queue\ =\textgreater{}\ "...")} →
\texttt{+\{id,\ name,\ args\_json,\ args,\ attempts,\ max\_attempts\}}
or undef. Atomically marks the picked job as \texttt{running} and
increments \texttt{attempts}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_job\_complete}}{web\_job\_complete}}\label{web_job_complete}

\texttt{web\_job\_complete(\$id)} --- mark a running job as
\texttt{done}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_job\_fail}}{web\_job\_fail}}\label{web_job_fail}

\texttt{web\_job\_fail(\$id,\ error\ =\textgreater{}\ "msg")} --- if
\texttt{attempts\ \textless{}\ max\_attempts}, returns to
\texttt{pending} for retry; otherwise marked \texttt{failed}. Returns
the new status.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_jobs\_list}}{web\_jobs\_list}}\label{web_jobs_list}

\texttt{web\_jobs\_list(queue\ =\textgreater{}\ "...",\ status\ =\textgreater{}\ "...",\ limit\ =\textgreater{}\ 50)}
→ arrayref of job hashrefs.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_jobs\_stats}}{web\_jobs\_stats}}\label{web_jobs_stats}

\texttt{web\_jobs\_stats()} →
\texttt{+\{pending,\ running,\ done,\ failed\}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_job\_purge}}{web\_job\_purge}}\label{web_job_purge}

\texttt{web\_job\_purge(status\ =\textgreater{}\ "done")} → number of
rows deleted.

\texttt{web\_db\_begin} --- operation \texttt{web\ db\ begin}. Category:

*web framework (docs/web\_framework.md)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p web\_rollback $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_validate}}{web\_validate}}\label{web_validate}

\texttt{web\_validate\ \$attrs,\ +\{title\ =\textgreater{}\ "presence,length:1..100",\ email\ =\textgreater{}\ "format:\^{}.+@.+\$"\}}
--- returns \texttt{+\{ok\ =\textgreater{}\ 1\}} or
\texttt{+\{ok\ =\textgreater{}\ 0,\ errors\ =\textgreater{}\ +\{...\}\}}.
Validators: \texttt{presence}, \texttt{length:MIN..MAX},
\texttt{format:REGEX}, \texttt{numericality},
\texttt{inclusion:a\textbar{}b\textbar{}c},
\texttt{confirmation:other\_field}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_permit}}{web\_permit}}\label{web_permit}

\texttt{web\_permit} --- operation \texttt{web\ permit}. Category: *web
framework (docs/web\_framework.md)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p web\_permit $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_password\_hash}}{web\_password\_hash}}\label{web_password_hash}

\texttt{web\_password\_hash\ \$pw} returns a
\texttt{web1\$saltHex\$digest} shaped string.
\texttt{web\_password\_verify\ \$pw,\ \$stored} returns 1/0. SHA-256 +
16-byte salt; constant-time compare.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_session}}{web\_session}}\label{web_session}

*web framework (docs/web\_framework.md)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p web\_session $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_session\_set}}{web\_session\_set}}\label{web_session_set}

Server-side session storage backed by a signed cookie.
\texttt{web\_session\_set("user\_id",\ 42)} /
\texttt{web\_session\_get("user\_id")}. Cookie value is base64-JSON;
HttpOnly + SameSite=Lax by default.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_jwt\_encode}}{web\_jwt\_encode}}\label{web_jwt_encode}

\texttt{web\_jwt\_encode\ +\{user\_id\ =\textgreater{}\ 1,\ role\ =\textgreater{}\ "admin"\}}
--- HS256-sign a JWT using \texttt{secret\_key\_base} from
\texttt{web\_application\_config}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_jwt\_decode}}{web\_jwt\_decode}}\label{web_jwt_decode}

\texttt{web\_jwt\_decode\ \$token} --- verify + decode an HS256 JWT.
Returns the payload hashref or undef on bad signature.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_bearer\_token}}{web\_bearer\_token}}\label{web_bearer_token}

\texttt{web\_bearer\_token} --- operation \texttt{web\ bearer\ token}.
Category: *web framework (docs/web\_framework.md)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p web\_bearer\_token $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{web\_otp\_secret}}{web\_otp\_secret}}\label{web_otp_secret}

TOTP / 2FA --- RFC 6238, 30-second window, ±1 step skew tolerance.
\texttt{web\_otp\_secret()} returns a base32 secret to share with the
authenticator app; \texttt{web\_otp\_generate(\$secret)} returns the
current 6-digit code; \texttt{web\_otp\_verify(\$secret,\ \$code)}
returns 1/0.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-stress-telemetry-docs-stress-testing-md}
\chapter{Stress / telemetry
(docs/STRESS\_TESTING.md)}\label{stress-telemetry-docsstress_testing.md}

42 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_fp}}{stress\_fp}}\label{stress_fp}

\texttt{stress\_fp\ \$secs} --- float matrix-multiply pinning every
core. Returns total FLOP count.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_int}}{stress\_int}}\label{stress_int}

\texttt{stress\_int\ \$secs} --- integer pipeline pinning every core.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_cache}}{stress\_cache}}\label{stress_cache}

\texttt{stress\_cache\ \$secs,\ \$kb} --- pound a working set of
\texttt{kb} KiB per core in random order. Default 1024
(\textasciitilde{} L2/L3 boundary).

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

*stress / telemetry extensions*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p stress\_sort $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Highlighting}[]

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_disk}}{stress\_disk}}\label{stress_disk}

\texttt{stress\_disk\ \$path,\ \$secs,\ \$mb\_per\_write} --- sustained
sequential write + fsync + read across all cores.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_iops}}{stress\_iops}}\label{stress_iops}

\texttt{stress\_iops\ \$path,\ \$secs,\ \$block\_kb} --- small random
reads/writes for IOPS testing. Default 4KB blocks.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_net}}{stress\_net}}\label{stress_net}

\texttt{stress\_net\ "host:port",\ \$secs,\ \$conns} --- open
\texttt{conns} TCP connections per core, pump bytes.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_http}}{stress\_http}}\label{stress_http}

\texttt{stress\_http\ \$url,\ \$secs,\ \$conns} --- HTTP GET storm.
Counts 2xx responses.

\texttt{stress\_all\ \$secs} --- run every stress kernel above in
parallel for \texttt{secs}. Returns a hashref of per-kernel counts.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_cores}}{stress\_cores}}\label{stress_cores}

*stress / telemetry extensions*.

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_temp}}{stress\_temp}}\label{stress_temp}

Telemetry helpers: \texttt{stress\_temp()} reads CPU temp from
\texttt{/sys/class/thermal} (Linux), \texttt{stress\_freq()} averages
cpufreq across cores, \texttt{stress\_throttled()} returns 1 when freq
\textless{} 80\% of max AND temp \textgreater{} 80°C,
\texttt{stress\_load()} returns \texttt{+\{m1,\ m5,\ m15\}},
\texttt{stress\_meminfo()} returns total/used/free/swap bytes.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_thermal\_zones}}{stress\_thermal\_zones}}\label{stress_thermal_zones}

\texttt{stress\_thermal\_zones} --- operation
\texttt{stress\ thermal\ zones}. Category: *stress / telemetry
extensions*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p stress\_thermal\_zones $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_metrics\_record}}{stress\_metrics\_record}}\label{stress_metrics_record}

\texttt{stress\_metrics\_record("name",\ \$value,\ k1\ =\textgreater{}\ v1,\ ...)}
--- append a sample to the metrics history. Labels are stringified.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_metrics\_clear}}{stress\_metrics\_clear}}\label{stress_metrics_clear}

\texttt{stress\_metrics\_clear()} --- wipe the metrics history. Pair
with \texttt{stress\_metrics\_count()} before/after to verify.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_metrics\_count}}{stress\_metrics\_count}}\label{stress_metrics_count}

\texttt{stress\_metrics\_count()} → current number of samples in the
history.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_metrics\_export}}{stress\_metrics\_export}}\label{stress_metrics_export}

\texttt{stress\_metrics\_export("path",\ format\ =\textgreater{}\ "csv"\textbar{}"json"\textbar{}"prom")}
→ number of samples written.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_metrics\_prometheus}}{stress\_metrics\_prometheus}}\label{stress_metrics_prometheus}

\texttt{stress\_metrics\_prometheus()} → string in Prometheus text
exposition format. Latest value per (name, labels) wins. Drop into a
\texttt{/metrics} HTTP handler.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_metrics\_json}}{stress\_metrics\_json}}\label{stress_metrics_json}

\texttt{stress\_metrics\_json()} → JSON string of the entire metrics
history.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_metrics\_csv}}{stress\_metrics\_csv}}\label{stress_metrics_csv}

\texttt{stress\_metrics\_csv()} → CSV string with
\texttt{ts\_ms,name,value,labels} columns.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stress\_metrics\_watch}}{stress\_metrics\_watch}}\label{stress_metrics_watch}

\texttt{stress\_metrics\_watch(field\ =\textgreater{}\ "stress\_temp",\ interval\_ms\ =\textgreater{}\ 1000,\ max\_ticks\ =\textgreater{}\ 60)}
→ arrayref of values sampled at the requested cadence. Polls the metrics
history for new entries matching \texttt{field}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{audit\_log}}{audit\_log}}\label{audit_log}

\texttt{audit\_log("event\_name",\ k1\ =\textgreater{}\ v1,\ ...)} ---
append one JSONL line to \texttt{\textasciitilde{}/.stryke/audit.log}
(or \texttt{\$STRYKE\_AUDIT\_LOG}). Never throws; returns 1/0.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{audit\_log\_path}}{audit\_log\_path}}\label{audit_log_path}

\texttt{audit\_log\_path()} → absolute path the next \texttt{audit\_log}
call will write to.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{secrets\_encrypt}}{secrets\_encrypt}}\label{secrets_encrypt}

\texttt{secrets\_encrypt(\$plain,\ key\ =\textgreater{}\ \$key)} →
base64 envelope (nonce(12) \textbar\textbar{} ciphertext
\textbar\textbar{} tag(16)). AES-256-GCM. Key must be 32 raw bytes or
32-byte base64.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{secrets\_decrypt}}{secrets\_decrypt}}\label{secrets_decrypt}

\texttt{secrets\_decrypt(\$envelope,\ key\ =\textgreater{}\ \$key)} →
plaintext, or undef on auth failure.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{secrets\_random\_key}}{secrets\_random\_key}}\label{secrets_random_key}

\texttt{secrets\_random\_key()} → fresh 32-byte AES-256 key as base64.
Reads \texttt{/dev/urandom} on Unix.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{secrets\_kdf}}{secrets\_kdf}}\label{secrets_kdf}

\texttt{secrets\_kdf(\$password,\ salt\ =\textgreater{}\ "...",\ iterations\ =\textgreater{}\ 600\_000)}
→ 32-byte key as base64. PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-pty-expect-docs-expect-feature-idea-md}
\chapter{PTY / expect
(docs/expect-feature-idea.md)}\label{pty-expect-docsexpect-feature-idea.md}

13 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_spawn}}{pty\_spawn}}\label{pty_spawn}

\texttt{pty\_spawn\ "cmd\ args"} or
\texttt{pty\_spawn("cmd",\ "arg1",\ "arg2")} --- allocate a PTY,
fork+exec the child, return a handle. Master fd is non-blocking.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $h = pty\_spawn("ssh user@host");}
\NormalTok{pty\_expect($h, qr/password:/, 30);}
\NormalTok{pty\_send($h, "hunter2\textbackslash{}n");}
\NormalTok{pty\_close($h);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_send}}{pty\_send}}\label{pty_send}

\texttt{pty\_send\ \$h,\ "text"} --- write to the PTY master. Handles
EAGAIN/EINTR; returns bytes written.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_read}}{pty\_read}}\label{pty_read}

\texttt{pty\_read} --- read of \texttt{pty}. Category: *mcp (model
context protocol)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p pty\_read $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_expect}}{pty\_expect}}\label{pty_expect}

\texttt{pty\_expect\ \$h,\ qr/regex/,\ \$timeout\_secs} --- loop reading
until the regex matches the accumulated buffer or \texttt{timeout\_secs}
elapses. Returns the matched substring or undef on timeout/EOF. Buffer
is consumed up to (and including) the match.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_expect\_table}}{pty\_expect\_table}}\label{pty_expect_table}

\texttt{pty\_expect\_table\ \$h,\ {[}+\{re\ =\textgreater{}\ qr/.../,\ do\ =\textgreater{}\ sub\ \{...\}\},\ ...{]},\ \$timeout}
--- multi-pattern dispatch (Tcl \texttt{expect\ \{\ ...\ \}} block).
First match wins; the matched branch\textquotesingle s \texttt{do} sub
is called.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_buffer}}{pty\_buffer}}\label{pty_buffer}

\texttt{pty\_buffer} --- operation \texttt{pty\ buffer}. Category: *mcp
(model context protocol)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p pty\_buffer $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_alive}}{pty\_alive}}\label{pty_alive}

\texttt{pty\_alive} --- operation \texttt{pty\ alive}. Category: *mcp
(model context protocol)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p pty\_alive $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_eof}}{pty\_eof}}\label{pty_eof}

\texttt{pty\_eof} --- operation \texttt{pty\ eof}. Category: *mcp (model
context protocol)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p pty\_eof $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_close}}{pty\_close}}\label{pty_close}

\texttt{pty\_close\ \$h} --- SIGTERM → 200ms grace → SIGKILL. Returns
exit status.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_interact}}{pty\_interact}}\label{pty_interact}

\texttt{pty\_interact\ \$h} --- raw-mode handoff: forwards stdin →
master and master → stdout until EOF or Ctrl-{]}. Saves and restores tty
state.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_strip\_ansi}}{pty\_strip\_ansi}}\label{pty_strip_ansi}

\texttt{pty\_strip\_ansi\ \$text} → string with CSI/OSC/ESC sequences
removed. Useful before regex-matching against PTY output that mixes
color codes into prompts.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_after\_eof}}{pty\_after\_eof}}\label{pty_after_eof}

\texttt{pty\_after\_eof\ \$h,\ "callback\_name"} --- register an EOF
callback. Spawns a watcher thread that flips a fired flag once the PTY
closes. Pair with \texttt{pty\_pending\_events()} in your main loop.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pty\_pending\_events}}{pty\_pending\_events}}\label{pty_pending_events}

\texttt{pty\_pending\_events()} → arrayref of
\texttt{+\{handle\_id,\ callback\}} for any \texttt{pty\_after\_eof}
registrations that have fired since the last call. Drains the fired
list.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ch-other}
\chapter{Other}\label{other}

8580 topics

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$!}}{\$!}}\label{section-55}

\texttt{\$!} --- the OS error from the most recent system call, in
stringified form (e.g. \texttt{"No\ such\ file\ or\ directory"}) or as
an \texttt{errno} integer in numeric context. Set by \texttt{open},
\texttt{close}, \texttt{chdir}, \texttt{read}, \texttt{write}, and other
syscalls when they fail. The value persists until the next failing call,
so always check it *immediately* after the failing operation ---
intermediate clean Perl code does not touch it, but any subsequent
syscall will.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{open my $fh, \textquotesingle{}\textless{}\textquotesingle{}, $path or die "open $path: $!"}
\NormalTok{unless (chdir $dir) \{ warn "chdir: $!"; return \}}
\NormalTok{\# Numeric context for errno{-}based dispatch:}
\NormalTok{use Errno qw(ENOENT)}
\NormalTok{if ($! == ENOENT) \{ p "missing" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\$?} (child exit), \texttt{\$@} (eval error),
\texttt{\$\^{}E} (extended OS error).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$"}}{\$"}}\label{section-56}

\texttt{\$"} --- the list separator used when an array variable is
interpolated inside a double-quoted string. Defaults to a single space.
Setting it changes how \texttt{"@array"} renders --- useful for ad-hoc
CSV-like joining without \texttt{join}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @xs = (1, 2, 3)}
\NormalTok{p "@xs"           \# 1 2 3}
\NormalTok{local $" = ","}
\NormalTok{p "@xs"           \# 1,2,3}
\NormalTok{local $" = " | "}
\NormalTok{p "row: @xs"    \# row: 1 | 2 | 3}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Only affects double-quoted interpolation, not bare \texttt{print\ @xs}
(which uses \texttt{\$,}). Use \texttt{local\ \$"} to scope the change.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\$}}{\$\$}}\label{section-57}

\texttt{\$\$} --- the process ID of the running stryke interpreter.
Read-only, set once at startup. Useful for PID files, log filenames, and
child/parent disambiguation after \texttt{fork}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p "started, pid=$$"}
\NormalTok{open my $pf, \textquotesingle{}\textgreater{}\textquotesingle{}, "/tmp/myapp.pid" or die $!}
\NormalTok{print $pf "$$\textbackslash{}n"}
\NormalTok{close $pf}

\NormalTok{if (my $kid = fork()) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "parent $$ spawned $kid"}
\NormalTok{\} else \{}
\NormalTok{    p "child $$ running"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{getppid()} for the parent PID, \texttt{fork()} to
spawn children.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\&}}{\$\&}}\label{section-58}

\texttt{\$\&} --- the entire string matched by the most recent
successful regex match in the current scope. Reset on every match. In
Perl 5 ≥ 5.20 this no longer carries the historical performance penalty
(the pre-5.20 issue forced compilation of a slower regex engine
globally); stryke\textquotesingle s regex engine has no such penalty.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{if ("hello world" =\textasciitilde{} /\textbackslash{}w+/) \{}
\NormalTok{    p $\&        \# hello}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{my $s = "abc123def"}
\NormalTok{if ($s =\textasciitilde{} /\textbackslash{}d+/) \{ p $\&; \}   \# 123}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also:
\$\texttt{\ }\texttt{\ (prematch),\ }\$\textquotesingle{}\texttt{\ (postmatch),\ }\$1\texttt{..}\$9\texttt{\ (capture\ groups),\ }@-\texttt{\ /\ }@+\texttt{\ (offsets),\ }\$\{\^{}MATCH\}\texttt{\ /\ }\$\{\^{}PREMATCH\}\texttt{\ /\ }\$\{\^{}POSTMATCH\}\texttt{\ (no-penalty\ equivalents\ under\ }use
re \textquotesingle p\textquotesingle`).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\textquotesingle{}}}{\$\textquotesingle{}}}\label{section-59}

\texttt{\$\textquotesingle{}} --- the substring *following* the last
successful regex match ("postmatch"). Combined with
\$\texttt{\ }\texttt{\ (prematch)\ and\ }\$\&\texttt{\ (match),\ reconstructs\ the\ entire\ input:\ }"\$\texttt{\ }\$\&\$\textquotesingle"
eq \$original`. Reset on every match.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{if ("hello world" =\textasciitilde{} /\textbackslash{}s/) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "pre  = $\textasciigrave{}"      \# pre  = hello}
\NormalTok{    p "match= $\&"       \# match=  (a space)}
\NormalTok{    p "post = $\textquotesingle{}"        \# post = world}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also:
\$\texttt{\ }\texttt{,\ }\$\&\texttt{,\ }\$\{\^{}POSTMATCH\}\texttt{\ (cleaner\ under\ }use
re \textquotesingle p\textquotesingle`).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$(}}{\$(}}\label{section-60}

\texttt{\$(} --- the real group ID of the running process. In list
context (or when stringified with whitespace) yields the primary GID
followed by every supplementary group, space-separated. Use
\texttt{getgrgid(\$()} to map a GID to its name. Assigning to
\texttt{\$(} requires appropriate privileges.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p "real gid: $("             \# 1000 1000 4 24 27 …}
\NormalTok{my ($primary, @supp) = split \textquotesingle{} \textquotesingle{}, $(}
\NormalTok{p "primary group: $primary"}
\NormalTok{use POSIX qw(getgrgid)}
\NormalTok{p scalar getgrgid($primary) || $primary}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\$)} (effective gid), \texttt{\$\textless{}} (real
uid), \texttt{\$\textgreater{}} (effective uid).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$)}}{\$)}}\label{section-61}

\texttt{\$)} --- the effective group ID of the running process. Like
\texttt{\$(}, returns the primary EGID plus supplementary groups
space-separated in stringified form. Affects which permissions apply to
filesystem operations. Assigning to \texttt{\$)} requires privileges
(typically root).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p "effective gid: $)"}
\NormalTok{if ($( != $)) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "running setgid: real=$( effective=$)"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\$(} (real gid), \texttt{\$\textgreater{}} (effective
uid).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$+}}{\$+}}\label{section-62}

\texttt{\$+} --- the contents of the \textbf{last} parenthesised capture
group that successfully matched in the most recent regex. When the regex
has multiple \texttt{(...)} groups, this gives you the rightmost one
that participated in the match --- useful when alternation may match
different groups depending on input.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{if ("name=alice" =\textasciitilde{} /\^{}(\textbackslash{}w+)=(\textbackslash{}w+)$/) \{}
\NormalTok{    p $+    \# alice (last group)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{if ("foo" =\textasciitilde{} /(bar)|(foo)|(baz)/) \{}
\NormalTok{    p $+    \# foo  (only the second group matched)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\$1}..\texttt{\$9} (positional captures), \texttt{\%+}
(named captures), \texttt{@+} (end-of-match offsets).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$,}}{\$,}}\label{section-63}

\texttt{\$,} --- the output field separator inserted between arguments
by \texttt{print} (default: empty string). Does *not* affect
\texttt{printf}/\texttt{sprintf} (those use the format string) or
\texttt{say}/\texttt{p} (which still respect it). Setting \texttt{\$,}
is the classic way to print comma-separated lists without an explicit
\texttt{join}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{print 1, 2, 3, "\textbackslash{}n"        \# 123}
\NormalTok{local $, = ","}
\NormalTok{print 1, 2, 3, "\textbackslash{}n"        \# 1,2,3,}
\NormalTok{local $, = " | "}
\NormalTok{print qw(a b c), "\textbackslash{}n"      \# a | b | c |}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\$\textbackslash{}} (output record separator),
\texttt{\$"} (list interpolation separator).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$.}}{\$.}}\label{section-64}

\texttt{\$.} --- the current input line number of the most recently read
filehandle, as tracked by \texttt{\textless{}\textgreater{}},
\texttt{\textless{}\$fh\textgreater{}}, and \texttt{readline}. Counts
lines as defined by \texttt{\$/} (input record separator). Reset to 0
when a filehandle is closed (but \textbf{not} when
\texttt{\textless{}\textgreater{}} rolls onto the next ARGV file ---
line numbers continue across files unless you reset it manually).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{while (\textless{}\textgreater{}) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "$ARGV:$." if /error/i}
\NormalTok{    close ARGV if eof   \# reset $. between input files}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\$ARGV} (current filename), \texttt{eof} (file
boundary detection).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$/}}{\$/}}\label{section-65}

\texttt{\$/} --- the input record separator used by
\texttt{\textless{}\textgreater{}}/\texttt{readline}. Default:
\texttt{"\textbackslash{}n"} (one line per read). Set to \texttt{undef}
to slurp the whole file in one read; set to \texttt{""} for *paragraph
mode* (records separated by runs of blank lines); set to a reference to
a positive integer for fixed-size block reads.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{local $/ = undef         \# slurp}
\NormalTok{my $whole = \textless{}$fh\textgreater{}}

\NormalTok{local $/ = ""             \# paragraph mode}
\NormalTok{while (my $para = \textless{}$fh\textgreater{}) \{}
\NormalTok{    p length $para}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{local $/ = \textbackslash{}4096           \# 4KB blocks}
\NormalTok{while (read $fh, my $blk, 4096) \{ ... \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Always \texttt{local}-ise the change so it doesn\textquotesingle t leak.
See also: \texttt{\$\textbackslash{}} (output record separator),
\texttt{\$,}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$0}}{\$0}}\label{section-66}

\texttt{\$0} --- the program name as the OS sees it. Initialised from
\texttt{argv{[}0{]}}. Assignment to \texttt{\$0} updates the visible
process name on many Unix systems (Linux, BSDs), making \texttt{ps} /
\texttt{top} show the new value --- handy for long-running daemons that
want to reflect what they\textquotesingle re doing.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p "running as: $0"}
\NormalTok{$0 = "myapp worker [idle]"}
\NormalTok{sleep 5}
\NormalTok{$0 = "myapp worker [processing job 42]"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{@ARGV} (the rest of the command line),
\texttt{\_\_FILE\_\_} (compile-time source path).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$1}}{\$1}}\label{section-67}

\texttt{\$1} --- the substring matched by the \textbf{first}
parenthesised capture group of the most recent successful regex. Undef
if the group didn\textquotesingle t participate in the match or if the
match failed.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{if ("alice@example.com" =\textasciitilde{} /\^{}(\textbackslash{}w+)@(\textbackslash{}S+)$/) \{}
\NormalTok{    p $1    \# alice}
\NormalTok{    p $2    \# example.com}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{"ip: 192.168.1.1" =\textasciitilde{} /(\textbackslash{}d+\textbackslash{}.\textbackslash{}d+\textbackslash{}.\textbackslash{}d+\textbackslash{}.\textbackslash{}d+)/ and p $1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\$2}..\texttt{\$9}, \texttt{\%+} (named captures via
\texttt{(?\textless{}name\textgreater{}...)}), \texttt{@-} / \texttt{@+}
(offsets), \texttt{\$+} (last successful group).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$2}}{\$2}}\label{section-68}

\texttt{\$2} --- second capture group from the most recent regex match.
See \texttt{\$1} for full semantics.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$3}}{\$3}}\label{section-69}

\texttt{\$3} --- third capture group from the most recent regex match.
See \texttt{\$1} for full semantics.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$4}}{\$4}}\label{section-70}

\texttt{\$4} --- fourth capture group from the most recent regex match.
See \texttt{\$1} for full semantics.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$5}}{\$5}}\label{section-71}

\texttt{\$5} --- fifth capture group from the most recent regex match.
See \texttt{\$1} for full semantics.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$6}}{\$6}}\label{section-72}

\texttt{\$6} --- sixth capture group from the most recent regex match.
See \texttt{\$1} for full semantics.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$7}}{\$7}}\label{section-73}

\texttt{\$7} --- seventh capture group from the most recent regex match.
See \texttt{\$1} for full semantics.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$8}}{\$8}}\label{section-74}

\texttt{\$8} --- eighth capture group from the most recent regex match.
See \texttt{\$1} for full semantics.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$9}}{\$9}}\label{section-75}

\texttt{\$9} --- ninth capture group from the most recent regex match.
Captures past \texttt{\$9} work fine in stryke (no Perl 4 limit), but
the conventional way to access many groups is named captures
(\texttt{(?\textless{}name\textgreater{}...)} + \texttt{\%+}) or
\texttt{@-}/\texttt{@+} offsets.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$;}}{\$;}}\label{section-76}

\texttt{\$;} --- the multidimensional-array subscript separator. When
you write \texttt{\$h\{a,b,c\}} Perl joins the keys with \texttt{\$;}
(default: \texttt{\textbackslash{}034}, the ASCII FS / file-separator
control character) to produce a single string key. This emulates
multi-dim arrays on top of the flat hash store. Rarely used in new code
--- prefer real nested data structures (\texttt{\$h\{a\}\{b\}\{c\}} or
arrayrefs).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{$grid\{1,2\} = "x"      \# really $grid\{"1\textbackslash{}034 2"\} = "x"}
\NormalTok{p exists $grid\{1,2\}    \# 1}
\NormalTok{local $; = "|"        \# if you want readable keys for debugging}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: prefer \texttt{\$h\{a\}\{b\}\{c\}} autovivification for nested
storage.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\textless{}}}{\$\textless{}}}\label{section-77}

\texttt{\$\textless{}} --- the real user ID of the running process.
Read-only on most systems unless the process has appropriate privileges.
Use \texttt{getpwuid(\$\textless{})} to map to a username.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p "real uid: $\textless{}"}
\NormalTok{use POSIX qw(getpwuid)}
\NormalTok{p "running as: " . (getpwuid($\textless{}))[0]}
\NormalTok{if ($\textless{} != $\textgreater{}) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "setuid: real=$\textless{} effective=$\textgreater{}"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\$\textgreater{}} (effective uid), \texttt{\$(} /
\texttt{\$)} (gids).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\textgreater{}}}{\$\textgreater{}}}\label{section-78}

\texttt{\$\textgreater{}} --- the effective user ID of the running
process. Determines filesystem permission checks. Setuid binaries start
with \texttt{\$\textless{}} (real) ≠ \texttt{\$\textgreater{}}
(effective). Assignment requires privileges; swapping
\texttt{\$\textless{}} and \texttt{\$\textgreater{}} is a common pattern
for setuid scripts that want to temporarily drop privileges.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p "effective uid: $\textgreater{}"}
\NormalTok{($\textless{}, $\textgreater{}) = ($\textgreater{}, $\textless{})        \# drop to real uid (where supported)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\$\textless{}}, \texttt{\$(} / \texttt{\$)}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$?}}{\$?}}\label{section-79}

\texttt{\$?} --- the status of the last child process: a backtick
command, \texttt{system()} call, or piped-\texttt{open} close. The raw
value packs the exit code, signal number, and core-dump flag into a
single integer. Extract the exit code with
\texttt{\$?\ \textgreater{}\textgreater{}\ 8}; the signal with
\texttt{\$?\ \&\ 127}; the core-dump flag with \texttt{\$?\ \&\ 128}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $out = \textasciigrave{}ls /missing 2\textgreater{}\&1\textasciigrave{}}
\NormalTok{if ($?) \{}
\NormalTok{    my $exit = $? \textgreater{}\textgreater{} 8}
\NormalTok{    my $sig  = $? \& 127}
\NormalTok{    die "ls failed: exit=$exit sig=$sig\textbackslash{}n"}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{system(\textquotesingle{}grep\textquotesingle{}, $pat, $file) == 0 or warn "grep: $? \textgreater{}\textgreater{} 8"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\$!} (syscall error), \texttt{system}, \texttt{exec},
\texttt{wait}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$@}}{\$@}}\label{section-80}

\texttt{\$@} --- the error message left by the most recent
\texttt{eval\ \{\ \}} (or \texttt{die} inside one). Empty string when
the eval succeeded; non-empty string when it caught an exception. For
structured errors \texttt{die}d as references, \texttt{\$@} holds the
reference itself, not a string.

Classic gotcha: any code between \texttt{eval} and \texttt{if\ (\$@)}
that runs another \texttt{eval} (including destructors and \texttt{END}
blocks) can clobber \texttt{\$@}. Save it locally if you need it later.
In stryke, prefer the \texttt{try}/\texttt{catch} statement which gives
you a properly scoped exception variable and avoids this hazard
entirely.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{eval \{ dangerous\_call() \}}
\NormalTok{if (my $err = $@) \{}
\NormalTok{    warn "caught: $err"}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{\# Modern: try/catch — no $@ clobbering}
\NormalTok{try \{ dangerous\_call() \} catch ($e) \{ warn "caught: $e" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{try}, \texttt{catch}, \texttt{eval}, \texttt{die}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$ARGV}}{\$ARGV}}\label{argv}

\texttt{\$ARGV} --- the name of the file currently being read via the
diamond operator (\texttt{\textless{}\textgreater{}}) when iterating
over \texttt{@ARGV}. Set automatically each time
\texttt{\textless{}\textgreater{}} opens the next argv file. Useful
inside loops that grep across many files to know which one produced a
hit.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{while (\textless{}\textgreater{}) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "$ARGV:$.: $\_" if /TODO/}
\NormalTok{    close ARGV if eof   \# reset $. between files}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{@ARGV} (the list of files), \texttt{\$.} (line
number).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\textbackslash{}}}{\$\textbackslash{}}}\label{section-81}

\texttt{\$\textbackslash{}} --- the output record separator appended
automatically by \texttt{print} (default: empty string). Pairs with
\texttt{\$,} (field separator between args). Useful for switching
between Unix \texttt{\textbackslash{}n} and Windows
\texttt{\textbackslash{}r\textbackslash{}n} line endings without
rewriting every \texttt{print}. Does not affect \texttt{say}/\texttt{p}
(which always append \texttt{\textbackslash{}n}) or \texttt{printf}
(which uses the format string).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{local $\textbackslash{} = "\textbackslash{}n"}
\NormalTok{print "hello"           \# hello\textbackslash{}n}
\NormalTok{print "world"           \# world\textbackslash{}n}

\NormalTok{local $\textbackslash{} = "\textbackslash{}r\textbackslash{}n"}
\NormalTok{print "crlf line"       \# crlf line\textbackslash{}r\textbackslash{}n}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\$,} (output field separator), \texttt{\$/} (input
record separator).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\^{}A}}{\$\^{}A}}\label{a}

\texttt{\$\^{}A} --- the accumulator for \texttt{format}/\texttt{write}
output. Each \texttt{formline} call appends formatted text here;
\texttt{write} flushes the accumulator to the selected filehandle and
resets it. Direct manipulation is rare except when implementing custom
report writers without \texttt{write}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{formline \textquotesingle{}@\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{}\textless{} @\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textgreater{}\textquotesingle{}, "name", 42}
\NormalTok{p $\^{}A             \# "name           42"}
\NormalTok{$\^{}A = \textquotesingle{}\textquotesingle{}           \# clear}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{format}, \texttt{formline}, \texttt{write}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\^{}C}}{\$\^{}C}}\label{c}

\texttt{\$\^{}C} --- true when Perl/stryke is running in *compile-only*
mode (\texttt{-c} flag). Useful for guarding code that should be skipped
during syntax checking (e.g. tests that would actually run, network
calls). Read-only.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{BEGIN \{ exit 0 if $\^{}C \}      \# don\textquotesingle{}t run setup during {-}c}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{-c} command-line flag.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\^{}D}}{\$\^{}D}}\label{d-1}

\texttt{\$\^{}D} --- the debug flag bitmask (\texttt{-D} command-line
flag in debug-enabled builds). Stryke debug builds expose the same flag
set as Perl; production builds have it stubbed. Numeric, set from the
\texttt{-D} digits or letters on the command line.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p "debug mask: $\^{}D" if $\^{}D}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{-D} flag, \texttt{use\ diagnostics}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\^{}E}}{\$\^{}E}}\label{e}

\texttt{\$\^{}E} --- extended OS error info beyond \texttt{\$!}. On
Windows this carries Win32 error codes / messages distinct from the
POSIX errno that \texttt{\$!} reports. On Unix it is usually identical
to \texttt{\$!}. Useful for platform-specific diagnostics.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{open my $fh, \textquotesingle{}\textless{}\textquotesingle{}, $path or die "open: $! ($\^{}E)"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\$!}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\^{}F}}{\$\^{}F}}\label{f}

\texttt{\$\^{}F} --- the maximum file descriptor considered "system"
(default: 2). File descriptors at or below this number are *not* closed
across \texttt{exec()} calls; higher fds are closed automatically. Raise
it before \texttt{exec}-ing a child if you need extra fds to survive.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{$\^{}F = 10               \# keep fds 0{-}10 across exec}
\NormalTok{open my $fh, \textquotesingle{}\textless{}\textquotesingle{}, $path or die $!}
\NormalTok{exec \textquotesingle{}helper\textquotesingle{}, fileno($fh)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{exec}, \texttt{fcntl}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\^{}H}}{\$\^{}H}}\label{h}

\texttt{\$\^{}H} --- the compile-time lexical hint bitmask (32 bits of
integer flags). Pragmas like \texttt{use\ strict},
\texttt{use\ warnings}, \texttt{use\ utf8} flip bits here to communicate
their state to the parser within a lexical scope. Reading at runtime is
mostly useless (it reflects current-line compile state); manipulation is
for pragma authors.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{BEGIN \{ p sprintf "hint bits: 0x\%08x", $\^{}H \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\%\^{}H} (lexical hint hash), \texttt{use\ strict},
\texttt{use\ warnings}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\^{}I}}{\$\^{}I}}\label{i}

\texttt{\$\^{}I} --- the in-place edit extension. Set by the \texttt{-i}
command-line flag and controls \texttt{\textless{}\textgreater{}}
rewriting: when \texttt{\$\^{}I} is defined, lines read from
\texttt{@ARGV} files are written to a backup-named or in-place
replacement. Empty string \texttt{""} means "no backup"; a non-empty
value (e.g. \texttt{".bak"}) means "save original as \texttt{FILE.bak}".

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# Equivalent to: stryke {-}i.bak {-}pe \textquotesingle{}s/foo/bar/g\textquotesingle{} file.txt}
\NormalTok{BEGIN \{ $\^{}I = \textquotesingle{}.bak\textquotesingle{} \}}
\NormalTok{while (\textless{}\textgreater{}) \{ s/foo/bar/g; print \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{-i} flag, \texttt{-p} / \texttt{-n}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\^{}L}}{\$\^{}L}}\label{l}

\texttt{\$\^{}L} --- the formfeed character emitted between pages by
\texttt{format}/\texttt{write}. Default: \texttt{"\textbackslash{}f"}.
Rarely changed; mainly useful when emitting paginated output to a
printer-like destination.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{local $\^{}L = "\textbackslash{}n{-}{-}{-}\textbackslash{}n"}
\NormalTok{write                \# custom page break}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{format}, \texttt{write}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\^{}M}}{\$\^{}M}}\label{m}

\texttt{\$\^{}M} --- an emergency memory pool reserved for handling
out-of-memory \texttt{die}s. Pre-allocate a chunk early (e.g.
\texttt{\$\^{}M\ =\ \textquotesingle{}x\textquotesingle{}\ x\ 65536}),
and Perl will release it when allocation otherwise fails so cleanup code
has room to run. Niche; only relevant in long-running daemons that might
OOM.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{BEGIN \{ $\^{}M = \textquotesingle{}x\textquotesingle{} x 65536 \}   \# 64 KB emergency reserve}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{die}, \texttt{END} block.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\^{}N}}{\$\^{}N}}\label{n}

\texttt{\$\^{}N} --- the substring matched by the most recently
\textbf{closed} parenthesised group in the current regex match. Inside
an embedded code assertion \texttt{(?\{\ ...\ \})} this lets you grab
the group that just closed without committing to a
\texttt{\$1}/\texttt{\$2} number.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{"abc" =\textasciitilde{} /(.)(.)(.)(?\{ p $\^{}N \})/   \# prints c}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\$+}, \texttt{\$1}..\texttt{\$9},
\texttt{(?\{\ ...\ \})} embedded code.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\^{}O}}{\$\^{}O}}\label{o}

\texttt{\$\^{}O} --- the OS name string set at build time. Common
values: \texttt{darwin} (macOS), \texttt{linux}, \texttt{freebsd},
\texttt{MSWin32}, \texttt{cygwin}. Use it for platform-specific
branching without shelling out to \texttt{uname}. Read-only.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{if ($\^{}O eq \textquotesingle{}darwin\textquotesingle{}) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "on macOS"}
\NormalTok{\} elsif ($\^{}O eq \textquotesingle{}linux\textquotesingle{}) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "on Linux"}
\NormalTok{\} elsif ($\^{}O =\textasciitilde{} /MSWin32|cygwin/) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "on Windows"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{uname} (full system info), \texttt{Config::\%Config}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\^{}P}}{\$\^{}P}}\label{p-2}

\texttt{\$\^{}P} --- the internal debugger control flag bitmask. Set to
non-zero to enable the embedded debugger hooks; specific bits control
which events (subroutine entry, line execution, file load, \ldots)
trigger callbacks. Debugger / profiler authors only.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p "debugger flags: $\^{}P"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: stryke debugger / profiler tooling.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\^{}R}}{\$\^{}R}}\label{r}

\texttt{\$\^{}R} --- the value returned by the most recent
\texttt{(?\{\ ...\ \})} or \texttt{(??\{\ ...\ \})} regex
code-assertion. Useful when building accumulators inside complex regex
matches that compute as they go.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{"42" =\textasciitilde{} /(\textbackslash{}d+)(?\{ $\^{}R = $1 * 2 \})/}
\NormalTok{p $\^{}R              \# 84}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{(?\{\ ...\ \})}, \texttt{(??\{\ ...\ \})},
\texttt{\$\^{}N}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\^{}S}}{\$\^{}S}}\label{s-1}

\texttt{\$\^{}S} --- the current exception-handling state:
\texttt{undef} while compiling, \texttt{0} while executing the main
program, \texttt{1} while executing inside an \texttt{eval\ \{\ \}}.
Inside a \texttt{\$SIG\{\_\_DIE\_\_\}} handler this tells you whether
the die will be caught (\texttt{\$\^{}S\ ==\ 1}) or fatal
(\texttt{\$\^{}S\ ==\ 0}).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{$SIG\{\_\_DIE\_\_\} = fn ($msg) \{}
\NormalTok{    if ($\^{}S) \{ return \}    \# let eval catch it}
\NormalTok{    log\_fatal($msg)         \# uncaught — log it}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{eval}, \texttt{die}, \texttt{\$SIG\{\_\_DIE\_\_\}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\^{}T}}{\$\^{}T}}\label{t}

\texttt{\$\^{}T} --- the time at which the program started, as a Unix
epoch (seconds since 1970). Used by \texttt{-A}, \texttt{-M},
\texttt{-C} file-test operators (which return age in days relative to
\texttt{\$\^{}T}). Read-write --- set it to a different epoch to change
what those tests mean.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p "started at: " . scalar(localtime $\^{}T)}
\NormalTok{my @stale = grep \{ {-}M $\_ \textgreater{} 30 \} @files   \# older than 30 days}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{-M} / \texttt{-A} / \texttt{-C} file tests,
\texttt{time()}, \texttt{localtime}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\^{}V}}{\$\^{}V}}\label{v}

\texttt{\$\^{}V} --- the running stryke / Perl version as a version
object (v-string). Compare with \texttt{\$\^{}V\ ge\ v5.20.0} style
expressions for capability gating. Stringifies to \texttt{"v5.38.2"}
etc.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p "running $\^{}V"}
\NormalTok{die "need v5.20+" unless $\^{}V ge v5.20.0}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\$stryke::VERSION}, \texttt{use\ VERSION}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\^{}W}}{\$\^{}W}}\label{w}

\texttt{\$\^{}W} --- the global warnings flag (boolean). Set by the
\texttt{-w} command-line flag or \texttt{BEGIN\ \{\ \$\^{}W\ =\ 1\ \}}.
The modern preferred mechanism is lexical \texttt{use\ warnings} /
\texttt{no\ warnings}, which doesn\textquotesingle t pollute distant
scopes; reach for \texttt{\$\^{}W} only when matching legacy Perl 4 /
early Perl 5 idioms.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{local $\^{}W = 0       \# silence warnings in this scope (old style)}
\NormalTok{\# Modern:}
\NormalTok{no warnings \textquotesingle{}uninitialized\textquotesingle{}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{use\ warnings}, \texttt{-w},
\texttt{\$\{\^{}WARNING\_BITS\}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\^{}X}}{\$\^{}X}}\label{x-2}

\texttt{\$\^{}X} --- the path used to invoke the stryke / Perl
executable, as taken from \texttt{argv{[}0{]}} at startup. Useful for
re-\texttt{exec}-ing the same interpreter from a child script or
constructing
\texttt{system(\$\^{}X,\ \textquotesingle{}-e\textquotesingle{},\ ...)}
calls that are guaranteed to use the same binary.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p "interpreter: $\^{}X"}
\NormalTok{system $\^{}X, \textquotesingle{}{-}e\textquotesingle{}, \textquotesingle{}p "hello from a fresh stryke"\textquotesingle{}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\$0} (program name), \texttt{@ARGV}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\_}}{\$\_}}\label{section-82}

\texttt{\$\_} --- the default topic variable. Many builtins
(\texttt{print}, \texttt{say}/\texttt{p}, \texttt{chomp},
\texttt{length}, \texttt{uc}, \texttt{lc}, regex
\texttt{=\textasciitilde{}} without an explicit target, \texttt{-X} file
tests) operate on \texttt{\$\_} when called without arguments.
\texttt{for}/\texttt{foreach} loops and
\texttt{while\ (\textless{}\textgreater{})} automatically bind each
element/line to \texttt{\$\_} unless you declare a loop variable. In
stryke pipelines (\texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}}), the unsigiled
\texttt{\_} is the topic --- pipe stages see the current value as
\texttt{\_}, and \texttt{\$\_} remains compatible for Perl 5 idioms.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{for (1:5) \{ p $\_ * 2 \}            \# 2 4 6 8 10}
\NormalTok{while (\textless{}\textgreater{}) \{ chomp; p \}            \# chomp $\_; p $\_}
\NormalTok{@names |\textgreater{} e \{ p uc \}               \# uc $\_}

\NormalTok{\# Explicit aliasing — mutations propagate:}
\NormalTok{my @xs = (1, 2, 3)}
\NormalTok{for (@xs) \{ $\_ *= 10 \}}
\NormalTok{p "@xs"   \# 10 20 30}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Use \texttt{local\ \$\_} to scope a topic change. In stryke, \texttt{\_}
(no sigil) is the preferred pipeline topic; \texttt{\$\_} is the
Perl-5-compatible form.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\textasciigrave{}}}{\$`}}\label{section-83}

\$\texttt{\ }\texttt{\ —\ the\ substring\ *preceding*\ the\ last\ successful\ regex\ match\ ("prematch").\ With\ }\$\&\texttt{\ and\ }\$\textquotesingle`,
reconstructs the full input. Reset on every successful match.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{if ("hello world" =\textasciitilde{} /\textbackslash{}s/) \{}
\NormalTok{    p $\textasciigrave{}         \# hello}
\NormalTok{    p $\&         \# (space)}
\NormalTok{    p $\textquotesingle{}         \# world}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\$\&}, \texttt{\$\textquotesingle{}},
\texttt{\$\{\^{}PREMATCH\}} (cleaner under
\texttt{use\ re\ \textquotesingle{}p\textquotesingle{}}).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$stryke::VERSION}}{\$stryke::VERSION}}\label{strykeversion}

\texttt{\$stryke::VERSION} --- the stryke language version string (e.g.
\texttt{"0.13.4"}). Matches the version baked into the binary at build
time from \texttt{Cargo.toml}. Use it for feature gating and
diagnostics.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p "stryke $stryke::VERSION"}
\NormalTok{die "need stryke 0.14+" if $stryke::VERSION lt "0.14"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\$\^{}V} (interpreter version), \texttt{-\/-version}
CLI flag.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\textbar{}}}{\$\textbar{}}}\label{section-84}

\texttt{\$\textbar{}} --- the autoflush flag for the currently selected
output filehandle. When true (any non-zero value), every
\texttt{print}/\texttt{say} flushes the buffer immediately. Essential
for progress bars, prompts that should appear before
\texttt{\textless{}STDIN\textgreater{}}, and pipelines where downstream
consumers need data without buffering delay. Affects only the *currently
selected* handle --- pair with \texttt{select} to set autoflush on a
specific handle.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{$| = 1                       \# autoflush STDOUT}
\NormalTok{for (1:10) \{}
\NormalTok{    print "."               \# appears immediately}
\NormalTok{    sleep 1}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{\# Autoflush a specific handle:}
\NormalTok{my $old = select STDERR; $| = 1; select $old}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{select}, \texttt{IO::Handle::autoflush}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\$\textasciitilde{}}}{\$\textasciitilde{}}}\label{section-85}

\texttt{\$\textasciitilde{}} --- the name of the current format used by
\texttt{write}. Defaults to the name of the filehandle being written to.
Set it to switch formats on the fly when the same handle should emit
different report shapes.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{format HEADER = ...}
\NormalTok{.}
\NormalTok{format BODY = ...}
\NormalTok{.}

\NormalTok{$\textasciitilde{} = \textquotesingle{}HEADER\textquotesingle{}; write}
\NormalTok{$\textasciitilde{} = \textquotesingle{}BODY\textquotesingle{};    write while @rows}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\$\^{}} (top-of-page header format), \texttt{format},
\texttt{write}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%ENV}}{\%ENV}}\label{env}

\texttt{\%ENV} --- the process environment as a hash keyed by variable
name. Reads return the current value (or \texttt{undef} for unset);
assignments take effect immediately and propagate to child processes
spawned afterwards via \texttt{system}/\texttt{exec}/backticks.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $ENV\{HOME\}}
\NormalTok{p $ENV\{PATH\}}
\NormalTok{$ENV\{MYAPP\_DEBUG\} = 1}
\NormalTok{system \textquotesingle{}env\textquotesingle{} |\textgreater{} grep /MYAPP/ |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{delete $ENV\{TMPDIR\}             \# unset}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

In stryke, \texttt{\%ENV} is a live view of the OS environment --- no
caching. See also: \texttt{getenv}, \texttt{setenv}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%INC}}{\%INC}}\label{inc}

\texttt{\%INC} --- the registry of files loaded via \texttt{require} /
\texttt{use}. Keys are the relative module path (e.g.
\texttt{"My/Module.pm"}); values are the absolute path on disk.
\texttt{require} consults \texttt{\%INC} before loading: if the key
already exists, the require is a no-op (this is how Perl avoids loading
the same module twice). Delete a key to force a reload (advanced use,
e.g. plugin reloading).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{use File::Spec}
\NormalTok{p $INC\{\textquotesingle{}File/Spec.pm\textquotesingle{}\}        \# absolute path to the loaded file}
\NormalTok{keys \%INC |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} e p       \# every loaded module}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{@INC} (search path), \texttt{require}, \texttt{use}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%SIG}}{\%SIG}}\label{sig}

\texttt{\%SIG} --- the signal handler table keyed by signal name
(without the \texttt{SIG} prefix: \texttt{INT}, \texttt{TERM},
\texttt{HUP}, \ldots). Assign a coderef to install a handler; assign
\texttt{\textquotesingle{}IGNORE\textquotesingle{}} to ignore; assign
\texttt{\textquotesingle{}DEFAULT\textquotesingle{}} to restore default
behavior. Two pseudo-keys hook the interpreter itself:
\texttt{\$SIG\{\_\_DIE\_\_\}} runs before any \texttt{die} (including
caught), and \texttt{\$SIG\{\_\_WARN\_\_\}} intercepts every
\texttt{warn}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{$SIG\{INT\}  = fn ($sig) \{ p "got SIGINT"; exit 0 \}}
\NormalTok{$SIG\{TERM\} = \textquotesingle{}IGNORE\textquotesingle{}}
\NormalTok{$SIG\{\_\_WARN\_\_\} = fn ($msg) \{ log\_warn($msg) \}}

\NormalTok{\# Block a signal temporarily:}
\NormalTok{local $SIG\{INT\} = \textquotesingle{}IGNORE\textquotesingle{}}
\NormalTok{critical\_section()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{kill}, \texttt{POSIX::sigaction}, \texttt{\_\_DIE\_\_}
/ \texttt{\_\_WARN\_\_} pseudo-signals.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%\^{}H}}{\%\^{}H}}\label{h-1}

\texttt{\%\^{}H} --- the lexical hint hash, paired with
\texttt{\$\^{}H}. Pragma authors store per-scope state here, keyed by
their package name. The contents are visible at compile time only ---
runtime code in the same scope sees an empty hash unless the pragma uses
\texttt{\$\{\^{}\_RE\_TRIE\_MAXBUF\}} or similar magic.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{BEGIN \{ $\^{}H\{\textquotesingle{}My::Pragma\textquotesingle{}\} = \{ strict\_mode =\textgreater{} 1 \} \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Niche; almost exclusively used by \texttt{use} pragma implementations.
See also: \texttt{\$\^{}H}, \texttt{use}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%\^{}HOOK}}{\%\^{}HOOK}}\label{hook}

\texttt{\%\^{}HOOK} --- the runtime hook registry used by stryke / Perl
extensions to register callbacks for compile-time events (function
entry/exit, regex match, signal arrival). Keys are well-known hook
names; values are coderefs. Modifying this directly is a sharp edge ---
usually you\textquotesingle d use a pragma or module that hooks for you
(e.g. AOP intercepts in zshrs).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p keys \%\^{}HOOK    \# registered hook names}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: stryke AOP / intercept facilities.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%a}}{\%a}}\label{a-1}

\texttt{\%a} --- short alias for \texttt{\%stryke::aliases}. Maps every
alias spelling to its canonical primary name. Keys are the 2nd-and-later
names in each \texttt{try\_builtin} match arm; values are the primary.
For O(1) *reverse* lookup (primary → all its aliases), use \texttt{\%p}
/ \texttt{\%stryke::primaries}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $stryke::aliases\{tj\}                          \# to\_json}
\NormalTok{p $stryke::aliases\{bn\}                          \# basename}
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%stryke::aliases                  \# total alias count}
\NormalTok{keys \%stryke::aliases |\textgreater{} grep /\^{}p/ |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} e p   \# every alias starting with p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\%stryke::aliases}, \texttt{\%p} /
\texttt{\%stryke::primaries}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%all}}{\%all}}\label{all-1}

\texttt{\%all} --- short alias for \texttt{\%stryke::all}. Every
callable *spelling* (primaries \textbf{and} aliases) → category. Aliases
inherit their primary\textquotesingle s category.

Use \texttt{\%all} when you want "how many names can I type?" or want to
look up an alias\textquotesingle s category without hopping through
\texttt{\%a}. Use \texttt{\%b} when you want unique operations only.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%all                            \# total callable{-}spellings count}
\NormalTok{p $all\{tj\}                                    \# "serialization"  (alias inherits)}
\NormalTok{p $all\{to\_json\}                               \# "serialization"}
\NormalTok{keys \%all |\textgreater{} pager                            \# browse every spelling}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\%stryke::all}, \texttt{\%b} /
\texttt{\%stryke::builtins} (primaries-only), \texttt{\%a} /
\texttt{\%stryke::aliases} (alias→primary).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%b}}{\%b}}\label{b}

\texttt{\%b} --- short alias for \texttt{\%stryke::builtins}. Maps every
\textbf{primary} callable name to its category string. Primaries-only
--- aliases live in \texttt{\%a} / \texttt{\%stryke::aliases}.
\texttt{scalar\ keys\ \%b} is the clean unique-operation count of
stryke\textquotesingle s runtime.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $stryke::builtins\{pmap\}                       \# "parallel"}
\NormalTok{p $stryke::builtins\{to\_json\}                    \# "serialization"}
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%stryke::builtins                 \# unique{-}op count}
\NormalTok{keys \%stryke::builtins |\textgreater{} grep \{ $stryke::builtins\{$\_\} eq \textquotesingle{}parallel\textquotesingle{} \} |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\%stryke::builtins}, \texttt{\%c} /
\texttt{\%stryke::categories}, \texttt{\%a} /
\texttt{\%stryke::aliases}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%c}}{\%c}}\label{c-1}

\texttt{\%c} --- short alias for \texttt{\%stryke::categories}. Inverted
index on \texttt{\%b}: category string → arrayref of names in that
category. Gives O(1) reverse-lookup for "list every op of kind X"
queries that would otherwise be O(n) \texttt{grep}s. Name lists are
alphabetized.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{$c\{parallel\} |\textgreater{} e p                          \# every parallel op}
\NormalTok{p scalar @\{ $c\{parallel\} \}                   \# how many?}
\NormalTok{p join ", ", @\{ $c\{"array / list"\} \}       \# joined roster}
\NormalTok{keys \%c |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} e p                        \# every category name}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\%stryke::categories}, \texttt{\%b} /
\texttt{\%stryke::builtins}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%d}}{\%d}}\label{d-2}

\texttt{\%d} --- short alias for \texttt{\%stryke::descriptions}. Maps
each callable name to its one-line summary (the first sentence of its
LSP hover doc). Sparse --- only names that have a hand-written hover doc
appear, so \texttt{exists\ \$d\{\$name\}} doubles as "is this
documented?".

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $d\{pmap\}                       \# one{-}line summary of pmap}
\NormalTok{p $d\{to\_json\}                    \# "Serialize a StrykeValue to a JSON string."}
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%d                 \# count of documented ops}
\NormalTok{keys \%d |\textgreater{} grep \{ $d\{\_\} =\textasciitilde{} /parallel/i \} |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\%stryke::descriptions}, the LSP hover system.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%e}}{\%e}}\label{e-1}

\texttt{\%e} --- short alias for \texttt{\%stryke::extensions}.
Stryke-only names, name → category. Disjoint from \texttt{\%pc} (Perl 5
core). Everything \texttt{-\/-compat} mode rejects at parse time, plus
dispatch primaries (like \texttt{basename}, \texttt{ddump}) that are
extensions at runtime even without a parser entry.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $e\{pmap\}                                    \# "parallel"}
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%e                              \# stryke{-}only name count}
\NormalTok{keys \%e |\textgreater{} grep /\^{}p/ |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} e p           \# every p* parallel op}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\%stryke::extensions}, \texttt{\%pc} /
\texttt{\%stryke::perl\_compats}, \texttt{\%b} /
\texttt{\%stryke::builtins}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%k}}{\%k}}\label{k}

\texttt{\%k} --- short alias for \texttt{\%stryke::keywords}. Maps every
stryke language keyword (\texttt{fn}, \texttt{match}, \texttt{class},
\texttt{struct}, \texttt{enum}, \texttt{mysync}, \texttt{frozen},
\ldots) to a one-line category/description. Use this to discover the
full keyword set programmatically.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%k                 \# total keyword count}
\NormalTok{keys \%k |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} e p           \# every keyword}
\NormalTok{keys \%k |\textgreater{} grep \{ $k\{\_\} =\textasciitilde{} /control/i \} |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\%stryke::keywords}, \texttt{\%b} /
\texttt{\%stryke::builtins}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%limits}}{\%limits}}\label{limits}

\texttt{\%limits} --- reflection hash exposing stryke\textquotesingle s
hard and soft runtime limits (max thread count, max recursion depth, max
string interpolation depth, regex backtrack budget, etc.). Keys are
limit names; values are the numeric ceilings.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p keys \%limits |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{p $limits\{max\_threads\}}
\NormalTok{p $limits\{regex\_backtrack\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: stryke runtime / scheduler internals.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%o}}{\%o}}\label{o-1}

\texttt{\%o} --- short alias for \texttt{\%stryke::operators}. Maps
every symbol operator stryke recognises (arithmetic, comparison,
bitwise, pipeline arrows, assignment forms, \ldots) to its category
string.

Key is the spelling as the lexer emits it (\texttt{"+"},
\texttt{"\textless{}=\textgreater{}"}, \texttt{"//="},
\texttt{"\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{}"},
\texttt{"\textbar{}\textgreater{}"}, \texttt{"-\textgreater{}"},
\texttt{"=\textasciitilde{}"}, \texttt{"..."}); value is one of
\texttt{arith}, \texttt{compare}, \texttt{logical}, \texttt{bitwise},
\texttt{assign}, \texttt{string}, \texttt{binding}, \texttt{pipeline},
\texttt{range}, \texttt{arrow}, \texttt{deref}, \texttt{inc},
\texttt{ternary}, \texttt{sigil}, \texttt{punct}. Word operators
(\texttt{and}, \texttt{or}, \texttt{eq}, \texttt{cmp}, \texttt{x},
\texttt{not}) live in \texttt{\%k} with category \texttt{"operator"},
not here.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $o\{"+"\}                         \# "arith"}
\NormalTok{p $o\{"\textless{}=\textgreater{}"\}                       \# "compare"}
\NormalTok{p $o\{"|\textgreater{}"\}                        \# "pipeline"}
\NormalTok{p $o\{"=\textasciitilde{}"\}                        \# "binding"}
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%o                  \# total symbol{-}operator count}
\NormalTok{keys \%o |\textgreater{} grep \{ $o\{\_\} eq "pipeline" \} |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{keys \%o |\textgreater{} grep \{ $o\{\_\} eq "assign" \} |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\%stryke::operators}, \texttt{\%k} /
\texttt{\%stryke::keywords} (word operators), \texttt{\%v} /
\texttt{\%stryke::special\_vars}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%overload::}}{\%overload::}}\label{overload}

\texttt{\%overload::} --- the runtime symbol table of the
\texttt{overload} package, exposing operator-overloading machinery
(\texttt{overload::Method}, \texttt{overload::StrVal},
\texttt{overload::Overloaded}). Inspecting it tells you which packages
have overloaded operators registered. Rarely accessed directly; use the
\texttt{overload} pragma to register operators in your own classes.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p keys \%overload::}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{use\ overload}, \texttt{overload::Method},
\texttt{overload::Overloaded}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%p}}{\%p}}\label{p-3}

\texttt{\%p} --- short alias for \texttt{\%stryke::primaries}. Primary
dispatcher name → arrayref of its aliases. Inverted \texttt{\%a} /
\texttt{\%stryke::aliases}. Primaries with no aliases still have an
entry (empty arrayref), so \texttt{exists\ \$p\{foo\}} reliably answers
"is foo a dispatch primary?" in O(1).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{$p\{to\_json\} |\textgreater{} e p                                       \# ["tj"]}
\NormalTok{p scalar @\{ $p\{basename\} \}                               \# alias count for basename}
\NormalTok{\# every primary that has at least one alias:}
\NormalTok{keys \%p |\textgreater{} grep \{ scalar @\{$p\{\_\}\} \} |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Not to be confused with the \textbf{\texttt{p} builtin} (stryke
print-with-newline) --- \texttt{\%p} is the *reflection hash*,
\texttt{p\ "…"} is the *output verb*. The sigil disambiguates.

See also: \texttt{\%stryke::primaries}, \texttt{\%a} /
\texttt{\%stryke::aliases}, \texttt{\%b} / \texttt{\%stryke::builtins}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%parameters}}{\%parameters}}\label{parameters}

\texttt{\%parameters} --- reflection hash mapping each stryke builtin to
its parameter signature (argument names, types, optionality, default
values). Populated at build time from the function definitions in
\texttt{strykelang/builtins*.rs}. Drives LSP signature help and type
checking.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $parameters\{pmap\}              \# signature for pmap}
\NormalTok{keys \%parameters |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} e p   \# every documented signature}
\NormalTok{keys \%parameters |\textgreater{} grep \{ @\{$parameters\{\_\}\} \textgreater{} 3 \} |\textgreater{} e p   \# 4+ arg builtins}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\%b} / \texttt{\%stryke::builtins}, LSP signature
provider.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%pc}}{\%pc}}\label{pc}

\texttt{\%pc} --- short alias for \texttt{\%stryke::perl\_compats}. Perl
5 core names only, name → category. Subset of \texttt{\%b} restricted to
names from \texttt{is\_perl5\_core}. Direct O(1) access for the "show me
just Perl core" query.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $pc\{map\}                                    \# "array / list"}
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%pc                             \# core{-}only count}
\NormalTok{keys \%pc |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} e p                       \# enumerate every Perl core name}
\NormalTok{keys \%pc |\textgreater{} grep \{ $pc\{$\_\} eq "array / list" \} |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\%stryke::perl\_compats}, \texttt{\%e} /
\texttt{\%stryke::extensions}, \texttt{\%b} /
\texttt{\%stryke::builtins}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%stryke::aliases}}{\%stryke::aliases}}\label{strykealiases}

\texttt{\%stryke::aliases} (short: \texttt{\%a}) --- alias spelling →
canonical primary.

Keys are the 2nd-and-later names in each \texttt{try\_builtin} match
arm. For O(1) *reverse* lookup (primary → all its aliases), use
\texttt{\%stryke::primaries} / \texttt{\%p}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $stryke::aliases\{tj\}                                  \# "to\_json"}
\NormalTok{p $stryke::aliases\{bn\}                                  \# "basename"}
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%stryke::aliases                          \# total alias count}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%stryke::all}}{\%stryke::all}}\label{strykeall}

\texttt{\%stryke::all} (short: \texttt{\%all}) --- every callable
*spelling* (primaries \textbf{and} aliases) → category. Aliases inherit
their primary\textquotesingle s category.

Use \texttt{\%all} when you want "how many names can I type?" or want to
look up an alias\textquotesingle s category without hopping through
\texttt{\%aliases}. Use \texttt{\%builtins} when you want unique
operations.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%all   \# total callable{-}spellings count}
\NormalTok{p $all\{tj\}           \# "serialization"  (alias resolves via inheritance)}
\NormalTok{p $all\{to\_json\}      \# "serialization"}
\NormalTok{keys \%all |\textgreater{} pager   \# browse every spelling}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%stryke::builtins}}{\%stryke::builtins}}\label{strykebuiltins}

\texttt{\%stryke::builtins} (short: \texttt{\%b}) --- every
\textbf{primary} callable name → its category. Primaries-only, so
\texttt{scalar\ keys\ \%b} is a clean unique-operation count. For the
"everything you can type" view (primaries + aliases), use
\texttt{\%stryke::all} / \texttt{\%all}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $stryke::builtins\{pmap\}               \# "parallel"}
\NormalTok{p $stryke::builtins\{to\_json\}            \# "serialization"}
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%stryke::builtins         \# unique{-}op count}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%stryke::categories}}{\%stryke::categories}}\label{strykecategories}

\texttt{\%stryke::categories} (short: \texttt{\%c}) --- category string
→ arrayref of names in that category.

Inverted index on \texttt{\%builtins}. Gives O(1) reverse-lookup for
"list every op of kind X" queries that would otherwise be O(n)
\texttt{grep}s. Name lists are alphabetized.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{$c\{parallel\} |\textgreater{} e p                  \# every parallel op}
\NormalTok{p scalar @\{ $c\{parallel\} \}           \# how many?}
\NormalTok{p join ", ", @\{ $c\{"array / list"\} \} \# joined roster}
\NormalTok{keys \%c |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} p                 \# all category names}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%stryke::descriptions}}{\%stryke::descriptions}}\label{strykedescriptions}

\texttt{\%stryke::descriptions} (short: \texttt{\%d}) --- name →
one-line summary.

First sentence of each LSP hover doc (\texttt{doc\_for\_label\_text}),
harvested at build time. Sparse --- only names that have a hover doc
appear, so \texttt{exists\ \$d\{\$name\}} doubles as "is this
documented?".

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $d\{pmap\}                                \# one{-}line summary}
\NormalTok{p $d\{to\_json\}                             \# "Serialize a StrykeValue to a JSON string."}
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%d                          \# count of documented ops}
\NormalTok{keys \%d |\textgreater{} grep \{ $d\{\_\} =\textasciitilde{} /parallel/i \} |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%stryke::extensions}}{\%stryke::extensions}}\label{strykeextensions}

\texttt{\%stryke::extensions} (short: \texttt{\%e}) --- stryke-only
names, name → category.

Disjoint from \texttt{\%perl\_compats}. Everything \texttt{-\/-compat}
mode rejects at parse time, plus dispatch primaries like
\texttt{basename}/\texttt{ddump} that are extensions at runtime even
without a parser entry.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $e\{pmap\}                                \# "parallel"}
\NormalTok{keys \%e |\textgreater{} grep /\^{}p/ |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} p         \# every p* parallel op}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%stryke::keywords}}{\%stryke::keywords}}\label{strykekeywords}

\texttt{\%stryke::keywords} (short: \texttt{\%k}) --- language keyword →
one-line category/description. Includes \texttt{fn}, \texttt{match},
\texttt{class}, \texttt{struct}, \texttt{enum}, \texttt{mysync},
\texttt{frozen}, \texttt{async}/\texttt{await}/\texttt{spawn},
control-flow keywords, declaration keywords, etc.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%stryke::keywords}
\NormalTok{keys \%stryke::keywords |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\%k} (short alias), \texttt{\%stryke::builtins}
(callable names rather than keywords).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%stryke::operators}}{\%stryke::operators}}\label{strykeoperators}

\texttt{\%stryke::operators} (short: \texttt{\%o}) --- symbol operator
spelling → category. Built from the \texttt{OPERATORS} table in
\texttt{builtins.rs}, which mirrors the operator token set in
\texttt{token.rs} (every \texttt{Token::Plus} /
\texttt{Token::Spaceship} / \texttt{Token::ThreadArrow} \ldots).
Disjoint from \texttt{\%stryke::keywords}: word operators (\texttt{and},
\texttt{or}, \texttt{eq}, \texttt{cmp}, \texttt{x}, \texttt{not}) appear
in \texttt{\%k} with category \texttt{"operator"}, while only
symbol/punctuation operators appear here.

Categories: \texttt{arith}, \texttt{compare}, \texttt{logical},
\texttt{bitwise}, \texttt{assign}, \texttt{string}, \texttt{binding},
\texttt{pipeline}, \texttt{range}, \texttt{arrow}, \texttt{deref},
\texttt{inc}, \texttt{ternary}, \texttt{sigil}, \texttt{punct}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%stryke::operators}
\NormalTok{keys \%stryke::operators |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{keys \%stryke::operators |\textgreater{} grep \{ $stryke::operators\{\_\} eq "pipeline" \} |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\%o} (short alias), \texttt{\%stryke::keywords} (word
operators), \texttt{\%stryke::special\_vars}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%stryke::perl\_compats}}{\%stryke::perl\_compats}}\label{strykeperl_compats}

\texttt{\%stryke::perl\_compats} (short: \texttt{\%pc}) --- Perl 5 core
names only, name → category.

Subset of \texttt{\%builtins} restricted to names from
\texttt{is\_perl5\_core}. Direct O(1) access for the "show me just Perl
core" query.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $pc\{map\}                    \# "array / list"}
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%pc             \# core{-}only count}
\NormalTok{keys \%pc |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} p         \# enumerate every Perl core name}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%stryke::primaries}}{\%stryke::primaries}}\label{strykeprimaries}

\texttt{\%stryke::primaries} (short: \texttt{\%p}) --- primary
dispatcher name → arrayref of its aliases.

Inverted \texttt{\%aliases}. Primaries with no aliases still have an
entry (empty arrayref), so \texttt{exists\ \$p\{foo\}} reliably answers
"is foo a dispatch primary?" O(1).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{$p\{to\_json\} |\textgreater{} e p              \# ["tj"]}
\NormalTok{p scalar @\{ $p\{basename\} \}      \# how many aliases does basename have?}
\NormalTok{\# find every primary that has at least one alias:}
\NormalTok{keys \%p |\textgreater{} grep \{ scalar @\{$p\{\_\}\} \} |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%stryke::special\_vars}}{\%stryke::special\_vars}}\label{strykespecial_vars}

\texttt{\%stryke::special\_vars} (short: \texttt{\%v}) --- special
variable spelling → category. Every key carries its sigil
(\texttt{"\$!"}, \texttt{"@ARGV"}, \texttt{"\%ENV"}, \texttt{"\$\^{}X"},
\texttt{"\$stryke::VERSION"}, \texttt{"\_\_FILE\_\_"}) so a single hash
covers scalar/array/hash/caret/compile-time forms uniformly. Every entry
has a hand-written hover doc in \texttt{lsp.rs::doc\_for\_label\_text},
so \texttt{exists\ \$v\{\$name\}} doubles as "is this documented?".

Categories partition by purpose: \texttt{topic}, \texttt{error},
\texttt{pid}, \texttt{regex-match}, \texttt{regex-capture}, \texttt{io},
\texttt{process}, \texttt{args}, \texttt{env}, \texttt{signal},
\texttt{script}, \texttt{caret}, \texttt{subscript}, \texttt{version},
\texttt{reflection}, \texttt{format}, \texttt{sort}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%stryke::special\_vars}
\NormalTok{p $stryke::special\_vars\{\textquotesingle{}$!\textquotesingle{}\}                   \# "error"}
\NormalTok{p $stryke::special\_vars\{\textquotesingle{}@ARGV\textquotesingle{}\}                \# "args"}
\NormalTok{p $stryke::special\_vars\{\textquotesingle{}\%ENV\textquotesingle{}\}                 \# "env"}
\NormalTok{p $stryke::special\_vars\{\textquotesingle{}$\^{}X\textquotesingle{}\}                  \# "caret"}
\NormalTok{keys \%stryke::special\_vars |\textgreater{} grep \{ $stryke::special\_vars\{\_\} eq "regex{-}capture" \} |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\%v} (short alias), \texttt{\%stryke::operators},
\texttt{\%stryke::keywords}, \texttt{\%stryke::builtins}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%term}}{\%term}}\label{term}

\texttt{\%term} --- reflection hash describing the controlling terminal:
width, height, color depth, capabilities (Unicode support, true-color,
focus events, alternate screen). Populated lazily on first read by
querying \texttt{tput} / \texttt{tcgetattr} / \texttt{TIOCGWINSZ}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $term\{width\}             \# columns}
\NormalTok{p $term\{height\}            \# rows}
\NormalTok{p $term\{color\}             \# "truecolor" / "256" / "16" / "none"}
\NormalTok{p keys \%term |\textgreater{} sort}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{\%uname} (OS info),
\texttt{term\_width}/\texttt{term\_height} builtins.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{\%v}}{\%v}}\label{v-1}

\texttt{\%v} --- short alias for \texttt{\%stryke::special\_vars}. Maps
every special variable spelling (sigil included) to its category.

One hash covers every kind of special variable: scalar (\texttt{\$!},
\texttt{\$\_}, \texttt{\$@}, \texttt{\$1}..\texttt{\$9},
\texttt{\$\^{}X}), array (\texttt{@\_}, \texttt{@ARGV}, \texttt{@+},
\texttt{@-}, \texttt{@\^{}CAPTURE}), hash (\texttt{\%ENV},
\texttt{\%INC}, \texttt{\%SIG}, \texttt{\%\^{}H}), caret
(\texttt{\$\^{}A}..\texttt{\$\^{}X}, \texttt{\%\^{}H},
\texttt{\%\^{}HOOK}), compile-time (\texttt{\_\_FILE\_\_},
\texttt{\_\_LINE\_\_}, \texttt{\_\_PACKAGE\_\_}, \texttt{\_\_SUB\_\_}),
and version (\texttt{\$stryke::VERSION}). Categories: \texttt{topic},
\texttt{error}, \texttt{pid}, \texttt{regex-match},
\texttt{regex-capture}, \texttt{io}, \texttt{process}, \texttt{args},
\texttt{env}, \texttt{signal}, \texttt{script}, \texttt{caret},
\texttt{subscript}, \texttt{version}, \texttt{reflection},
\texttt{format}, \texttt{sort}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p $v\{\textquotesingle{}$!\textquotesingle{}\}                                \# "error"}
\NormalTok{p $v\{\textquotesingle{}@ARGV\textquotesingle{}\}                             \# "args"}
\NormalTok{p $v\{\textquotesingle{}$1\textquotesingle{}\}                                \# "regex{-}capture"}
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%v                          \# total special{-}var count}
\NormalTok{keys \%v |\textgreater{} grep \{ $v\{\_\} eq "caret" \} |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{keys \%v |\textgreater{} grep \{ $v\{\_\} eq "regex{-}capture" \} |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Every key has a matching LSP hover entry, so
\texttt{exists\ \$v\{\$name\}} doubles as "is this special var
documented?".

See also: \texttt{\%stryke::special\_vars}, \texttt{\%o} /
\texttt{\%stryke::operators}, \texttt{\%k} /
\texttt{\%stryke::keywords}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{@+}}{@+}}\label{section-86}

\texttt{@+} --- array of byte offsets *one past the end* of each match
group in the most recent successful regex. \texttt{\$+{[}0{]}} is the
end of the whole match; \texttt{\$+{[}1{]}} is the end of \texttt{\$1};
etc. Pair with \texttt{@-} (start offsets) for \texttt{substr}-based
slicing without re-running the regex.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $s = "foo bar baz"}
\NormalTok{if ($s =\textasciitilde{} /(\textbackslash{}w+) (\textbackslash{}w+)/) \{}
