\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{comm}}{comm}}\label{comm}

\texttt{comm(\textbackslash{}@A,\ \textbackslash{}@B)} --- 3-column
compare of two SORTED lists. Returns an arrayref
\texttt{{[}\$only\_a,\ \$only\_b,\ \$both{]}} where each entry is an
arrayref of lines. Mirrors POSIX \texttt{comm}: input must already be
sorted (lexical); use \texttt{sort} first if unsure.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $r = comm([sort @set\_a], [sort @set\_b])}
\NormalTok{p "only in A: " . scalar @\{$r{-}\textgreater{}[0]\}}
\NormalTok{p "only in B: " . scalar @\{$r{-}\textgreater{}[1]\}}
\NormalTok{p "in both: "   . scalar @\{$r{-}\textgreater{}[2]\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cond}}{cond}}\label{cond}

\texttt{matrix\_cond} (aliases \texttt{mcond}, \texttt{cond}) estimates
the condition number of a matrix (ratio of largest to smallest singular
value). Large values indicate ill-conditioning.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cond([[1,0],[0,1]])    \# 1 (perfect)}
\NormalTok{p cond([[1,2],[2,4]])    \# Inf (singular)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{conditional\_entropy}}{conditional\_entropy}}\label{conditional_entropy}

\texttt{conditional\_entropy(joint\_matrix)\ →\ number} ---
H(X\textbar Y) from joint distribution P(X,Y).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{confint}}{confint}}\label{confint}

\texttt{confint\_lm} (alias \texttt{confint}) --- confidence intervals
for model coefficients. Returns hash with intercept\_lower/upper,
slope\_lower/upper.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $ci = confint(lm([1,2,3,4,5], [2,4,5,4,5]))}
\NormalTok{p $ci{-}\textgreater{}\{slope\_lower\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{consistent\_hash}}{consistent\_hash}}\label{consistent_hash}

\texttt{hash\_ring(VNODES\_PER\_NODE=128)} --- consistent-hash ring
(Karger-style virtual nodes). Adds and removes nodes with minimal key
remapping (≈ 1/(N+1) of keys move when a node joins N existing nodes).
xxh3-64 hashing; binary search lookup is O(log V).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $hr = hash\_ring(128)}
\NormalTok{hr\_add($hr, "shard{-}a", "shard{-}b", "shard{-}c")}
\NormalTok{my $owner = hr\_get($hr, "user{-}42")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{constants\_au\_meters()\ →\ number} --- 1 AU = 1.495978707·10¹¹
m (exact).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{constants\_avogadro\_n}}{constants\_avogadro\_n}}\label{constants_avogadro_n}

\texttt{constants\_avogadro\_n()\ →\ number} --- N\_A = 6.02214076·10²³
mol⁻¹ (exact by SI definition).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{constants\_bohr\_radius}}{constants\_bohr\_radius}}\label{constants_bohr_radius}

\texttt{constants\_bohr\_radius()\ →\ number} --- a₀ =
5.29177210903·10⁻¹¹ m.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{constants\_boltzmann\_k}}{constants\_boltzmann\_k}}\label{constants_boltzmann_k}

\texttt{constants\_boltzmann\_k()\ →\ number} --- k\_B = 1.380649·10⁻²³
J/K (exact).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{constants\_earth\_mass}}{constants\_earth\_mass}}\label{constants_earth_mass}

\texttt{constants\_earth\_mass()\ →\ number} --- M\_⊕ = 5.972·10²⁴ kg.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{constants\_earth\_radius}}{constants\_earth\_radius}}\label{constants_earth_radius}

\texttt{constants\_earth\_radius()\ →\ number} --- R\_⊕ = 6.371·10⁶ m
(mean).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{constants\_electron\_charge}}{constants\_electron\_charge}}\label{constants_electron_charge}

\texttt{constants\_electron\_charge()\ →\ number} --- e =
1.602176634·10⁻¹⁹ C (exact by SI definition).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{constants\_electron\_mass}}{constants\_electron\_mass}}\label{constants_electron_mass}

\texttt{constants\_electron\_mass()\ →\ number} --- m\_e =
9.1093837015·10⁻³¹ kg.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{constants\_faraday}}{constants\_faraday}}\label{constants_faraday}

\texttt{constants\_faraday()\ →\ number} --- F = 96,485.33212 C/mol.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{constants\_gas\_r}}{constants\_gas\_r}}\label{constants_gas_r}

\texttt{constants\_gas\_r()\ →\ number} --- R = 8.314462618 J/(mol·K).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{constants\_gravitational\_g}}{constants\_gravitational\_g}}\label{constants_gravitational_g}

\texttt{constants\_gravitational\_g()\ →\ number} --- G = 6.6743·10⁻¹¹
m³/(kg·s²).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{constants\_lightyear\_meters}}{constants\_lightyear\_meters}}\label{constants_lightyear_meters}

\texttt{constants\_lightyear\_meters()\ →\ number} --- 1 ly =
9.4607304725808·10¹⁵ m.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{constants\_neutron\_mass}}{constants\_neutron\_mass}}\label{constants_neutron_mass}

\texttt{constants\_neutron\_mass()\ →\ number} --- m\_n =
1.67492749804·10⁻²⁷ kg.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{constants\_parsec\_meters}}{constants\_parsec\_meters}}\label{constants_parsec_meters}

\texttt{constants\_parsec\_meters()\ →\ number} --- 1 pc =
3.085677581·10¹⁶ m.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{constants\_planck}}{constants\_planck}}\label{constants_planck}

\texttt{constants\_planck()\ →\ number} --- Planck\textquotesingle s
constant h = 6.62607015·10⁻³⁴ J·s.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{constants\_planck\_h}}{constants\_planck\_h}}\label{constants_planck_h}

\texttt{constants\_planck\_h()\ →\ number} --- Planck constant (alias).

\texttt{constants\_planck\_hbar()\ →\ number} --- Reduced Planck

\texttt{constants\_proton\_mass()\ →\ number} --- m\_p =

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{constants\_rydberg}}{constants\_rydberg}}\label{constants_rydberg}

\texttt{constants\_rydberg()\ →\ number} --- R\_∞ = 1.0973731568160·10⁷

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{constants\_solar\_mass}}{constants\_solar\_mass}}\label{constants_solar_mass}

\texttt{constants\_solar\_mass()\ →\ number} --- M\_⊙ = 1.989·10³⁰ kg.

\texttt{constants\_solar\_radius()\ →\ number} --- R\_⊙ = 6.957·10⁸ m.

\texttt{contour\_centroid(points)\ →\ {[}x,\ y{]}} --- area-weighted
centroid of the closed polygon (\texttt{M10/M00,\ M01/M00} per OpenCV).
Falls back to vertex mean if the polygon has zero signed area.

\texttt{contour\_find(image,\ thresh=128)\ →\ array} --- boundary cells
of foreground regions as \texttt{{[}{[}row,\ col{]},\ …{]}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{contour\_perimeter}}{contour\_perimeter}}\label{contour_perimeter}

\texttt{contour\_perimeter(points)\ →\ number} --- perimeter of a
polygon defined by an ordered point list.

\texttt{conv1d\_apply(signal,\ kernel)\ →\ array} --- 1D convolution
with zero-padding.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{conv2d\_apply}}{conv2d\_apply}}\label{conv2d_apply}

\texttt{conv2d\_apply(image,\ kernel)\ →\ matrix} --- true mathematical
2D convolution: kernel is \textbf{flipped} in both axes before the
sum-of-products. Zero-padded; output has same dimensions as input. Use
\texttt{correlate2d} for the cross-correlation form (no flip).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p conv2d\_apply($img, gaussian\_kernel(5, 1.0))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

set.

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

distance in meters.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p coulomb(1e{-}6, 1e{-}6, 0.1)   \# \textasciitilde{}0.9 N (two 1μC charges 10cm apart)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{crc10\_atm}}{crc10\_atm}}\label{crc10_atm}

\texttt{crc10\_atm(s)\ →\ int} --- CRC-10/ATM AAL5 checksum.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{crc12\_dect}}{crc12\_dect}}\label{crc12_dect}

\texttt{crc12\_dect(s)\ →\ int} --- CRC-12/DECT (poly 0x80F).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{crc16}}{crc16}}\label{crc16}

\texttt{crc16(s)\ →\ int} --- CRC-16/Modbus (poly 0xA001, init 0xFFFF,
reflected). Verifies serial-protocol frames.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p crc16("123456789")  \# 0x4B37}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{crc16\_ccitt}}{crc16\_ccitt}}\label{crc16_ccitt}

\texttt{crc16\_ccitt} --- CRC checksum variant \texttt{16\ ccitt}.
Category: *misc*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p crc16\_ccitt $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{crc16\_xmodem}}{crc16\_xmodem}}\label{crc16_xmodem}

\texttt{crc16\_xmodem(s)\ →\ int} --- CRC-16/XMODEM (poly 0x1021, init
0). Used in XMODEM file transfer.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p crc16\_xmodem("123456789")  \# 0x31C3}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{crc24}}{crc24}}\label{crc24}

\texttt{crc24(s)\ →\ int} --- CRC-24/OpenPGP (poly 0x864CFB, init
0xB704CE).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p crc24("")  \# 0xB704CE}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{crc32\_bzip2}}{crc32\_bzip2}}\label{crc32_bzip2}

\texttt{crc32\_bzip2(s)\ →\ int} --- CRC-32/BZIP2 (non-reflected).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{crc32\_jamcrc}}{crc32\_jamcrc}}\label{crc32_jamcrc}

\texttt{crc32\_jamcrc(s)\ →\ int} --- CRC-32/JAMCRC (reflected, xorout
0).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{crc32\_mpeg2}}{crc32\_mpeg2}}\label{crc32_mpeg2}

\texttt{crc32\_mpeg2(s)\ →\ int} --- CRC-32/MPEG-2 (non-reflected,
xorout 0).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{crc32\_xfer}}{crc32\_xfer}}\label{crc32_xfer}

\texttt{crc32\_xfer(s)\ →\ int} --- CRC-32/XFER (XFER protocol).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{crc32\_zlib}}{crc32\_zlib}}\label{crc32_zlib}

\texttt{crc32\_zlib(s)\ →\ int} --- CRC-32/ISO-HDLC (zlib/gzip
standard).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p crc32\_zlib("123456789")  \# 0xCBF43926}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{crc32c}}{crc32c}}\label{crc32c}

\texttt{crc32c(s)\ →\ int} --- CRC-32C (Castagnoli, poly 0x82F63B78).
Used by iSCSI/Btrfs/SCTP.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p crc32c("123456789")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{crc64\_ecma}}{crc64\_ecma}}\label{crc64_ecma}

\texttt{crc64\_ecma(s)\ →\ int} --- CRC-64/ECMA-182 (poly
0x42F0E1EBA9EA3693).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p crc64\_ecma("abc")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{crc64\_xz}}{crc64\_xz}}\label{crc64_xz}

\texttt{crc64\_xz(s)\ →\ int} --- CRC-64/XZ (reflected). Used in xz/lzma
file integrity.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{crc6\_itu}}{crc6\_itu}}\label{crc6_itu}

\texttt{crc6\_itu(s)\ →\ int} --- CRC-6/ITU (poly 0x03, reflected).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{crc8}}{crc8}}\label{crc8}

\texttt{crc8(s)\ →\ int} --- CRC-8 (polynomial 0x07, init 0). Common for
sensor checksums.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p crc8("123456789")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

--- central credible interval from a Beta posterior.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{credible\_interval\_normal}}{credible\_interval\_normal}}\label{credible_interval_normal}

\texttt{credible\_interval\_normal(mean,\ sd,\ level=0.95)\ →\ {[}lo,\ hi{]}}
--- central credible interval from a Normal posterior.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{crf\_log\_likelihood}}{crf\_log\_likelihood}}\label{crf_log_likelihood}

\texttt{crf\_log\_likelihood} --- operation

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p crf\_log\_likelihood $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{crindex}}{crindex}}\label{crindex}

\texttt{crindex\ STRING,\ SUBSTRING\ {[},\ FROM{]}} ---
codepoint-indexed equivalent of Perl 5\textquotesingle s
\texttt{rindex}. Returns the last codepoint position where SUBSTRING
occurs (at or before FROM, defaulting to end-of-string), or -1 on miss.
Counterpart to \texttt{cindex}; pairs with \texttt{{[}{]}} slice
operators and \texttt{len}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $path = "foo/bar/─/baz.gz"}
\NormalTok{p rindex  $path, "/"        \# 13 (bytes)}
\NormalTok{p crindex $path, "/"        \# 11 (codepoints)}
\NormalTok{my $i = crindex $path, "/"}
\NormalTok{p $path[$i + 1 : len($path) {-} 1]   \# "baz.gz"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cross\_correlation}}{cross\_correlation}}\label{cross_correlation}

\texttt{cross\_correlation(\textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b)}
(alias \texttt{xcorr}) --- computes the cross-correlation of two
signals. Measures similarity as a function of time-lag. Peak location
indicates time delay between signals.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $xcor = xcorr(\textbackslash{}@signal1, \textbackslash{}@signal2)}
\NormalTok{my $lag = max\_index(@$xcor)   \# find peak lag}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cumtrapz}}{cumtrapz}}\label{cumtrapz}

\texttt{cumtrapz} cumulative trapezoidal integration. Returns running
integral array.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @y = (1, 2, 3, 4)}
\NormalTok{my @F = @\{cumtrapz(\textbackslash{}@y)\}  \# [0, 1.5, 4.0, 7.5]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{curl\_get}}{curl\_get}}\label{curl_get}

\texttt{curl\_get(URL)} → response body as a string, or \texttt{undef}
on failure. Shell-named wrapper over the existing \texttt{ureq} HTTP
client. For full HTTP semantics (custom headers, redirects, JSON
parsing), use the lower-level \texttt{fetch\_val} /
\texttt{fetch\_async} / \texttt{http\_*} family.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $page = curl\_get("https://example.com")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{curl\_post}}{curl\_post}}\label{curl_post}

\texttt{curl\_post(URL,\ BODY)} → POST \texttt{BODY} to \texttt{URL}.
\texttt{Content-Type} auto-detected: \texttt{application/json} when
\texttt{BODY} parses as JSON, else \texttt{text/plain}. Returns response
body as a string, or \texttt{undef} on failure.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cut}}{cut}}\label{cut}

\texttt{cut} --- bin continuous values into intervals. Returns integer
bin indices. Like R\textquotesingle s cut().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cut([1.5, 3.2, 7.8], [0, 2, 5, 10])  \# [1, 2, 3]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cutree}}{cutree}}\label{cutree}

\texttt{cutree} --- cut a dendrogram into k clusters. Takes merge list
from hclust and k. Returns cluster assignments. Like R\textquotesingle s
cutree().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @clusters = @\{cutree(hclust(dist\_mat($data)), 3)\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cyber\_banner}}{cyber\_banner}}\label{cyber_banner}

\texttt{cyber\_banner} (alias \texttt{neon\_banner}) --- large neon
block-letter banner with gradient coloring and border. Args: text.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cyber\_banner("STRYKE")}
\NormalTok{p cyber\_banner("HACK THE PLANET")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cyber\_circuit}}{cyber\_circuit}}\label{cyber_circuit}

\texttt{cyber\_circuit} --- circuit board pattern with traces,
intersections, and glowing nodes. Args: {[}width, height, seed{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cyber\_circuit()             \# 60x20 default}
\NormalTok{p cyber\_circuit(80, 30, 42)   \# custom}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cyber\_city}}{cyber\_city}}\label{cyber_city}

\texttt{cyber\_city} --- procedural neon cityscape with buildings,
windows, stars, and antennas. Args: {[}width, height, seed{]}. Output:
ANSI-colored string for terminal.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cyber\_city()              \# 80x24 default}
\NormalTok{p cyber\_city(120, 40, 99)   \# custom size and seed}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cyber\_eye}}{cyber\_eye}}\label{cyber_eye}

\texttt{cyber\_eye} --- cyberpunk all-seeing eye motif with layered
glow. Args: {[}size{]}. Size: "small" (default) or "large".

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cyber\_eye()          \# small eye}
\NormalTok{p cyber\_eye("large")   \# large eye}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cyber\_glitch}}{cyber\_glitch}}\label{cyber_glitch}

\texttt{cyber\_glitch} (alias \texttt{glitch\_text}) --- glitch-distort
text with ANSI corruption, screen tears, and neon color bleeding. Args:
text {[}, intensity 1-10{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cyber\_glitch("SYSTEM BREACH", 7)}
\NormalTok{p cyber\_glitch("hello world")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cyber\_grid}}{cyber\_grid}}\label{cyber_grid}

\texttt{cyber\_grid} --- retro perspective grid with vanishing point and
neon glow (Tron/synthwave style). Args: {[}width, height{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cyber\_grid()           \# 80x24 default}
\NormalTok{p cyber\_grid(120, 30)    \# wider grid}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cyber\_rain}}{cyber\_rain}}\label{cyber_rain}

\texttt{cyber\_rain} (alias \texttt{matrix\_rain}) --- matrix-style
digital rain with Japanese katakana and green phosphor glow. Args:
{[}width, height, seed{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cyber\_rain()              \# 80x24 default}
\NormalTok{p cyber\_rain(120, 40, 42)   \# custom}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{cyber\_skull}}{cyber\_skull}}\label{cyber_skull}

\texttt{cyber\_skull} --- neon skull ASCII art with glitch effects.
Args: {[}size{]}. Size: "small" (default) or "large".

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p cyber\_skull()          \# small skull}
\NormalTok{p cyber\_skull("large")   \# large skull}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_add\_days}}{date\_add\_days}}\label{date_add_days}

\texttt{date\_add\_days(unix\_or\_iso,\ n)\ →\ int} --- returns unix
timestamp \texttt{n} days later.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p date\_add\_days("2024{-}01{-}01", 30)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_add\_months}}{date\_add\_months}}\label{date_add_months}

\texttt{date\_add\_months(unix\_or\_iso,\ n)\ →\ int} --- adds whole
calendar months; clamps the day to the last valid day of the target
month (so Jan 31 + 1mo → Feb 28/29, May 31 + 1mo → Jun 30).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p date\_add\_months("2024{-}01{-}31", 1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_add\_years}}{date\_add\_years}}\label{date_add_years}

\texttt{date\_add\_years(unix\_or\_iso,\ n)\ →\ int} --- adds whole
calendar years; clamps Feb 29 to Feb 28 in non-leap target years;
otherwise preserves the original day.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p date\_add\_years("2024{-}02{-}29", 1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_business\_days\_between}}{date\_business\_days\_between}}\label{date_business_days_between}

\texttt{date\_business\_days\_between(d1,\ d2)\ →\ int} --- weekday
count (Mon-Fri) strictly between two dates.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p date\_business\_days\_between("2024{-}01{-}01", "2024{-}01{-}15")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_day}}{date\_day}}\label{date_day}

\texttt{date\_day(unix\_or\_iso)\ →\ int} --- day of month 1-31.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_dayofweek}}{date\_dayofweek}}\label{date_dayofweek}

\texttt{date\_dayofweek(unix\_or\_iso)\ →\ int} --- day of week 0-6,
Sunday=0.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p date\_dayofweek("2024{-}03{-}15")  \# 5 (Friday)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_dayofyear}}{date\_dayofyear}}\label{date_dayofyear}

\texttt{date\_dayofyear(unix\_or\_iso)\ →\ int} --- ordinal day 1-366.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p date\_dayofyear("2024{-}12{-}31")  \# 366}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_days\_in\_month}}{date\_days\_in\_month}}\label{date_days_in_month}

\texttt{date\_days\_in\_month(year,\ month)\ →\ int} --- days in given
month accounting for leap years.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p date\_days\_in\_month(2024, 2)  \# 29}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_diff\_days}}{date\_diff\_days}}\label{date_diff_days}

\texttt{date\_diff\_days(d1,\ d2)\ →\ int} --- \texttt{d2\ -\ d1} in
whole days.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p date\_diff\_days("2024{-}01{-}01", "2024{-}12{-}31")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_diff\_hours}}{date\_diff\_hours}}\label{date_diff_hours}

\texttt{date\_diff\_hours(d1,\ d2)\ →\ int} --- hours between two
timestamps.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_diff\_minutes}}{date\_diff\_minutes}}\label{date_diff_minutes}

\texttt{date\_diff\_minutes(d1,\ d2)\ →\ int} --- minutes between two
timestamps.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_diff\_seconds}}{date\_diff\_seconds}}\label{date_diff_seconds}

\texttt{date\_diff\_seconds(d1,\ d2)\ →\ int} --- seconds between two
timestamps.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_easter}}{date\_easter}}\label{date_easter}

\texttt{date\_easter(year)\ →\ string} --- Western Christian Easter
Sunday date (Anonymous Gregorian algorithm).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p date\_easter(2024)  \# "2024{-}03{-}31"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_first\_of\_month}}{date\_first\_of\_month}}\label{date_first_of_month}

\texttt{date\_first\_of\_month(unix\_or\_iso)\ →\ int} --- unix
timestamp of the first day of the same month at 00:00 UTC.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p date\_first\_of\_month("2024{-}03{-}15")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_hour}}{date\_hour}}\label{date_hour}

\texttt{date\_hour(unix\_or\_iso)\ →\ int} --- hour 0-23 (UTC).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_is\_leap}}{date\_is\_leap}}\label{date_is_leap}

\texttt{date\_is\_leap(year)\ →\ 0\textbar{}1} --- Gregorian leap-year
rule: divisible by 4, except by 100, except by 400.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p date\_is\_leap(2000)  \# 1}
\NormalTok{p date\_is\_leap(2100)  \# 0}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_is\_weekend}}{date\_is\_weekend}}\label{date_is_weekend}

\texttt{date\_is\_weekend(unix\_or\_iso)\ →\ 0\textbar{}1} --- 1 if
Saturday or Sunday.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p date\_is\_weekend("2024{-}03{-}16")  \# 1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_iso\_format}}{date\_iso\_format}}\label{date_iso_format}

\texttt{date\_iso\_format(unix\_or\_iso)\ →\ string} --- RFC3339
representation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p date\_iso\_format(0)  \# "1970{-}01{-}01T00:00:00+00:00"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_iso\_week}}{date\_iso\_week}}\label{date_iso_week}

\texttt{date\_iso\_week(unix\_or\_iso)\ →\ string} --- ISO 8601 week
format \texttt{YYYY-Www}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p date\_iso\_week("2024{-}03{-}15")  \# "2024{-}W11"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_last\_of\_month}}{date\_last\_of\_month}}\label{date_last_of_month}

\texttt{date\_last\_of\_month(unix\_or\_iso)\ →\ int} --- unix timestamp
of the last day of the same month.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p date\_last\_of\_month("2024{-}02{-}15")  \# 2024{-}02{-}29}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_minute}}{date\_minute}}\label{date_minute}

\texttt{date\_minute(unix\_or\_iso)\ →\ int} --- minute 0-59.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_month}}{date\_month}}\label{date_month}

\texttt{date\_month(unix\_or\_iso)\ →\ int} --- month 1-12.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_quarter}}{date\_quarter}}\label{date_quarter}

\texttt{date\_quarter(unix\_or\_iso)\ →\ int} --- calendar quarter 1-4.

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_str\_to\_unix}}{date\_str\_to\_unix}}\label{date_str_to_unix}

\texttt{date\_str\_to\_unix(s,\ fmt="\%Y-\%m-\%d\ \%H:\%M:\%S")\ →\ int}
--- parse a date string back to unix timestamp.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]

\end{Highlighting}

\texttt{date\_unix\_to\_str(unix,\ fmt="\%Y-\%m-\%d\ \%H:\%M:\%S")\ →\ string}
--- strftime-format a unix timestamp.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p date\_unix\_to\_str(1672531200, "\%Y{-}\%m{-}\%d")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_weekofyear}}{date\_weekofyear}}\label{date_weekofyear}

\texttt{date\_weekofyear(unix\_or\_iso)\ →\ int} --- ISO 8601 week

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p date\_weekofyear("2024{-}01{-}01")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{date\_year}}{date\_year}}\label{date_year}

\texttt{date\_year(unix\_or\_iso)\ →\ int} --- calendar year of a UTC

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p date\_year("2024{-}03{-}15")  \# 2024}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dct}}{dct}}\label{dct}

\texttt{dct} computes the Type-II Discrete Cosine Transform of a signal.
Used in JPEG, MP3, and speech processing.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @coeffs = @\{dct([1,2,3,4])\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{de\_broglie\_wavelength}}{de\_broglie\_wavelength}}\label{de_broglie_wavelength}

\texttt{de\_broglie\_wavelength(\$mass,\ \$velocity)} (alias
\texttt{debroglie}) --- quantum wavelength λ = h/(mv). Defaults to
electron mass.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p debroglie(9.109e{-}31, 1e6)   \# \textasciitilde{}7.3e{-}10 m (electron at 1 km/s)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{decimate}}{decimate}}\label{decimate}

\texttt{downsample(\textbackslash{}@signal,\ \$factor)} (alias
\texttt{decimate}) --- reduces sample rate by keeping every nth sample.
Apply anti-aliasing filter first to avoid aliasing.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $decimated = decimate(\textbackslash{}@signal, 4)  \# keep every 4th sample}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

each subsequent with the difference from its predecessor.

\NormalTok{p delta\_encode([10,12,15,20])  \# [10,2,3,5]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{2·EMA\ −\ EMA(EMA)}. Lower lag than EMA at the same period.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p dema(\textbackslash{}@prices, 14)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{density}}{density}}\label{density}

\texttt{density} --- kernel density estimation with Gaussian kernel and
Silverman bandwidth. Returns {[}{[}x\_grid{]}, {[}density\_values{]}{]}.
Like R\textquotesingle s density().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($x, $y) = @\{density([1,2,2,3,3,3,4,4,5])\}}
\NormalTok{\# $x is grid of 512 points, $y is estimated density}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{depth\_of\_field\_far}}{depth\_of\_field\_far}}\label{depth_of_field_far}

\texttt{depth\_of\_field\_far(focal\_mm,\ aperture,\ dist\_mm,\ coc=0.03)\ →\ number}
--- far limit of DoF (∞ if subject is at/beyond hyperfocal).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{depth\_of\_field\_near}}{depth\_of\_field\_near}}\label{depth_of_field_near}

\texttt{depth\_of\_field\_near(focal\_mm,\ aperture,\ dist\_mm,\ coc=0.03)\ →\ number}
--- near limit of DoF in mm.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{derangement\_count}}{derangement\_count}}\label{derangement_count}

\texttt{derangement\_count(n)\ →\ int} --- number of permutations with
no fixed points: \texttt{D\_n\ =\ (n-1)·(D\_\{n-1\}\ +\ D\_\{n-2\})}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p derangement\_count(5)  \# 44}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{derangements}}{derangements}}\label{derangements}

\texttt{derangements} counts the number of derangements (subfactorial
!n) --- permutations with no fixed points.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p derangements(4)   \# 9}
\NormalTok{p derangements(5)   \# 44}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{deroman}}{deroman}}\label{deroman}

\texttt{deroman} --- operation \texttt{deroman}. Alias for
\texttt{roman\_to\_int}. Category: *roman numerals*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p deroman $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{describe}}{describe}}\label{describe}

\texttt{describe(@data)} --- returns a hashref with comprehensive
descriptive statistics: count, mean, std, min, 25\%, 50\%, 75\%, max.
Similar to pandas DataFrame.describe().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $stats = describe(1:100)}
\NormalTok{p $stats{-}\textgreater{}\{mean\}    \# 50.5}
\NormalTok{p $stats{-}\textgreater{}\{std\}     \# \textasciitilde{}29.0}
\NormalTok{p $stats{-}\textgreater{}\{"50\%"\}   \# median}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{destination\_lat\_lon}}{destination\_lat\_lon}}\label{destination_lat_lon}

\texttt{destination\_lat\_lon(lat,\ lon,\ bearing\_deg,\ distance\_m)\ →\ {[}lat,\ lon{]}}
--- destination point along a great circle from origin.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p destination\_lat\_lon(0, 0, 90, 111320)  \# \textasciitilde{}1° east}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p destination\_point $x}
\end{Highlighting}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{det}}{det}}\label{det}

\texttt{matrix\_det\_general} (aliases \texttt{mdetg}, \texttt{det})
computes the determinant of any NxN matrix via LU decomposition.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p det([[1,2,3],[4,5,6],[7,8,0]])  \# 27}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{detab}}{detab}}\label{detab}

\texttt{detab(s,\ width=8)\ →\ string} --- expand tab characters to
spaces, respecting tab stops.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p detab("\textbackslash{}tab", 4)  \# "    ab"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dft}}{dft}}\label{dft}

\texttt{dft(\textbackslash{}@signal)} --- computes the Discrete Fourier
Transform. Returns arrayref of complex numbers as {[}re, im{]} pairs.
O(n²) reference implementation; for large n, use FFT libraries.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @signal = map \{ sin(2 * 3.14159 * \_ / 64) \} 0:63}
\NormalTok{my $spectrum = dft(\textbackslash{}@signal)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{diff\_array}}{diff\_array}}\label{diff_array}

\texttt{numerical\_diff} (aliases \texttt{numdiff},
\texttt{diff\_array}) computes the numerical first derivative via
central differences. Returns an array.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @y = map \{ \_ ** 2 \} 0:10}
\NormalTok{my @dy = @\{numdiff(\textbackslash{}@y)\}  \# ≈ [0, 2, 4, 6, ...]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{diff\_coeff\_aqueous\_estimate}}{diff\_coeff\_aqueous\_estimate}}\label{diff_coeff_aqueous_estimate}

\texttt{diff\_coeff\_aqueous\_estimate} --- operation
\texttt{diff\ coeff\ aqueous\ estimate}. Category: *electrochemistry,
batteries, fuel cells*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p diff\_coeff\_aqueous\_estimate $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{diff\_in\_diff} --- operation \texttt{diff\ in\ diff}. Category:
*economics + game theory*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p diff\_in\_diff $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{diff\_lag}}{diff\_lag}}\label{diff_lag}

\texttt{diff\_lag} (alias \texttt{diff\_ts}) --- lagged differences.
Args: vec {[}, lag, differences{]}. Like R\textquotesingle s diff().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p diff\_lag([1,3,6,10])       \# [2,3,4] (lag=1)}
\NormalTok{p diff\_lag([1,3,6,10], 2)    \# [5,7] (lag=2)}
\NormalTok{p diff\_lag([1,3,6,10], 1, 2) \# [1,1] (second differences)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{diff\_myers}}{diff\_myers}}\label{diff_myers}

\texttt{myers\_diff(\textbackslash{}@A,\ \textbackslash{}@B)} → list of
\texttt{{[}op,\ value{]}} arrayrefs (op = \texttt{"="}, \texttt{"+"},
\texttt{"-"}). Eugene Myers\textquotesingle{} O((N+M)·D)
longest-common-subsequence diff. Foundational text-diff primitive.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @a = ("alpha", "beta",  "gamma")}
\NormalTok{my @b = ("alpha", "BETA",  "gamma")}
\NormalTok{myers\_diff(\textbackslash{}@a, \textbackslash{}@b) |\textgreater{} e \{ p "$\_{-}\textgreater{}[0] $\_{-}\textgreater{}[1]" \}}
\NormalTok{\# = alpha}
\NormalTok{\# {-} beta}
\NormalTok{\# + BETA}
\NormalTok{\# = gamma}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{diff\_patience}}{diff\_patience}}\label{diff_patience}

\texttt{patience\_diff(\textbackslash{}@A,\ \textbackslash{}@B)} → list
of \texttt{{[}op,\ value{]}} arrayrefs. Bram Cohen\textquotesingle s
patience diff: finds the longest common subsequence of *unique* lines as
anchors, then runs Myers on the gaps between anchors. Produces more
human-readable diffs than Myers on code-like inputs where repeated short
lines (braces, blank lines) make Myers pick noisy alignments.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{diff\_pct}}{diff\_pct}}\label{diff_pct}

\texttt{diff\_pct(series)\ →\ array} --- percentage change between
consecutive points: \texttt{(x{[}i{]}/x{[}i-1{]})\ -\ 1}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p diff\_pct([100, 110, 99])}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{diff\_series}}{diff\_series}}\label{diff_series}

\texttt{diff\_series(series)\ →\ array} --- first-order differences
\texttt{x{[}i+1{]}\ -\ x{[}i{]}}. Used to make a series stationary.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p diff\_series([1,3,6,10,15])  \# [2,3,4,5]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dijkstra}}{dijkstra}}\label{dijkstra}

\texttt{dijkstra} (alias \texttt{shortest\_path}) computes shortest
paths from a source node. Graph is a hash of \{node =\textgreater{}
{[}{[}neighbor, weight{]}, ...{]}\}. Returns \{node =\textgreater{}
distance\}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $g = \{A =\textgreater{} [["B",1],["C",4]], B =\textgreater{} [["C",2]], C =\textgreater{} []\}}
\NormalTok{my $d = dijkstra($g, "A")  \# \{A=\textgreater{}0, B=\textgreater{}1, C=\textgreater{}3\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dijkstra\_relax}}{dijkstra\_relax}}\label{dijkstra_relax}

\texttt{dijkstra\_relax} --- operation \texttt{dijkstra\ relax}.
Category: *networkx graph algorithms*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p dijkstra\_relax $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{disk\_avail}}{disk\_avail}}\label{disk_avail}

\texttt{disk\_avail\ PATH} --- available disk space in bytes for
non-root users. Uses \texttt{f\_bavail} from \texttt{statvfs}, which
excludes blocks reserved for the superuser.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(disk\_avail)               \# 76.3 GiB}
\NormalTok{gauge(1 {-} disk\_avail / disk\_total) |\textgreater{} p  \# usage gauge}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{disk\_free}}{disk\_free}}\label{disk_free}

\texttt{disk\_free\ PATH} --- free disk space in bytes (superuser view).
Uses \texttt{f\_bfree} from \texttt{statvfs}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(disk\_free)                \# 76.3 GiB}
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(disk\_free("/tmp"))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{disk\_total}}{disk\_total}}\label{disk_total}

\texttt{disk\_total\ PATH} --- total disk space in bytes for the
filesystem containing PATH (default \texttt{/}). Uses \texttt{statvfs}
on unix. Returns \texttt{undef} on unsupported platforms.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(disk\_total)               \# 1.8 TiB}
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(disk\_total("/home"))       \# specific mount}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{disk\_used}}{disk\_used}}\label{disk_used}

\texttt{disk\_used\ PATH} --- used disk space in bytes (total - free).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(disk\_used)                \# 1.7 TiB}
\NormalTok{my $pct = int(disk\_used / disk\_total * 100)}
\NormalTok{p "$pct\% disk used"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dissoc}}{dissoc}}\label{dissoc}

\texttt{dissoc} --- operation \texttt{dissoc}. Category: *algebraic
match*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p dissoc $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dist}}{dist}}\label{dist}

\texttt{dist} --- operation \texttt{dist}. Alias for \texttt{distance}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dist\_mat}}{dist\_mat}}\label{dist_mat}

\texttt{dist\_matrix} (alias \texttt{dist\_mat}) --- pairwise distance
matrix. Supports \textquotesingle euclidean\textquotesingle{} (default),
\textquotesingle manhattan\textquotesingle,
\textquotesingle maximum\textquotesingle. Like R\textquotesingle s
dist().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $D = dist\_mat([[0,0],[1,0],[0,1]])  \# 3x3 distance matrix}
\NormalTok{my $D2 = dist\_mat([[0,0],[1,1]], "manhattan")  \# Manhattan}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dna\_at\_content}}{dna\_at\_content}}\label{dna_at_content}

\texttt{dna\_at\_content(seq)\ →\ number} --- fraction of A, T, or U
bases.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dna\_complement}}{dna\_complement}}\label{dna_complement}

\texttt{dna\_complement(seq)\ →\ string} --- Watson-Crick complement
(A↔T, G↔C). Case-preserving; non-ACGT chars pass through.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p dna\_complement("ATGC")  \# "TACG"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dna\_gc\_content}}{dna\_gc\_content}}\label{dna_gc_content}

\texttt{dna\_gc\_content(seq)\ →\ number} --- fraction of G or C bases
in {[}0,1{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p dna\_gc\_content("ATGCGCATAT")  \# 0.4}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dna\_kmer\_count}}{dna\_kmer\_count}}\label{dna_kmer_count}

\texttt{dna\_kmer\_count(seq,\ k=3)\ →\ hash} --- frequency map of all
k-mers in the sequence.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p dna\_kmer\_count("ATGATC", 2)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dna\_kmer\_index}}{dna\_kmer\_index}}\label{dna_kmer_index}

\texttt{dna\_kmer\_index(seq,\ k=3)\ →\ hash} --- for each k-mer, the
list of start positions where it occurs.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dna\_melting\_temp}}{dna\_melting\_temp}}\label{dna_melting_temp}

\texttt{dna\_melting\_temp(seq)\ →\ number} --- Tm in °C. Uses Wallace
rule for length \textless14, Marmur for longer.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p dna\_melting\_temp("ATGC")  \# 12}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dna\_reverse\_complement}}{dna\_reverse\_complement}}\label{dna_reverse_complement}

\texttt{dna\_reverse\_complement(seq)\ →\ string} ---
reverse-then-complement. Equivalent to the antisense strand read
5\textquotesingle→3\textquotesingle.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p dna\_reverse\_complement("AAATGGC")  \# "GCCATTT"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dna\_transcribe}}{dna\_transcribe}}\label{dna_transcribe}

\texttt{dna\_transcribe(dna)\ →\ string} --- replace T with U to produce
mRNA.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p dna\_transcribe("ATGC")  \# "AUGC"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dna\_translate}}{dna\_translate}}\label{dna_translate}

\texttt{dna\_translate(dna)\ →\ string} --- translate DNA to protein
(single-letter codes); stops at stop codon.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p dna\_translate("ATGGCAGAATAA")  \# MAE}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dnbinom}}{dnbinom}}\label{dnbinom}

\texttt{dnbinom} --- negative binomial PMF. Args: k, size, prob.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p dnbinom(3, 5, 0.5)  \# P(3 failures before 5 successes)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{dnds\_ratio}}{dnds\_ratio}}\label{dnds_ratio}

\texttt{dnds\_ratio} --- ratio of \texttt{dnds}. Category:
*bioinformatics deep*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p dnds\_ratio $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{dnorm} --- operation \texttt{dnorm}. Category: *r / scipy
distributions and tests*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p dnorm $x}
\end{Highlighting}

\texttt{dnrm2} --- operation \texttt{dnrm2}. Category: *blas / lapack*.

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p dnrm2 $x}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{do\_call}}{do\_call}}\label{do_call}

\texttt{do\_call} --- call a function with args from a list. Like
R\textquotesingle s do.call().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $result = docall(fn \{ \_0 + \_1 \}, [3, 4])  \# 7}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{docs}}{docs}}\label{docs}

\texttt{docs(TOPIC)} --- return the raw hover-doc string for
\texttt{TOPIC} (the same source the \texttt{stryke\ docs} browser
renders). \texttt{TOPIC} may be any primary builtin name, alias
spelling, keyword, operator, or sigil-prefixed reflection-hash name
(\texttt{\%stryke::builtins}, \texttt{\textasciitilde{}\textgreater{}},
\texttt{\$\_}, \ldots). Returns \texttt{undef} when no doc page exists
for the topic.

With multiple args, returns a hashref of
\texttt{\{\ name\ =\textgreater{}\ doc\ \}} so a list of topics
round-trips in one call. With no args, returns an arrayref of every
documented topic name (sorted, deduped) --- the in-language equivalent
of \texttt{stryke\ docs\ -\/-list}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p docs("pmap")                  \# raw markdown doc string for pmap}
\NormalTok{p docs("tj")                    \# alias spellings work too}
\NormalTok{my \%d = \%\{docs(qw(pmap pgrep psort))\}}
\NormalTok{p scalar @\{docs()\}              \# total count of documented topics}
\NormalTok{docs("foo") // p "no such topic"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{stryke\ docs} CLI subcommand (interactive TUI pager),
\texttt{\%stryke::descriptions} (one-line summaries),
\texttt{\%stryke::builtins} (name → category).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{doctor}}{doctor}}\label{doctor}

\texttt{doctor} (alias \texttt{health}) --- runtime diagnostic report.
Prints version + binary path, runtime flags (\texttt{-\/-compat},
\texttt{-\/-no-interop}, \texttt{bigint}, \texttt{strict\_vars},
\texttt{strict\_refs}), environment overrides (\texttt{STRYKE\_NO\_TTY},
\texttt{STRYKE\_NO\_JIT}, \texttt{RUST\_LOG}, \ldots), reflection counts
(\texttt{\%b} primaries, \texttt{\%all} spellings, categories,
list-builtin extras), concurrency (logical CPUs + rayon pool size),
home/cache/config dirs, toolchain availability
(\texttt{rustc}/\texttt{cargo}/\texttt{perl}/\texttt{git}/\texttt{rg}),
and a battery of sanity checks. Returns the warning count as an integer
(0 = healthy).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{doctor              \# full report}
\NormalTok{my $w = doctor}
\NormalTok{exit($w == 0 ? 0 : 1)   \# CI{-}friendly health gate}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{doppler}}{doppler}}\label{doppler}

\texttt{doppler\_frequency(\$source\_freq,\ \$source\_vel,\ \$observer\_vel)}
(alias \texttt{doppler}) --- observed frequency with Doppler effect.
Uses speed of sound (343 m/s).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p doppler(440, {-}30, 0)   \# \textasciitilde{}482 Hz (ambulance approaching at 30 m/s)}
\NormalTok{p doppler(440, 30, 0)    \# \textasciitilde{}405 Hz (ambulance receding)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{drag\_force}}{drag\_force}}\label{drag_force}

\texttt{drag\_force} (alias \texttt{fdrag}) computes aerodynamic drag: F
= 0.5·Cd·ρ·A·v². Args: drag\_coeff, air\_density, area, velocity.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p fdrag(0.47, 1.225, 0.01, 30)  \# drag on a ball at 30 m/s}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{duration\_modified(cashflows,\ rate)\ →\ number} ---
\texttt{macaulay\ /\ (1\ +\ rate)}. Direct linear sensitivity to yield
change.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p duration\_modified([100,100,1100], 0.05)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ei}}{ei}}\label{ei}

\texttt{ei(x)\ →\ number} --- exponential integral
\texttt{Ei(x)\ =\ -∫\_\{-x\}\^{}∞\ e\^{}-t\ /\ t\ dt}. Uses series + log
expansion.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ei(1)  \# \textasciitilde{}1.895}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{eig}}{eig}}\label{eig}

\texttt{matrix\_eigenvalues} (aliases \texttt{meig}, \texttt{eig})
computes eigenvalues of a square matrix via QR iteration. Returns an
array of eigenvalues.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @eigs = @\{eig([[2,1],[1,2]])\}   \# [3, 1]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Ramanujan\textquotesingle s approximation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ellper(5, 3)   \# \textasciitilde{}25.5}
\NormalTok{p ellper(10, 10)   \# \textasciitilde{}62.8 (circle)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{elu}}{elu}}\label{elu}

alpha*(e\^{}x - 1) otherwise.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p elu(1)     \# 1}
\NormalTok{p elu({-}1)    \# {-}0.632}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ema}}{ema}}\label{ema}

\texttt{ema(prices,\ period=10)\ →\ array} --- exponential moving
average with smoothing factor \texttt{α\ =\ 2\ /\ (period\ +\ 1)}.
Seeded with the first sample so output length equals input length.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ema([10,11,12,13,14], 3)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{embed\_ts}}{embed\_ts}}\label{embed_ts}

\texttt{embed\_ts} --- time-delay embedding. Converts a time series into
a matrix of lagged values. Like R\textquotesingle s embed().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p embed\_ts([1,2,3,4,5], 3)  \# [[3,2,1],[4,3,2],[5,4,3]]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{enum}}{enum}}\label{enum}

\texttt{enum} declares a tagged-union / algebraic data type --- a finite
set of named variants, each optionally carrying a typed payload. Stryke
enums pair with \texttt{match} for exhaustive pattern dispatch (the
compiler errors on missing arms unless you write
\texttt{\_\ =\textgreater{}}).

\textbf{Declaration:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{enum Sig \{ Hup, Int, Term, Kill \}                    \# tag{-}only variants}
\NormalTok{enum Color \{ Red, Green, Blue \}}
\NormalTok{enum ResultStatus \{ Ok =\textgreater{} Int, Err =\textgreater{} Str \}          \# variants with payload}
\NormalTok{enum Shape \{                                          \# variants with payload + methods}
\NormalTok{    Circle =\textgreater{} Float,}
\NormalTok{    Square =\textgreater{} Float,}
\NormalTok{    fn area($self) \{}
\NormalTok{        match ($self) \{}
\NormalTok{            Shape::Circle(\textbackslash{}$r) =\textgreater{} 3.14159 * \textbackslash{}$r * \textbackslash{}$r,}
\NormalTok{            Shape::Square(\textbackslash{}$s) =\textgreater{} \textbackslash{}$s * \textbackslash{}$s,}
\NormalTok{        \}}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Constructing variants:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \textbackslash{}$s = Sig::Term                                   \# tag{-}only construction}
\NormalTok{my \textbackslash{}$ok = ResultStatus::Ok(42)                       \# payload construction}
\NormalTok{my \textbackslash{}$err = ResultStatus::Err("boom")}
\NormalTok{p \textbackslash{}$ok                                               \# ResultStatus::Ok(42)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Pattern matching:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fn handle(\textbackslash{}$sig) \{}
\NormalTok{    match (\textbackslash{}$sig) \{}
\NormalTok{        Sig::Hup  =\textgreater{} "reload config",}
\NormalTok{        Sig::Int  =\textgreater{} "graceful shutdown",}
\NormalTok{        Sig::Term =\textgreater{} "drain + stop",}
\NormalTok{        Sig::Kill =\textgreater{} "(no handler — kernel reaps)",}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{\# Bind the payload in the arm:}
\NormalTok{match (\textbackslash{}$result) \{}
\NormalTok{    ResultStatus::Ok(\textbackslash{}$value)  =\textgreater{} p "got \textbackslash{}$value",}
\NormalTok{    ResultStatus::Err(\textbackslash{}$msg)   =\textgreater{} die \textbackslash{}$msg,}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Iteration / reflection:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{for my \textbackslash{}$v (Sig::Hup, Sig::Int, Sig::Term, Sig::Kill) \{ p \textbackslash{}$v \}}
\NormalTok{p Sig::values |\textgreater{} ep                                  \# every variant}
\NormalTok{p ResultStatus::Ok(1){-}\textgreater{}variant                       \# "Ok"}
\NormalTok{p ResultStatus::Ok(1){-}\textgreater{}payload                       \# 1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Note: \texttt{EnumName::Variant} namespaces the variant tag;
\texttt{EnumName::Variant(payload)} constructs one with data. The
compiler tracks payload types per variant --- \texttt{match} arms must
bind a compatible pattern.

See also: \texttt{match}, \texttt{struct} (single-shape records),
\texttt{class} (full OO).

\begin{Shaded}

\NormalTok{my $env = envelope(\textbackslash{}@signal)}
\NormalTok{\# $env traces the amplitude of oscillations}
\end{Highlighting}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{exec\_script}}{exec\_script}}\label{exec_script}

\texttt{run(TARGET,\ ...args)} → exit code. Spawn a stryke script or
external binary as a subprocess. Inherits stdin/stdout/stderr so output
streams to the parent. Returns the child\textquotesingle s exit code
(\texttt{-1} on spawn failure).

Routing:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \texttt{TARGET} ending in \texttt{.stk} → spawned via the current
  stryke binary (\texttt{\$STRYKE\_EXE\ script.stk\ ARGS…})
\item
  \texttt{TARGET} containing \texttt{/} → spawned as a direct path
\item
  bare name → resolved via \texttt{PATH}
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $rc = run("echo", "hello")             \# 0}
\NormalTok{my $rc = run("./build.stk", "{-}{-}release")  \# spawns isolated stryke proc}
\NormalTok{run("git", "status") == 0 or die "git failed"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Isolation: state defined in a script run via \texttt{run} does NOT leak
into the caller (subprocess). Use \texttt{source} if you want the
script\textquotesingle s \texttt{our} vars / \texttt{fn} defs in your
REPL.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{extends}}{extends}}\label{extends}

\texttt{extends} specifies parent classes for inheritance. Child classes
inherit all fields and methods from parents.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{class Animal \{ name: Str \}}
\NormalTok{class Dog extends Animal \{ breed: Str \}}
\NormalTok{class Hybrid extends Dog, Cat \{ \}  \# multiple inheritance}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Inherited fields appear first in construction order. Methods are
resolved child-first (override pattern).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{file}}{file}}\label{file}

\texttt{file\ PATH} --- one-line description in the spirit of
\texttt{file(1)}: symlink targets, \texttt{directory},
FIFO/socket/device nodes (Unix), and magic-byte sniff for regular files
(\texttt{PNG}, \texttt{ELF}, \texttt{gzip}, shebang scripts,
\texttt{ASCII\ text}, etc.). With no arguments, inspects \texttt{\$\_}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p file("/bin/sh")}
\NormalTok{p file("Cargo.toml")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $fit = fit\_curve\_least\_squares([0,1,2,3], [1,3,5,7])}
\NormalTok{p "$fit{-}\textgreater{}\{a\} + $fit{-}\textgreater{}\{b\}*x"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{five\_number\_summary}}{five\_number\_summary}}\label{five_number_summary}

\texttt{five\_number\_summary(@data)} (alias \texttt{fivenum}) ---
returns {[}min, Q1, median, Q3, max{]}, the classic five-number summary
used for box plots. Provides a quick view of data distribution.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @data = 1:100}
\NormalTok{my $f = fivenum(@data)}
\NormalTok{p "min=$f{-}\textgreater{}[0] Q1=$f{-}\textgreater{}[1] med=$f{-}\textgreater{}[2] Q3=$f{-}\textgreater{}[3] max=$f{-}\textgreater{}[4]"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{flat\_maps}}{flat\_maps}}\label{flat_maps}

\texttt{flat\_maps\ \{\ BLOCK\ \}\ LIST} --- a lazy streaming flat-map
that evaluates the block for each element and flattens the resulting
lists into a single iterator. Where \texttt{maps} expects the block to
return one value, \texttt{flat\_maps} handles blocks that return zero or
more values per element and concatenates them seamlessly. This is the
streaming equivalent of calling \texttt{map} with a multi-value block.
Use it in \texttt{\textbar{}\textgreater{}} chains when each input
element fans out into multiple outputs and you want lazy evaluation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{1:3 |\textgreater{} flat\_maps \{ (\_, \_ * 10) \} |\textgreater{} e p}
\NormalTok{\# 1 10 2 20 3 30}
\NormalTok{@nested |\textgreater{} flat\_maps \{ @\_ \} |\textgreater{} e p   \# flatten array{-}of{-}arrays}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{format\_strftime}}{format\_strftime}}\label{format_strftime}

\texttt{strftime(FORMAT,\ EPOCH\_SECS?)} → format a Unix timestamp using
strftime directives (\texttt{\%Y} \texttt{\%m} \texttt{\%d} \texttt{\%H}
\texttt{\%M} \texttt{\%S} \texttt{\%a} \texttt{\%A} etc.). Default
timestamp is \texttt{Local::now()}. Wraps the existing \texttt{chrono}
dep.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p strftime("\%Y{-}\%m{-}\%d \%H:\%M:\%S")     \# 2026{-}05{-}14 17:19:48}
\NormalTok{p strftime("\%FT\%TZ", 1700000000)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

--- earliest start index where \texttt{needle} matches a substring of
\texttt{haystack} within Levenshtein distance \texttt{max\_dist};
\texttt{-1} if no match.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p fuzzy\_substring\_match("hello", "yellow helo world", 1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gamma\_approx}}{gamma\_approx}}\label{gamma_approx}

\texttt{gamma\_approx(\$z)} --- Gamma function Γ(z) using Lanczos
approximation. Extends factorial: Γ(n) = (n-1)!

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gamma(5)    \# 24 (same as 4!)}
\NormalTok{p gamma(0.5)  \# \textasciitilde{}1.772 (√π)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gamma\_correct}}{gamma\_correct}}\label{gamma_correct}

\texttt{gamma\_correct(v,\ gamma=2.2)\ →\ number} --- apply gamma
correction \texttt{v\^{}(1/γ)} to a {[}0,1{]} value.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gamma\_correct(0.5, 2.2)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gamma\_pdf}}{gamma\_pdf}}\label{gamma_pdf}

\texttt{gamma\_pdf} (alias \texttt{gammapdf}) evaluates the Gamma
distribution PDF at x with shape k and scale theta.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gammapdf(2.0, 2, 1)  \# Gamma(2,1) at x=2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gamma\_regularized\_p}}{gamma\_regularized\_p}}\label{gamma_regularized_p}

\texttt{gamma\_regularized\_p(a,\ x)\ →\ number} --- lower regularised
incomplete gamma \texttt{P(a,\ x)\ =\ γ(a,x)/Γ(a)}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gamma\_regularized\_p(2, 1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gamma\_regularized\_q}}{gamma\_regularized\_q}}\label{gamma_regularized_q}

\texttt{gamma\_regularized\_q(a,\ x)\ →\ number} --- upper regularised
incomplete gamma \texttt{Q(a,\ x)\ =\ 1\ -\ P(a,\ x)}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gamma\_regularized\_q(2, 1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{v\^{}γ}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{geometric\_series}}{geometric\_series}}\label{geometric_series}

\texttt{geometric\_series(\$a1,\ \$r,\ \$n)} (alias \texttt{geomser})
--- sum of n terms of geometric sequence. Formula: a1×(1-rⁿ)/(1-r). If
r=1, returns a1×n.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p geomser(1, 2, 10)      \# 1023 (1+2+4+...+512)}
\NormalTok{p geomser(1, 0.5, 10)    \# \textasciitilde{}1.998 (converging to 2)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_branches}}{gh\_branches}}\label{gh_branches}

\texttt{gh\_branches(OWNER,\ REPO)} --- paginated list of branches.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @br = gh\_branches("MenkeTechnologies", "zpwr")}
\NormalTok{p pluck(\textbackslash{}@br, "name")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_commits}}{gh\_commits}}\label{gh_commits}

\texttt{gh\_commits(OWNER,\ REPO)} --- paginated list of commits.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @c = gh\_commits("MenkeTechnologies", "zpwr")}
\NormalTok{p pluck(\textbackslash{}@c, "sha")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_contributors}}{gh\_contributors}}\label{gh_contributors}

\texttt{gh\_contributors(OWNER,\ REPO)} --- paginated list of
contributors.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @c = gh\_contributors("MenkeTechnologies", "zpwr")}
\NormalTok{p pluck(\textbackslash{}@c, "login")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_emojis}}{gh\_emojis}}\label{gh_emojis}

\texttt{gh\_emojis()} --- \texttt{\{\ name\ =\textgreater{}\ url\ \}}
hashref of GitHub\textquotesingle s emoji set.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_followers}}{gh\_followers}}\label{gh_followers}

\texttt{gh\_followers(USER)} --- paginated list of a
user\textquotesingle s followers (flat list of hashrefs).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @f = gh\_followers("MenkeTechnologies")}
\NormalTok{p "followers: " . len(@f)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_following}}{gh\_following}}\label{gh_following}

\texttt{gh\_following(USER)} --- paginated list of users a user is
following.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @f = gh\_following("MenkeTechnologies")}
\NormalTok{p pluck(\textbackslash{}@f, "login")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_forks}}{gh\_forks}}\label{gh_forks}

\texttt{gh\_forks(OWNER,\ REPO)} --- paginated list of forks.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @f = gh\_forks("MenkeTechnologies", "zpwr")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_get}}{gh\_get}}\label{gh_get}

\texttt{gh\_get(PATH)} --- GET an arbitrary GitHub REST endpoint.
\texttt{PATH} is a relative path (\texttt{/users/octocat}) or full URL.
Returns parsed JSON as a stryke value (hashref/arrayref/scalar); 404 →
\texttt{undef}. Authenticates via \texttt{GITHUB\_TOKEN} env var when
present (5000 req/hour); otherwise unauthenticated (60 req/hour).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $u = gh\_get("/users/MenkeTechnologies")}
\NormalTok{p $u{-}\textgreater{}\{public\_repos\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_gist}}{gh\_gist}}\label{gh_gist}

\texttt{gh\_gist(ID)} --- fetch a single gist by ID.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $g = gh\_gist("aa5a315d61ae9438b18d")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_gists}}{gh\_gists}}\label{gh_gists}

\texttt{gh\_gists(USER)} --- paginated list of a user\textquotesingle s
public gists.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @g = gh\_gists("MenkeTechnologies")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_issues}}{gh\_issues}}\label{gh_issues}

\texttt{gh\_issues(OWNER,\ REPO)} --- paginated list of issues for a
repo (includes PRs by GitHub convention; filter
\texttt{\_-\textgreater{}\{pull\_request\}} to separate).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @issues = gh\_issues("MenkeTechnologies", "zpwr")}
\NormalTok{my @real = grep \{ !exists \_{-}\textgreater{}\{pull\_request\} \} @issues}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_langs}}{gh\_langs}}\label{gh_langs}

\texttt{gh\_languages(OWNER,\ REPO)} (alias \texttt{gh\_langs}) ---
\texttt{\{\ language\ =\textgreater{}\ bytes\ \}} hashref of language
breakdown.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $h = gh\_languages("MenkeTechnologies", "zpwr")}
\NormalTok{for my $k (sort \{ $h{-}\textgreater{}\{\_1\} \textless{}=\textgreater{} $h{-}\textgreater{}\{\_0\} \} keys \%$h) \{ p "\#\{$k\}: \#\{$h{-}\textgreater{}\{$k\}\}" \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_meta}}{gh\_meta}}\label{gh_meta}

\texttt{gh\_meta()} --- GitHub service metadata hashref (IP ranges,
hooks, importer).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_org}}{gh\_org}}\label{gh_org}

\texttt{gh\_org(ORG)} --- fetch an organization\textquotesingle s
profile as a hashref.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $o = gh\_org("github")}
\NormalTok{p "\#\{$o{-}\textgreater{}\{login\}\}: \#\{$o{-}\textgreater{}\{public\_repos\}\} repos"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_org\_repos}}{gh\_org\_repos}}\label{gh_org_repos}

\texttt{gh\_org\_repos(ORG)} --- paginated list of an
org\textquotesingle s repos.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @r = gh\_org\_repos("github")}
\NormalTok{p "\#\{len(@r)\} repos"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_prs}}{gh\_prs}}\label{gh_prs}

\texttt{gh\_prs(OWNER,\ REPO)} (alias \texttt{gh\_pulls}) --- paginated
list of pull requests.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @prs = gh\_prs("MenkeTechnologies", "zpwr")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_rate\_limit}}{gh\_rate\_limit}}\label{gh_rate_limit}

\texttt{gh\_rate\_limit()} --- current rate-limit status as a hashref.
Use to check budget before launching a \texttt{pmap}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $rl = gh\_rate\_limit()}
\NormalTok{p $rl{-}\textgreater{}\{rate\}\{remaining\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_readme}}{gh\_readme}}\label{gh_readme}

\texttt{gh\_readme(OWNER,\ REPO)} --- README content as a decoded UTF-8
string (handles base64 transparently).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $md = gh\_readme("MenkeTechnologies", "zpwr")}
\NormalTok{p "\#\{len($md)\} bytes"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_releases}}{gh\_releases}}\label{gh_releases}

\texttt{gh\_releases(OWNER,\ REPO)} --- paginated list of releases.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @r = gh\_releases("MenkeTechnologies", "zpwr")}
\NormalTok{p pluck(\textbackslash{}@r, "tag\_name")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_repo}}{gh\_repo}}\label{gh_repo}

\texttt{gh\_repo(OWNER,\ REPO)} or \texttt{gh\_repo("OWNER/REPO")} ---
fetch a single repo as a hashref. 404 → \texttt{undef}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $r = gh\_repo("MenkeTechnologies/zpwr")}
\NormalTok{p "★ \#\{$r{-}\textgreater{}\{stargazers\_count\}\}  ⑂ \#\{$r{-}\textgreater{}\{forks\_count\}\}  lang \#\{$r{-}\textgreater{}\{language\}\}"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_repos}}{gh\_repos}}\label{gh_repos}

\texttt{gh\_repos(USER)} --- paginated list of a user\textquotesingle s
public repos (flat list of hashrefs). Pairs naturally with \texttt{pmap}
for parallel per-repo work.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @repos = gh\_repos("MenkeTechnologies")}
\NormalTok{my @top = sort \{ \_1{-}\textgreater{}\{stargazers\_count\} \textless{}=\textgreater{} \_0{-}\textgreater{}\{stargazers\_count\} \} @repos}
\NormalTok{p pluck(\textbackslash{}@top[0:9], "full\_name")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_runs}}{gh\_runs}}\label{gh_runs}

\texttt{gh\_runs(OWNER,\ REPO)} --- array of workflow-run hashrefs
(auto-extracts \texttt{workflow\_runs}).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @r = gh\_runs("MenkeTechnologies", "zpwr")}
\NormalTok{p pluck(\textbackslash{}@r[0:9], "status")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_search\_code}}{gh\_search\_code}}\label{gh_search_code}

\texttt{gh\_search\_code(QUERY)} --- paginated code search results
(requires auth; pass \texttt{GITHUB\_TOKEN}).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @hits = gh\_search\_code("org:MenkeTechnologies fn main")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_search\_issues}}{gh\_search\_issues}}\label{gh_search_issues}

\texttt{gh\_search\_issues(QUERY)} --- paginated issue / PR search
results.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @i = gh\_search\_issues("author:MenkeTechnologies is:pr is:merged")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gh\_search\_repos}}{gh\_search\_repos}}\label{gh_search_repos}

\texttt{gh\_search\_repos(QUERY)} --- paginated repo search results.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @r = gh\_search\_repos("language:rust stars:\textgreater{}10000")}
\NormalTok{p pluck(\textbackslash{}@r[0:9], "full\_name")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{gini}}{gini}}\label{gini}

\texttt{gini(@data)} (alias \texttt{gini\_coefficient}) --- computes the
Gini coefficient measuring inequality in a distribution. Returns a value
from 0 (perfect equality) to 1 (perfect inequality). Commonly used for
income distribution analysis.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p gini(1, 1, 1, 1)     \# 0 (perfect equality)}
\NormalTok{p gini(0, 0, 0, 100)   \# \textasciitilde{}0.75 (high inequality)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{god}}{god}}\label{god}

\texttt{god\ EXPR} --- omniscient runtime introspection. Returns a
structured multi-line dump string showing information that
\texttt{pp}/\texttt{ddump} do not surface: the canonical type tag, the
heap pointer (so two refs to the same heap object are visibly aliases),
the \texttt{Arc} strong / weak counts, a byte hex preview for byte
buffers, generator state (\texttt{pc}, \texttt{exhausted}), pipeline
state (queued ops, source length), and closure captures.

\textbf{Cycle-safe.} Every heap pointer descended through is tracked in
a per-call \texttt{HashSet}; a back-edge to an already-visited pointer
is annotated \texttt{...cycle} instead of recursing, so \texttt{god}
terminates on self-referential structures.

\textbf{Pure} --- returns the dump as a string. Pipe through \texttt{p}
/ \texttt{warn} / \texttt{die} when you want output. Cleanly composable
with assertions:
\texttt{god(\$x)\ =\textasciitilde{}\ /strong=2/\ \textbar{}\textgreater{}\ aok}.

\textbf{Sibling reflectors:}

\textbar{} Builtin \textbar{} What it shows \textbar{}
\textbar-\/-\/-\textbar-\/-\/-\textbar{} \textbar{} \texttt{pp}
\textbar{} Human-friendly value formatting \textbar{} \textbar{}
\texttt{ddump} \textbar{} Deep structural dump (recursive value
structure) \textbar{} \textbar{} \texttt{god} \textbar{} Runtime
metadata (type tag, heap ptr, refcounts, internals) \textbar{}

Reach for \texttt{god} when debugging refcount surprises, aliasing bugs,
closure-capture leaks, generator-pc state, or pipeline-op accumulation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $h = \{ a =\textgreater{} 1, b =\textgreater{} [10, 20], c =\textgreater{} "hi" \};}
\NormalTok{p god $h;                          \# HASH @ 0xXXXX (strong=1, ..., entries=3) + recursive}

\NormalTok{\# Detect aliasing — two refs to the same Arc show identical heap pointers.}
\NormalTok{my $shared = [];}
\NormalTok{my $a = \textbackslash{}$shared;}
\NormalTok{my $b = \textbackslash{}$shared;}
\NormalTok{warn god $a;}
\NormalTok{warn god $b;                       \# same 0x… for the inner ARRAY}

\NormalTok{\# Cycle handling — self{-}referential hash terminates with an annotation.}
\NormalTok{my \%cyc;}
\NormalTok{$cyc\{self\} = \textbackslash{}\%cyc;}
\NormalTok{p god \textbackslash{}\%cyc;                       \# ...cycle at the back{-}edge, no stack overflow}

\NormalTok{\# Generator state mid{-}iteration.}
\NormalTok{my $g = gen \{ yield 1; yield 2; yield 3 \};}
\NormalTok{$g{-}\textgreater{}next;}
\NormalTok{p god $g;                          \# Generator @ 0xXXXX pc=1/3 exhausted=false}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

See also: \texttt{pp} (pretty), \texttt{ddump} (deep structure),
\texttt{ref} (just the type tag).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{goertzel}}{goertzel}}\label{goertzel}

\texttt{goertzel} computes the magnitude of a single DFT frequency bin
using the Goertzel algorithm. Much faster than full FFT when you need
one frequency.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $mag = goertzel(\textbackslash{}@signal, 440, 44100)  \# 440 Hz component}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{goldb}}{goldb}}\label{goldb}

\texttt{goldb} --- operation \texttt{goldb}. Alias for
\texttt{goldbach}. Category: *math / numeric extras*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p goldb $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{goldbach} --- operation \texttt{goldbach}. Category: *math /
numeric extras*.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{golden}}{golden}}\label{golden}

\texttt{golden\_section} (aliases \texttt{golden}, \texttt{gss}) finds
the minimum of f on {[}a,b{]} via golden-section search.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $xmin = golden(fn \{ (\_0{-}3)**2 \}, 0, 10)  \# 3.0}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{golden\_ratio}}{golden\_ratio}}\label{golden_ratio}

\texttt{golden\_ratio} (alias \texttt{phi}) --- φ = (1+√5)/2 ≈ 1.618.
The golden ratio, appears throughout nature and art.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p phi   \# 1.618033988749895}
\NormalTok{\# Fibonacci limit: fib(n)/fib(n{-}1) → φ}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p graph\_betweenness($g)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

traversal order starting at \texttt{start}.

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p graph\_bridges($g)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{graph\_closeness}}{graph\_closeness}}\label{graph_closeness}

\texttt{graph\_closeness(adj\_list,\ v)\ →\ number} --- closeness

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p graph\_closeness($g, 0)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

local clustering: fraction of v\textquotesingle s neighbour pairs that
are themselves connected. ∈ {[}0,1{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p graph\_clustering\_coefficient($g, 1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\NormalTok{p graph\_density $x}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{graph\_dfs}}{graph\_dfs}}\label{graph_dfs}

\texttt{graph\_dfs(adj\_list,\ start)\ →\ array} --- depth-first

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p graph\_dfs([[1,2],[3],[3],[]], 0)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{graph\_dijkstra}}{graph\_dijkstra}}\label{graph_dijkstra}

\texttt{graph\_dijkstra(weighted\_adj,\ start)\ →\ array} ---
shortest-path distances from \texttt{start} (Dijkstra with binary heap).
Returns \texttt{+∞} for unreachable nodes.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p graph\_dijkstra($g, 0)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{graph\_eccentricity}}{graph\_eccentricity}}\label{graph_eccentricity}

\texttt{graph\_eccentricity(weighted\_adj,\ v)\ →\ number} --- max
shortest-path distance from \texttt{v} to any reachable node.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p graph\_eccentricity($g, 0)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{graph\_eccentricity\_all}}{graph\_eccentricity\_all}}\label{graph_eccentricity_all}

\texttt{graph\_eccentricity\_all} --- graph algorithm
\texttt{eccentricity\ all}. Category: *misc*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p graph\_eccentricity\_all $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{graph\_eigenvector\_centrality}}{graph\_eigenvector\_centrality}}\label{graph_eigenvector_centrality}

\texttt{graph\_eigenvector\_centrality(adj\_list,\ iters=100)\ →\ array}
--- power-iteration eigenvector centrality on the (possibly weighted)
adjacency matrix: \texttt{x\ ←\ A·x\ /\ ‖A·x‖}. Early-exits when
\texttt{‖x\_new\ −\ x\_old‖₁\ \textless{}\ 1e-12}. Pass \texttt{iters}
to cap iterations.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p graph\_eigenvector\_centrality($g, 100)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{graph\_floyd\_warshall}}{graph\_floyd\_warshall}}\label{graph_floyd_warshall}

\texttt{graph\_floyd\_warshall(matrix)\ →\ matrix} --- all-pairs
shortest paths via Floyd-Warshall (O(n³)). Input is an adjacency matrix
where \texttt{0} indicates no edge and any non-zero value is the edge
weight. Output cells are \texttt{Infinity} for unreachable pairs.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p graph\_floyd\_warshall($adj\_matrix)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{graph\_from\_edges}}{graph\_from\_edges}}\label{graph_from_edges}

\texttt{graph\_from\_edges(edges,\ directed=0)\ →\ adj\_list} --- build
adjacency list from \texttt{{[}{[}u,\ v,\ w?{]},\ …{]}}. Edge weight
defaults to 1.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p graph\_from\_edges([[0,1,4],[0,2,1],[1,3,1]], 0)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{graph\_has\_path}}{graph\_has\_path}}\label{graph_has_path}

\texttt{graph\_has\_path(adj\_list,\ s,\ t)\ →\ 0\textbar{}1} --- BFS
reachability check.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p graph\_has\_path([[1],[2],[3],[]], 0, 3)  \# 1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{graph\_in\_degree}}{graph\_in\_degree}}\label{graph_in_degree}

\texttt{graph\_in\_degree(adj\_list,\ v)\ →\ int} --- incoming edge
count (scan all adjacency lists).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p graph\_in\_degree($g, 3)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

2-colouring BFS; 1 if no odd-length cycle.

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p graph\_is\_bipartite([[1,3],[0,2],[1,3],[0,2]])  \# 1}
\end{Highlighting}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{graph\_is\_connected}}{graph\_is\_connected}}\label{graph_is_connected}

\texttt{graph\_is\_connected(adj\_list)\ →\ 0\textbar{}1} --- BFS-checks
that all nodes are reachable from node 0.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p graph\_is\_connected([[1],[0,2],[1]])  \# 1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p graph\_is\_tree $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{graph\_kosaraju}}{graph\_kosaraju}}\label{graph_kosaraju}

\texttt{graph\_kosaraju(adj\_list)\ →\ array} --- SCC labelling via
Kosaraju (alias of \texttt{graph\_strongly\_connected\_components}).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p graph\_kosaraju($g)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Shaded}

\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{harris\_response}}{harris\_response}}\label{harris_response}

\texttt{harris\_response(image,\ k=0.04)\ →\ matrix} --- Harris corner
response \texttt{det(M)\ −\ k·trace(M)²} per pixel. \texttt{M} is the
2×2 structure tensor
\texttt{Σ\_w\ {[}{[}Iₓ²,\ IₓIᵧ{]},\ {[}IₓIᵧ,\ Iᵧ²{]}{]}} summed over a
3×3 window (without the window sum, \texttt{det(M)} would be identically
zero per pixel and the response could not distinguish corners from
edges).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{haversine}}{haversine}}\label{haversine}

\texttt{haversine\_distance(\$lat1,\ \$lon1,\ \$lat2,\ \$lon2)} (alias
\texttt{haversine}) --- computes the great-circle distance between two
points on Earth given latitude/longitude in degrees. Returns distance in
kilometers.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# NYC to LA}
\NormalTok{p haversine(40.7128, {-}74.0060, 34.0522, {-}118.2437)}
\NormalTok{\# \textasciitilde{}3940 km}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

R\textquotesingle s hclust().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $merges = hclust(dist\_mat([[0,0],[1,0],[10,10]]))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{highpass\_filter}}{highpass\_filter}}\label{highpass_filter}

\texttt{highpass\_filter(\textbackslash{}@signal,\ \$cutoff)} (alias
\texttt{hpf}) --- applies a simple high-pass filter to remove
low-frequency components (DC offset, drift). Signal = original -
lowpass.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @with\_dc = map \{ 5 + sin(\_/10) \} 1:100}
\NormalTok{my $ac\_only = hpf(\textbackslash{}@with\_dc, 0.1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{history}}{history}}\label{history}

\texttt{history} / \texttt{history(N)} / \texttt{history(-N)} --- REPL
history access. No args: full history buffer as a list. Positive N: the
last N lines. Negative N: the N-th-from-last line as a scalar. The REPL
frontend pushes accepted lines into this buffer via
\texttt{repl\_history\_push()}; from a non-REPL script, history is
whatever the embedding application wires.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @last10 = history(10)}
\NormalTok{p "prev: " . history({-}1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}
