\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hll}}{hll}}\label{hll}

\texttt{hll(PRECISION=14)} --- HyperLogLog cardinality (distinct-count)
sketch with \texttt{2\^{}precision} 8-bit registers. Precision clamped
to \texttt{{[}4,\ 18{]}}; typical \texttt{14} gives \textasciitilde1.3\%
relative error in 16 KB. Replaces the older slow
\texttt{hyperloglog\_pp\_*} family with a HeapObject-backed primitive
that is \texttt{\textasciitilde{}1000x} faster on \texttt{\_add} (no
allocation per insert).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $h = hll(14)}
\NormalTok{hll\_add($h, "user\_$\_") for 1..100\_000}
\NormalTok{printf "\textasciitilde{}\%d\textbackslash{}n", hll\_count($h)        \# \textasciitilde{}99672 — \textasciitilde{}0.33\% error}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Family: \texttt{hll\_add}, \texttt{hll\_count}, \texttt{hll\_merge},
\texttt{hll\_clear}, \texttt{hll\_precision}, \texttt{hll\_serialize},
\texttt{hll\_deserialize}. Legacy aliases
(\texttt{hyperloglog\_pp\_new}, \texttt{hyperloglog\_pp\_add},
\texttt{hyperloglog\_pp\_estimate}, \texttt{hyperloglog\_new}, etc.) all
route here.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hll\_add}}{hll\_add}}\label{hll_add}

\texttt{hll\_add(HLL,\ KEY)} --- fold \texttt{KEY} into the sketch (one
xxh3-64 + one register update). Returns the same HLL for chaining.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hll\_clear}}{hll\_clear}}\label{hll_clear}

\texttt{hll\_clear(HLL)} --- zero every register.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hll\_count}}{hll\_count}}\label{hll_count}

\texttt{hll\_count(HLL)} → estimated number of distinct items seen. Uses
alpha-corrected standard HLL with linear-counting fallback for small
ranges.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hll\_deserialize}}{hll\_deserialize}}\label{hll_deserialize}

\texttt{hll\_deserialize(BYTES)} → \texttt{HllSketch\ \textbar{}\ undef}
--- round-trip from \texttt{hll\_serialize}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hll\_merge}}{hll\_merge}}\label{hll_merge}

\texttt{hll\_merge(HLL,\ OTHER)} --- register-wise max into the receiver
(union of two distinct sets). Returns \texttt{1} on success, \texttt{0}
on precision mismatch.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hll\_precision}}{hll\_precision}}\label{hll_precision}

\texttt{hll\_precision(HLL)} → integer precision parameter (4-18) ---
the sketch holds \texttt{2\^{}precision} registers.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hll\_serialize}}{hll\_serialize}}\label{hll_serialize}

\texttt{hll\_serialize(HLL)} → BYTES ---
\texttt{STKHLL\textbackslash{}x01} magic + 4-byte precision + register
vector.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hma}}{hma}}\label{hma}

\texttt{hma(prices,\ period=10)\ →\ array} --- Hull moving average.
Reduces lag relative to SMA/EMA via
\texttt{WMA(2·WMA(half)\ −\ WMA(full),\ √period)}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p hma(\textbackslash{}@prices, 14)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

hex.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{hurst\_exponent}}{hurst\_exponent}}\label{hurst_exponent}

\texttt{hurst\_exponent(series)\ →\ number} --- multi-scale R/S
analysis: subdivides the series into dyadic chunk sizes (8, 16, 32,
\ldots, n/2), averages rescaled-range \texttt{R/S} per size, then
returns the OLS slope of \texttt{log(R/S)} vs \texttt{log(n)}.
\texttt{H\ ≈\ 0.5} random walk; \texttt{\textgreater{}0.5} trending;
\texttt{\textless{}0.5} mean-reverting. Returns UNDEF if
\texttt{n\ \textless{}\ 16} or fewer than 2 dyadic scales fit.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p hurst\_exponent(\textbackslash{}@returns)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{idct}}{idct}}\label{idct}

\texttt{idct} computes the inverse DCT (Type-III). Reconstructs a signal
from DCT coefficients.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @signal = @\{idct(dct([1,2,3,4]))\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ideal\_gas}}{ideal\_gas}}\label{ideal_gas}

\texttt{ideal\_gas} (alias \texttt{pv\_nrt}) solves PV=nRT for the
unknown (pass 0 for the value to solve). Args: P, V, n, T.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p pv\_nrt(0, 0.0224, 1, 273.15)  \# pressure at STP}
\NormalTok{p pv\_nrt(101325, 0, 1, 273.15)   \# volume at STP}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{idft}}{idft}}\label{idft}

Discrete Fourier Transform. Takes arrayref of {[}re, im{]} pairs and
returns the time-domain signal.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $spectrum = dft(\textbackslash{}@signal)}
\NormalTok{my $reconstructed = idft($spectrum)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{impl}}{impl}}\label{impl}

\texttt{impl} declares which traits a class implements.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{trait Printable \{ fn to\_str \}}
\NormalTok{class Item impl Printable \{}
\NormalTok{    name: Str}
\NormalTok{    fn to\_str \{ $self{-}\textgreater{}name \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Multiple traits: \texttt{class\ X\ impl\ A,\ B,\ C\ \{\ \}}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{interp\_lagrange}}{interp\_lagrange}}\label{interp_lagrange}

\texttt{interp\_lagrange(xs,\ ys,\ x)\ →\ number} --- Lagrange
polynomial interpolation through all sample points.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p interp\_lagrange([0,1,2], [1,4,9], 1.5)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{interp\_linear}}{interp\_linear}}\label{interp_linear}

\texttt{interp\_linear(xs,\ ys,\ x)\ →\ number} --- linear
interpolation. Binary-searches \texttt{xs} to find the bracketing
interval.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p interp\_linear([0,1,2], [0,10,20], 0.5)  \# 5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{interpolate}}{interpolate}}\label{interpolate}

\texttt{upsample(\textbackslash{}@signal,\ \$factor)} (alias
\texttt{interpolate}) --- increases sample rate by inserting zeros and
filtering. Factor must be positive integer.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $upsampled = interpolate(\textbackslash{}@signal, 4)  \# 4x sample rate}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{interpolation\_search}}{interpolation\_search}}\label{interpolation_search}

\texttt{interpolation\_search(sorted,\ target)\ →\ int} --- estimate
position by linear interpolation. \texttt{O(log\ log\ n)} on uniform
data.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{interval\_at}}{interval\_at}}\label{interval_at}

\texttt{it\_query\_point(IT,\ P)} → list of
\texttt{{[}start,\ end,\ payload{]}} arrayrefs containing point
\texttt{P}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{interval\_count}}{interval\_count}}\label{interval_count}

\texttt{it\_len(IT)} → number of intervals stored.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{interval\_insert}}{interval\_insert}}\label{interval_insert}

\texttt{it\_insert(IT,\ START,\ END,\ PAYLOAD=undef)} --- append an
interval. Endpoints normalized so \texttt{START\ ≤\ END}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{interval\_name}}{interval\_name}}\label{interval_name}

\texttt{interval\_name(semitones)\ →\ string} --- Western music interval
label: P1, m2, M2, m3, M3, P4, TT, P5, m6, M6, m7, M7.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p interval\_name(7)  \# P5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{interval\_overlap}}{interval\_overlap}}\label{interval_overlap}

\texttt{it\_query\_range(IT,\ LO,\ HI)} → list of
\texttt{{[}start,\ end,\ payload{]}} arrayrefs overlapping range
\texttt{{[}LO,\ HI{]}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{interval\_quality\_size}}{interval\_quality\_size}}\label{interval_quality_size}

\texttt{interval\_quality\_size} --- operation
\texttt{interval\ quality\ size}. Category: *music theory*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p interval\_quality\_size $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{interval\_remove}}{interval\_remove}}\label{interval_remove}

\texttt{it\_remove(IT,\ START,\ END)} → number of removed intervals with
exact endpoints.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{interval\_semitones}}{interval\_semitones}}\label{interval_semitones}

\texttt{interval\_semitones} --- operation \texttt{interval\ semitones}.

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{interval\_tree}}{interval\_tree}}\label{interval_tree}

\texttt{interval\_tree()} --- store \texttt{{[}start,\ end{]}} intervals
(inclusive) with associated payloads; query for all intervals
overlapping a point or another range.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $it = interval\_tree()}
\NormalTok{it\_insert($it, 1, 10, "meeting{-}a")}
\NormalTok{it\_insert($it, 8, 15, "meeting{-}b")}
\NormalTok{my @hits = it\_query\_point($it, 9)     \# both overlap}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{irr}}{irr}}\label{irr}

\texttt{irr(@cashflows)} --- computes the Internal Rate of Return for a
series of cash flows. First value is typically negative (initial
investment). Uses Newton-Raphson iteration.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @cf = ({-}1000, 300, 420, 680)}
\NormalTok{p irr(@cf)   \# \textasciitilde{}0.166 (16.6\%)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{is\_probable\_prime}}{is\_probable\_prime}}\label{is_probable_prime}

\texttt{miller\_rabin} (aliases \texttt{millerrabin},
\texttt{is\_probable\_prime}) performs a probabilistic primality test
with k rounds (default 20).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p millerrabin(104729)     \# 1 (prime)}
\NormalTok{p millerrabin(104730)     \# 0 (composite)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{is\_ulid}}{is\_ulid}}\label{is_ulid}

\texttt{is\_ulid\ SCALAR} → \texttt{1}/\texttt{0} per the 26-char
Crockford-Base32 format (\texttt{{[}0-9A-HJKMNP-TV-Z{]}\{26\}}).
Excludes \texttt{I}, \texttt{L}, \texttt{O}, \texttt{U} per Crockford
spec.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{justify\_center}}{justify\_center}}\label{justify_center}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{justify\_left}}{justify\_left}}\label{justify_left}

\texttt{justify\_left(s,\ width)\ →\ string} --- pad right with spaces
to reach \texttt{width}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p justify\_left("hi", 5)  \# "hi   "}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{justify\_right}}{justify\_right}}\label{justify_right}

\texttt{justify\_right(s,\ width)\ →\ string} --- pad left with spaces
to reach \texttt{width}.

\begin{Highlighting}[]

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kahan}}{kahan}}\label{kahan}

\texttt{kahan\_sum\ LIST} --- Neumaier-variant compensated summation.
Recovers \textasciitilde16-17 digits of precision on long float sums
where naive \texttt{sum} loses precision to cancellation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p sum(1e20, 1, {-}1e20, 1, {-}1e20, 1e20)         \# 0  (precision lost)}
\NormalTok{p kahan\_sum(1e20, 1, {-}1e20, 1, {-}1e20, 1e20)   \# 2  (recovered)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

World\textquotesingle s-first as scripting-lang stdlib pipe-friendly
builtin: Python\textquotesingle s \texttt{math.fsum} is closest but not
pipeable; Ruby / Node / Perl have no stdlib equivalent.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kaiser}}{kaiser}}\label{kaiser}

\texttt{window\_kaiser(\$n,\ \$beta)} (alias \texttt{kaiser}) ---
generates a Kaiser window with parameter beta controlling the tradeoff
between main lobe width and sidelobe level. Beta=0 is rectangular,
beta\textasciitilde5 is similar to Hamming.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $w = kaiser(1024, 5.0)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kama}}{kama}}\label{kama}

\texttt{kama(prices,\ period=10)\ →\ array} --- Kaufman adaptive moving
average. Smoothing constant tracks volatility:
\texttt{sc\ =\ (er·(fast\ −\ slow)\ +\ slow)²}, so the average
accelerates in trends and idles in chop.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p kama(\textbackslash{}@prices, 10)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kv}}{kv}}\label{kv}

\texttt{kv} --- operation \texttt{kv}. Category: *numpy +
scipy.special*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p kv $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kv\_batch}}{kv\_batch}}\label{kv_batch}

\texttt{kv\_batch(STORE,\ {[}{[}"put",\ k,\ v{]},\ {[}"del",\ k{]},\ ...{]})}
→ number of ops applied. All-or-nothing on the in-memory state: if any
op rejects (unknown kind, etc.), the entries map is rolled back to the
snapshot taken at entry. Caller invokes \texttt{kv\_commit} afterwards
for durability.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{kv\_batch($db, [}
\NormalTok{    ["put", "counter:a", 1],}
\NormalTok{    ["put", "counter:b", 2],}
\NormalTok{    ["del", "old\_key"],}
\NormalTok{]);}
\NormalTok{kv\_commit($db);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kv\_close}}{kv\_close}}\label{kv_close}

\texttt{kv\_close(STORE)} → \texttt{1}. Auto-commits if dirty, then
drops the handle (Arc decref). Safe to omit if \texttt{kv\_commit} was
already called.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kv\_commit}}{kv\_commit}}\label{kv_commit}

\texttt{kv\_commit(STORE)} → \texttt{1}. Atomic rewrite of the whole
archive to disk via tmp + rename + fsync. No-op if not dirty. Versioned
header (\texttt{STKV} magic + \texttt{format\_version}) lets future
stryke versions reject mismatched archives instead of silently
mis-deserialising.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kv\_count}}{kv\_count}}\label{kv_count}

\texttt{kv\_len(STORE)} → entry count.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kv\_del}}{kv\_del}}\label{kv_del}

\texttt{kv\_del(STORE,\ KEY)} → \texttt{1} if the key existed,
\texttt{0} otherwise. Marks store dirty so the next \texttt{kv\_commit}
persists the removal.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kv\_exists}}{kv\_exists}}\label{kv_exists}

\texttt{kv\_exists(STORE,\ KEY)} → \texttt{1} / \texttt{0}.
Constant-time hash lookup.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kv\_get}}{kv\_get}}\label{kv_get}

\texttt{kv\_get(STORE,\ KEY)} → value or \texttt{undef}. Compound values
come back as refs (\texttt{-\textgreater{}{[}i{]}} /
\texttt{-\textgreater{}\{k\}} arrow deref works directly). Reads are
zero-copy validation on the mmap\textquotesingle d archive when no write
has occurred since open.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kv\_info}}{kv\_info}}\label{kv_info}

\texttt{kv\_stats(STORE)} → hashref with \texttt{path},
\texttt{entries}, \texttt{dirty}, \texttt{format\_version},
\texttt{created\_at\_secs}, \texttt{last\_commit\_secs},
\texttt{commit\_count}, \texttt{stryke\_version}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kv\_keys}}{kv\_keys}}\label{kv_keys}

\texttt{kv\_keys(STORE\ {[},\ PREFIX{]})} → sorted array of keys,
optionally filtered by prefix. Use prefixes like \texttt{users:} /
\texttt{log:} as namespaces.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{for my $k (kv\_keys($db, "users:")) \{ p kv\_get($db, $k) \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kv\_new}}{kv\_new}}\label{kv_new}

\texttt{kv\_open(PATH)} → KvStore handle. Open or create a rkyv-archived
KV store at \texttt{PATH}. Missing file → empty store, no I/O until
first commit. Existing file → mmap + check\_archived\_root + deserialize
into the in-memory mirror.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $db = kv\_open("/tmp/cache.rkyv");}
\NormalTok{kv\_put($db, "users:alice", \{ name =\textgreater{} "Alice", age =\textgreater{} 30 \});}
\NormalTok{kv\_commit($db);}
\NormalTok{my $u = kv\_get($db, "users:alice");}
\NormalTok{p $u{-}\textgreater{}\{name\}                           \# Alice}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

World\textquotesingle s-first as scripting-lang stdlib: zero-copy
archive KV with SQLite-shaped ergonomics. Reads are
\texttt{mmap\ +\ validate\ +\ cast}; no parse, no allocate per row.
Family: \texttt{kv\_put}, \texttt{kv\_get}, \texttt{kv\_del},
\texttt{kv\_exists}, \texttt{kv\_keys}, \texttt{kv\_scan},
\texttt{kv\_len}, \texttt{kv\_commit}, \texttt{kv\_batch},
\texttt{kv\_close}, \texttt{kv\_stats}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kv\_put}}{kv\_put}}\label{kv_put}

\texttt{kv\_put(STORE,\ KEY,\ VALUE)} → previous value or
\texttt{undef}. Scalars round-trip lossless; arrayrefs become
\texttt{Array}, hashrefs become \texttt{Hash}. Mutation is in-memory
until \texttt{kv\_commit} rewrites the archive atomically (tmp +
rename).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{kv\_scan}}{kv\_scan}}\label{kv_scan}

\texttt{kv\_scan(STORE,\ PREFIX)} → array of \texttt{{[}KEY,\ VALUE{]}}
arrayref pairs (sorted by key). For lazy streaming over network
connections see Phase 2 \texttt{kv\_connect} /
\texttt{WireFrame::StreamChunk}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{for my $row (kv\_scan($db, "users:")) \{}
\NormalTok{    my ($key, $val) = @$row;}
\NormalTok{    say "$key {-}\textgreater{} $val{-}\textgreater{}\{name\}";}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lambert\_project}}{lambert\_project}}\label{lambert_project}

\texttt{lambert\_project(lat,\ lon,\ lat0,\ lon0)\ →\ {[}x,\ y{]}} ---
Lambert Conformal Conic on a sphere with one standard parallel
\texttt{lat0} and central meridian \texttt{lon0}. Degenerates to
equirectangular when \texttt{lat0\ ≈\ 0}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p lambert\_project(37, {-}122, 40, {-}100)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lambert\_w}}{lambert\_w}}\label{lambert_w}

\texttt{lambert\_w} (aliases \texttt{lambertw}, \texttt{productlog})
computes the Lambert W function (principal branch): the inverse of f(w)
= w·e\^{}w.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p lambertw(1)   \# 0.5671 (Omega constant)}
\NormalTok{p lambertw(exp(1))  \# 1.0}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{leaky\_bucket}}{leaky\_bucket}}\label{leaky_bucket}

\texttt{leaky\_bucket(CAPACITY,\ DRAIN\_PER\_SEC)} --- leaky-bucket rate
limiter (drains over time, fills as requests arrive).
\texttt{rl\_try\_take(cost)} succeeds if the fill plus \texttt{cost}
doesn\textquotesingle t overflow.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{leaky\_relu}}{leaky\_relu}}\label{leaky_relu}

\texttt{leaky\_relu} (alias \texttt{lrelu}) applies Leaky ReLU: x if
x≥0, alpha*x otherwise (default alpha=0.01).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p lrelu(5)     \# 5}
\NormalTok{p lrelu({-}5)    \# {-}0.05}
\NormalTok{p lrelu({-}5, 0.1)  \# {-}0.5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{leap\_year\_gregorian}}{leap\_year\_gregorian}}\label{leap_year_gregorian}

\texttt{leap\_year\_gregorian} --- operation
\texttt{leap\ year\ gregorian}. Category: *calendrical algorithms*.

\begin{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lerp}}{lerp}}\label{lerp}

\texttt{lerp(\$x,\ \$y,\ \$t)} --- linear interpolation between a and b.
When t=0 returns a, t=1 returns b, t=0.5 returns midpoint.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p lerp(0, 100, 0.5)   \# 50}
\NormalTok{p lerp(10, 20, 0.25)  \# 12.5}
\NormalTok{\# Animation: smoothly transition between values}
\NormalTok{for my $t (0:10) \{ p lerp($start, $end, $t/10) \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{less}}{less}}\label{less}

\texttt{LIST\ \textbar{}\textgreater{}\ pager} / \texttt{pager\ LIST}
--- pipe each element (one per line) into the user\textquotesingle s
\texttt{\$PAGER} (default \texttt{less\ -R}; falls back to
\texttt{more}, then plain stdout). Bypasses the pager when stdout
isn\textquotesingle t a TTY so pipelines like
\texttt{stryke\ \textquotesingle{}...\ \textbar{}\textgreater{}\ pager\textquotesingle{}\ \textbar{}\ grep}
still compose.

Blocks until the user quits the pager; returns \texttt{undef}.

Aliases: \texttt{pager}, \texttt{pg}, \texttt{less}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# browse every callable spelling interactively}
\NormalTok{keys \%all |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} pager}

\NormalTok{\# filter the reference for parallel ops}
\NormalTok{keys \%stryke::builtins |\textgreater{} grep \{ $stryke::builtins\{$\_\} eq "parallel" \} |\textgreater{} pager}

\NormalTok{\# whole file, one screen at a time}
\NormalTok{slurp("README.md") |\textgreater{} pager}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{line\_intersection}}{line\_intersection}}\label{line_intersection}

\texttt{line\_intersection(\$x1,\ \$y1,\ \$x2,\ \$y2,\ \$x3,\ \$y3,\ \$x4,\ \$y4)}
--- finds the intersection point of two lines defined by points
(x1,y1)-(x2,y2) and (x3,y3)-(x4,y4). Returns {[}\$x, \$y{]} or undef if
parallel.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $pt = line\_intersection(0, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1, 1, 0)}
\NormalTok{p @$pt   \# (0.5, 0.5)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{list\_count}}{list\_count}}\label{list_count}

\texttt{list\_count} (alias \texttt{list\_size}) returns the total
number of elements after flattening a nested list structure. Unlike
\texttt{count} which returns the top-level element count,
\texttt{list\_count} recursively descends into array references and
counts only leaf values. This is useful when you have a list of lists
and need to know the total number of individual items rather than the
number of sublists.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @nested = ([1, 2], [3, 4, 5], [6])}
\NormalTok{p list\_count @nested   \# 6}
\NormalTok{my @deep = ([1, [2, 3]], [4])}
\NormalTok{p list\_size @deep      \# 4}
\NormalTok{p list\_count 1, 2, 3   \# 3  (flat list works too)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lm}}{lm}}\label{lm}

\texttt{lm\_fit} (alias \texttt{lm}) --- simple linear regression.
Returns hash with intercept, slope, r\_squared, residuals, fitted. Like
R\textquotesingle s lm().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%m = \%\{lm([1,2,3,4,5], [2,4,5,4,5])\}}
\NormalTok{p $m\{slope\}       \# slope}
\NormalTok{p $m\{r\_squared\}   \# R²}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{load\_avg}}{load\_avg}}\label{load_avg}

\texttt{load\_avg} --- system load averages as a 3-element arrayref
\texttt{{[}1min,\ 5min,\ 15min{]}}. Uses \texttt{getloadavg()} on unix.
Returns \texttt{undef} on unsupported platforms.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $la = load\_avg}
\NormalTok{p "1m=$la{-}\textgreater{}[0] 5m=$la{-}\textgreater{}[1] 15m=$la{-}\textgreater{}[2]"}
\NormalTok{load\_avg |\textgreater{} spark |\textgreater{} p                   \# sparkline of load}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{loan\_payment}}{loan\_payment}}\label{loan_payment}

\texttt{loan\_payment(principal,\ rate\_per\_period,\ n\_periods)\ →\ number}
--- amortising loan payment formula:
\texttt{P·r·(1+r)\^{}n\ /\ ((1+r)\^{}n\ −\ 1)}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p loan\_payment(100000, 0.05/12, 360)  \# 536.82}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{locale\_calendar}}{locale\_calendar}}\label{locale_calendar}

\texttt{locale\_calendar(locale)\ →\ string} --- preferred calendar
system (gregorian, persian, hebrew, \ldots).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{locale\_canonical}}{locale\_canonical}}\label{locale_canonical}

\texttt{locale\_canonical(name)\ →\ string} --- canonicalised CLDR
locale identifier.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{locale\_collation}}{locale\_collation}}\label{locale_collation}

\texttt{locale\_collation(locale)\ →\ string} --- collation tag (e.g.
"standard", "phonebk").

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{locale\_currency}}{locale\_currency}}\label{locale_currency}

\texttt{locale\_currency(locale)\ →\ string} --- ISO 4217 currency code
for region.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{locale\_date\_format}}{locale\_date\_format}}\label{locale_date_format}

\texttt{locale\_date\_format(locale)\ →\ string} --- date pattern (e.g.
"MM/dd/yyyy").

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{locale\_decimal\_separator}}{locale\_decimal\_separator}}\label{locale_decimal_separator}

\texttt{locale\_decimal\_separator(locale)\ →\ string} --- decimal point
character ("." or ",").

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{locale\_first\_day\_of\_week}}{locale\_first\_day\_of\_week}}\label{locale_first_day_of_week}

\texttt{locale\_first\_day\_of\_week(locale)\ →\ int} --- 0=Sunday,
1=Monday.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{locale\_format}}{locale\_format}}\label{locale_format}

\texttt{locale\_format(template,\ locale,\ vars)\ →\ string} ---
locale-aware ICU-like message format.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{locale\_group\_separator}}{locale\_group\_separator}}\label{locale_group_separator}

\texttt{locale\_group\_separator(locale)\ →\ string} --- thousands
separator.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{locale\_language}}{locale\_language}}\label{locale_language}

\texttt{locale\_language(locale)\ →\ string} --- language portion.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{locale\_likely\_subtags}}{locale\_likely\_subtags}}\label{locale_likely_subtags}

\texttt{locale\_likely\_subtags(locale)\ →\ string} --- fill in likely
script/region per CLDR likely-subtags table.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{locale\_measurement\_system}}{locale\_measurement\_system}}\label{locale_measurement_system}

\texttt{locale\_measurement\_system(locale)\ →\ string} --- "metric" or
"US".

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{locale\_minimize}}{locale\_minimize}}\label{locale_minimize}

\texttt{locale\_minimize} --- locale / CLDR helper \texttt{minimize}.
Category: *extras*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p locale\_minimize $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{locale\_number\_format}}{locale\_number\_format}}\label{locale_number_format}

\texttt{locale\_number\_format(n,\ locale)\ →\ string} --- render number
with locale separators.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{locale\_parse}}{locale\_parse}}\label{locale_parse}

\texttt{locale\_parse(name)\ →\ hash} --- parse POSIX/CLDR locale id
into language/region/script/variant.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{locale\_region}}{locale\_region}}\label{locale_region}

\texttt{locale\_region(locale)\ →\ string} --- region portion.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{locale\_script}}{locale\_script}}\label{locale_script}

\texttt{locale\_script(locale)\ →\ string} --- script portion.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{locale\_time\_format}}{locale\_time\_format}}\label{locale_time_format}

\texttt{locale\_time\_format(locale)\ →\ string} --- time pattern.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{locale\_variant}}{locale\_variant}}\label{locale_variant}

\texttt{locale\_variant(locale)\ →\ string} --- variant portion.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{loess}}{loess}}\label{loess}

\texttt{lowess} (alias \texttt{loess}) --- locally-weighted scatterplot

weighting.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @smooth = @\{lowess([1,2,3,4,5], [2,1,4,3,5])\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lorenz}}{lorenz}}\label{lorenz}

\texttt{lorenz\_curve(@data)} (alias \texttt{lorenz}) --- computes the
Lorenz curve as an arrayref of {[}cumulative\_share\_of\_population,
cumulative\_share\_of\_wealth{]} pairs. Used to visualize inequality.
The diagonal represents perfect equality.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $curve = lorenz(10, 20, 30, 40)}
\NormalTok{for my $pt (@$curve) \{}
\NormalTok{    p "$pt{-}\textgreater{}[0], $pt{-}\textgreater{}[1]"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lowpass\_filter}}{lowpass\_filter}}\label{lowpass_filter}

\texttt{lowpass\_filter(\textbackslash{}@signal,\ \$cutoff)} (alias
\texttt{lpf}) --- applies a simple low-pass filter to remove
high-frequency components. Cutoff is normalized (0-1). Uses exponential
moving average.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @noisy = map \{ sin(\_/10) + rand(0.2) \} 1:100}
\NormalTok{my $smooth = lpf(\textbackslash{}@noisy, 0.1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{lp\_simplex\_min(c,\ A,\ b)\ →\ number} --- minimise

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ls}}{ls}}\label{ls}

\texttt{ls} / \texttt{ls(DIR)} --- long directory listing in the style
of \texttt{ls\ -l}. With no arguments, lists the interpreter working
directory (same base \texttt{cd} uses; not \texttt{\$\_} and not
necessarily the OS \texttt{getcwd}). With one or more directory paths,
lists each directory; if more than one path is given, each block is
prefixed with \texttt{DIR:} like GNU \texttt{ls}. Returns a single
multiline string (suitable for \texttt{p} or \texttt{spurt}). On Unix,
includes a \texttt{total} line (1024-byte block units), mode bits, link
count, numeric uid/gid, size, mtime, and name; symbolic links show
\texttt{-\textgreater{}\ target}. On Windows, lines use file attributes,
size, and a simplified timestamp.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ls()           \# logical cwd (see \textasciigrave{}cd\textasciigrave{})}
\NormalTok{p ls("/tmp")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lse}}{lse}}\label{lse}

\texttt{lse} --- operation \texttt{lse}. Alias for
\texttt{log\_sum\_exp}. Category: *misc*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p lse $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lsp\_completion\_words}}{lsp\_completion\_words}}\label{lsp_completion_words}

\texttt{lsp\_completion\_words} (alias \texttt{lsp\_words}) --- emits
every name LSP tab-complete should know about, sorted ASCII. Three
sources merged: (1) every callable bare-name spelling from
\texttt{\%all} (\textasciitilde10.6k names, primaries + aliases), (2)
sigil-prefixed live bindings (\texttt{\%a}, \texttt{\%all},
\texttt{\%b}, \texttt{\%parameters}, \texttt{@ARGV}, \texttt{@INC},
\texttt{@fpath}, \texttt{@path}, \texttt{\$\_},
\texttt{\$stryke::VERSION}, \ldots), (3) \texttt{CORE::}/\texttt{main::}
qualified spellings of every callable / unqualified binding, plus stryke
language keywords (\texttt{fn}, \texttt{match}, \texttt{class},
\texttt{struct}, \texttt{enum}, \texttt{mysync}, \texttt{frozen},
\ldots). Drives the canonical
\texttt{strykelang/lsp\_completion\_words.txt} snapshot consumed by both
the static analyzer (linter) and LSP completion provider.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{lsp\_words |\textgreater{} ep                                              \# browse everything}
\NormalTok{p len(lsp\_words)                                              \# \textasciitilde{}19,000 entries}
\NormalTok{\# Regenerate the on{-}disk snapshot:}
\NormalTok{\# stryke {-}e \textquotesingle{}lsp\_words |\textgreater{} ep\textquotesingle{} \textgreater{} strykelang/lsp\_completion\_words.txt}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{lucas\_nth}}{lucas\_nth}}\label{lucas_nth}

\texttt{lucas\_nth(n)\ →\ int} --- n-th Lucas number
\texttt{L(n)\ =\ L(n-1)\ +\ L(n-2)}, L(0)=2, L(1)=1.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p lucas\_nth(10)  \# 123}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{macd}}{macd}}\label{macd}

\texttt{macd(prices,\ fast=12,\ slow=26)\ →\ array} --- MACD line:
\texttt{EMA(fast)\ −\ EMA(slow)}. Pair with \texttt{macd\_signal} and
\texttt{macd\_histogram} for full divergence analysis.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p macd(\textbackslash{}@closes, 12, 26)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{macd\_histogram}}{macd\_histogram}}\label{macd_histogram}

\texttt{macd\_histogram(prices,\ fast=12,\ slow=26,\ sig=9)\ →\ array}
--- \texttt{macd\ −\ macd\_signal}. Positive bars = bullish momentum;
bar height tracks divergence size.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p macd\_histogram(\textbackslash{}@closes)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{macd\_signal}}{macd\_signal}}\label{macd_signal}

\texttt{macd\_signal(prices,\ fast=12,\ slow=26,\ sig=9)\ →\ array} ---
signal line for MACD: \texttt{EMA(macd\_line,\ sig)}. Crossover with
\texttt{macd} gives buy/sell ticks.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p macd\_signal(\textbackslash{}@closes, 12, 26, 9)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{mahalanobis\_sq(x,\ mean,\ cov\_inv)\ →\ number} --- squared
Mahalanobis distance with precision (inverse-covariance) matrix.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{main}}{main}}\label{main}

\texttt{main::} --- the default package every script starts in.
\texttt{our} variables and \texttt{fn} declarations without an explicit
\texttt{package} directive land in \texttt{main::}; bare references like
\texttt{\$x} / \texttt{@a} / \texttt{\%h} resolve to \texttt{\$main::x}
/ \texttt{@main::a} / \texttt{\%main::h} when looked up via the symbol
table.

Use \texttt{main::} qualifier when you need to disambiguate inside a
nested package, or when introspecting a script from a library context.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{our $verbose = 1}
\NormalTok{p $main::verbose                              \# 1 — same scalar}

\NormalTok{\# Inside a class, the bare name resolves to the class — main:: gets you out:}
\NormalTok{class Foo \{}
\NormalTok{    fn check \{ p $main::verbose \}            \# reads top{-}level our}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{\# \%main:: is the symbol{-}table stash. Lists every name declared}
\NormalTok{\# at the top level (subs, our, our @, our \%).}
\NormalTok{p scalar keys \%main::                         \# symbol{-}table size}
\NormalTok{keys \%main:: |\textgreater{} sort |\textgreater{} p                    \# browse every top{-}level name}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Related namespaces: \texttt{CORE::} (every builtin), \texttt{stryke::}
(reflection hashes --- \texttt{\%stryke::builtins} etc.), and
\texttt{Pkg::} for any user-declared \texttt{package\ Pkg}.

--- Wilcoxon rank-sum test (Mann-Whitney U). Returns U statistic. Like
R\textquotesingle s wilcox.test().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $U = wilcox([1,2,3], [4,5,6])  \# 0 (no overlap)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mann\_whitney\_u}}{mann\_whitney\_u}}\label{mann_whitney_u}

\texttt{mann\_whitney\_u(a,\ b)\ →\ \{statistic,\ df,\ p\_value\}} ---
Mann-Whitney U (Wilcoxon rank-sum) non-parametric two-sample test.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mann\_whitney\_u(\textbackslash{}@group\_a, \textbackslash{}@group\_b)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mat4\_inverse}}{mat4\_inverse}}\label{mat4_inverse}

\texttt{mat4\_inverse(m)\ →\ matrix} --- explicit 4×4 inverse via
cofactor expansion. Returns identity if singular.

view matrix.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mat4\_look\_at([0,0,5], [0,0,0], [0,1,0])}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{mat4\_multiply(a,\ b)\ →\ matrix} --- \texttt{a\ ·\ b} matrix
product.

\texttt{mat4\_orthographic(l,\ r,\ b,\ t,\ n,\ f)\ →\ matrix} ---

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mat4\_perspective(1.5708, 16/9, 0.1, 100)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{mat4\_rotate\_x(angle\_rad)\ →\ matrix} --- rotation about X
axis.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mat4\_rotate\_y}}{mat4\_rotate\_y}}\label{mat4_rotate_y}

\texttt{mat4\_rotate\_y(angle\_rad)\ →\ matrix} --- rotation about Y

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mat4\_rotate\_z}}{mat4\_rotate\_z}}\label{mat4_rotate_z}

\texttt{mat4\_rotate\_z(angle\_rad)\ →\ matrix} --- rotation about Z

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{{[}{[}i,j{]},\ …{]}}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matching\_bipartite\_hungarian([[4,1,3],[2,0,5],[3,2,2]])}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{mathieu\_b} --- operation \texttt{mathieu\ b}. Category: *numpy
+ scipy.special*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mathieu\_b $x}
\end{Highlighting}

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mathieu\_ce0 $x}

\texttt{mathieu\_se1} --- operation \texttt{mathieu\ se1}. Category:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mathieu\_se1 $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matmul}}{matmul}}\label{matmul}

\texttt{matmul} --- operation \texttt{matmul}. Alias for
\texttt{matrix\_mul}. Category: *extended stdlib*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matmul $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_add}}{matrix\_add}}\label{matrix_add}

\texttt{matrix\_add} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater) \texttt{add}.
Category: *extended stdlib*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_add $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_adjugate}}{matrix\_adjugate}}\label{matrix_adjugate}

\texttt{matrix\_adjugate(m)\ →\ matrix} --- adjugate (classical
adjoint): transpose of the cofactor matrix.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_adjugate([[1,2],[3,4]])}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_anti\_diagonal}}{matrix\_anti\_diagonal}}\label{matrix_anti_diagonal}

\texttt{matrix\_anti\_diagonal} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater)
\texttt{anti\ diagonal}. Category: *misc*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_anti\_diagonal $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_cholesky\_decompose}}{matrix\_cholesky\_decompose}}\label{matrix_cholesky_decompose}

\texttt{matrix\_cholesky\_decompose(m)\ →\ matrix} --- Cholesky
\texttt{L} such that \texttt{L·Lᵀ\ =\ m}. Requires \texttt{m} symmetric
positive-definite.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_cholesky\_decompose([[4,12,{-}16],[12,37,{-}43],[{-}16,{-}43,98]])}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_cofactor}}{matrix\_cofactor}}\label{matrix_cofactor}

\texttt{matrix\_cofactor(m)\ →\ matrix} --- cofactor matrix: each entry
\texttt{(-1)\^{}(i+j)\ ·\ \textbar{}minor(i,j)\textbar{}}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_cofactor([[1,2],[3,4]])}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_col}}{matrix\_col}}\label{matrix_col}

\texttt{matrix\_col} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater) \texttt{col}.
Category: *extended stdlib*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_col $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_cols}}{matrix\_cols}}\label{matrix_cols}

\texttt{matrix\_cols(m)\ →\ int} --- number of columns (length of first
row).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_cols([[1,2,3],[4,5,6]])  \# 3}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_concat\_h}}{matrix\_concat\_h}}\label{matrix_concat_h}

\texttt{matrix\_concat\_h(a,\ b)\ →\ matrix} --- horizontal
concatenation (\texttt{{[}A\textbar{}B{]}}); rows must match.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_concat\_h([[1,2]], [[3,4]])}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_concat\_v}}{matrix\_concat\_v}}\label{matrix_concat_v}

\texttt{matrix\_concat\_v(a,\ b)\ →\ matrix} --- vertical concatenation
(\texttt{{[}A;B{]}}); columns must match.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_concat\_v([[1,2]], [[3,4]])}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_det}}{matrix\_det}}\label{matrix_det}

\texttt{matrix\_det} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater) \texttt{det}.
Category: *extended stdlib*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_det $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_determinant}}{matrix\_determinant}}\label{matrix_determinant}

\texttt{matrix\_determinant(m)\ →\ number} --- determinant via Gaussian
elimination with partial pivoting. O(n³).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_determinant([[1,2],[3,4]])  \# {-}2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_diag}}{matrix\_diag}}\label{matrix_diag}

\texttt{matrix\_diag} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater) \texttt{diag}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_diag $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_diagonal}}{matrix\_diagonal}}\label{matrix_diagonal}

\texttt{matrix\_diagonal} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater)
\texttt{diagonal}. Category: *misc*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_diagonal $x}
\end{Highlighting}

\texttt{matrix\_get(m,\ r,\ c)\ →\ number} --- element at

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_get([[1,2],[3,4]], 1, 0)  \# 3}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{matrix\_hadamard} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater)

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_hadamard $x}

Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater)
\texttt{identity}. Category: *extended stdlib*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_identity $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_identity\_q}}{matrix\_identity\_q}}\label{matrix_identity_q}

\texttt{matrix\_identity\_q} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater)
\texttt{identity\ q}. Category: *more extensions (2)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_identity\_q $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_inverse}}{matrix\_inverse}}\label{matrix_inverse}

\texttt{matrix\_inverse} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater) \texttt{inverse}.
Category: *matrix operations extras*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_inverse $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_kronecker}}{matrix\_kronecker}}\label{matrix_kronecker}

\texttt{matrix\_kronecker(a,\ b)\ →\ matrix} --- Kronecker (tensor)
product. Result is \texttt{(ra·rb)\ ×\ (ca·cb)}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_kronecker([[1,2],[3,4]], [[0,5],[6,7]])}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_lu}}{matrix\_lu}}\label{matrix_lu}

\texttt{matrix\_lu} (alias \texttt{mlu}) computes the LU decomposition
with partial pivoting. Returns {[}L, U, P{]} where PA = LU.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($L, $U, $P) = @\{mlu([[4,3],[6,3]])\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_lu\_decompose}}{matrix\_lu\_decompose}}\label{matrix_lu_decompose}

\texttt{matrix\_lu\_decompose(m)\ →\ {[}L,\ U{]}} --- LU decomposition
without pivoting (Doolittle). Returns lower-triangular L (unit diag) and
upper-triangular U.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($L, $U) = @\{matrix\_lu\_decompose([[4,3],[6,3]])\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_map}}{matrix\_map}}\label{matrix_map}

\texttt{matrix\_map} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater) \texttt{map}.
Category: *matrix operations extras*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_map $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_max}}{matrix\_max}}\label{matrix_max}

\texttt{matrix\_max} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater) \texttt{max}.
Category: *matrix operations extras*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_max $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_min}}{matrix\_min}}\label{matrix_min}

\texttt{matrix\_min} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater) \texttt{min}.
Category: *matrix operations extras*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_min $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_minor}}{matrix\_minor}}\label{matrix_minor}

\texttt{matrix\_minor(m,\ skip\_r,\ skip\_c)\ →\ matrix} --- submatrix
with row \texttt{skip\_r} and column \texttt{skip\_c} removed.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_minor([[1,2,3],[4,5,6],[7,8,9]], 1, 1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_mul}}{matrix\_mul}}\label{matrix_mul}

\texttt{matrix\_mul} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater) \texttt{mul}.
Category: *extended stdlib*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_mul $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_mult}}{matrix\_mult}}\label{matrix_mult}

\texttt{matrix\_mult} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater) \texttt{mult}.
Category: *extended stdlib*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_mult $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_multiply}}{matrix\_multiply}}\label{matrix_multiply}

\texttt{matrix\_multiply} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater)
\texttt{multiply}. Category: *matrix / linear algebra*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_multiply $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_new}}{matrix\_new}}\label{matrix_new}

\texttt{matrix\_new(rows,\ cols=rows,\ fill=0)\ →\ matrix} --- allocate
an \texttt{rows×cols} matrix filled with \texttt{fill}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_new(3, 4, 0)  \# 3x4 zero matrix}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_norm}}{matrix\_norm}}\label{matrix_norm}

\texttt{matrix\_norm} (alias \texttt{mnorm}) computes a matrix norm.
Default is Frobenius; pass 1 for max-column-sum, Inf for max-row-sum.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mnorm([[3,4]])           \# 5 (Frobenius)}
\NormalTok{p mnorm([[1,2],[3,4]], 1)  \# 6 (1{-}norm)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_norm\_frobenius}}{matrix\_norm\_frobenius}}\label{matrix_norm_frobenius}

\texttt{matrix\_norm\_frobenius(m)\ →\ number} ---
\texttt{√(Σ\ \textbar{}m{[}i{]}{[}j{]}\textbar{}²)}. Matrix L2 in the
entry-wise sense.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_norm\_frobenius([[1,2],[3,4]])  \# sqrt(30) ≈ 5.477}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_norm\_l1}}{matrix\_norm\_l1}}\label{matrix_norm_l1}

\texttt{matrix\_norm\_l1(m)\ →\ number} --- max absolute column sum.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_norm\_l1([[1,{-}2],[3,4]])}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_norm\_linf}}{matrix\_norm\_linf}}\label{matrix_norm_linf}

\texttt{matrix\_norm\_linf(m)\ →\ number} --- max absolute row sum.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_norm\_linf([[1,{-}2],[3,4]])}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_ones}}{matrix\_ones}}\label{matrix_ones}

\texttt{matrix\_ones} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater) \texttt{ones}.
Category: *extended stdlib*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_ones $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_orthogonal\_q}}{matrix\_orthogonal\_q}}\label{matrix_orthogonal_q}

\texttt{matrix\_orthogonal\_q} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater)
\texttt{orthogonal\ q}. Category: *misc*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_orthogonal\_q $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_outer\_product}}{matrix\_outer\_product}}\label{matrix_outer_product}

\texttt{matrix\_outer\_product(u,\ v)\ →\ matrix} --- outer product
\texttt{u·vᵀ}; result is \texttt{len(u)\ ×\ len(v)}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_outer\_product([1,2,3], [4,5])}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_pinv}}{matrix\_pinv}}\label{matrix_pinv}

\texttt{matrix\_pinv} (aliases \texttt{mpinv}, \texttt{pinv}) computes
the Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse via (A\^{}T A)\^{}\{-1\} A\^{}T.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $A\_plus = pinv([[1,2],[3,4],[5,6]])}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_power}}{matrix\_power}}\label{matrix_power}

\texttt{matrix\_power} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater) \texttt{power}.
Category: *matrix operations extras*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_power $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_qr}}{matrix\_qr}}\label{matrix_qr}

\texttt{matrix\_qr} (alias \texttt{mqr}) computes the QR decomposition
via Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization. Returns {[}Q, R{]} where A = QR.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($Q, $R) = @\{mqr([[1,1],[1,{-}1]])\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_qr\_decompose}}{matrix\_qr\_decompose}}\label{matrix_qr_decompose}

\texttt{matrix\_qr\_decompose(m)\ →\ {[}Q,\ R{]}} --- Gram-Schmidt QR
decomposition. Q has orthonormal columns; R is upper-triangular.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($Q, $R) = @\{matrix\_qr\_decompose($m)\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_random\_normal}}{matrix\_random\_normal}}\label{matrix_random_normal}

\texttt{matrix\_random\_normal} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater)
\texttt{random\ normal}. Category: *more extensions (2)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_random\_normal $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_random\_uniform}}{matrix\_random\_uniform}}\label{matrix_random_uniform}

\texttt{matrix\_random\_uniform} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater)
\texttt{random\ uniform}. Category: *more extensions (2)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_random\_uniform $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{matrix\_reshape(m,\ new\_rows,\ new\_cols)\ →\ matrix} ---
reshape preserving element order (row-major flatten then refill).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_reshape([[1,2,3,4]], 2, 2)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_row}}{matrix\_row}}\label{matrix_row}

Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater) \texttt{row}.

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_row $x}

\texttt{matrix\_rows(m)\ →\ int} --- number of rows.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_rows([[1,2],[3,4],[5,6]])  \# 3}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{matrix\_scalar} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater) \texttt{scalar}.

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_scalar $x}

\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_scale}}{matrix\_scale}}\label{matrix_scale}

\texttt{matrix\_scale} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater) \texttt{scale}.
Category: *extended stdlib*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_scale $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_set}}{matrix\_set}}\label{matrix_set}

\texttt{matrix\_set(m,\ r,\ c,\ v)\ →\ matrix} --- returns a new matrix
with \texttt{m{[}r{]}{[}c{]}\ =\ v}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_set([[1,2],[3,4]], 0, 1, 99)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_shape}}{matrix\_shape}}\label{matrix_shape}

\texttt{matrix\_shape} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater) \texttt{shape}.
Category: *extended stdlib*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_shape $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_solve}}{matrix\_solve}}\label{matrix_solve}

\texttt{matrix\_solve} (aliases \texttt{msolve}, \texttt{solve}) solves
the linear system Ax=b via Gaussian elimination with partial pivoting.
Returns the solution vector x.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $A = [[2,1],[{-}1,1]]}
\NormalTok{my $y = [5,2]}
\NormalTok{my $x = solve($A, $y)   \# [1, 3]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_sub}}{matrix\_sub}}\label{matrix_sub}

\texttt{matrix\_sub} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater) \texttt{sub}.
Category: *extended stdlib*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_sub $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_submatrix}}{matrix\_submatrix}}\label{matrix_submatrix}

\texttt{matrix\_submatrix(m,\ r0,\ c0,\ nr,\ nc)\ →\ matrix} ---
\texttt{nr×nc} block starting at \texttt{(r0,c0)}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_submatrix([[1,2,3],[4,5,6],[7,8,9]], 1, 1, 2, 2)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_sum}}{matrix\_sum}}\label{matrix_sum}

\texttt{matrix\_sum} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater) \texttt{sum}.
Category: *matrix operations extras*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_sum @xs}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_swap\_cols}}{matrix\_swap\_cols}}\label{matrix_swap_cols}

\texttt{matrix\_swap\_cols(m,\ i,\ j)\ →\ matrix} --- swap two columns
across all rows.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_swap\_rows}}{matrix\_swap\_rows}}\label{matrix_swap_rows}

\texttt{matrix\_swap\_rows(m,\ i,\ j)\ →\ matrix} --- swap two rows.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_symmetric\_q}}{matrix\_symmetric\_q}}\label{matrix_symmetric_q}

\texttt{matrix\_symmetric\_q} --- matrix op (dense
Vec\textless Vec\textless f64\textgreater\textgreater)
\texttt{symmetric\ q}. Category: *misc*.

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p matrix\_symmetric\_q $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{matrix\_to\_string}}{matrix\_to\_string}}\label{matrix_to_string}

\texttt{matrix\_to\_string(m)\ →\ string} --- pretty-print rows joined
by newlines, each row comma-separated with 4 decimal places.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{print matrix\_to\_string([[1.5,2.7],[3.14,4.2]])}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{max\_drawdown}}{max\_drawdown}}\label{max_drawdown}

\texttt{max\_drawdown(@equity\_curve)} (alias \texttt{mdd}) --- computes
the maximum peak-to-trough decline in a series of values. Returns as a
decimal (0.2 = 20\% drawdown). Key risk metric.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @equity = (100, 120, 90, 110, 85, 130)}
\NormalTok{p mdd(@equity)   \# \textasciitilde{}0.29 (29\% max drawdown)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{medfilt}}{medfilt}}\label{medfilt}

\texttt{median\_filter(\textbackslash{}@signal,\ \$window\_size)} (alias
\texttt{medfilt}) --- applies a median filter for noise removal. Each
output is the median of the surrounding window. Excellent for removing
impulse noise while preserving edges.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @spiky = (1, 2, 100, 3, 4)   \# 100 is spike}
\NormalTok{my $clean = medfilt(\textbackslash{}@spiky, 3)}
\NormalTok{p @$clean   \# spike removed}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{medfilt\_1d}}{medfilt\_1d}}\label{medfilt_1d}

*economics + game theory*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p medfilt\_1d $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mem\_free}}{mem\_free}}\label{mem_free}

\texttt{mem\_free} --- free/available physical RAM in bytes. On Linux
reads \texttt{MemAvailable} from \texttt{/proc/meminfo} (falls back to
\texttt{MemFree}). On macOS uses \texttt{vm.page\_free\_count} via
sysctl. Returns \texttt{undef} on unsupported platforms.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mem\_free                               \# 3821666304}
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(mem\_free)                 \# 3.6 GiB}
\NormalTok{my $pct = int(mem\_free / mem\_total * 100)}
\NormalTok{p "$pct\% free"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mem\_total}}{mem\_total}}\label{mem_total}

\texttt{mem\_total} --- total physical RAM in bytes. Uses
\texttt{/proc/meminfo} on Linux, \texttt{sysctlbyname("hw.memsize")} on
macOS. Returns \texttt{undef} on unsupported platforms.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mem\_total                              \# 68719476736}
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(mem\_total)                \# 64.0 GiB}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mem\_used}}{mem\_used}}\label{mem_used}

\texttt{mem\_used} --- used physical RAM in bytes (total - free).
Returns \texttt{undef} if either \texttt{mem\_total} or
\texttt{mem\_free} is unavailable.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(mem\_used)                 \# 60.4 GiB}
\NormalTok{gauge(mem\_used / mem\_total) |\textgreater{} p         \# memory usage gauge}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mercator\_project\_x}}{mercator\_project\_x}}\label{mercator_project_x}

\texttt{mercator\_project\_x(lon\_deg)\ →\ number} --- spherical
Mercator x in meters using WGS84 radius.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mercator\_project\_x({-}122.4194)}

\NormalTok{p mineral\_mohs\_hardness $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{minhash}}{minhash}}\label{minhash}

\texttt{minhash(K=128)} --- MinHash Jaccard-similarity signature with
\texttt{K} independent hash functions (Kirsch-Mitzenmacher
double-hashing over xxh3-128). \texttt{mh\_jaccard(a,\ b)} ≈ true
Jaccard with error O(1/sqrt(K)).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $a = minhash(256); my $b = minhash(256)}
\NormalTok{mh\_add($a, $\_) for @shingles\_a}
\NormalTok{mh\_add($b, $\_) for @shingles\_b}
\NormalTok{p mh\_jaccard($a, $b)                  \# \textasciitilde{}|A∩B|/|A∪B|}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{minimax\_value}}{minimax\_value}}\label{minimax_value}

\texttt{minimax\_value(leaves,\ depth)\ →\ number} --- minimax over a
complete binary tree of leaf payoffs. Even depth = max layer.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p minimax\_value([3,5,2,9], 2)  \# 3}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mish}}{mish}}\label{mish}

\texttt{mish} applies the Mish activation: x·tanh(softplus(x)). Often
outperforms ReLU/Swish.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mish(1)    \# 0.8651}
\NormalTok{p mish({-}1)   \# {-}0.3034}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mixed\_strategy\_2x2}}{mixed\_strategy\_2x2}}\label{mixed_strategy_2x2}

\texttt{mixed\_strategy\_2x2(payoff)\ →\ \{row\_strategy,\ col\_strategy\}}
--- Nash equilibrium probabilities for a 2×2 \textbf{zero-sum} game
given the row player\textquotesingle s payoff matrix
\texttt{{[}{[}a,b{]},{[}c,d{]}{]}}. Returns row\textquotesingle s prob
of row 1 and col\textquotesingle s prob of col 1. For non-zero-sum, both
players\textquotesingle{} payoff matrices are required and this function
does not apply.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mixed\_strategy\_2x2([[1,{-}1],[{-}1,1]])  \# 0.5/0.5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mkdtemp}}{mkdtemp}}\label{mkdtemp}

\texttt{mktempdir(PREFIX="stryke")} --- create a fresh temp directory
and return its absolute path. NOT auto-deleted.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mktemp}}{mktemp}}\label{mktemp}

\texttt{mktemp(PREFIX="stryke")} --- create a fresh temp file under
\texttt{\$TMPDIR} and return its absolute path as a string. File is NOT
auto-deleted (caller must \texttt{rm} it); use the existing
\texttt{tempfile()} builtin for auto-cleanup semantics. Wraps the
\texttt{tempfile} crate.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $p = mktemp("build")              \# /tmp/build{-}AbCdEf}
\NormalTok{spurt($p, "…")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ml\_softmax\_layer([1,2,3])  \# sums to 1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mod\_dur}}{mod\_dur}}\label{mod_dur}

\texttt{modified\_duration(\$face,\ \$coupon\_rate,\ \$ytm,\ \$periods)}
(alias \texttt{mod\_dur}) --- computes modified duration, which directly
measures price sensitivity to yield changes. Equal to Macaulay duration
/ (1 + ytm).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mod\_dur(1000, 0.05, 0.05, 10)   \# \textasciitilde{}7.3}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mod\_exp}}{mod\_exp}}\label{mod_exp}

\texttt{mod\_exp} (aliases \texttt{modexp}, \texttt{powmod}) computes
modular exponentiation: base\^{}exp mod m, using fast binary
exponentiation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p powmod(2, 10, 1000)  \# 24 (2\^{}10 mod 1000)}
\NormalTok{p powmod(3, 13, 50)    \# 7}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mod\_inv}}{mod\_inv}}\label{mod_inv}

\texttt{mod\_inv} (alias \texttt{modinv}) computes the modular
multiplicative inverse via extended Euclidean algorithm. Errors if no
inverse exists.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p modinv(3, 7)   \# 5 (3*5 ≡ 1 mod 7)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{mod\_op} --- modular-arithmetic helper \texttt{op}. Category:
*trig / math*.

\begin{Shaded}

\NormalTok{p mod\_op $x}

\end{Shaded}

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mones $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{money\_add}}{money\_add}}\label{money_add}

\texttt{money\_add(a,\ b)\ →\ number} --- add two dollar amounts by
converting each to integer cents via banker\textquotesingle s rounding,
summing in i64 (saturating), then dividing by 100. Avoids accumulated
float drift.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{money\_compare}}{money\_compare}}\label{money_compare}

\texttt{money\_compare(a,\ b)\ →\ -1\textbar{}0\textbar{}1} ---
three-way compare after converting both to integer cents
(banker\textquotesingle s rounding). Avoids float-equality pitfalls.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{money\_div}}{money\_div}}\label{money_div}

\texttt{money\_div(amount,\ divisor)\ →\ number} --- divide amount (in
cents) by \texttt{divisor}, then round half-to-even back to cents. UNDEF
on \texttt{divisor\ ==\ 0}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{money\_mul}}{money\_mul}}\label{money_mul}

\texttt{money\_mul(amount,\ factor)\ →\ number} --- multiply amount (in
cents) by \texttt{factor}, then round half-to-even
(banker\textquotesingle s rounding) back to cents.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{money\_sub}}{money\_sub}}\label{money_sub}

\texttt{money\_sub(a,\ b)\ →\ number} --- subtract dollar amounts in
integer cents with banker\textquotesingle s rounding on input.

\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mulberry32\_next}}{mulberry32\_next}}\label{mulberry32_next}

\texttt{mulberry32\_next(seed)\ →\ int} --- Mulberry32 PRNG (Tommy
Ettinger 2017). Advances state by \texttt{+0x6D2B79F5} then permutes to
32 bits. To iterate, caller tracks state and adds \texttt{0x6D2B79F5}
between calls.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mutex}}{mutex}}\label{mutex}

\texttt{mutex()\ →\ mutex} --- create a fresh, unlocked exclusive mutex.
Pair with \texttt{mutex\_lock} / \texttt{mutex\_unlock} (or
\texttt{mutex\_try\_lock}) to serialize access to shared state.
Reference-shared across \texttt{async}/\texttt{spawn} blocks; safe under
contention via \texttt{parking\_lot::Condvar} (waiters park, no busy
spin).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{mysync $m = mutex()}
\NormalTok{mysync $counter = 0}
\NormalTok{my @t}
\NormalTok{for (1:10) \{}
\NormalTok{    push @t, async \{}
\NormalTok{        mutex\_lock($m)}
\NormalTok{        defer \{ mutex\_unlock($m) \}}
\NormalTok{        $counter++}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{await($\_) for @t}
\NormalTok{p $counter   \# 10}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mutex\_is\_locked}}{mutex\_is\_locked}}\label{mutex_is_locked}

\texttt{mutex\_is\_locked(\$m)\ →\ 1\textbar{}0} --- \texttt{1} when
currently held, \texttt{0} when free. Inspection only; no
acquire/release side effect.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p mutex\_is\_locked($m)   \# 0}
\NormalTok{mutex\_lock($m)}
\NormalTok{p mutex\_is\_locked($m)   \# 1}
\NormalTok{mutex\_unlock($m)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mutex\_lock}}{mutex\_lock}}\label{mutex_lock}

\texttt{mutex\_lock(\$m)\ →\ undef} --- block until the mutex is
acquired, marking it held. Use with
\texttt{defer\ \{\ mutex\_unlock(\$m)\ \}} for RAII-style release on
scope exit (covers early returns and exceptions).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{mutex\_lock($m)}
\NormalTok{defer \{ mutex\_unlock($m) \}}
\NormalTok{\# critical section — exclusive access until block exit}
\NormalTok{update\_shared\_state()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mutex\_try\_lock}}{mutex\_try\_lock}}\label{mutex_try_lock}

\texttt{mutex\_try\_lock(\$m)\ →\ 1\textbar{}0} --- non-blocking
acquire. Returns \texttt{1} if the mutex was free (and is now held);
\texttt{0} if it was already held. Useful for opportunistic work that
should be skipped under contention.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{if (mutex\_try\_lock($m)) \{}
\NormalTok{    defer \{ mutex\_unlock($m) \}}
\NormalTok{    do\_work()}
\NormalTok{\} else \{}
\NormalTok{    p "someone else has it — skipping"}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{mutex\_unlock}}{mutex\_unlock}}\label{mutex_unlock}

\texttt{mutex\_unlock(\$m)\ →\ undef} --- release the lock and wake one
waiter (if any). Unlocking a free mutex is a tolerated no-op.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{mutex\_lock($m)}
\NormalTok{try \{}
\NormalTok{    work()}
\NormalTok{\} finally \{}
\NormalTok{    mutex\_unlock($m)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{newton}}{newton}}\label{newton}

\texttt{newton\_method} (aliases \texttt{newton},
\texttt{newton\_raphson}) finds a root via Newton-Raphson. Takes f,
f\textquotesingle, x0, and optional tolerance.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $root = newton(fn \{ \_0**2 {-} 2 \}, fn \{ 2*\_0 \}, 1.5)  \# √2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{newtons\_to\_lbf}}{newtons\_to\_lbf}}\label{newtons_to_lbf}

\texttt{newtons\_to\_lbf} --- convert from \texttt{newtons} to
\texttt{lbf}. Category: *file stat / path*.

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p newtons\_to\_lbf $value}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ngram}}{ngram}}\label{ngram}

\texttt{ngram} --- operation \texttt{ngram}. Alias for \texttt{ngrams}.
Category: *string processing extras*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ngram $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ngram\_perplexity}}{ngram\_perplexity}}\label{ngram_perplexity}

\texttt{ngram\_perplexity(model,\ tokens,\ n=2)\ →\ number} ---
perplexity over a held-out sequence.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ngram\_prob}}{ngram\_prob}}\label{ngram_prob}

\texttt{ngram\_prob(model,\ context,\ next\_token)\ →\ number} ---
conditional probability \texttt{P(next\ \textbar{}\ context)}. Returns 0
for unseen.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ngram\_top\_k\_next}}{ngram\_top\_k\_next}}\label{ngram_top_k_next}

\texttt{ngram\_top\_k\_next(model,\ context,\ k=5)\ →\ array} --- top-k
most likely next tokens with probabilities.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ngram\_top\_k\_next($model, "the", 5)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ngram\_train}}{ngram\_train}}\label{ngram_train}

\texttt{ngram\_train(tokens,\ n=2)\ →\ hash} --- context → next-token
probability hash. Maximum-likelihood estimate.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $model = ngram\_train(["the","cat","sat","the","cat"], 2)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ngrams}}{ngrams}}\label{ngrams}

\texttt{ngrams} --- operation \texttt{ngrams}. Category: *string
processing extras*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ngrams $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{nice}}{nice}}\label{nice}

\texttt{nice(PRIORITY=10)} --- set this process\textquotesingle s
priority adjustment via \texttt{libc::setpriority}. Range
\texttt{{[}-20,\ 19{]}} (negative is higher priority; requires
CAP\_SYS\_NICE / root to set). Returns the new priority on success or
\texttt{undef} on permission denial. Standard Unix \texttt{nice}
semantics.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{nice(5)                              \# be polite}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{normal\_cdf}}{normal\_cdf}}\label{normal_cdf}

\texttt{normal\_cdf(\$x,\ \$mu,\ \$sigma)} (alias \texttt{normcdf}) ---
cumulative distribution function. Returns probability that a value is ≤
x.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p normcdf(0)              \# 0.5 (half below mean)}
\NormalTok{p normcdf(1.96)           \# \textasciitilde{}0.975 (95\% confidence bound)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{normal\_pdf}}{normal\_pdf}}\label{normal_pdf}

\texttt{normal\_pdf(\$x,\ \$mu,\ \$sigma)} (alias \texttt{normpdf}) ---
normal/Gaussian distribution probability density function. Default: μ=0,
σ=1 (standard normal).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p normpdf(0)              \# \textasciitilde{}0.399 (peak of standard normal)}
\NormalTok{p normpdf(0, 100, 15)     \# PDF at IQ=100, mean=100, stddev=15}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{normalize\_signal(\textbackslash{}@signal)} (alias
\texttt{normsig}) --- scales signal to range {[}-1, 1{]} based on its
peak absolute value. Useful for audio processing.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $norm = normsig(\textbackslash{}@audio)}
\NormalTok{p max(map \{ abs \} @$norm)   \# 1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{now}}{now}}\label{now}

\texttt{now} --- Unix epoch seconds. Returns the current time as the
number of seconds since \texttt{1970-01-01T00:00:00Z}, like
Perl\textquotesingle s \texttt{time()}. Pass a timezone string (e.g.
\texttt{now("UTC")} or \texttt{now("America/New\_York")}) to get an
ISO-8601 datetime string in that zone instead --- that path forwards to
\texttt{datetime\_now\_tz}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p now              \# 1778262882}
\NormalTok{p now("UTC")       \# 2026{-}05{-}08T17:54:42.577935Z}
\NormalTok{my $start = now}
\NormalTok{\# ... do work ...}
\NormalTok{printf "elapsed: \%d s\textbackslash{}n", now {-} $start}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ode\_euler}}{ode\_euler}}\label{ode_euler}

\texttt{ode\_euler(derivatives,\ dt=0.01,\ y0=0)\ →\ array} --- explicit
Euler integration: \texttt{y{[}i+1{]}\ =\ y{[}i{]}\ +\ dt·f{[}i{]}} with
initial condition \texttt{y0}. Returns a trajectory of length
\texttt{len(derivatives)\ +\ 1}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ode\_euler(\textbackslash{}@dx\_dt, 0.1, 1.0)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{open\_url}}{open\_url}}\label{open_url}

\texttt{openurl(URL\_OR\_PATH)} --- launch the OS default handler for
\texttt{URL\_OR\_PATH}. macOS → \texttt{open}, Linux →
\texttt{xdg-open}, Windows → \texttt{cmd\ /c\ start}. Returns \texttt{1}
on launch success, \texttt{0} otherwise.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{openurl("https://stryke.menketech.com")}
\NormalTok{openurl("./report.pdf")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

dilation. Removes small bright specks.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{orbital\_period}}{orbital\_period}}\label{orbital_period}

\texttt{orbital\_period(\$mass,\ \$radius)} (alias \texttt{orbper}) ---
period for circular orbit: T = 2π√(r³/GM). Returns seconds.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $t = orbper       \# \textasciitilde{}5560 s (ISS orbital period)}
\NormalTok{p $t / 60            \# \textasciitilde{}93 minutes}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{orbital\_period\_au}}{orbital\_period\_au}}\label{orbital_period_au}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{orbital\_velocity}}{orbital\_velocity}}\label{orbital_velocity}

\texttt{orbital\_velocity(\$mass,\ \$radius)} (alias \texttt{orbvel})
--- velocity for circular orbit: v = √(GM/r). Defaults to LEO around
Earth.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p orbvel    \# \textasciitilde{}7672 m/s (ISS orbital speed)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{oursync}}{oursync}}\label{oursync}

Package-global counterpart of \texttt{mysync}. Same
\texttt{Arc\textless{}Mutex\textgreater{}} backing, but the binding is
keyed by the package-qualified stash name (\texttt{Pkg::x}) so all
packages and parallel workers share one cell.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{package Counter}
\NormalTok{oursync $total = 0}
\NormalTok{fn bump \{ $total++ \}}
\NormalTok{package main}
\NormalTok{fan 10000 \{ Counter::bump() \}}
\NormalTok{p $Counter::total   \# 10000}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Use \texttt{oursync} for cross-package shared state; use \texttt{mysync}
for lexically scoped shared state.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{outer}}{outer}}\label{outer}

\texttt{outer} --- outer product of two vectors. Returns matrix where
M{[}i{]}{[}j{]} = v1{[}i{]} * v2{[}j{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $m = outer([1,2,3], [10,20])  \# [[10,20],[20,40],[30,60]]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{outliers\_iqr}}{outliers\_iqr}}\label{outliers_iqr}

\texttt{outliers\_iqr(@data)} --- identifies outliers using the IQR
method (1.5 × IQR beyond Q1/Q3). Returns an arrayref of outlier values.
Standard approach for detecting unusual data points.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @data = (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 100)}
\NormalTok{my $out = outliers\_iqr(@data)}
\NormalTok{p @$out   \# (100)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pca}}{pca}}\label{pca}

\texttt{prcomp} (alias \texttt{pca}) --- Principal Component Analysis
via eigendecomposition of covariance matrix. Returns eigenvalues
(variance explained). Like R\textquotesingle s prcomp().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @var = @\{pca([[1,2],[3,4],[5,6],[7,8]])\}}
\NormalTok{\# variance explained by each component}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pcauchy}}{pcauchy}}\label{pcauchy}

\texttt{pcauchy} --- Cauchy CDF. P(X ≤ x). Args: x {[}, location,
scale{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p pcauchy(0, 0, 1)  \# 0.5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pcg32\_next}}{pcg32\_next}}\label{pcg32_next}

\texttt{pcg32\_next(state,\ inc)\ →\ int} --- PCG32 output for the
current state (O\textquotesingle Neill 2014). To iterate, compute the
next state as \texttt{state·6364136223846793005\ +\ inc} and call again.
\texttt{inc} must be odd; it identifies the PCG stream and does not
affect this call\textquotesingle s output.

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}

\NormalTok{my @scores = 1:100}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{perfview}}{perfview}}\label{perfview}

\texttt{perfview} (alias \texttt{pfv}) --- read the wall-clock perf log
written by stryke runs launched with the \texttt{-\/-record} CLI flag
(or with \texttt{STRYKE\_RECORD=1} in the environment). Every recorded
invocation writes one row to
\texttt{\textasciitilde{}/.stryke/perf.sqlite} (path, argv, started\_ns,
duration\_ns, exit\_code, version, host, pid, parent\_pid).
\texttt{perfview} queries that table and returns an arrayref of
hashrefs.

No args = top 20 slowest. Options pass as
\texttt{key\ =\textgreater{}\ value} pairs:

\texttt{top\ =\textgreater{}\ N} --- limit to N rows (default 20; pass
\texttt{all\ =\textgreater{}\ 1} for no limit)
\texttt{name\ =\textgreater{}\ "SUB"} --- substring filter on
\texttt{path} \texttt{re\ =\textgreater{}\ "REGEX"} --- regex filter on
\texttt{path} (alias: \texttt{regex})
\texttt{path\ =\textgreater{}\ "/abs"} --- exact-match filter on
\texttt{path} \texttt{since\ =\textgreater{}\ "7d"} --- only rows newer
than 7d / 24h / 30m / 90s / 2w \texttt{slowest\ =\textgreater{}\ 1} ---
order by duration desc (default) \texttt{fastest\ =\textgreater{}\ 1}
--- order by duration asc \texttt{recent\ =\textgreater{}\ 1} --- order
by id desc (most recent first)

From the CLI, \texttt{s\ perfview} wraps the result in \texttt{ddump} so
the table prints directly; from stryke code it composes with
\texttt{grep} / \texttt{dd} / \texttt{to\_csv} naturally.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# CLI quick view: top 20 slowest recorded runs}
\NormalTok{\# $ s {-}{-}record t examples/rosetta/t                  \# record one row per test file}
\NormalTok{\# $ s perfview                                        \# show top 20 slowest}
\NormalTok{\# $ s perfview top 5                                  \# top 5}
\NormalTok{\# $ s perfview name rosetta                           \# filter by path substring}

\NormalTok{\# From stryke code:}
\NormalTok{perfview(top =\textgreater{} 10) |\textgreater{} e \{ p "$\_{-}\textgreater{}\{duration\_ms\}ms  $\_{-}\textgreater{}\{path\}" \}}
\NormalTok{perfview(name =\textgreater{} "rosetta", since =\textgreater{} "24h") |\textgreater{} dd}
\NormalTok{perfview(path =\textgreater{} "/abs/path/to/script.stk") |\textgreater{} e \{ p $\_{-}\textgreater{}\{duration\_ns\} \}}
\NormalTok{perfview(all =\textgreater{} 1) |\textgreater{} grep \{ $\_{-}\textgreater{}\{exit\_code\} \} |\textgreater{} e p   \# all failed runs}
\NormalTok{perfview(fastest =\textgreater{} 1, top =\textgreater{} 5) |\textgreater{} to\_csv |\textgreater{} p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Schema columns returned: \texttt{id}, \texttt{path}, \texttt{argv},
\texttt{started\_ns}, \texttt{duration\_ns}, \texttt{duration\_ms},
\texttt{exit\_code}, \texttt{version}, \texttt{host}, \texttt{pid},
\texttt{parent\_pid}. Auto-prunes rows older than 90 days every
\textasciitilde1000 inserts. SQLite is in WAL mode so \texttt{perfview}
reads don\textquotesingle t block concurrent parallel-test inserts.

See also: \texttt{-\/-record} CLI flag,
\texttt{\textasciitilde{}/.stryke/perf.sqlite}, \texttt{-\/-profile} /
\texttt{-\/-flame} (per-function profiling within one script ---
orthogonal to wall-clock per-script recording).
