\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{perlin\_3d}}{perlin\_3d}}\label{perlin_3d}

\texttt{perlin\_3d(x,\ y,\ z)\ →\ number} --- Ken
Perlin\textquotesingle s 3D gradient noise: looks up 8 corner gradients
of the unit cube, computes dot products with offset vectors, trilinearly
interpolates through \texttt{fade(xf,\ yf,\ zf)}. Range
\textasciitilde{[}-1, 1{]}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{perm}}{perm}}\label{perm}

\texttt{permutations(\$n,\ \$r)} (alias \texttt{perm}) --- number of
ways to arrange r items from n. Formula: n!/(n-r)!. Order matters.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p perm(5, 3)    \# 60 (ways to arrange 3 items from 5)}
\NormalTok{p perm(10, 2)   \# 90}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{phase\_spectrum}}{phase\_spectrum}}\label{phase_spectrum}

\texttt{phase\_spectrum(\textbackslash{}@signal)} --- computes the phase
angle (in radians) at each frequency bin from the DFT.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $phases = phase\_spectrum(\textbackslash{}@signal)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{phaser\_simple}}{phaser\_simple}}\label{phaser_simple}

\texttt{phaser\_simple(signal,\ rate=0.5,\ depth=1,\ sr=44100)\ →\ array}
--- 4-stage all-pass cascade with LFO-modulated coefficient. Each stage
runs \texttt{y{[}n{]}\ =\ -a·x{[}n{]}\ +\ x{[}n-1{]}\ +\ a·y{[}n-1{]}}
where \texttt{a\ =\ depth·sin(2π·rate·t)}. Output is 50/50 wet/dry mix.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pip}}{pip}}\label{pip}

\texttt{point\_in\_polygon(\$x,\ \$y,\ @polygon)} (alias \texttt{pip})
--- tests if a point lies inside a polygon. Polygon is given as flat
list {[}x1,y1,x2,y2,...{]}. Returns 1 if inside, 0 if outside. Uses
ray-casting algorithm.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @square = (0,0, 4,0, 4,4, 0,4)}
\NormalTok{p pip(2, 2, @square)   \# 1 (inside)}
\NormalTok{p pip(5, 5, @square)   \# 0 (outside)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pmt}}{pmt}}\label{pmt}

\texttt{pmt(\$rate,\ \$nper,\ \$pv)} --- computes the periodic payment
for a loan given interest rate, number of periods, and present value.
Standard amortization formula.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# $100k loan, 5\% annual rate, 30 years}
\NormalTok{p pmt(0.05/12, 360, 100000)   \# \textasciitilde{}$537/month}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pnorm}}{pnorm}}\label{pnorm}

\texttt{pnorm} --- normal CDF. P(X ≤ x) for N(mu, sigma). Args: x {[},
mu, sigma{]}. Default standard normal.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p pnorm(0)          \# 0.5}
\NormalTok{p pnorm(1.96)       \# 0.975}
\NormalTok{p pnorm(100, 90, 15) \# z{-}score for IQ 100}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

c1·x + c2·x² + ... using Horner\textquotesingle s method.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p polyval([1, 0, 1], 3)  \# 1 + 0*3 + 1*9 = 10}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{polyfit}}{polyfit}}\label{polyfit}

\texttt{polynomial\_fit} (alias \texttt{polyfit}) performs least-squares
polynomial fitting. Returns coefficients {[}c0, c1, ..., cn{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $c = polyfit([0,1,2,3], [1,3,5,7], 1)  \# linear fit}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{polygon\_offset}}{polygon\_offset}}\label{polygon_offset}

\texttt{polygon\_offset(points,\ d=1)\ →\ array} ---
perpendicular-distance offset using the corner-bisector formula
\texttt{vertex\ +\ d·(n1\ +\ n2)/(1\ +\ n1·n2)}, where \texttt{n1},
\texttt{n2} are the outward unit normals of the adjacent edges. Gives an
exact perpendicular offset (not just \texttt{d}·bisector\_dir); falls
back to bisector direction when \texttt{1\ +\ n1·n2\ ≈\ 0}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{polygon\_orientation}}{polygon\_orientation}}\label{polygon_orientation}

\texttt{polygon\_orientation} --- 2D polygon op \texttt{orientation}.
Category: *complex / geom / color / trig*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p polygon\_orientation $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{polygon\_perimeter}}{polygon\_perimeter}}\label{polygon_perimeter}

\texttt{polygon\_perimeter(@points)} (alias \texttt{polyper}) ---
computes the perimeter of a polygon. Points as flat list
{[}x1,y1,x2,y2,...{]}. Assumes closed polygon.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @square = (0,0, 1,0, 1,1, 0,1)}
\NormalTok{p polyper(@square)   \# 4}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{popcount\_u32}}{popcount\_u32}}\label{popcount_u32}

\texttt{popcount\_u32(x)\ →\ int} --- population count of the 32 lowest
bits.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{popcount\_u64}}{popcount\_u64}}\label{popcount_u64}

\texttt{popcount\_u64(x)\ →\ int} --- population count over all 64 bits
(alias for \texttt{bit\_count\_ones}).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{popd}}{popd}}\label{popd}

\texttt{popd} --- pop the top entry off the directory stack and
\texttt{chdir} to it. Returns the new cwd. Errors when the stack is
empty (\texttt{popd:\ directory\ stack\ empty}). Pair with
\texttt{pushd}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $psd = psd(\textbackslash{}@signal)}
\NormalTok{@$psd |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{priv}}{priv}}\label{priv}

\texttt{priv} marks a field or method as private. Private members can
only be accessed from within the class.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{class Secret \{}
\NormalTok{    priv hidden: Int = 42}
\NormalTok{    priv fn internal \{ \}}
\NormalTok{    pub fn reveal \{ $self{-}\textgreater{}hidden \}  \# internal access ok}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{\# $obj{-}\textgreater{}hidden  \# ERROR: field is private}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{proc\_mem}}{proc\_mem}}\label{proc_mem}

\texttt{proc\_mem} (alias \texttt{rss}) --- current process resident set
size (RSS) in bytes. On Linux reads \texttt{VmRSS} from
\texttt{/proc/self/status}. On macOS uses
\texttt{getrusage(RUSAGE\_SELF)}. Returns \texttt{undef} on unsupported
platforms.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(rss)                      \# 18.5 MiB}
\NormalTok{my $before = rss}
\NormalTok{do\_work()}
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(rss {-} $before) . " allocated"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{projectile\_max\_height}}{projectile\_max\_height}}\label{projectile_max_height}

\texttt{projectile\_max\_height(\$velocity,\ \$angle)} (alias
\texttt{projheight}) --- maximum height H = v²sin²(θ)/(2g).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p projheight(100, 45)   \# \textasciitilde{}255 m}
\NormalTok{p projheight(100, 90)   \# \textasciitilde{}510 m (straight up)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{projectile\_position}}{projectile\_position}}\label{projectile_position}

\texttt{projectile\_position(v0,\ angle\_deg,\ g=9.81,\ t)\ →\ {[}x,\ y{]}}
--- position at time \texttt{t} for given launch velocity/angle.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p projectile\_position(10, 45, 9.81, 0.5)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{projectile\_range}}{projectile\_range}}\label{projectile_range}

\texttt{projectile\_range(\$velocity,\ \$angle)} (alias
\texttt{projrange}) --- horizontal range R = v²sin(2θ)/g. Angle in
degrees.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p projrange(100, 45)   \# \textasciitilde{}1020 m (optimal angle for range)}
\NormalTok{p projrange(100, 30)   \# \textasciitilde{}884 m}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{projectile\_time}}{projectile\_time}}\label{projectile_time}

\texttt{projectile\_time} --- operation \texttt{projectile\ time}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p projectile\_time $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{projectile\_velocity}}{projectile\_velocity}}\label{projectile_velocity}

\texttt{projectile\_velocity(v0,\ angle\_deg,\ g=9.81,\ t)\ →\ {[}vx,\ vy{]}}
--- velocity components at time \texttt{t}.

\texttt{projtime} --- operation \texttt{projtime}. Alias for

\texttt{prop\_table} --- convert a count matrix to proportions (each
cell / total). Like R\textquotesingle s prop.table().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p prop\_table([[10,20],[30,40]])  \# [[0.1,0.2],[0.3,0.4]]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{prop\_test}}{prop\_test}}\label{prop_test}

\texttt{prop\_test} --- one-sample proportion z-test. Returns
{[}z-statistic, p-value{]}. Like R\textquotesingle s prop.test().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($z, $pval) = @\{proptest(55, 100, 0.5)\}  \# test if 55/100 differs from 50\%\%}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{proportion\_test(x,\ n,\ p0=0.5)\ →\ \{statistic,\ df,\ p\_value\}}
--- one-sample z-test for a proportion.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p proportion\_test(45, 100, 0.5)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{protein\_charge\_at\_ph(seq,\ ph=7)\ →\ number} --- net charge
at given pH using Henderson-Hasselbalch on each ionisable residue.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{protein\_hydrophobicity}}{protein\_hydrophobicity}}\label{protein_hydrophobicity}

\texttt{protein\_hydrophobicity(seq)\ →\ number} --- average
Kyte-Doolittle hydropathy.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p protein\_hydrophobicity("GAVIL")  \# 2.78}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{protein\_molecular\_weight}}{protein\_molecular\_weight}}\label{protein_molecular_weight}

\texttt{protein\_molecular\_weight(seq)\ →\ number} --- molecular weight
in Da. Sums per-residue weights, subtracts (n-1)·18.02 (water lost in
peptide bonds).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p protein\_molecular\_weight("GAVIL")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{protein\_pI}}{protein\_pI}}\label{protein_pi}

\texttt{protein\_pI(seq)\ →\ number} --- isoelectric point via bisection
on net-charge curve using standard pKa table.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p protein\_pI("MKVL")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{punct}}{punct}}\label{punct}

\texttt{punctuation} (alias \texttt{punct}) extracts all ASCII
punctuation characters from a string and returns them as a list. Filters
out letters, digits, whitespace --- keeps only punctuation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p join "", punctuation("Hello, world!")  \# ,!}
\NormalTok{"a.b{-}c" |\textgreater{} punct |\textgreater{} cnt |\textgreater{} p             \# 2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{punif}}{punif}}\label{punif}

\texttt{punif} --- uniform CDF. P(X ≤ x) for Uniform(a, b). Args: x, a,
b.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p punif(0.5, 0, 1)  \# 0.5}
\NormalTok{p punif(3, 0, 10)   \# 0.3}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{pushd}}{pushd}}\label{pushd}

\texttt{pushd(DIR)} --- save current cwd onto a process-wide directory
stack, then \texttt{chdir(DIR)}. Returns the new cwd. Bare
\texttt{pushd()} with no argument swaps the top of the stack with cwd
(zsh convention). Also updates \texttt{stryke\_pwd} so relative-path
builtins (\texttt{glob}, \texttt{slurp}, \texttt{slurp\_self}, \ldots)
follow the new directory.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{pushd("/tmp")                       \# save cwd, chdir /tmp}
\NormalTok{\# … work in /tmp …}
\NormalTok{popd()                              \# restore previous cwd}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{qcauchy}}{qcauchy}}\label{qcauchy}

\texttt{qcauchy} --- Cauchy quantile. Args: p {[}, location, scale{]}.

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{qchisq}}{qchisq}}\label{qchisq}

\texttt{qchisq} --- chi-squared quantile. Args: p, df.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p qchisq(0.95, 1)  \# 3.84 (critical value)}
\NormalTok{p qchisq(0.95, 5)  \# 11.07}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{qdisc}}{qdisc}}\label{qdisc}

\texttt{quadratic\_discriminant(\$x,\ \$y,\ \$c)} (alias \texttt{qdisc})
--- computes b² - 4ac. Positive = two real roots, zero = one repeated
root, negative = complex roots.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p qdisc(1, {-}5, 6)   \# 1 (two distinct real roots)}
\NormalTok{p qdisc(1, 2, 1)    \# 0 (one repeated root)}
\NormalTok{p qdisc(1, 2, 5)    \# {-}16 (complex roots)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{qexp}}{qexp}}\label{qexp}

\texttt{qexp} --- exponential quantile. Args: p {[}, rate{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p qexp(0.5, 1)       \# 0.693 (median of Exp(1))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{qf}}{qf}}\label{qf}

\texttt{qf} --- F-distribution quantile. Args: p, d1, d2.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{qnorm}}{qnorm}}\label{qnorm}

\texttt{qnorm} --- normal quantile (inverse CDF). Returns x such that
P(X ≤ x) = p. Args: p {[}, mu, sigma{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p qnorm(0.975)      \# 1.96}
\NormalTok{p qnorm(0.5)        \# 0.0}
\NormalTok{p qnorm(0.95, 100, 15)  \# 90th percentile IQ}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{qntl}}{qntl}}\label{qntl}

\texttt{qntl} --- operation \texttt{qntl}. Alias for \texttt{quantile}.

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p qntl $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{qntls}}{qntls}}\label{qntls}

\texttt{quantiles\ \textbackslash{}@DATA,\ \textbackslash{}@PROBS} ---
batch quantile lookup. Returns one element per probability in
\texttt{\textbackslash{}@PROBS}, computed via the same linear
interpolation as \texttt{quantile}. Sorts the data exactly once across
all probability points so callers don\textquotesingle t pay an O(n log
n) sort per quantile. Probabilities outside {[}0,1{]} are clamped.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @d = (1..1000)}
\NormalTok{my @qs = quantiles(\textbackslash{}@d, [0.25, 0.5, 0.75])}
\NormalTok{printf "q25=\%.2f q50=\%.2f q75=\%.2f\textbackslash{}n", @qs}
\NormalTok{\# q25=250.75 q50=500.50 q75=750.25}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{qpe\_iteration} --- operation \texttt{qpe\ iteration}. Category:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p qpe\_iteration $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{qroots}}{qroots}}\label{qroots}

\texttt{quadratic\_roots(\$x,\ \$y,\ \$c)} (alias \texttt{qroots}) ---
solves ax² + bx + c = 0. Returns {[}x1, x2{]} for real roots, undef if
no real solutions (discriminant \textless{} 0).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @roots = quadratic\_roots(1, {-}5, 6)  \# [3, 2] (x² {-} 5x + 6 = 0)}
\NormalTok{p qroots(1, 0, {-}4)                      \# [2, {-}2]}
\NormalTok{p qroots(1, 2, 5)                       \# undef (complex roots)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{quartiles}}{quartiles}}\label{quartiles}

\texttt{quartiles(@data)} --- returns the three quartile values {[}Q1,
Q2, Q3{]} of a dataset. Q1 is the 25th percentile, Q2 is the median
(50th), Q3 is the 75th percentile. Useful for understanding data
distribution and computing IQR.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @data = 1:100}
\NormalTok{my $q = quartiles(@data)}
\NormalTok{p "Q1=$q{-}\textgreater{}[0] Q2=$q{-}\textgreater{}[1] Q3=$q{-}\textgreater{}[2]"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{quarts} --- operation \texttt{quarts}. Alias for
\texttt{quartiles}. Category: *statistics (extended)*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p quarts $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{quat\_conjugate}}{quat\_conjugate}}\label{quat_conjugate}

\texttt{quat\_conjugate(q)\ →\ {[}w,-x,-y,-z{]}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{quat\_dot}}{quat\_dot}}\label{quat_dot}

\texttt{quat\_dot(a,\ b)\ →\ number} --- 4-component dot product.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{quat\_from\_euler}}{quat\_from\_euler}}\label{quat_from_euler}

\texttt{quat\_from\_euler(roll,\ pitch,\ yaw)\ →\ {[}w,x,y,z{]}} ---
quaternion from XYZ Euler angles (radians).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p quat\_from\_euler(0, 0, 3.14159/2)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{quat\_identity}}{quat\_identity}}\label{quat_identity}

\texttt{quat\_identity()\ →\ {[}w,x,y,z{]}} --- identity quaternion
\texttt{{[}1,0,0,0{]}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{quat\_inverse}}{quat\_inverse}}\label{quat_inverse}

\texttt{quat\_inverse(q)\ →\ {[}w,x,y,z{]}} ---
\texttt{conjugate(q)\ /\ \textbar{}q\textbar{}²}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{quat\_multiply}}{quat\_multiply}}\label{quat_multiply}

\texttt{quat\_multiply(a,\ b)\ →\ {[}w,x,y,z{]}} --- Hamilton product
\texttt{a\ ⊗\ b}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{quat\_normalize}}{quat\_normalize}}\label{quat_normalize}

\texttt{quat\_normalize(q)\ →\ {[}w,x,y,z{]}} --- divide by magnitude.
Required after iterative composition.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{quat\_to\_euler}}{quat\_to\_euler}}\label{quat_to_euler}

\texttt{quat\_to\_euler(q)\ →\ {[}roll,\ pitch,\ yaw{]}} --- inverse of
\texttt{quat\_from\_euler}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{quat\_to\_mat4}}{quat\_to\_mat4}}\label{quat_to_mat4}

\texttt{quat\_to\_mat4(q)\ →\ matrix} --- convert quaternion to 4×4
rotation matrix.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{r72}}{r72}}\label{r72}

\texttt{rule\_of\_72(\$rate)} (alias \texttt{r72}) --- estimates years
to double investment using the Rule of 72: 72 / (rate × 100). Quick
mental math approximation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p r72(0.06)   \# 12 years to double at 6\%}
\NormalTok{p r72(0.10)   \# 7.2 years at 10\%}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

count newly inserted.

\begin{Highlighting}[]

\end{Highlighting}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rbeta}}{rbeta}}\label{rbeta}

\texttt{rbeta} --- generate n random beta variates. Args: n, alpha,
beta. Like R\textquotesingle s rbeta().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @x = @\{rbeta(100, 2, 5)\}     \# Beta(2,5)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rbind}}{rbind}}\label{rbind}

\texttt{rbind} --- bind matrices/vectors by rows (vertical stack). Like
R\textquotesingle s rbind().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $m = rbind([1,2,3], [4,5,6])  \# [[1,2,3],[4,5,6]]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rbinom}}{rbinom}}\label{rbinom}

\texttt{rbinom} --- generate n random binomial variates. Args: n, size,
prob. Like R\textquotesingle s rbinom().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @x = @\{rbinom(100, 10, 0.3)\} \# 100 draws from Binom(10,0.3)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rbm}}{rbm}}\label{rbm}

\texttt{roaring(LIST\ \textbar{}\ \textbackslash{}@ARR\ \textbar{}\ ...)}
--- Roaring Bitmap (compressed \texttt{u32} set). Apache-licensed
\texttt{roaring} crate; the canonical impl used by
quickwit/tantivy/lucene-rs. O(1) \texttt{add}/\texttt{contains},
O(n/run-length) set ops; typically 10-100× smaller than
\texttt{HashSet\textless{}u32\textgreater{}} on natural datasets.
\texttt{rb} short alias reserved for \texttt{read\_bytes}; use
\texttt{rbm}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $a = roaring(1, 2, 3, 5, 8)}
\NormalTok{my $b = roaring([2, 4, 6, 8, 10])}
\NormalTok{rb\_and($a, $b)                        \# in{-}place intersect}
\NormalTok{p join(",", rb\_to\_array($a))         \# 2,8}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Family: \texttt{rb\_add}, \texttt{rb\_remove}, \texttt{rb\_contains},
\texttt{rb\_or} (alias \texttt{rb\_union}), \texttt{rb\_and}
(\texttt{rb\_intersect}), \texttt{rb\_xor} (\texttt{rb\_symdiff}),
\texttt{rb\_andnot} (\texttt{rb\_diff}, \texttt{rb\_minus}),
\texttt{rb\_len}, \texttt{rb\_min}, \texttt{rb\_max}, \texttt{rb\_rank},
\texttt{rb\_to\_array}, \texttt{rb\_clear}, \texttt{rb\_serialize},
\texttt{rb\_deserialize}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{reflect\_point}}{reflect\_point}}\label{reflect_point}

\texttt{reflect\_point(\$x,\ \$y,\ \$axis)} --- reflects a point across
an axis. Axis can be \textquotesingle x\textquotesingle,
\textquotesingle y\textquotesingle, or
\textquotesingle origin\textquotesingle. Returns the reflected
coordinates.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p reflect\_point(3, 4, \textquotesingle{}x\textquotesingle{})       \# [3, {-}4]}
\NormalTok{p reflect\_point(3, 4, \textquotesingle{}y\textquotesingle{})       \# [{-}3, 4]}
\NormalTok{p reflect\_point(3, 4, \textquotesingle{}origin\textquotesingle{})  \# [{-}3, {-}4]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{repl\_alias}}{repl\_alias}}\label{repl_alias}

\texttt{repl\_alias(NAME,\ EXPANSION)} /
\texttt{repl\_alias("NAME=EXPANSION")} --- register a REPL alias. With
one arg as a query (\texttt{repl\_alias("ll")}), returns the expansion
or \texttt{undef}. With no args, returns the full alias table as
\texttt{name=expansion} strings. The REPL prompt rewriter consults this
table before each parse.

*Renamed from bare \texttt{alias} so Perl typeglob assignment
(\texttt{*alias\ =\ \textbackslash{}\&sub}) keeps working.*

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{repl\_alias("ll", "ls {-}la")}
\NormalTok{repl\_alias("g=git")                 \# equals{-}form}
\NormalTok{p repl\_alias("ll")                  \# ls {-}la}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{repl\_unalias}}{repl\_unalias}}\label{repl_unalias}

\texttt{repl\_unalias(NAME)} --- drop one REPL alias. Returns \texttt{1}
if removed, \texttt{0} if not present.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{resample}}{resample}}\label{resample}

\texttt{resample(\textbackslash{}@signal,\ \$factor)} --- resamples
signal by a rational factor. Factor \textgreater{} 1 upsamples, factor
\textless{} 1 downsamples. Uses linear interpolation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $up = resample(\textbackslash{}@signal, 2)     \# double sample rate}
\NormalTok{my $down = resample(\textbackslash{}@signal, 0.5) \# halve sample rate}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{reverse\_lines}}{reverse\_lines}}\label{reverse_lines}

\texttt{tac(\textbackslash{}@LIST)} → reverse the order of a list.
Mirrors POSIX \texttt{tac}. Composes with \texttt{cat} / pipes for
traditional shell idioms.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @rev = tac([sort @items])}
\NormalTok{my @recent\_first = tac([read\_lines("history.log")])}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rgb\_blend\_color\_burn}}{rgb\_blend\_color\_burn}}\label{rgb_blend_color_burn}

\texttt{rgb\_blend\_color\_burn(a,\ b)\ →\ {[}r,g,b{]}} --- color burn:
\texttt{255\ -\ (255-a)·255/b}, clamped.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rgb\_blend\_color\_dodge}}{rgb\_blend\_color\_dodge}}\label{rgb_blend_color_dodge}

\texttt{rgb\_blend\_color\_dodge(a,\ b)\ →\ {[}r,g,b{]}} --- color
dodge: \texttt{a\ ·\ 255\ /\ (255\ -\ b)}, clamped.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rgb\_blend\_darken}}{rgb\_blend\_darken}}\label{rgb_blend_darken}

\texttt{rgb\_blend\_darken(a,\ b)\ →\ {[}r,g,b{]}} --- channelwise min.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rgb\_blend\_lighten}}{rgb\_blend\_lighten}}\label{rgb_blend_lighten}

\texttt{rgb\_blend\_lighten(a,\ b)\ →\ {[}r,g,b{]}} --- channelwise max.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rgb\_blend\_multiply}}{rgb\_blend\_multiply}}\label{rgb_blend_multiply}

\texttt{rgb\_blend\_multiply({[}r,g,b{]},\ {[}r,g,b{]})\ →\ {[}r,g,b{]}}
--- Photoshop-style multiply: \texttt{a\ ·\ b\ /\ 255}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p rgb\_blend\_multiply([255,128,0], [128,255,0])}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rgb\_blend\_normal}}{rgb\_blend\_normal}}\label{rgb_blend_normal}

\texttt{rgb\_blend\_normal({[}r,g,b{]},\ {[}r,g,b{]},\ alpha=1)\ →\ {[}r,g,b{]}}
--- alpha-blended overlay of two RGB triples (0-255).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p rgb\_blend\_normal([255,0,0], [0,255,0], 0.5)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rgb\_blend\_overlay}}{rgb\_blend\_overlay}}\label{rgb_blend_overlay}

\texttt{rgb\_blend\_overlay(a,\ b)\ →\ {[}r,g,b{]}} --- overlay =
multiply for dark base, screen for light.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rgb\_blend\_screen}}{rgb\_blend\_screen}}\label{rgb_blend_screen}

\texttt{rgb\_blend\_screen(a,\ b)\ →\ {[}r,g,b{]}} --- Photoshop screen:
\texttt{255\ -\ (255-a)·(255-b)/255}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rk4}}{rk4}}\label{rk4}

\texttt{rk4} (aliases \texttt{runge\_kutta}, \texttt{rk4\_ode}) solves
an ODE dy/dt = f(t,y) using 4th-order Runge-Kutta. Returns {[}{[}t,y{]},
...{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# dy/dt = {-}y, y(0) = 1 → y = e\^{}({-}t)}
\NormalTok{my $sol = rk4(fn \{ {-}$\_[1] \}, 0, 1, 0.1, 100)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rl\_discount\_returns}}{rl\_discount\_returns}}\label{rl_discount_returns}

\texttt{rl\_discount\_returns(rewards,\ gamma=0.9)\ →\ array} ---
backward-cumulative discounted returns
\texttt{G\_t\ =\ r\_t\ +\ γ·G\_\{t+1\}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rl\_n\_step\_return}}{rl\_n\_step\_return}}\label{rl_n_step_return}

\texttt{rl\_n\_step\_return(rewards,\ gamma=0.9)\ →\ number} ---
\texttt{Σ\ γ\^{}k\ ·\ r\_k}. Bootstrap-free n-step return.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rm}}{rm}}\label{rm}

\texttt{rm\ PATH...} --- remove one or more files. Returns the count
actually removed. Silently skips paths that don\textquotesingle t exist
(Perl \texttt{unlink} semantics applied to the shell-name).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{rm("/tmp/foo")}
\NormalTok{rm(@stale\_files)                    \# bulk}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

excess kurtosis.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rolling\_max}}{rolling\_max}}\label{rolling_max}

\texttt{rolling\_max(series,\ period=10)\ →\ array} --- sliding maximum.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rolling\_mean}}{rolling\_mean}}\label{rolling_mean}

\texttt{rolling\_mean(series,\ period=10)\ →\ array} --- sliding mean.
Output length is \texttt{len\ -\ period\ +\ 1}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p rolling\_mean([1,2,3,4,5], 3)  \# [2.0, 3.0, 4.0]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rolling\_median}}{rolling\_median}}\label{rolling_median}

\texttt{rolling\_median(series,\ period=10)\ →\ array} --- sliding
median; robust to outliers.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p rolling\_median(\textbackslash{}@xs, 5)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rolling\_min}}{rolling\_min}}\label{rolling_min}

\texttt{rolling\_min(series,\ period=10)\ →\ array} --- sliding minimum.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rolling\_skew}}{rolling\_skew}}\label{rolling_skew}

\texttt{rolling\_skew(series,\ period=10)\ →\ array} --- sliding
skewness (third central moment / σ³).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rolling\_std}}{rolling\_std}}\label{rolling_std}

\texttt{rolling\_std(series,\ period=10)\ →\ array} --- sliding
(population) standard deviation.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rolling\_sum}}{rolling\_sum}}\label{rolling_sum}

\texttt{rolling\_sum(series,\ period=10)\ →\ array} --- sliding sum.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p rolling\_sum([1,2,3,4,5], 3)  \# [6, 9, 12]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rolling\_var}}{rolling\_var}}\label{rolling_var}

\texttt{rolling\_var(series,\ period=10)\ →\ array} --- sliding
(population) variance.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rope}}{rope}}\label{rope}

\texttt{rope(INITIAL\_STRING="")} --- text data structure for fast
insert/delete in long strings. Chunk-balanced (1 KB max per chunk);
amortized O(log n) edits. Codepoint-safe across UTF-8 boundaries.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $r = rope("Hello, world!")}
\NormalTok{rope\_insert($r, 7, "beautiful ")}
\NormalTok{p rope\_to\_string($r)                  \# "Hello, beautiful world!"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rope\_delete}}{rope\_delete}}\label{rope_delete}

\texttt{rope\_delete(R,\ START,\ END)} --- delete codepoints
\texttt{{[}START,\ END)}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rope\_insert}}{rope\_insert}}\label{rope_insert}

\texttt{rope\_insert(R,\ POS,\ TEXT)} --- insert \texttt{TEXT} at
codepoint position \texttt{POS} (clamped to \texttt{{[}0,\ len{]}}).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rope\_len}}{rope\_len}}\label{rope_len}

\texttt{rope\_len(R)} → codepoint count.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rope\_str}}{rope\_str}}\label{rope_str}

\texttt{rope\_to\_string(R)} → materialize as a contiguous String.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rope\_substr}}{rope\_substr}}\label{rope_substr}

\texttt{rope\_substring(R,\ START,\ END)} → substring at codepoint range
\texttt{{[}START,\ END)}.

\texttt{row\_means} (alias \texttt{rowMeans}) --- mean of each row. Like
R\textquotesingle s rowMeans().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p rowMeans([[2,4],[6,8]])  \# [3, 7]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rowSums}}{rowSums}}\label{rowsums}

\texttt{row\_sums} (alias \texttt{rowSums}) --- sum of each row in a
matrix. Like R\textquotesingle s rowSums().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p row\_sums([[1,2,3],[4,5,6]])  \# [6, 15]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rsa\_decrypt\_pkcs1}}{rsa\_decrypt\_pkcs1}}\label{rsa_decrypt_pkcs1}

\texttt{rsa\_decrypt\_pkcs1} decrypts RSA-PKCS1v15 ciphertext.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $plain = rsa\_decrypt\_pkcs1($priv, $cipher)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rsa\_encrypt\_pkcs1}}{rsa\_encrypt\_pkcs1}}\label{rsa_encrypt_pkcs1}

\texttt{rsa\_encrypt\_pkcs1} encrypts with legacy RSA-PKCS1v15 padding.
Only for compatibility with old systems --- prefer OAEP.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $cipher = rsa\_encrypt\_pkcs1($pub, "data")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rsa\_encrypt\_simple}}{rsa\_encrypt\_simple}}\label{rsa_encrypt_simple}

\texttt{rsa\_encrypt\_simple} --- RSA cryptographic primitive
\texttt{encrypt\ simple}. Category: *cryptography deep*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p rsa\_encrypt\_simple $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rsa\_keypair\_simple}}{rsa\_keypair\_simple}}\label{rsa_keypair_simple}

\texttt{rsa\_keypair\_simple(p,\ q,\ e=17)\ →\ \{n,\ e,\ d\}} ---
textbook RSA from two primes. NOT cryptographically secure.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p rsa\_keypair\_simple(61, 53)  \# \{n:3233, e:17, d:2753\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rsa\_modular\_exp}}{rsa\_modular\_exp}}\label{rsa_modular_exp}

\texttt{rsa\_modular\_exp(base,\ exp,\ modulus)\ →\ string} ---
\texttt{base\^{}exp\ mod\ modulus} on arbitrary-precision BigUInts
(decimal strings).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p rsa\_modular\_exp("2", "10", "100")  \# "24"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rsamp}}{rsamp}}\label{rsamp}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{rsi}}{rsi}}\label{rsi}

\texttt{rsi(prices,\ period=14)\ →\ array} --- Relative Strength Index
(Wilder). Computes
\texttt{100\ −\ 100\ /\ (1\ +\ avg\_gain\ /\ avg\_loss)} over a moving
window; values \textgreater70 commonly mark overbought, \textless30
oversold.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p rsi(\textbackslash{}@closes, 14)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

+ b5 \textquotesingle blue note\textquotesingle.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{scale\_chromatic}}{scale\_chromatic}}\label{scale_chromatic}

\texttt{scale\_chromatic(root)\ →\ array} --- all 12 semitones from
root.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{scale\_dorian}}{scale\_dorian}}\label{scale_dorian}

\texttt{scale\_dorian(root)\ →\ array} --- Dorian mode (W-H-W-W-W-H-W).

\texttt{scale\_each} --- music-theory scale constructor \texttt{each}.

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{scale\_minor(root)\ →\ array} --- natural minor (W-H-W-W-H-W-W).

\texttt{scale\_mixolydian(root)\ →\ array} --- Mixolydian mode
(W-W-H-W-W-H-W).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{scale\_point}}{scale\_point}}\label{scale_point}

\texttt{scale\_point(\$x,\ \$y,\ \$sx,\ \$sy)} --- scales a 2D point by
factors sx and sy from the origin. Returns {[}\$x*\$sx, \$y*\$sy{]}. Use
for geometric transformations.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $p = scale\_point(2, 3, 2, 2)}
\NormalTok{p @$p   \# (4, 6)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{scentroid}}{scentroid}}\label{scentroid}

\texttt{spectral\_centroid(\textbackslash{}@spectrum)} (alias
\texttt{scentroid}) --- computes the spectral centroid (center of mass)
of a spectrum. Indicates the
\textquotesingle brightness\textquotesingle{} of a sound. Returns
frequency bin index.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $psd = psd(\textbackslash{}@signal)}
\NormalTok{p scentroid($psd)   \# brightness measure}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{scharr\_x\_kernel()\ →\ matrix} --- Scharr horizontal kernel;

\texttt{scharr\_y\_kernel()\ →\ matrix} --- Scharr vertical kernel.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{selu}}{selu}}\label{selu}

\texttt{selu} applies the Scaled ELU with fixed lambda=1.0507,
alpha=1.6733 for self-normalizing networks.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p selu(1)    \# 1.0507}
\NormalTok{p selu({-}1)   \# {-}1.1113}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sem}}{sem}}\label{sem}

\texttt{semaphore(N)\ →\ semaphore} (alias \texttt{sem}) --- counting
semaphore with \texttt{N} permits (must be \texttt{\textgreater{}=\ 0}).
Use to bound concurrent access: \texttt{semaphore(K)} lets at most
\texttt{K} threads pass \texttt{semaphore\_acquire} at once.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{mysync $s = semaphore(3)   \# at most 3 concurrent workers}
\NormalTok{my @t}
\NormalTok{for (1:10) \{}
\NormalTok{    push @t, async \{}
\NormalTok{        semaphore\_acquire($s)}
\NormalTok{        defer \{ semaphore\_release($s) \}}
\NormalTok{        do\_work()   \# at most 3 in flight at any moment}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{await($\_) for @t}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sem\_acquire}}{sem\_acquire}}\label{sem_acquire}

\texttt{semaphore\_acquire(\$s)\ →\ undef} (alias \texttt{sem\_acquire})
--- block until a permit is available, then take one. Pair with
\texttt{defer\ \{\ semaphore\_release(\$s)\ \}}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{semaphore\_acquire($s)}
\NormalTok{defer \{ semaphore\_release($s) \}}
\NormalTok{\# bounded critical section}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sem\_limit}}{sem\_limit}}\label{sem_limit}

\texttt{semaphore\_limit(\$s)\ →\ integer} (alias \texttt{sem\_limit})
--- initial permit count from the original \texttt{semaphore(N)}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $s = semaphore(8)}
\NormalTok{p semaphore\_limit($s)     \# 8 (constant for life of $s)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sem\_permits}}{sem\_permits}}\label{sem_permits}

\texttt{semaphore\_permits(\$s)\ →\ integer} (alias
\texttt{sem\_permits}) --- currently available permits (race-free
snapshot).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $s = semaphore(5)}
\NormalTok{p semaphore\_permits($s)   \# 5}
\NormalTok{semaphore\_acquire($s)}
\NormalTok{p semaphore\_permits($s)   \# 4}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sem\_release}}{sem\_release}}\label{sem_release}

\texttt{semaphore\_release(\$s)\ →\ undef} (alias \texttt{sem\_release})
--- release one permit and wake one waiter.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{semaphore\_acquire($s)}
\NormalTok{defer \{ semaphore\_release($s) \}}
\NormalTok{work()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sem\_try\_acquire}}{sem\_try\_acquire}}\label{sem_try_acquire}

\texttt{semaphore\_try\_acquire(\$s)\ →\ 1\textbar{}0} (alias
\texttt{sem\_try\_acquire}) --- non-blocking. Returns \texttt{1} if a
permit was taken, \texttt{0} if none available.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{if (semaphore\_try\_acquire($s)) \{}
\NormalTok{    defer \{ semaphore\_release($s) \}}
\NormalTok{    burst\_work()}
\NormalTok{\} else \{}
\NormalTok{    queue\_for\_later()}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{set\_term\_title}}{set\_term\_title}}\label{set_term_title}

\texttt{set\_title(TITLE)} --- emit the ANSI OSC sequence
\texttt{\textbackslash{}x1b{]}2;TITLE\textbackslash{}x07} (sets the
terminal window title in most modern terminals: macOS Terminal, iTerm2,
Alacritty, kitty, GNOME Terminal, Windows Terminal). Uses
\texttt{OSC\ 2} rather than \texttt{OSC\ 0} so the icon is left
untouched. Returns \texttt{1}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{shapiro}}{shapiro}}\label{shapiro}

\texttt{shapiro\_test} (alias \texttt{shapiro}) --- Shapiro-Wilk
normality test. Returns W statistic (close to 1 = normal). Like
R\textquotesingle s shapiro.test().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p shapiro([rnorm(100)])  \# W ≈ 0.99 for normal data}
\NormalTok{p shapiro([1,1,1,2,10]) \# W \textless{} 0.9 for non{-}normal}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sharpe}}{sharpe}}\label{sharpe}

\texttt{sharpe\_ratio(\textbackslash{}@returns,\ \$risk\_free\_rate)}
(alias \texttt{sharpe}) --- computes the Sharpe ratio measuring
risk-adjusted returns. Higher is better. Returns (mean\_return - rf) /
stddev.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @returns = (0.05, {-}0.02, 0.08, 0.03)}
\NormalTok{p sharpe(\textbackslash{}@returns, 0.02)   \# risk{-}adjusted performance}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

windowing.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p si(3.14159)  \# \textasciitilde{}1.852}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{silu}}{silu}}\label{silu}

\texttt{silu} (alias \texttt{swish}) applies SiLU/Swish: x·sigmoid(x).
Smooth approximation to ReLU.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p silu(1)    \# 0.7311}
\NormalTok{p swish({-}2)  \# {-}0.2384}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{silver}}{silver}}\label{silver}

\texttt{silver\_ratio} (alias \texttt{silver}) --- δS = 1 + √2 ≈ 2.414.
Related to Pell numbers.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p silver   \# 2.414213562373095}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sim}}{sim}}\label{sim}

Category: *extended stdlib*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p sim $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{simhash}}{simhash}}\label{simhash}

\texttt{simhash()} --- Charikar 64-bit SimHash sketch. Each feature
flips +/- on 64 signed accumulators; final \texttt{digest()} is the
per-bit sign. Cosine similarity ≈
\texttt{(64\ -\ hamming(a,\ b))\ /\ 64}. Stateful upgrade to the deleted
\texttt{db\_simhash\_bit} spec primitive.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $sh = simhash()}
\NormalTok{sh\_add($sh, $\_) for @doc\_tokens}
\NormalTok{my $hash = sh\_digest($sh)}
\NormalTok{p sh\_similarity($a, $b)               \# 0..1}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{simps}}{simps}}\label{simps}

\texttt{simpson} (alias \texttt{simps}) integrates evenly-spaced samples
using Simpson\textquotesingle s rule. More accurate than trapz for
smooth functions.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @y = map \{ sin(\_ * 0.01) \} 0:314}
\NormalTok{p simps(\textbackslash{}@y, 0.01)  \# ≈ 2.0}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{simpson\_integrate}}{simpson\_integrate}}\label{simpson_integrate}

\texttt{simpson\_integrate(ys,\ dx=1)\ →\ number} --- composite
Simpson\textquotesingle s rule. Uses Simpson\textquotesingle s 1/3 over
even intervals; falls back to Simpson\textquotesingle s 3/8 on the
trailing 3 intervals when \texttt{n-1} is odd, so the rule is exact for
any \texttt{n\ ≥\ 3}. Falls back to trapezoidal for
\texttt{n\ \textless{}\ 3}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p simpson\_integrate([0,1,4,9,16,25,36,49,64,81,100], 1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{simpson\_rule}}{simpson\_rule}}\label{simpson_rule}

\NormalTok{p simpson\_rule $x}
\end{Highlighting}

\texttt{siphash\_keyed} computes SipHash-2-4 with a custom 128-bit key
(two 64-bit integers). The key should be random per application to
prevent collision attacks.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p siphash\_keyed("data", 0x123456789, 0xabcdef012)  \# keyed hash}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{size}}{size}}\label{size}

\texttt{size} returns the byte size of a file on disk --- equivalent to
Perl\textquotesingle s \texttt{-s\ FILE} file test. With no arguments,
it operates on \texttt{\_}; with one argument, it stats the given path;
with multiple arguments (or a flattened list), it returns an array of
sizes. Paths that can\textquotesingle t be stat\textquotesingle d return
\texttt{undef}. This is a stryke extension that makes pipelines over
filenames concise.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p size "Cargo.toml"                   \# 2013}
\NormalTok{f |\textgreater{} map +\{ \_ =\textgreater{} size \} |\textgreater{} tj |\textgreater{} p   \# [\{name =\textgreater{} bytes\}, ...]}
\NormalTok{f |\textgreater{} filter \{ size \textgreater{} 1024 \} |\textgreater{} e p    \# files larger than 1 KiB}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{skip\_while}}{skip\_while}}\label{skip_while}

\texttt{skip\_while\ \{\ COND\ \}\ LIST} --- skip leading elements while
the predicate is true (alias for \texttt{drop\_while}).

Behavior is identical to \texttt{drop\_while}: once the predicate
returns false, that element and all subsequent elements are emitted. The
\texttt{skip\_while} name is provided for users coming from Rust or
Kotlin where this is the conventional name. Both compile to the same
streaming operation internally.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{1:10 |\textgreater{} skip\_while \{ \_ \textless{} 5 \} |\textgreater{} e p \# 5 6 7 8 9 10}
\NormalTok{@sorted |\textgreater{} skip\_while \{ \_ le "m" \} |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sma}}{sma}}\label{sma}

\texttt{sma(prices,\ period=10)\ →\ array} --- simple moving average
over a price series. Output length is
\texttt{len(prices)\ -\ period\ +\ 1}; each value is the arithmetic mean
of the trailing \texttt{period} window.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p sma([10,11,12,13,14], 3)  \# [11.0, 12.0, 13.0]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{smoothstep}}{smoothstep}}\label{smoothstep}

\texttt{smoothstep(\$edge0,\ \$edge1,\ \$x)} --- smooth Hermite
interpolation. Returns 0 if x ≤ edge0, 1 if x ≥ edge1, otherwise smooth
S-curve. Great for animations.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p smoothstep(0, 1, 0.5)   \# 0.5 (but with smooth acceleration/deceleration)}
\NormalTok{p smoothstep(10, 20, 15)  \# 0.5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{snell}}{snell}}\label{snell}

\texttt{snells\_law(\$n1,\ \$n2,\ \$theta1)} (alias \texttt{snell}) ---
refraction angle using Snell\textquotesingle s law. Angles in degrees.
Returns undef for total internal reflection.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p snell(1, 1.5, 30)     \# \textasciitilde{}19.5° (air to glass)}
\NormalTok{p snell(1.5, 1, 45)     \# undef (total internal reflection)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

sample from softmax over logits.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{softplus}}{softplus}}\label{softplus}

\texttt{softplus} applies the Softplus activation: ln(1 + e\^{}x).
Smooth approximation to ReLU.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p softplus(0)   \# 0.6931 (ln 2)}
\NormalTok{p softplus(10)  \# ≈ 10}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sortino}}{sortino}}\label{sortino}

\texttt{sortino\_ratio(\textbackslash{}@returns,\ \$target)} (alias
\texttt{sortino}) --- computes the Sortino ratio, similar to Sharpe but
only penalizes downside volatility. Better for asymmetric return
distributions.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @returns = (0.05, {-}0.02, 0.08, {-}0.01)}
\NormalTok{p sortino(\textbackslash{}@returns, 0.0)   \# penalizes only negative returns}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{soundex\_v2}}{soundex\_v2}}\label{soundex_v2}

\texttt{soundex\_v2(name)\ →\ string} --- Apache Refined Soundex (longer
code, no length cap, 9 finer-grained groups: BP, FV, CKS, GJ, QXZ, DT,
L, MN, R).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p soundex\_v2("Robert")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{source}}{source}}\label{source}

\texttt{source(PATH)} → last expression value. Read a stryke script from
\texttt{PATH} and execute it in the *current* interpreter. Variables
(\texttt{our}/\texttt{my}), subroutines, classes, enums, and pragmas
defined in the file become visible in the calling scope --- same
semantics as \texttt{do\ "file"} (Perl 5) or \texttt{source\ FILE}
(POSIX shell).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{source("config.stk")}
\NormalTok{p $LIB\_VERSION                  \# leaked from config.stk}
\NormalTok{Demo::Lib::greet("alice")       \# leaked fn defined in config.stk}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Designed for REPL workflows: \texttt{source} a working environment
without forking. Returns \texttt{undef} if the file
can\textquotesingle t be parsed, dies on file-read failure. For isolated
execution (no state-leak), use \texttt{run} instead.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{spearman}}{spearman}}\label{spearman}

\texttt{spearman\_correlation(\textbackslash{}@x,\ \textbackslash{}@y)}
(alias \texttt{spearman}) --- computes Spearman\textquotesingle s rank
correlation coefficient between two datasets. Measures monotonic
relationships, robust to outliers. Returns a value from -1 to 1.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @x = (1, 2, 3, 4, 5)}
\NormalTok{my @y = (5, 6, 7, 8, 7)}
\NormalTok{p spearman(\textbackslash{}@x, \textbackslash{}@y)   \# high positive correlation}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{spectrogram}}{spectrogram}}\label{spectrogram}

\texttt{spectrogram(\textbackslash{}@signal,\ \$window\_size,\ \$hop\_size)}
(alias \texttt{stft}) --- computes Short-Time Fourier Transform. Returns
a 2D arrayref where each row is the spectrum of a windowed segment.
Useful for time-frequency analysis.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $spec = stft(\textbackslash{}@audio, 1024, 512)}
\NormalTok{\# $spec{-}\textgreater{}[t][f] is magnitude at time t, frequency f}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{stirling2}}{stirling2}}\label{stirling2}

\texttt{stirling2} (alias \texttt{stirling\_second}) computes the
Stirling number of the second kind S(n,k) --- the number of ways to
partition n elements into k non-empty subsets.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p stirling2(4, 2)   \# 7}
\NormalTok{p stirling2(5, 3)   \# 25}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sub}}{sub}}\label{sub}

\textbf{\texttt{sub} is a Perl 5 idiom that stryke rejects under
\texttt{-\/-no-interop}.} Use \texttt{fn} instead --- it opens the
stryke topic-variable protocol (\texttt{\_}, \texttt{\_0}, \texttt{\_1},
\texttt{\_\textless{}}) and supports expression-bodied one-liners
(\texttt{fn\ name\ =\ expr}).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{\# WRONG (legacy Perl 5)}
\NormalTok{sub double \{ my $x = shift; $x * 2 \}}
\NormalTok{sub area($w, $h) \{ return $w * $h \}}

\NormalTok{\# RIGHT (stryke)}
\NormalTok{fn double = \_ * 2                         \# expression{-}bodied}
\NormalTok{fn area($w, $h) = $w * $h                 \# named params, one{-}liner}
\NormalTok{fn shout \{ my $s = \_; uc($s) . "!" \}   \# block body, multi{-}statement}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

In \texttt{-\/-compat} mode \texttt{sub} still parses for Perl-5 ports,
but \textbf{all new stryke code should use \texttt{fn}}.

See also: \texttt{fn}, \texttt{\_}, \texttt{\_0}, \texttt{\_1},
style-guide §0 rule 1.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sun\_rise\_unix}}{sun\_rise\_unix}}\label{sun_rise_unix}

\texttt{sun\_rise\_unix(lat,\ lon,\ unix\_today)\ →\ int} --- sunrise
unix timestamp at given coords for that date. Uses solar declination
from day-of-year, hour angle from \texttt{acos(−tan(φ)·tan(δ))}, and
equation-of-time correction. Returns UNDEF for polar day/night
(\textbar−tan(φ)·tan(δ)\textbar{} \textgreater{} 1).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p sun\_rise\_unix(37.7749, {-}122.4194, time())}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sun\_set\_unix}}{sun\_set\_unix}}\label{sun_set_unix}

\texttt{sun\_set\_unix(lat,\ lon,\ unix\_today)\ →\ int} --- sunset unix
timestamp at given coords for that date. Same model as
\texttt{sun\_rise\_unix} with EoT correction; returns UNDEF for polar
day/night.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p sun\_set\_unix(37.7749, {-}122.4194, time())}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(swap\_free)}
\NormalTok{my $pct = swap\_total \textgreater{} 0 ? int(swap\_free / swap\_total * 100) : 100}
\NormalTok{p "$pct\% swap free"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{swap\_total}}{swap\_total}}\label{swap_total}

\texttt{swap\_total} --- total swap space in bytes. Linux:
\texttt{/proc/meminfo\ SwapTotal}. macOS: \texttt{vm.swapusage} sysctl.
Returns 0 if swap is disabled, \texttt{undef} on unsupported platforms.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p swap\_total                             \# 8589934592}
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(swap\_total)               \# 8.0 GiB}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{swap\_used}}{swap\_used}}\label{swap_used}

\texttt{swap\_used} --- used swap space in bytes (total - free).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p format\_bytes(swap\_used)}
\NormalTok{p "swap pressure" if swap\_used \textgreater{} swap\_total * 0.8}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{sys\_uptime}}{sys\_uptime}}\label{sys_uptime}

\texttt{sys\_uptime} --- system uptime in seconds (float). On Linux
reads \texttt{/proc/uptime}. On macOS uses \texttt{kern.boottime}
sysctl. This is the wall-clock uptime of the machine, not the stryke
process (see \texttt{uptime\_secs} for process uptime).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p sys\_uptime                             \# 147951.5}
\NormalTok{my $days = int(sys\_uptime / 86400)}
\NormalTok{p "up $days days"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{t1ha}}{t1ha}}\label{t1ha}

\texttt{t1ha} --- operation \texttt{t1ha}. Category: *b81-misc-utility*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p t1ha $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{t\_digest}}{t\_digest}}\label{t_digest}

\texttt{t\_digest(COMPRESSION=100)} --- streaming-quantile sketch
(Dunning). Larger compression → more centroids, more accuracy, linear
extra memory. Default \texttt{100} gives \textasciitilde1\% error at
p50, \textasciitilde5\% at p99 in O(100) bytes. Pending-buffer +
flush-on-query so per-\texttt{td\_add} is amortized O(1).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $td = t\_digest(200)}
\NormalTok{td\_add($td, $\_) for @latencies\_us}
\NormalTok{printf "p99: \%.1f us\textbackslash{}n", td\_quantile($td, 0.99)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Family: \texttt{td\_add}, \texttt{td\_quantile}, \texttt{td\_count},
\texttt{td\_min}, \texttt{td\_max}, \texttt{td\_sum}, \texttt{td\_mean},
\texttt{td\_merge}, \texttt{td\_clear}, \texttt{td\_serialize},
\texttt{td\_deserialize}. \texttt{td} short alias is reserved for
\texttt{toml\_decode}; use \texttt{tdg}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{t\_test\_one\_sample}}{t\_test\_one\_sample}}\label{t_test_one_sample}

\texttt{t\_test\_one\_sample(\$mu,\ @sample)} (alias \texttt{ttest1})
--- performs a one-sample t-test comparing a sample mean against a
hypothesized population mean mu. Returns {[}t\_statistic,
degrees\_of\_freedom{]}. Use t-distribution tables for p-value lookup.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @sample = (5.1, 4.9, 5.2, 5.0, 4.8)}
\NormalTok{my $result = ttest1(5.0, @sample)}
\NormalTok{p "t=$result{-}\textgreater{}[0] df=$result{-}\textgreater{}[1]"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{t\_test\_paired}}{t\_test\_paired}}\label{t_test_paired}

\texttt{t\_test\_paired(a,\ b)\ →\ \{statistic,\ df,\ p\_value\}} ---
paired t-test on equal-length samples; tests
\texttt{H0:\ mean(a-b)\ =\ 0}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p t\_test\_paired(\textbackslash{}@before, \textbackslash{}@after)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{t\_test\_two\_sample}}{t\_test\_two\_sample}}\label{t_test_two_sample}

\texttt{t\_test\_two\_sample(\textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b)}
(alias \texttt{ttest2}) --- performs an independent two-sample t-test
comparing means of two samples. Assumes equal variances. Returns
{[}t\_statistic, degrees\_of\_freedom{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @a = (5.1, 5.3, 4.9)}
\NormalTok{my @b = (4.2, 4.5, 4.1)}
\NormalTok{my $r = ttest2(\textbackslash{}@a, \textbackslash{}@b)}
\NormalTok{p "t=$r{-}\textgreater{}[0] df=$r{-}\textgreater{}[1]"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tableau\_branch\_close}}{tableau\_branch\_close}}\label{tableau_branch_close}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tapply}}{tapply}}\label{tapply}

\texttt{tapply} --- apply a function by group. Takes data, group labels,
and function. Returns hash. Like R\textquotesingle s tapply().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%means = \%\{tapply([1,2,3,4], ["a","a","b","b"], fn \{ avg(@\{\_0\}) \})\}}
\NormalTok{p $means\{a\}  \# 1.5}
\NormalTok{p $means\{b\}  \# 3.5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

values silently dropped.

*probabilistic data structures*.

\begin{Shaded}

\NormalTok{p td\_clear $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{td\_mean(TD)} --- exact mean of all inserted samples.

\texttt{td\_min(TD)} / \texttt{td\_max(TD)} --- exact min/max (t-digest
tracks both).

\texttt{td\_quantile(TD,\ Q)} → estimated \texttt{Q}-th quantile (0 ≤ Q
≤ 1).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tdilate}}{tdilate}}\label{tdilate}

\texttt{time\_dilation(\$proper\_time,\ \$velocity)} (alias
\texttt{tdilate}) --- dilated time Δt = Δt₀ × γ. Time appears to slow
for moving objects.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p tdilate(1, 0.99 * 3e8)   \# \textasciitilde{}7.09 s (1 second at 99\% c)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tema}}{tema}}\label{tema}

\texttt{tema(prices,\ period=10)\ →\ array} --- triple EMA:
\texttt{3·EMA\ −\ 3·EMA(EMA)\ +\ EMA(EMA(EMA))}. Designed to remove EMA
lag with no extra phase shift.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p tema(\textbackslash{}@prices, 21)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

(0 off-TTY).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{term\_dims}}{term\_dims}}\label{term_dims}

\texttt{term\_size} → \texttt{(cols,\ rows)} of the controlling terminal
via \texttt{TIOCGWINSZ} ioctl. Returns \texttt{(0,\ 0)} when STDOUT
isn\textquotesingle t a TTY (piped output, captured by a parent process,
etc.).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($c, $r) = term\_size()}
\NormalTok{p "$c x $r"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{term\_height}}{term\_height}}\label{term_height}

\texttt{term\_height} → integer row count of the controlling terminal (0
off-TTY).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ternary\_search}}{ternary\_search}}\label{ternary_search}

\texttt{ternary\_search(sorted,\ target)\ →\ int} --- divides search
range into thirds; \texttt{O(log\ n)} like binary search.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p ternary\_search([1,3,5,7,9,11,13], 9)  \# 4}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{to\_hash}}{to\_hash}}\label{to_hash}

\texttt{to\_hash\ LIST} --- collect a flat list of key-value pairs into
a Perl hash.

The list is consumed two elements at a time: odd-positioned elements
become keys and even-positioned elements become values. If there is an
odd number of elements, the last key maps to \texttt{undef}. This is a
terminal operation that materializes the full stream. Useful for
converting pipeline output into a lookup table.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my \%h = qw(a 1 b 2) |\textgreater{} to\_hash}
\NormalTok{@pairs |\textgreater{} to\_hash}
\NormalTok{my \%freq = @words |\textgreater{} map \{ \_, 1 \} |\textgreater{} to\_hash}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{to\_set}}{to\_set}}\label{to_set}

\texttt{to\_set\ LIST} --- collect a list or iterator into a
\texttt{set} object with O(1) membership testing.

The resulting set contains only unique elements (duplicates are
discarded). This is a terminal operation that materializes the full
stream. The returned set supports
\texttt{-\textgreater{}contains(\$val)},
\texttt{-\textgreater{}union(\$other)},
\texttt{-\textgreater{}intersection(\$other)}, and
\texttt{-\textgreater{}difference(\$other)}. Use this when you need fast
repeated lookups against a collection of values.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $s = 1:5 |\textgreater{} to\_set}
\NormalTok{@words |\textgreater{} to\_set                      \# deduplicated set}
\NormalTok{my $allowed = to\_set @whitelist}
\NormalTok{p $allowed{-}\textgreater{}contains("foo")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{token}}{token}}\label{token}

\texttt{token} --- operation \texttt{token}. Category: *id helpers*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p token $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{token\_bucket}}{token\_bucket}}\label{token_bucket}

\texttt{token\_bucket(CAPACITY=100,\ RATE=10)} --- token-bucket rate
limiter. \texttt{CAPACITY} is the max burst; \texttt{RATE} (tokens/sec)
is the steady-state allowance. Refills lazily on each
\texttt{rl\_try\_take} based on wall-clock --- no background thread.
Stateful upgrade over the deleted \texttt{db\_token\_bucket\_step} spec
primitive.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $rl = token\_bucket(100, 50)         \# 50 req/s, burst 100}
\NormalTok{if (rl\_try\_take($rl, 1)) \{ ... \} else \{ sleep(0.02) \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tokenize\_bpe}}{tokenize\_bpe}}\label{tokenize_bpe}

\texttt{tokenize\_bpe(s,\ merges)\ →\ array} --- Byte-Pair Encoding
tokenisation against a merge table.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tokenize\_sentencepiece}}{tokenize\_sentencepiece}}\label{tokenize_sentencepiece}

\texttt{tokenize\_sentencepiece(s)\ →\ array} --- SentencePiece-like
tokenisation; prepends ▁ to word starts.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p tokenize\_sentencepiece("hello world")  \# ["▁hello", "▁world"]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tokenize\_simple}}{tokenize\_simple}}\label{tokenize_simple}

\texttt{tokenize\_simple(s)\ →\ array} --- split on whitespace and basic
punctuation.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tokenize\_subword}}{tokenize\_subword}}\label{tokenize_subword}

\texttt{tokenize\_subword(s,\ max\_len=4)\ →\ array} --- naive subword
tokenisation: splits on non-alphanumeric boundaries, then chunks long
words into pieces of at most \texttt{max\_len} characters.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tokenize\_word}}{tokenize\_word}}\label{tokenize_word}

\texttt{tokenize\_word(s)\ →\ array} --- whitespace tokeniser.

\texttt{top} --- operation \texttt{top}. Category: *pipeline / string
helpers*.

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p top $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{top\_hat\_transform}}{top\_hat\_transform}}\label{top_hat_transform}

\texttt{top\_hat\_transform(image,\ size=3)\ →\ matrix} ---
\texttt{image\ -\ opening}. Extracts bright details smaller than the
structuring element.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{top\_k}}{top\_k}}\label{top_k}

\texttt{topk(K=10)} --- SpaceSaving top-K heavy-hitters sketch. Tracks
exactly \texttt{K} slots; on overflow the lowest-count slot is replaced
with a new key inheriting \texttt{min+1} count and the evicted count as
an error floor. \texttt{topk\_heavies(N)} returns
\texttt{{[}key,\ count,\ err{]}} rows sorted by count desc; truth lies
in \texttt{{[}count\ -\ err,\ count{]}}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $t = topk(10)}
\NormalTok{topk\_add($t, $\_) for @clickstream}
\NormalTok{my @hot = topk\_heavies($t)            \# top 10 by frequency}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{top\_k\_indices}}{top\_k\_indices}}\label{top_k_indices}

\texttt{top\_k\_indices(values,\ k=5)\ →\ array} --- indices of the
\texttt{k} largest values, sorted descending.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p top\_k\_indices([3,1,4,1,5,9,2,6], 3)  \# [5, 7, 4]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{trait}}{trait}}\label{trait}

\texttt{trait} declares an interface that classes can implement via
\texttt{impl}. Traits define method signatures that implementing classes
must provide.

\textbf{Declaration:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{trait Printable \{}
\NormalTok{    fn to\_str          \# required (no body)}
\NormalTok{    fn debug \{ ... \}   \# optional default impl}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Implementation:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{class Item impl Printable \{}
\NormalTok{    name: Str}
\NormalTok{    fn to\_str \{ $self{-}\textgreater{}name \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Multiple traits:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{class Widget impl Printable, Comparable \{}
\NormalTok{    ...}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Note: trait method bodies provide default implementations; classes can
override them.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{trapezoid}}{trapezoid}}\label{trapezoid}

\texttt{trapz} (alias \texttt{trapezoid}) integrates evenly-spaced
samples using the trapezoidal rule. Optional dx (default 1).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @y = map \{ \_ ** 2 \} 0:100}
\NormalTok{p trapz(\textbackslash{}@y, 0.01)  \# ≈ 0.3333}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{trapezoidal\_integrate}}{trapezoidal\_integrate}}\label{trapezoidal_integrate}

\texttt{trapezoidal\_integrate(ys,\ dx=1)\ →\ number} --- composite
trapezoidal rule on pre-computed \texttt{y} samples spaced \texttt{dx}
apart.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p trapezoidal\_integrate([0,1,4,9,16], 1)  \# 16}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tree}}{tree}}\label{tree}

\texttt{tree(PATH=".",\ max\_depth=5)} → recursive directory listing as
a single multi-line string with box-drawing characters (\texttt{├──\ },
\texttt{└──\ }, \texttt{│\ }). Skips hidden entries (leading
\texttt{.}). Sorted alphabetically per directory.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p tree(".", 3)                       \# 3{-}level deep tree of cwd}
\NormalTok{spurt("struct.txt", tree("/etc", 2))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{\{slope,\ intercept\}}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $t = trend\_line(\textbackslash{}@closes); p $t{-}\textgreater{}\{slope\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{trie\_count(trie)\ →\ int} --- number of stored words.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{trie\_insert}}{trie\_insert}}\label{trie_insert}

\texttt{trie\_insert(trie,\ word)\ →\ trie} --- insert word; returns
trie (mutates in place but lets you chain).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $t = trie\_new()}
\NormalTok{$t = trie\_insert($t, "hello")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{trie\_keys}}{trie\_keys}}\label{trie_keys}

\texttt{trie\_keys(trie)\ →\ array} --- all words stored in the trie
(empty prefix search).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{trie\_lookup}}{trie\_lookup}}\label{trie_lookup}

\texttt{trie\_lookup(trie,\ word)\ →\ 0\textbar{}1} --- 1 if word is in
the trie.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{trie\_new}}{trie\_new}}\label{trie_new}

\texttt{trie\_new()\ →\ trie} --- empty trie root:
\texttt{\{end:\ 0,\ children:\ \{\}\}}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{trie\_prefix\_search}}{trie\_prefix\_search}}\label{trie_prefix_search}

\texttt{trie\_prefix\_search(trie,\ prefix)\ →\ array} --- all stored
words sharing the given prefix.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p trie\_prefix\_search($t, "hel")}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{trie\_remove}}{trie\_remove}}\label{trie_remove}

\texttt{trie\_remove(trie,\ word)\ →\ trie} --- clear the \texttt{end}
flag for the given path; keeps structure for shared prefixes.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tty\_cooked}}{tty\_cooked}}\label{tty_cooked}

\texttt{tty\_cooked} --- flip the controlling TTY back into canonical
(line-buffered, echoing) mode. The default state for interactive shells.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{tty\_raw}}{tty\_raw}}\label{tty_raw}

\texttt{tty\_raw} --- flip the controlling TTY into raw mode: ICANON and
ECHO off, no line buffering, no input echo. Used for interactive
selectors and key-by-key input. Returns \texttt{1} on success,
\texttt{0} otherwise. Pair with \texttt{tty\_cooked} to restore.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{tty\_raw();}
\NormalTok{my $key = sysread(STDIN, my $buf, 1) ? $buf : undef;}
\NormalTok{tty\_cooked();}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{ulcer\_index(prices)\ →\ number} --- Peter
Martin\textquotesingle s measure of downside risk:

drawdowns than stddev.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ulid}}{ulid}}\label{ulid}

\texttt{ulid()} → 26-char Crockford-Base32 ULID --- 48-bit ms timestamp
+ 80-bit random. Lexicographically sortable; monotonic within the same
millisecond via a process-wide
\texttt{Mutex\textless{}(last\_ms,\ last\_rand)\textgreater{}}.
World\textquotesingle s-first as scripting-language stdlib builtin:
Python / Ruby / Node all need third-party packages. Use for trace IDs in
\texttt{ai}/\texttt{prompt} workflows where ordered insertion matters.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $trace\_id = ulid()                 \# 01KRJ8YC8AGW5XHM2BKHJ23ZRR}
\NormalTok{is\_ulid($trace\_id) ? "ok" : die}
\NormalTok{p ulid\_timestamp($trace\_id)           \# ms{-}since{-}epoch}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{ulid\_timestamp}}{ulid\_timestamp}}\label{ulid_timestamp}

\texttt{ulid\_timestamp(ULID)} → 48-bit ms-since-Unix-epoch extracted
from the timestamp prefix. Returns \texttt{undef} on malformed input.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{union\_find\_components}}{union\_find\_components}}\label{union_find_components}

\texttt{union\_find\_components(uf)\ →\ int} --- number of disjoint
components.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{union\_find\_find}}{union\_find\_find}}\label{union_find_find}

\texttt{union\_find\_find(uf,\ x)\ →\ int} --- root of x with path
compression.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{union\_find\_new}}{union\_find\_new}}\label{union_find_new}

\texttt{union\_find\_new(n)\ →\ \{parent,\ rank\}} --- disjoint-set
forest of n singletons.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{union\_find\_union}}{union\_find\_union}}\label{union_find_union}

\texttt{union\_find\_union(uf,\ x,\ y)\ →\ uf} --- union by rank.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $uf = union\_find\_new(5)}
\NormalTok{$uf = union\_find\_union($uf, 0, 1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vcross}}{vcross}}\label{vcross}

\texttt{vector\_cross(\textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b)} (alias
\texttt{vcross}) --- computes the cross product of two 3D vectors.
Returns a 3-element arrayref perpendicular to both inputs.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $c = vcross([1,0,0], [0,1,0])}
\NormalTok{p @$c   \# (0, 0, 1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vdot}}{vdot}}\label{vdot}

\texttt{vector\_dot(\textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b)} (alias
\texttt{vdot}) --- computes the dot product of two vectors. Returns the
scalar sum of element-wise products.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p vdot([1,2,3], [4,5,6])   \# 32}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec3\_add}}{vec3\_add}}\label{vec3_add}

\texttt{vec3\_add(a,\ b)\ →\ {[}x,y,z{]}} --- componentwise vector
addition.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p vec3\_add([1,2,3], [4,5,6])  \# [5,7,9]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec3\_cross}}{vec3\_cross}}\label{vec3_cross}

\texttt{vec3\_cross(a,\ b)\ →\ {[}x,y,z{]}} --- cross product
\texttt{a\ ×\ b}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p vec3\_cross([1,0,0], [0,1,0])  \# [0,0,1]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec3\_distance}}{vec3\_distance}}\label{vec3_distance}

\texttt{vec3\_distance(a,\ b)\ →\ number} --- Euclidean distance between
two points.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec3\_dot}}{vec3\_dot}}\label{vec3_dot}

\texttt{vec3\_dot(a,\ b)\ →\ number} --- dot product \texttt{Σ\ aᵢ·bᵢ}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p vec3\_dot([1,0,0], [0,1,0])  \# 0}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec3\_length}}{vec3\_length}}\label{vec3_length}

\texttt{vec3\_length(v)\ →\ number} --- Euclidean magnitude.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p vec3\_length([3,0,4])  \# 5}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec3\_lerp}}{vec3\_lerp}}\label{vec3_lerp}

\texttt{vec3\_lerp(a,\ b,\ t)\ →\ {[}x,y,z{]}} --- linear interpolation
with \texttt{t} clamped to {[}0,1{]}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec3\_normalize}}{vec3\_normalize}}\label{vec3_normalize}

\texttt{vec3\_normalize(v)\ →\ {[}x,y,z{]}} --- unit-length direction;
returns {[}0,0,0{]} for zero vector.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec3\_project}}{vec3\_project}}\label{vec3_project}

\texttt{vec3\_project(v,\ u)\ →\ {[}x,y,z{]}} --- projection of
\texttt{v} onto direction \texttt{u}:
\texttt{(v·u/\textbar{}u\textbar{}²)·u}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec3\_reflect}}{vec3\_reflect}}\label{vec3_reflect}

\texttt{vec3\_reflect(v,\ n)\ →\ {[}x,y,z{]}} --- reflect \texttt{v}
about unit-normal \texttt{n}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec3\_refract}}{vec3\_refract}}\label{vec3_refract}

\texttt{vec3\_refract(v,\ n,\ eta)\ →\ {[}x,y,z{]}} ---
Snell\textquotesingle s law refraction with index ratio \texttt{eta};
returns 0 vector for total internal reflection.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec3\_scale}}{vec3\_scale}}\label{vec3_scale}

\texttt{vec3\_scale(v,\ s)\ →\ {[}x,y,z{]}} --- scalar multiplication.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec3\_sub}}{vec3\_sub}}\label{vec3_sub}

\texttt{vec3\_sub(a,\ b)\ →\ {[}x,y,z{]}} --- componentwise subtraction.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec4\_add}}{vec4\_add}}\label{vec4_add}

\texttt{vec4\_add(a,\ b)\ →\ {[}x,y,z,w{]}} --- 4-component
componentwise addition.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec4\_dot}}{vec4\_dot}}\label{vec4_dot}

\texttt{vec4\_dot(a,\ b)\ →\ number} --- 4-component dot product.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec4\_length}}{vec4\_length}}\label{vec4_length}

\texttt{vec4\_length(v)\ →\ number} --- 4-component magnitude.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec4\_scale}}{vec4\_scale}}\label{vec4_scale}

\texttt{vec4\_scale(v,\ s)\ →\ {[}x,y,z,w{]}} --- scalar multiply.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vec4\_sub}}{vec4\_sub}}\label{vec4_sub}

\texttt{vec4\_sub(a,\ b)\ →\ {[}x,y,z,w{]}} --- 4-component subtraction.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vector\_angle}}{vector\_angle}}\label{vector_angle}

\texttt{vector\_angle(\textbackslash{}@a,\ \textbackslash{}@b)} (alias
\texttt{vangle}) --- computes the angle in radians between two vectors
using the dot product formula.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p vangle([1,0], [0,1])   \# \textasciitilde{}1.5708 (π/2)}
\NormalTok{p vangle([1,0], [1,0])   \# 0}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vector\_magnitude}}{vector\_magnitude}}\label{vector_magnitude}

\texttt{vector\_magnitude(\textbackslash{}@v)} (alias \texttt{vmag}) ---
computes the Euclidean length (L2 norm) of a vector.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p vmag([3, 4])   \# 5}
\NormalTok{p vmag([1, 2, 2])   \# 3}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vector\_mean}}{vector\_mean}}\label{vector_mean}

\texttt{vector\_mean(v)\ →\ number} --- arithmetic mean.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{vector\_normalize}}{vector\_normalize}}\label{vector_normalize}

\texttt{vector\_normalize(\textbackslash{}@v)} (alias \texttt{vnorm})
--- returns a unit vector in the same direction. Divides by magnitude.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $u = vnorm([3, 4])}
\NormalTok{p @$u   \# (0.6, 0.8)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{weingarten\_map\_step} --- single-step update of
\texttt{weingarten\ map}. Category: *electrochemistry, batteries, fuel
cells*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p weingarten\_map\_step $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{welch\_psd\_avg}}{welch\_psd\_avg}}\label{welch_psd_avg}

\texttt{welch\_psd\_avg} --- operation \texttt{welch\ psd\ avg}.
Category: *economics + game theory*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p welch\_psd\_avg $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{welch\_ttest}}{welch\_ttest}}\label{welch_ttest}

\texttt{welch\_ttest} (alias \texttt{welcht}) performs
Welch\textquotesingle s t-test for two independent samples with unequal
variances. Returns {[}t-statistic, degrees of freedom{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my ($t, $df) = @\{welcht([1,2,3,4,5], [3,4,5,6,7])\}}
\NormalTok{p "t=$t df=$df"}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{welch\_window}}{welch\_window}}\label{welch_window}

\texttt{welch\_window} --- operation \texttt{welch\ window}. Category:
*signal processing*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p welch\_window $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{welford\_mean}}{welford\_mean}}\label{welford_mean}

\texttt{welford\_mean\ LIST} --- single-pass online mean via
Welford\textquotesingle s algorithm. Numerically stable for long streams
where naive \texttt{sum\ /\ len} overflows or loses precision.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{welford\_pop\_variance}}{welford\_pop\_variance}}\label{welford_pop_variance}

\texttt{welford\_pop\_variance\ LIST} --- population variance (n
denominator), counterpart to \texttt{welford\_variance} (sample, n-1).

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{welford\_sd}}{welford\_sd}}\label{welford_sd}

\texttt{welford\_stddev\ LIST} --- sqrt of \texttt{welford\_variance};
numerically-stable sample standard deviation.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{welford\_var}}{welford\_var}}\label{welford_var}

\texttt{welford\_variance\ LIST} --- single-pass sample variance (n-1
denominator) via Welford\textquotesingle s algorithm.

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p wh $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{wha}}{wha}}\label{wha}

Return a list of all absolute paths matching the given command name
across every directory in \texttt{PATH}, not just the first match. The
short alias \texttt{wha} keeps things concise. This is useful for
detecting shadowed executables or auditing which versions of a tool are
installed.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @all = which\_all("python3")}
\NormalTok{@all |\textgreater{} e p                \# /usr/local/bin/python3}
\NormalTok{                             \# /usr/bin/python3}
\NormalTok{p scalar wha("perl")        \# number of perls on PATH}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{when\_false}}{when\_false}}\label{when_false}

\texttt{when\_false} --- operation \texttt{when\ false}. Category:
*functional combinators*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p when\_false $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{when\_true}}{when\_true}}\label{when_true}

\texttt{when\_true} --- operation \texttt{when\ true}. Category:
*functional combinators*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p when\_true $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{whereis}}{whereis}}\label{whereis}

\texttt{whereis(CMD)} → list of every match for \texttt{CMD} on
\texttt{\$PATH}, then every man-page file in \texttt{\$MANPATH} (or
\texttt{/usr/share/man} if unset). Returns an array of absolute paths.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @hits = whereis("git")}
\NormalTok{\# /usr/bin/git, /usr/share/man/man1/git.1, …}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{which\_fn}}{which\_fn}}\label{which_fn}

\texttt{which\_fn} --- return indices where a predicate is true. Like
R\textquotesingle s which().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @idx = @\{which\_fn([1,5,3,8,2], fn \{ \_0 \textgreater{} 3 \})\}  \# [1, 3]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{whoami}}{whoami}}\label{whoami}

\texttt{whoami} → current user name. Wraps \texttt{libc::getuid()} + the
existing \texttt{getpwuid} lookup. Returns the bare string username (NOT
the passwd-list array that \texttt{getpwuid} returns in list context).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p "running as: " . whoami}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Williams \%R:
\texttt{-100\ ·\ (highest\_high\ −\ close)\ /\ (highest\_high\ −\ lowest\_low)}.
Mirrors stochastic-K in {[}-100, 0{]}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p williams\_r(\textbackslash{}@hi, \textbackslash{}@lo, \textbackslash{}@cl, 14)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{window\_blackman\_harris(n)\ →\ array} --- 4-term
Blackman-Harris window. Very low side-lobes (\textasciitilde-92 dB).

\texttt{window\_flat\_top(n)\ →\ array} --- flat-top window. Trades
wider main-lobe for flat passband (amplitude calibration).
