\texttt{window\_gaussian(n,\ sigma=0.4)\ →\ array} --- Gaussian window
of length \texttt{n}.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{work}}{work}}\label{work}

F×d×cos(θ) (Joules). Angle in degrees, default 0.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p work(100, 10)       \# 1000 J (force parallel to motion)}
\NormalTok{p work(100, 10, 60)   \# 500 J (force at 60°)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{xargs}}{xargs}}\label{xargs}

\texttt{xargs(\textbackslash{}\&CALLABLE,\ \textbackslash{}@ARGS)} →
invoke \texttt{CALLABLE} once per element of
\texttt{\textbackslash{}@ARGS}, returning a list of results. Sequential;
for parallel use \texttt{pmap} directly. Composes naturally with
\texttt{tac} / \texttt{comm} / pipe forwarding.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @hashes = xargs(\textbackslash{}\&sha256, \textbackslash{}@filenames)}
\NormalTok{xargs(\textbackslash{}\&open\_url, ["https://a.com", "https://b.com"])}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

attribute value.

\texttt{xattr\_list(path)\ →\ array} --- names of all extended
attributes.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{xattr\_set}}{xattr\_set}}\label{xattr_set}

\texttt{xattr\_set(path,\ name,\ value)\ →\ 0\textbar{}1} --- write an
extended attribute.

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{xirr}}{xirr}}\label{xirr}

\texttt{xirr(\textbackslash{}@cashflows,\ \textbackslash{}@dates)} ---
computes IRR for irregularly-spaced cash flows. Dates can be epoch
timestamps or date strings. More accurate than \texttt{irr} for
real-world investments.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @cf = ({-}1000, 500, 600)}
\NormalTok{my @dates = (\textquotesingle{}2020{-}01{-}01\textquotesingle{}, \textquotesingle{}2020{-}07{-}01\textquotesingle{}, \textquotesingle{}2021{-}01{-}01\textquotesingle{})}
\NormalTok{p xirr(\textbackslash{}@cf, \textbackslash{}@dates)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{xxd}}{xxd}}\label{xxd}

\texttt{xxd\ PATH} --- hex dump (\texttt{xxd(1)} layout: offset, paired
hex, ASCII). Optional second positional sets bytes per line (default 16,
max 32). Trailing hash: \texttt{cols\ =\textgreater{}\ N},
\texttt{max\ =\textgreater{}\ BYTES} (default 1\_048\_576 bytes read).
No path uses \texttt{\$\_}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p xxd("Cargo.toml", 8)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{z\_score}}{z\_score}}\label{z_score}

\texttt{z\_score(\$value,\ \$mean,\ \$stddev)} --- computes the z-score
(standard score) of a value given the population mean and standard
deviation. Returns how many standard deviations the value is from the
mean. Useful for standardizing data and detecting outliers.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my $z = z\_score(85, 75, 10)}
\NormalTok{p $z   \# 1 (one stddev above mean)}
\NormalTok{p z\_score(50, 60, 5)   \# {-}2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{z\_score\_robust}}{z\_score\_robust}}\label{z_score_robust}

\texttt{z\_score\_robust} --- operation \texttt{z\ score\ robust}.
Category: *misc*.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p z\_score\_robust $x}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{z\_scores}}{z\_scores}}\label{z_scores}

\texttt{z\_scores(@data)} --- computes z-scores for all values in a
list, returning an arrayref of standardized values. Each z-score
indicates how many standard deviations that value is from the mean of
the dataset. Useful for comparing values across different scales.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{my @grades = (70, 80, 90)}
\NormalTok{my $zs = z\_scores(@grades)}
\NormalTok{p @$zs   \# ({-}1, 0, 1)}
\NormalTok{@data |\textgreater{} z\_scores |\textgreater{} e p}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{\texorpdfstring{\texttt{zernike\_radial}}{zernike\_radial}}\label{zernike_radial}

\texttt{zernike\_radial(n,\ m,\ rho)\ →\ number} --- Zernike radial
polynomial \texttt{R\_n\^{}\textbar{}m\textbar{}(ρ)}. Returns 0 if
\texttt{(n−\textbar{}m\textbar{})} is odd or
\texttt{\textbar{}m\textbar{}\ \textgreater{}\ n}. Negative \texttt{m}
is accepted and uses \texttt{\textbar{}m\textbar{}} per the convention
\texttt{R\_n\^{}m\ =\ R\_n\^{}\{−m\}}.

\texttt{zero\_sum\_value(payoff)\ →\ number} --- value of a two-player
zero-sum game. Returns the saddle-point value when maximin == minmax,
the 2×2 mixed-strategy value (ad − bc)/(a + d − b − c) otherwise, or
UNDEF for larger matrices with no saddle (caller should use LP).

\texttt{zigzag\_encode(n)\ →\ int} --- map signed int to unsigned via
Protobuf ZigZag:
\texttt{(n\ \textless{}\textless{}\ 1)\ \^{}\ (n\ \textgreater{}\textgreater{}\ 63)}.
Lets small magnitudes pack into few bytes.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p zigzag\_encode({-}1)  \# 1}
\NormalTok{p zigzag\_encode(1)   \# 2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}
